Abstract: Recent Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in video reasoning through reinforcement learning (RL). However, existing RL pipelines rely heavily on human-annotated tasks and solutions, making them costly to scale and fundamentally constrained by human expertise. Self-evolving frameworks have recently emerged as a promising alternative through autonomous Questioner-Solver self-play. Unfortunately, these approaches are primarily designed for static modalities such as text and images, fundamentally failing to capture the temporal dynamics that are central to video reasoning. In this work, we propose $\textbf{EvoVid}$, a temporal-centric self-evolving framework that enables Video-LLMs to improve directly from raw, unannotated videos. Specifically, we introduce two complementary temporal-centric rewards: a temporal-aware Questioner reward that encourages temporally dependent question generation through temporal perturbation sensitivity, and a temporal-grounded Solver reward that provides automatic temporal supervision via inherent video segment localization. Extensive experiments across four base models and six benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over both base models and existing self-evolving baselines, achieving competitive performance with supervised methods. These results highlight temporal-centric self-evolution as an effective and scalable paradigm for video understanding and reasoning.

</details>


### [28] [MLLMs Know When Before Speaking: Revealing and Recovering Temporal Grounding via Attention Cues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21954)
*Dazhao Du,Liao Duan,Jian Liu,Tao Han,Yujia Zhang,Eric Liu,Xi Chen,Song Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MLLMs know when events happen during prefill (via Temporal Grounding Heads) but lose this signal during decoding; the paper proposes a read-then-regenerate inference framework that extracts the grounding interval from prefill attention and re-generates answers with restricted visual context, yielding consistent VTG improvements without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video temporal grounding (VTG) is a critical benchmark for MLLMs, but existing models produce unreliable timestamp predictions. Post-training on temporal annotations is costly, and training-free heuristics are coarse. The paper aims to bridge this gap without parameter updates or architectural changes.

Method: 1) Probe cross-modal attention and discover Temporal Grounding Heads (TG-Heads) that attend to ground-truth intervals during prefill. 2) Convert TG-Head prefill attention into a debiased frame-level relevance signal to extract the high-attention interval. 3) Re-invoke the MLLM with visual context restricted to this interval via video cropping or attention masking to suppress distractors.

Result: Consistent improvements on MiMo-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B, and TimeLens-8B across three VTG benchmarks, with gains up to +3.5 mIoU.

Conclusion: The perception-generation gap is a key limitation of MLLMs for temporal grounding. The proposed inference-time read-then-regenerate framework effectively mitigates this gap, showing that the grounding signal exists in prefill attention but is lost during autoregressive decoding, and can be recovered without retraining.

Abstract: Video temporal grounding (VTG), which localizes the start and end times of a queried event in an untrimmed video, is a key test of whether multimodal large language models (MLLMs) understand not only what happens but also when it happens. Although modern MLLMs describe video content fluently, their timestamp predictions remain unreliable, while existing remedies either require costly post-training on temporal annotations or rely on coarse training-free heuristics. In this work, we probe the cross-modal attention of MLLMs and uncover a perception-generation gap. Our key finding is that MLLMs often know the target interval during prefill, but lose this signal when generating the final answer. In the prefill stage, a sparse set of attention heads, which we call \emph{Temporal Grounding Heads} (TG-Heads), concentrates query-to-video attention on the ground-truth interval. During autoregressive decoding, however, the answer tokens shift attention away from this interval toward visually salient but query-irrelevant segments. This observation motivates an inference-time read-then-regenerate framework. We first convert TG-Head prefill attention into a debiased frame-level relevance signal and extract the high-attention interval it highlights. We then re-invoke the MLLM with visual context restricted to this interval, using video cropping or attention masking to suppress distractors. Without parameter updates and architectural changes, our framework consistently improves MiMo-VL-7B, Qwen3-VL-8B, and TimeLens-8B on three VTG benchmarks, with gains of up to +3.5 mIoU. The project website can be found at https://ddz16.github.io/mllmsknowwhen.github.io/.

</details>


### [29] [Bounding-Box Trajectories Matter for Video Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21957)
*Inpyo Song,Jangwon Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TrajVAD uses bounding-box trajectories (previously overlooked) as a primary cue for video anomaly detection, outperforming pose-based methods. TrajVAD-T (trajectory-only) achieves 87.7% AP on ShanghaiTech, and TrajVAD-P (with pose) reaches 88.6% AUROC and 90.9% AP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pose-based anomaly detection methods overlook bounding-box trajectories even though they are inherently available. The authors aim to leverage these trajectories as a primary signal for detecting anomalies.

Method: TrajVAD models multi-class bounding-box trajectories using normalizing flows to learn normal kinematic patterns. Two variants: TrajVAD-T (trajectory-only, no pose) and TrajVAD-P (incorporates pose information).

Result: TrajVAD-T surpasses all compared pose-based methods on ShanghaiTech with 87.7% AP, and achieves best results on MSAD. TrajVAD-P further improves to 88.6% AUROC and 90.9% AP on ShanghaiTech.

Conclusion: Bounding-box trajectories are an effective yet underexplored modality for video anomaly detection, offering strong performance even without pose information.

Abstract: Video anomaly detection is critical for public safety and security, yet remains highly challenging despite extensive research due to large variations in appearance, viewpoint, and scene dynamics. Among existing approaches, human pose-based methods have emerged as a major line of research, showing strong performance since many anomalies in public datasets involve humans and pose representations are robust to appearance changes while providing compact motion descriptions. However, these methods often overlook bounding-box trajectories, although such information is inherently available in pose-based pipelines. In this paper, we explicitly leverage these trajectories as a primary anomaly cue. We present TrajVAD, a framework that models multi-class bounding-box trajectories using normalizing flows to learn normal kinematic patterns. Its trajectory-only variant (TrajVAD-T) eliminates pose estimation and surpasses all compared pose-based methods on ShanghaiTech in AP (87.7%), while achieving the best results on MSAD. An extended version (TrajVAD-P) incorporates pose information and further improves performance to 88.6% AUROC and 90.9% AP on ShanghaiTech, highlighting bounding-box trajectories as an effective yet underexplored modality for video anomaly detection.

</details>


### [30] [Dual-Integrated Low-Latency Single-Lens Infrared Computational Imaging for Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21964)
*Xuquan Wang,Guishuo Yang,Dapeng Yan,Yujie Xing,Xuanyu Qian,Kai Zhang,Xiong Dun,Jiande Sun,Zhanshan Wang,Xinbin Cheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PDI-Net is a low-latency framework that integrates infrared image reconstruction and object detection, using optical priors (PALS-Bridge) to avoid full image reconstruction during inference, achieving 84% faster inference and 5% better mAP on M3FD benchmark while reducing system weight by 50%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep-learning pipelines for computational infrared imaging that combine reconstruction and detection suffer from substantial inference latency. Current acceleration strategies compress the reconstruction network but overlook physical priors from the optical path, creating a trade-off between accuracy and speed.

Method: PDI-Net uses a supervised U-Net during training, but during inference, a semi-U-Net encoder shares features directly with a YOLO-based detector (bypassing full image reconstruction). A physics-aware large-small bridge (PALS-Bridge) modulates multiscale convolutional branches using field-dependent point spread function priors to bridge fidelity-oriented features and detection semantics. A physics-informed optical degradation simulation pipeline is also used.

Result: Deployed on a single-lens infrared camera (50% lighter than multi-lens designs). On M3FD benchmark under low-SNR conditions, PDI-Net reduces inference time by 84.06% vs. Rec+Det with pruning, while improving mAP@0.5:0.95 by 5.07%.

Conclusion: PDI-Net demonstrates that embedding optical priors into a low-latency dual-integrated framework enables compact, efficient computational infrared imaging for real-time object detection on resource-constrained platforms.

Abstract: Computational imaging enables compact infrared systems, but deep-learning pipelines that combine image reconstruction and object detection often introduce substantial inference latency. Most existing acceleration strategies compress the reconstruction network while overlooking physical priors from the optical path, leaving a trade-off between accuracy and speed. We present Physics-aware Dual-Integrated Network (PDI-Net), a low-latency framework that integrates infrared reconstruction with object detection and further embeds optical priors into the learning process. PDI-Net uses a supervised U-Net during training, while a semi-U-Net encoder shares features directly with a YOLO-based detector during inference, avoiding full image reconstruction. To bridge the gap between fidelity-oriented reconstruction features and detection-oriented semantics, we introduce a physics-aware large-small bridge (PALS-Bridge), which uses field-dependent point spread function priors to adaptively modulate multiscale convolutional branches. A physics-informed optical degradation simulation pipeline is also developed for training and validation. The method is deployed on a single-lens infrared camera, reducing system weight by about 50% compared with traditional multi-lens designs. On the M3FD benchmark under low-SNR conditions, PDI-Net reduces inference time by 84.06% compared with the Rec+Det with pruning strategy while improving mAP@0.5:0.95 by 5.07%. These results demonstrate compact, low-latency computational infrared imaging for real-time object detection on resource-constrained platforms.

</details>


### [31] [Foresee-to-Ground: From Predictive Temporal Perception to Evidence-Driven Reasoning for Video Temporal Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21973)
*Zelin Zheng,Xinyan Liu,Ruixin Li,Antoni B. Chan,Guorong Li,Qingming Huang,Laiyun Qing*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: F2G reformulates Video Temporal Grounding as a verifiable Identify-then-Measure problem by integrating Predictive Temporal Perception with Evidence-Driven Reasoning, building candidate event segments as citable evidence units for LLMs, which stabilizes grounding and improves accuracy across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Video-LLM approaches for VTG rely on direct timestamp generation from unstructured visual-token streams, leading to brittle numerics and inconsistent boundaries.

Method: F2G decouples event identification from boundary measurement by (1) learning boundary-sensitive temporal representations to build a video-wide evidence pool of candidate event segments, and (2) exposing these segments to the LLM as citable evidence units that bind boundary prediction to explicit event hypotheses.

Result: F2G consistently improves grounding accuracy across diverse benchmarks, transfers robustly across different Video-LLM backbones, and preserves general video understanding capabilities.

Conclusion: The Identify-then-Measure paradigm with evidence-driven reasoning stabilizes VTG and makes predictions verifiable, offering a robust alternative to direct timestamp generation.

Abstract: Current Video-LLM approaches for Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) typically rely on direct timestamp generation from an unstructured visual-token stream, often leading to brittle numerics and inconsistent boundaries. To address this, we propose Foresee-to-Ground (F2G), a framework that reformulates VTG as a verifiable Identify-then-Measure problem. F2G integrates Predictive Temporal Perception with Evidence-Driven Reasoning: it learns boundary-sensitive temporal representations to build a video-wide evidence pool of candidate event segments, and exposes these segments to the LLM as citable evidence units that bind boundary prediction to explicit event hypotheses. By decoupling event identification from precise boundary measurement, F2G stabilizes grounding and makes predictions verifiable. Extensive experiments demonstrate that F2G consistently improves grounding accuracy across diverse benchmarks, transfers robustly across different Video-LLM backbones, and preserves general video understanding capabilities.

</details>


### [32] [Video as Natural Augmentation: Towards Unified AI-Generated Image and Video Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21977)
*Zhengcen Li,Chenyang Jiang,Liangxu Su,Tong Shao,Shiyang Zhou,Ming Tao,Jingyong Su*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies that SOTA AI-generated image detectors fail on video frames due to synthesis-agnostic processing shifts and model fingerprints. The authors propose VINA, a unified detection framework using video frames as natural augmentations with cross-modal contrastive learning, achieving SOTA on 14 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI-generated image detectors collapse when applied to video frames due to cross-modal gaps from video processing shifts (color conversion, codec compression, resizing, blur) and model-specific video generator fingerprints.

Method: VINA (Video as Natural Augmentation): a unified detection framework that jointly trains on images and videos, using video frames as physically grounded natural augmentations with a cross-modal supervised contrastive objective to align image/video representations under a shared real/fake decision boundary.

Result: Evaluated on 14 image, video, and in-the-wild benchmarks, VINA delivers bidirectional gains, improves robustness and transferability, achieving SOTA across nearly all settings without complex augmentation or dataset-specific tuning.

Conclusion: Using video frames as natural augmentations with cross-modal contrastive learning effectively bridges the cross-modal gap for AIGC detection, enabling strong generalization without dataset-specific adjustments.

Abstract: AI-generated content (AIGC) is rapidly improving, creating an urgent need for detectors that generalize across data sources, deployment pipelines, and visual modalities. A strongly generalizable detector should remain robust under distributional variations. However, we identify a consistent failure mode: SOTA AI-generated image detectors often collapse when applied to frames extracted from videos. Through systematic analysis, we show that this cross-modal gap arises from both entangled synthesis-agnostic video processing shifts, including color conversion, codec compression, resizing, and blur, and model-specific fingerprints introduced by modern video generators. Motivated by these findings, we propose VINA (Video as Natural Augmentation), a unified AIGC detection framework that jointly trains on image and video data. VINA uses video frames as physically grounded natural augmentations and further introduces a cross-modal supervised contrastive objective to align image and video representations under a shared real/fake decision boundary. Extensive experiments on 14 image, video, and in-the-wild benchmarks show that VINA delivers bidirectional gains, improves robustness and transferability, and achieves state-of-the-art performance across nearly all evaluated settings without complex augmentation or dataset-specific tuning.

</details>


### [33] [Interpreting and Enhancing Emotional Circuits in Large Vision-Language Models via Cross-Modal Information Flow](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21980)
*Chengsheng Zhang,Chenghao Sun,Zhining Xie,Xinmei Tian*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework to analyze how LVLMs process emotional information through a three-stage "Adapt-Aggregate-Execute" mechanism. The authors discover functional decoupling where visual emotional cues are aggregated in middle layers via sentiment-specific attention heads, then translated into narrative generation in deep layers via emotion-general pathways. They use this insight to regulate emotional information routing, improving performance and reducing emotional hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap in understanding how LVLMs internally translate abstract visual stimuli into coherent emotional narratives, which remains unexplored due to scarcity of visual counterfactuals and diffuse nature of emotional expression.

Method: Steering-vector-based causal attribution framework; specialized dataset construction; three-stage "Adapt-Aggregate-Execute" mechanism analysis; inference-time intervention to regulate emotional information routing.

Result: Discovery of functional decoupling in emotional circuits; significant performance improvement on MER-UniBench; effective mitigation of emotional hallucinations through inference-time intervention.

Conclusion: The discovered circuits have causal fidelity, and regulating emotional information routing can effectively improve LVLM emotional reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) represent a significant leap towards empathetic agents, demonstrating remarkable capabilities in emotion understanding. However, the internal mechanisms governing how LVLMs translate abstract visual stimuli into coherent emotional narratives remain largely unexplored, primarily due to the scarcity of visual counterfactuals and the diffuse nature of emotional expression. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing a steering-vector-based causal attribution framework tailored for descriptive emotional reasoning. To this end, we construct a specialized dataset to demystify the emotional circuits underlying the three-stage ``Adapt-Aggregate-Execute'' mechanism. Crucially, we discover a functional decoupling: visual emotional cues are aggregated in middle layers via sentiment-specific attention heads, but are subsequently translated into narrative generation in deep layers through emotion-general pathways. Guided by these insights, we regulate the emotional information routing to strengthen attention flow and amplify the semantic activation to consolidate expression. Extensive experiments on the comprehensive MER-UniBench demonstrate that our methods significantly improve performance via inference-time intervention, effectively mitigating emotional hallucinations and corroborating the causal fidelity of the discovered circuits.

</details>


### [34] [RiT: Vanilla Diffusion Transformers Suffice in Representation Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21981)
*Le Zhang,Ning Mang,Aishwarya Agrawal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RiT trains a vanilla Diffusion Transformer with x-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, achieving strong FID scores (1.45 unconditional, 1.14 with guidance) on ImageNet 256×256 while being 19% smaller than DiT-DH-XL, enabled by DINOv2's superior statistical properties (higher effective rank, better conditioning, lower kurtosis).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether pretrained representation spaces (beyond pixel space) provide more favorable distributions for flow-matching learning, and to exploit DINOv2's statistical advantages to simplify prior diffusion methods.

Method: Compare pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 feature spaces along four geometric axes; propose RiT — a vanilla Diffusion Transformer using x-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features with a dimension-aware noise schedule and joint [CLS]-patch modeling.

Result: DINOv2 has 7.3× higher effective rank, 35× better covariance conditioning, 11.5× lower excess kurtosis, 1.7× lower on-manifold interpolation error vs pixel. RiT achieves FID 1.45 (no guidance) and 1.14 (with CFG) on ImageNet 256×256, outperforming DiT-DH-XL with 19% fewer parameters; 5 Heun steps reach FID 2.0 with CFG.

Conclusion: Representation spaces from pretrained models (especially DINOv2) offer statistically superior distributions for flow matching, enabling simpler architectures and efficient coarse-step sampling without distillation.

Abstract: Flow matching with $x$-prediction -- regressing the clean data point rather than the ambient velocity -- is known to exploit low-dimensional manifold structure effectively in pixel space \cite{li2025back}. We ask whether a pretrained representation space, while containing a low-dimensional data manifold of comparable intrinsic dimensionality, offers a distribution more favorable for flow-matching learning. Comparing pixel, SD-VAE, and DINOv2 features along four geometric axes, we find that pixel and DINOv2 share nearly identical intrinsic dimensionalities (both $\hat{d}\!\approx\!33$) yet DINOv2 exhibits $7.3\times$ higher effective rank, $35\times$ better covariance conditioning, $11.5\times$ lower excess kurtosis, and $1.7\times$ lower on-manifold interpolation error; SD-VAE latents are consistently intermediate, indicating that the advantage stems from representation-learning objectives rather than mere compression. These statistical properties render the flow-matching regression well-conditioned and remove the need for the specialized prediction heads or Riemannian transport used by prior DINOv2 diffusion methods. We propose the \emph{Representation Image Transformer} (RiT): a vanilla Diffusion Transformer trained by $x$-prediction on frozen DINOv2 features, augmented only by a dimension-aware noise schedule and joint \texttt{[CLS]}-patch modeling. On ImageNet $256{\times}256$, RiT attains FID 1.45 without guidance and 1.14 with classifier-free guidance, outperforming DiT$^\text{DH}$-XL with $19\%$ fewer parameters (676M vs.\ 839M). The resulting ODE is efficiently solvable at coarse discretizations: with classifier-free guidance, $5$ Heun steps already reach FID 2.0 and $10$ steps reach 1.25, without distillation or consistency training. Code at https://github.com/lezhang7/RiT.

</details>


### [35] [Learning Spatiotemporal Sensitivity in Video LLMs via Counterfactual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21988)
*Dazhao Du,Jian Liu,Jialong Qin,Tao Han,Bohai Gu,Fangqi Zhu,Yujia Zhang,Eric Liu,Xi Chen,Song Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CRPO is a dual-branch RL framework that improves spatiotemporal sensitivity in Video LLMs by training on original and counterfactual videos (horizontal flips, temporal reversals) with a Counterfactual Relation Reward that encourages answer changes for dynamic questions and stability for static ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video LLMs often rely on shortcuts (single-frame cues, language priors) rather than tracking spatiotemporal dynamics, and RL post-training with correctness-only rewards can reinforce these shortcut policies.

Method: CRPO uses a dual-branch RL framework: (1) constructs counterfactual videos via horizontal flips and temporal reversals, (2) trains on both original and counterfactual branches, (3) introduces Counterfactual Relation Reward (CRR) that rewards answer changes for dynamic questions and answer consistency for static questions.

Result: On Qwen3-VL-8B, CRPO improves DyBench P-Acc by +7.7 and TimeBlind I-Acc by +8.2 over the base model, outperforming prior RL methods on spatiotemporal-sensitive evaluations while maintaining general video performance.

Conclusion: CRPO effectively improves spatiotemporal sensitivity in Video LLMs by using counterfactual reasoning to prevent shortcut policies, validated through the new DyBench benchmark with paired counterfactual videos.

Abstract: Video large language models (Video LLMs) achieve strong benchmark accuracy, yet often answer video questions through shortcuts such as single-frame cues and language priors rather than by tracking spatiotemporal dynamics. This issue is exacerbated in RL post-training, where correctness-only rewards can further reinforce shortcut policies that obtain high reward without tracking video dynamics. We address this by asking a controlled counterfactual question: if the visual world changed while the question remained fixed, should the answer change or stay the same? Based on this view, we propose \textbf{Counterfactual Relational Policy Optimization (CRPO)}, a dual-branch RL framework for improving \emph{spatiotemporal sensitivity}. CRPO constructs counterfactual videos through horizontal flips and temporal reversals, trains on both original and counterfactual branches, and introduces a \textbf{Counterfactual Relation Reward (CRR)} between their answers. CRR encourages answers to change for dynamic questions and remain unchanged for static questions. This cross-branch constraint makes it difficult for shortcut policies to be consistently rewarded across both branches. To evaluate this property, we introduce \textbf{DyBench}, a paired counterfactual video benchmark with 3,014 videos covering reversible dynamics, moving direction, and event sequence, together with a strict pair-accuracy metric that prevents fixed-answer shortcuts from inflating scores. Experiments show that CRPO outperforms prior RL methods on spatiotemporal-sensitive evaluations while maintaining competitive general video performance. On Qwen3-VL-8B, CRPO improves DyBench P-Acc by +7.7 and TimeBlind I-Acc by +8.2 over the base model, indicating improved spatiotemporal sensitivity rather than stronger reliance on static shortcuts. The project website can be found at https://ddz16.github.io/crpo.github.io/ .

</details>


### [36] [Virtual 3D H&E Staining from Phase-contrast Back-illumination Interference Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22000)
*Anthony Song,Boyan Zhou,Mayank Golhar,Marisa Morakis,Alex Baras,Nicholas Durr*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HistoBIT3D introduces the first voxel-wise paired dataset and a virtual staining framework to convert BIT phase microscopy volumes into realistic H&E-like 3D images, achieving state-of-the-art realism and improved nuclei segmentation accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: BIT provides rapid, non-destructive 3D imaging of unprocessed tissues, but translating BIT volumes into clinically interpretable H&E images is challenging due to shift-variant contrast and lack of quantitative validation benchmarks.

Method: A novel virtual staining framework using bidirectional multiscale content consistency and cross-domain style reuse, trained on the first voxel-wise paired BIT and fluorescence-labeled nuclei dataset.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art realism metrics and significantly improves 3D nuclei segmentation accuracy and boundary preservation under zero-shot Cellpose evaluation.

Conclusion: Establishes a quantitatively validated, structurally faithful, and scalable pipeline for 3D virtual H&E staining, advancing slide-free volumetric computational histopathology.

Abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) histopathology of unprocessed tissues has the potential to transform disease management by enabling volumetric characterization of tissue microarchitecture and in-vivo assessment. Back-illumination Interference Tomography (BIT) is a new phase microscopy technology that provides rapid, non-destructive volumetric imaging of unprocessed tissues. However, translating BIT volumes into clinically interpretable H&E images remains challenging, particularly due to shift-variant contrast and the absence of quantitative validation benchmarks. We introduce HistoBIT3D, the first voxel-wise paired BIT and fluorescence-labeled nuclei dataset, enabling quantitative evaluation of structural preservation in unsupervised virtual staining against ground-truth nuclear distributions. Using this dataset, we present a novel virtual staining framework that translates BIT volumes with shift-variant contrast into realistic H&E volumes by leveraging bidirectional multiscale content consistency and cross-domain style reuse to enhance structural fidelity and perceptual realism. Our method achieves state-of-the-art realism metrics while significantly improving 3D nuclei segmentation accuracy and boundary preservation under zero-shot Cellpose evaluation. Together, these contributions establish a quantitatively validated, structurally faithful, and scalable pipeline for 3D virtual H&E staining, advancing the paradigm of slide-free, volumetric computational histopathology. Our data and code are available at: https://github.com/aasong113/HistoBIT3D_VirtualStaining.

</details>


### [37] [ConvNeXt-FD: A Fractal-Based Deep Model for Robust Biomedical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22002)
*Joao Batista Florindo,Amanda Pontes de Oliveira Ornelas*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ConvNeXt-FD is a U-Net-like encoder-decoder architecture using ConvNeXt backbone with a hybrid loss function (Dice + differentiable Fractal Dimension regularization) for robust biomedical image segmentation, achieving superior results across six diverse datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Biomedical image segmentation faces challenges due to variability, noise, and complex morphology in medical images. Existing methods struggle to capture both high-level semantic features and fine-grained boundary details simultaneously.

Method: Proposes ConvNeXt-FD: a U-Net-like encoder-decoder with ConvNeXt backbone, using a hybrid loss combining Dice coefficient with a boundary-aware regularization term based on differentiable Fractal Dimension formulation.

Result: Evaluated on six datasets (BUSI, DDTI, FluoCells, IDRiD, ISIC2018, MoNuSeg). Achieves competitive/superior performance across Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and FPR. ImageNet pre-training further boosts results.

Conclusion: ConvNeXt-FD effectively integrates a strong backbone encoder with boundary-aware regularization, yielding accurate and reliable segmentations in challenging biomedical contexts.

Abstract: Biomedical image segmentation is a critical task in medical diagnosis and treatment planning, enabling precise delineation of anatomical structures and pathological regions. Despite significant advancements, challenges persist due to the inherent variability, noise, and complex morphology present in diverse medical imaging modalities. This paper introduces ConvNeXt-FD, a novel deep learning architecture for robust biomedical image segmentation, built upon a U-Net-like encoder-decoder framework leveraging the powerful ConvNeXt backbone. Our approach integrates a hybrid loss function combining the Dice coefficient with a boundary-aware regularization term inspired by a differentiable formulation of Fractal Dimension, designed to enhance the model's sensitivity to object boundaries and shape fidelity. We rigorously evaluate ConvNeXt-FD across six distinct biomedical datasets: BUSI (Breast Ultrasound Images), DDTI (Thyroid Ultrasound Images), FluoCells (Fluorescent Cell Images), IDRiD (Diabetic Retinopathy Images for Optic Disc Segmentation), ISIC2018 (Skin Lesion Images), and MoNuSeg (Nuclei Segmentation). Experimental results demonstrate that ConvNeXt-FD, particularly when initialized with ImageNet pre-trained weights, achieves competitive and often superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art methods across various metrics, including Dice, Jaccard, Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, and False Positive Rate. The integration of ConvNeXt as a strong encoder, coupled with the boundary-aware regularization, proves effective in capturing both high-level semantic features and fine-grained boundary details, leading to more accurate and reliable segmentations in challenging biomedical contexts.

</details>


### [38] [Rethinking Token Reduction for Diffusion Models via Output-Similarity-Awareness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22011)
*Hangyeol Lee,Hyojeong Lee,Joo-Young Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DiTo is a novel token reduction paradigm for Diffusion Transformers that focuses on output token similarity, using prior-step similarities as a proxy and introducing PMR-guided Interval Scheduling and Frequency-aware Token Matching to achieve better image quality (1.6-3.9 dB higher PSNR) at comparable speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token reduction methods for DiTs rely on input token similarity inherited from ViT paradigms, which misaligns with the primary objective of generative models—minimizing recovery error. This oversight leads to suboptimal performance.

Method: DiTo shifts to output-centric token reduction by: (1) using prior-step output token similarities as a proxy at a Matching timestep, reused across multiple Reduction timesteps; (2) PMR-guided Interval Scheduling to optimize matching frequency; (3) Frequency-aware Token Matching with a selection-frequency penalty to mitigate blocking artifacts from repeated reuse.

Result: DiTo consistently outperforms existing TR methods with 1.6-3.9 dB higher PSNR at comparable speedups, achieving a superior Pareto frontier.

Conclusion: The output-centric token reduction paradigm effectively bridges the gap between reduction objectives and generative model goals, demonstrating that leveraging output token similarity is key to efficient and high-quality DiT inference.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) achieve superior image generation quality but suffer from quadratic computational complexity relative to token count. While various token reduction (TR) methods have been proposed to mitigate this cost, they overlook the primary objective of generative models: minimizing recovery error, which requires reflecting output token similarity. They rely solely on input token similarity inherited from reduction-only ViT paradigms, leading to a fundamental misalignment with this objective.
  To bridge this gap, we propose DiTo, a novel TR paradigm that shifts the focus toward output-centric token reduction. Based on the observation that output token similarity is consistently preserved across adjacent timesteps, DiTo utilizes prior-step similarities as an effective proxy to establish token correspondences at a Matching timestep, which are then reused across multiple subsequent Reduction timesteps. To optimize this interleaved scheduling, we propose Pair Match Ratio (PMR)-guided Interval Scheduling to determine the optimal matching frequency. Furthermore, to mitigate localized approximation errors and resulting blocking artifacts caused by repeated reuse, we propose Frequency-aware Token Matching by incorporating a selection-frequency penalty. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DiTo consistently outperforms existing TR methods with 1.6-3.9 dB higher PSNR at comparable speedups, achieving a superior Pareto frontier.

</details>


### [39] [PointLLM-R: Enhancing 3D Point Cloud Reasoning via Chain-of-Thought](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22013)
*Chaoqi Chen,Qile Xu,Wenjun Zhou,Hui Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a data-centric framework to build PoCoTI, a large-scale Chain-of-Thought reasoning dataset for 3D point cloud understanding, and fine-tunes PointLLM on it to create PointLLM-R, achieving SOTA performance on 3D classification and captioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D point cloud understanding via language is challenging due to irregular data structure and lack of explicit reasoning in existing 3D multimodal models. CoT reasoning has been effective in LLMs and 2D MLLMs but is underexplored in 3D.

Method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) refine point-text instruction data using VLM-based quality evaluation and reference-guided refinement; (2) synthesize reasoning paths via Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization (HiLPO). Builds the PoCoTI dataset (55K samples) and fine-tunes PointLLM to obtain PointLLM-R.

Result: PointLLM-R achieves state-of-the-art performance on generative 3D classification and captioning, and generalizes robustly to real-world scanned point clouds and multi-turn dialogue scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed data-centric CoT supervision framework effectively enhances reasoning capabilities in 3D multimodal models, setting a new benchmark for 3D point cloud understanding.

Abstract: Understanding 3D point clouds through language remains a fundamental challenge in computer graphics and visual computing, due to the irregular structure of point cloud data and the lack of explicit reasoning in existing 3D multimodal models. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning has shown strong effectiveness in LLMs and image-based MLLMs, its extension to 3D understanding remains largely underexplored. In this paper, we propose a data-centric framework for constructing large-scale CoT supervision tailored to 3D point cloud understanding. Our framework consists of a two-stage pipeline that first refines point-text instruction data via vision-language-model-based quality evaluation and reference-guided refinement, and then synthesizes high-quality reasoning paths through Human-in-the-Loop Prompt Optimization (HiLPO). Using this approach, we build PoCoTI, a CoT-enhanced point-text instruction-following dataset containing 55K samples with explicit reasoning paths. Fine-tuning PointLLM on PoCoTI yields PointLLM-R, a reasoning-capable 3D multimodal language model. Extensive experiments on generative 3D classification and captioning demonstrate that PointLLM-R achieves state-of-the-art performance and generalizes robustly to real-world scanned point clouds and multi-turn dialogue scenarios.

</details>


### [40] [ORBIS: Output-Guided Token Reduction with Distribution-Aware Matching for Video Diffusion Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22015)
*Hangyeol Lee,Joo-Young Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ORBIS is an SW-HW co-designed accelerator for video Diffusion Transformers that improves token reduction by using previous timestep activations for better similarity estimation and a Distribution-Aware Token Matching algorithm, achieving up to 4.5x speedup and 79.3% energy reduction over an NVIDIA A100 GPU.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video Diffusion Transformers suffer from high computational cost due to 3D Spatio-Temporal Attention increasing token length with more frames. Existing token reduction methods use inaccurate similarity estimates and lightweight matching, resulting in poor quality and marginal acceleration.

Method: ORBIS leverages output activations from the previous timestep for accurate inter-token similarity; introduces a Distribution-Aware Token Matching (DATM) algorithm capturing global token distribution and minimizing token-pair loss; and implements specialized, deeply pipelined hardware with quantization to hide DATM latency at minimal area cost (2.4%).

Result: ORBIS achieves ~2x higher token reduction ratio than AsymRnR, delivers up to 4.5x speedup and 79.3% energy reduction compared to an NVIDIA A100 GPU, with the hardware occupying only 2.4% of total area and negligible accuracy loss.

Conclusion: The proposed SW-HW co-design effectively addresses video DiT computational bottlenecks by improving both token matching quality and hardware efficiency, significantly outperforming prior approaches.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformer (DiT) has emerged as a powerful model architecture for generating high-quality images and videos. In the case of video DiT, 3D Spatio-Temporal Attention increases token length in proportion to the number of frames, sharply increasing computational cost. Token reduction methods mitigate this cost by exploiting spatial redundancy, but existing approaches rely on inaccurate similarity estimates and lightweight matching algorithms, resulting in poor matching quality and only marginal acceleration.
  To overcome these limitations, we propose ORBIS, an SW-HW co-designed accelerator for video DiT. ORBIS leverages the output activation from the previous timestep to obtain more accurate inter-token similarity, substantially improving matching quality and enabling a higher token reduction ratio. We further introduce a Distribution-Aware Token Matching (DATM) algorithm that captures global token distribution and explicitly minimizes token-pair loss for additional gains. To fully hide DATM latency, we design specialized, deeply pipelined hardware and minimize its hardware cost through quantization, occupying only 2.4% of total area with negligible accuracy loss. Extensive experiments show that ORBIS achieves about 2x higher token reduction ratio than the state-of-the-art approach, AsymRnR, while delivering up to 4.5x speedup and 79.3% energy reduction compared to an NVIDIA A100 GPU.

</details>


### [41] [Diverse Yet Consistent: Context-Guided Diffusion with Energy-Based Joint Refinement for Multi-Agent Motion Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22017)
*Lei Chu,Yuhuan Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A diffusion-based multi-agent motion prediction framework using historical context guidance and energy-based interaction consistency, outperforming existing methods on both marginal and joint metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing human motion prediction methods struggle to generate predictions that are both diverse and jointly consistent among interacting agents, and most are evaluated only on single-agent metrics that fail to capture multi-agent interaction dynamics.

Method: Proposes a diffusion-based framework with: (1) a guidance mechanism leveraging rich historical trajectory context for diverse and expressive predictions, and (2) an energy-based formulation that refines joint trajectory distributions while preserving individual trajectory plausibility.

Result: Outperforms existing methods consistently on four benchmark datasets; significantly improves both marginal (ADE/FDE) and joint (JADE/JFDE) metrics on ETH/UCY over strong baselines, and delivers marginal metric gains while maintaining competitive joint performance compared to prior joint prediction methods.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively balances diversity and interaction consistency in multi-agent motion prediction, demonstrating that incorporating historical context guidance and energy-based joint refinement leads to state-of-the-art performance on both marginal and joint evaluation metrics.

Abstract: Deepgenerative models havebecomeapromisingapproach for human motion prediction due to their ability to capture multimodal distributions and represent diverse human be haviors. However, generating predictions that are both di verse and jointly consistent among interacting agents re mains challenging. In addition, most existing approaches are primarily evaluated using single-agent (marginal) met rics, which fail to fully reflect the joint dynamics of multi agent interactions. We propose a diffusion-based frame work that improves multi-agent motion prediction by lever aging rich contextual information from historical trajecto ries. This information is incorporated through a guidance mechanism to enhance the diversity and expressiveness of predicted motions. To further enforce interaction consis tency, we introduce an energy-based formulation that re fines the joint trajectory distribution while preserving the plausibility of individual trajectories. Extensive experi ments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing methods. No tably, our approach substantially improves both marginal (ADE/FDE) and joint (JADE/JFDE) metrics on ETH/UCY over strong marginal baselines. Compared with prior joint prediction methods, it delivers significant gains in marginal metrics while maintaining competitive joint performance.

</details>


### [42] [FRED: A Multi-Modal Autonomous Driving Dataset for Flooded Road Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22018)
*Connor Malone,Sebastien Demmel,Sebastien Glaser*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FRED is the first multi-modal autonomous driving dataset focused on water hazards, providing camera, LiDAR, IMU/GNSS data from flooded roads with semantic labels for detection, localization, and SLAM tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autonomous driving datasets lack dedicated data for water hazard scenarios, which are critical for safe navigation in adverse conditions.

Method: The dataset was collected using a 2.3MP camera, 64-beam LiDAR, and IMU/GNSS across five flooded locations, released in KITTI-style and RTMaps formats with semantic labels.

Result: FRED provides multi-modal sensor data from real flooding events, including dry-condition baselines, with semantic annotations for water hazard detection.

Conclusion: FRED enables training and evaluation of water hazard detection methods and supports tasks like localization and SLAM for autonomous driving in flooded environments.

Abstract: The Flooded Road Environments Dataset (FRED) is, to our knowledge, the first multi-modal autonomous driving dataset specifically targeting the collection of data from scenarios involving water hazards on the road. The dataset contains images from a 2.3 MP FLIR Blackfly USB3 camera, 64-beam 360$^\circ$ point clouds from an Ouster OS1-64 LiDAR, and data from an iXblue ATLANS-C IMU corrected by a Geoflex RTK GNSS, from five separate locations captured both during and after flooding events. The data has been released in two formats: a KITTI-style format for easy integration with existing data tools, and the RTMaps format for direct replay of the vehicle's data capture. We provide semantic labels to enable the training and evaluation of both single-sensor and sensor-fusion methods for water hazard detection. Position and velocity, as well as data captured under dry conditions, are provided to enable the development of location-based detection methods that may incorporate maps, and to evaluate other tasks such as localisation and SLAM.

</details>


### [43] [ForeSplat: Optimization-Aware Foresight for Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22020)
*Yuke Li,Weihang Liu,Cheng Zhang,Yuefeng Zhang,Jiadi Cui,Zixuan Wang,Junran Ding,Haoyu Wu,Yujiao Shi,Jingyi Yu,Xin Lou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ForeSplat is an optimization-aware training framework for feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting that produces initializations designed for rapid refinement, using a lightweight meta-gradient training rule (MetaGrad) to bridge the gap between amortized prediction and per-scene optimization without adding inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feed-forward 3DGS models struggle to match per-scene optimization quality due to limited 3D annotations and capacity. The predict-then-refine pipeline is suboptimal because feed-forward models are trained only for zero-step rendering error, ignoring whether their output serves as a good initialization for downstream optimization.

Method: ForeSpatl introduces MetaGrad, a multi-anchor meta-gradient training rule that unrolls a short inner-loop refinement trajectory, samples anchor states, and back-propagates aggregated first-order gradients to the prediction head as a surrogate optimization-aware signal, avoiding costly higher-order differentiation.

Result: Across diverse backbones (AnySplat, Pi3X, distilled edge variant), ForeSplat-trained initializations converge in fewer refinement steps and achieve higher peak reconstruction quality than vanilla counterparts, even when fully converged. It consistently bridges the gap between amortized prediction and per-scene optimization.

Conclusion: ForeSplat establishes a practical path toward lightweight, high-fidelity 3D reconstruction by offloading scene-modeling burden to the optimizer, enabling high-quality reconstruction within seconds after a few refinement steps with no added inference cost.

Abstract: Feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) models offer fast single-pass reconstruction,but scaling them to match per-scene optimization quality is fundamentally hindered by the scarcity of large-scale 3D annotations.A practical compromise is predict-then-refine,where post-prediction optimization compensates for the limited capacity of the feed-forward network.However,standard feed-forward 3DGS is trained solely for zero-step rendering error,ignoring whether its output constitutes a good initialization for the downstream optimizer.We present ForeSplat,an optimization-aware training framework that equips feed-forward 3DGS models to produce initializations explicitly designed for rapid,effective refinement.By offloading part of the scene-modeling burden to the optimizer,ForeSplat substantially reduces the capacity pressure on the feed-forward model,making high-quality reconstruction feasible even with compact networks.At its core is MetaGrad,a lightweight multi-anchor meta-gradient training rule that bypasses costly higher-order differentiation through the 3DGS optimizer.MetaGrad unrolls a short inner-loop refinement trajectory,samples anchor states,and back-propagates aggregated first-order gradients to the prediction head as a surrogate optimization-aware signal.This fine-tuning adds no inference cost and enables high-quality reconstruction within seconds after a few refinement steps.We instantiate ForeSplat on diverse backbones,including AnySplat,Pi3X,and a distilled variant tailored for edge deployment.Across all tested architectures,a ForeSplat-trained initialization converges in fewer refinement steps and reaches a higher peak reconstruction quality than its vanilla counterpart,even fully converged.The framework consistently bridges the gap between amortized prediction and per-scene optimization,establishing a practical path toward lightweight,high-fidelity 3D reconstruction.

</details>


### [44] [SO-Mamba: State-Ownership Mamba for Unrolled MRI Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22031)
*Pengcheng Fang,Hongli Chen,Fangfang Tang,Feng Liu,Xiaohao Cai,Shanshan Shan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SO-Mamba proposes a State-Ownership Mamba regularizer for accelerated MRI reconstruction that separates resident (persistent) and non-resident (stage-dependent) evidence via a State-Ownership Router, improving reconstruction quality over CNN/Transformer/Mamba baselines on five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing unrolled Mamba-based reconstruction treats all evidence uniformly within recurrent content routes, failing to distinguish between persistent resident-carrier evidence (coherent across stages) and update-dependent non-resident evidence (tied to current iteration), leading to suboptimal reconstruction.

Method: SO-Mamba introduces a State-Ownership Router (SOR) that: (1) constructs a resident carrier for recurrent content; (2) routes non-resident evidence to affine modulation of B/C state interfaces and an output correction outlet, keeping it separate from the recurrent route. A two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic is also proposed.

Result: Outperforms CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines on five public MRI reconstruction benchmarks covering diverse anatomies, sampling patterns, and coil configurations, with competitive computational efficiency.

Conclusion: Explicitly separating resident and non-resident evidence in Mamba-based regularizers improves reconstruction quality. The state-ownership framework is a promising direction for learned MRI reconstruction.

Abstract: Accelerated MRI reconstruction requires recovering missing details while preserving anatomically coherent structures across large spatial regions. State-space models such as Mamba provide efficient long-range modeling, making them attractive learned regularizers for unrolled reconstruction. However, in a data-consistency-coupled unrolled solver, different stages operate on different reconstruction iterates, where the resident carrier should preserve coherent reconstruction content across stages while stage-dependent non-resident evidence is tied to the current update. Treating these roles uniformly can place persistent resident-carrier evidence and update-dependent non-resident evidence into the same recurrent content route. We therefore propose SO-Mamba, a state-ownership Mamba regularizer that assigns reconstruction evidence within each Mamba stage to recurrent residency, state-interface access, and non-state output correction. SO-Mamba implements this ownership rule with a State-Ownership Router (SOR), which constructs a resident carrier for recurrent content and routes non-resident evidence to affine modulation of the B/C state interfaces and an output correction outlet. The resident carrier supplies the Mamba content route, while the non-resident evidence stream adapts the state interfaces and contributes through the output outlet without entering the recurrent content route. We further introduce a two-level outer-band leakage diagnostic that separates hidden-state storage from readout expression by measuring outer-band energy in the selective-scan state trajectory and the post-scan Mamba readout. Experiments on five public MRI reconstruction benchmarks spanning diverse anatomies, sampling patterns, and coil configurations show that SO-Mamba consistently improves over CNN-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based baselines with competitive computational efficiency.

</details>


### [45] [AgroVG: A Large-Scale Multi-Source Benchmark for Agricultural Visual Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22034)
*Haocheng Li,Juepeng Zheng,Zenghao Yang,Kaiqi Du,Guilong Xiao,Gengmeng Pu,Haohuan Fu,Jianxi Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AgroVG is a multi-source benchmark for evaluating visual grounding in agriculture, covering 10K+ image-query pairs across six target families, testing models on bounding-box and instance-mask grounding with single/multi/zero-target queries. Best models achieve only 0.35 Set-F1 and <0.17 mask success rate, showing significant gaps remain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agricultural visual grounding is critical for AI-driven farming (weeding, disease monitoring, harvesting), but evaluation is challenging due to small, repetitive, occluded, irregularly shaped targets and variable referring expressions (one, many, or zero objects). No existing benchmark adequately tests localization, completeness, and abstention jointly.

Method: AgroVG aggregates 10,071 annotation-grounded image-query pairs from 10 source datasets across 6 target families (crop/weed, fruit, wheat head, pest, plant disease, tree canopy). It formulates grounding as generalized set prediction and provides two tasks: box grounding (T1) across all families and instance-mask grounding (T2) on pixel-annotated sources, with single-target, multi-target, and target-absent queries.

Result: Zero-shot evaluation of 26 model configurations (closed-source MLLMs, open-source VLMs, specialized grounding systems) shows best multi-target Set-F1 of only 0.35 and best positive-query mask success rate at IoU@0.75 below 0.17, indicating persistent gaps in agricultural visual grounding.

Conclusion: Current vision-language and grounding models struggle significantly on agricultural visual grounding, especially for multi-target and fine-grained mask tasks, highlighting the need for further research and benchmark-driven progress in this domain.

Abstract: Visual grounding, the task of localizing objects described by natural-language expressions, is a foundational capability for agricultural AI systems, enabling applications such as selective weeding, disease monitoring, and targeted harvesting. Reliable evaluation of agricultural visual grounding remains challenging because agricultural targets are often small, repetitive, occluded, or irregularly shaped, and instructions may refer to one, many, or no objects in an image. Evaluating this capability therefore requires jointly testing localization accuracy, target-set completeness, and existence-aware abstention. To address these challenges, we introduce \textbf{AgroVG}, a multi-source benchmark that formulates agricultural grounding as generalized set prediction: given an image and a referring expression, a model must return all matching target instances or abstain when no target is present. AgroVG contains 10{,}071 annotation-grounded image-query pairs from ten source datasets across six target families: crop/weed, fruit, wheat head, pest, plant disease, and tree canopy. It supports bounding-box grounding (T1) across all six families and instance-mask grounding (T2) on sources with reliable instance-level pixel annotations, with queries covering single-target, multi-target, and target-absent regimes. AgroVG further provides task-specific protocols for box-set matching and query-level mask coverage. Zero-shot evaluation of 26 model configurations spanning closed-source MLLMs, open-source VLMs, and specialized grounding systems reveals persistent gaps: the best multi-target Set-$F_1$ reaches only 0.35, and the best positive-query mask success rate at IoU@0.75 remains below 0.17. Data and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/AgroVG-5172/ .

</details>


### [46] [HyLoVQA: Dynamic Hypernetwork-Generated Low-Rank Adaptation for Continual Visual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22035)
*Yiran Wang,Chenyi Xiong,Ziyue Qin,Miao Zhang,Kui Xiao,Zhifei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyLoVQA proposes a continual VQA method using a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors and hypernetwork-generated LoRA adapters to dynamically adapt to tasks and objects while preventing cross-level interference, achieving SOTA on VQA v2 and NExT-QA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior continual VQA methods rely on largely shared parameter sets, which causes cross-level task interference and hinders accurate adaptation to both current tasks and objects.

Method: Maintains a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors storing visual object and textual task content, updated with current input features. A hypernetwork generates lightweight LoRA adapters conditioned on retrieved anchors. An alignment loss aligns semantic discrepancies in feature space with functional changes in parameter space to constrain adapters on the current task/object.

Result: Outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods on VQA v2 and NExT-QA under both standard and compositional settings.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses cross-level interference in continual VQA through dynamic, parameter-efficient adaptation.

Abstract: Continual Visual Question Answering (VQA) requires learning from non-stationary streams of visual inputs and questions while preserving past knowledge. Most prior methods adapt by updating a largely shared parameter set. This often leads to cross-level task interference, hindering accurate adaptation to the current task and object. To address this limitation, we propose HyLoVQA. It maintains a drift-resilient memory bank of anchors. The bank stores the content of visual objects and textual tasks, and they are updated using current input features. Conditioned on retrieved anchors, a hypernetwork generates lightweight Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters. This ensures parameter efficiency, allowing the model to adapt to each task and object dynamically. Additionally, we formulate an alignment loss that aligns semantic discrepancies in the feature space with functional changes in the parameter space, thereby constraining LoRA adapters to remain focused on the current task and object. Extensive experiments on VQA v2 and NExT-QA under both standard and compositional settings demonstrate the superiority of HyLoVQA over prior state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [47] [GA-VLN: Geometry-Aware BEV Representation for Efficient Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22036)
*Jiahao Yang,Zihan Wang,Xiangyang Li,Xing Zhu,Yujun Shen,Yinghao Xu,Shuqiang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes GA-BEV, a compact 3D-grounded BEV representation combining explicit depth projection and implicit 3D foundation model priors to improve VLN efficiency and spatial reasoning, achieving SOTA without costly data augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLN approaches rely on dense RGB videos with excessive tokens and lack explicit spatial structure, causing high computational overhead and limited spatial reasoning.

Method: Constructs BEV spatial maps from RGB-D by projecting visual features into 3D space and aggregating into agent-centric layout; incorporates features from a pretrained 3D foundation model as structural priors, combining explicit depth-based projection with implicit learned cues.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results using only navigation data, without DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training.

Conclusion: The GA-VLN framework is robust and data-efficient, proving that explicit+implicit geometric cues in a compact BEV representation effectively replace costly training augmentations.

Abstract: Despite significant progress in Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), existing approaches still rely on dense RGB videos that produce excessive patch tokens and lack explicit spatial structure, resulting in substantial computational overhead and limited spatial reasoning. To address these issues, we introduce the Geometry-Aware BEV (GA-BEV) - a compact, 3D-grounded feature representation that integrates both explicit and implicit geometric cues into multimodal large language model (MLLM) - based navigation systems. We construct BEV spatial maps from RGB-D inputs by projecting visual features into 3D space and aggregating them into an agent-centric layout that preserves geometric consistency while reducing token redundancy. To further enrich geometric understanding, we incorporate features from a pretrained 3D foundation model into the BEV space, injecting structural priors learned from large-scale 3D reconstruction tasks. Together, these complementary cues - explicit depth-based projection and implicit learned priors - yield compact yet spatially expressive representations that substantially improve navigation efficiency and performance. Experiments show that our method achieves state-of-the-art results using only navigation data, without DAgger augmentation or mixed VQA training, demonstrating the robustness and data efficiency of the proposed GA-VLN framework.

</details>


### [48] [Physiology and Anatomy Aware Inverse Inference of Myocardial Infarction for Cardiac Digital Twin](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22044)
*Mengxiao Wang,Yilin Lyu,Julia Camps,Ching Hui Sia,Mark Yan-Yee Chan,Yanrui Jin,Shuzhi Sam Ge,Chengliang Liu,Lei Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel framework for noninvasive myocardial infarction (MI) localization using cardiac digital twins. It introduces an anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis strategy to generate realistic irregular scars with border zones, constructs a virtual cohort to simulate QRS-T waveforms, and designs a Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) that jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs for infarct inference. The method significantly outperforms existing approaches, achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 and 0.5503 for scar and border zone segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate MI localization is critical but limited by existing inverse inference methods that overlook realistic scar morphology and cardiac repolarization, reducing sensitivity to subtle ECG variations and interpretability of infarct-induced electrophysiological changes.

Method: (1) Anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis for realistic irregular scars with border zones mimicking ischemic transmural progression; (2) Virtual cohort construction to simulate QRS-T waveforms capturing depolarization and repolarization; (3) Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) that jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct areas.

Result: Outperforms existing methods with Dice scores of 0.7391 for scar and 0.5503 for border zone segmentation, with enhanced interpretability of the ECG-infarct relationship.

Conclusion: The proposed digital twin framework bridges the simulation-reality gap, enables accurate noninvasive MI localization, and improves interpretability of how infarct properties manifest in ECG signals.

Abstract: Accurate localization of myocardial infarction is essential for risk stratification. While LGE-MRI remains the gold standard, it is resource-intensive. Integrating cine MRI with ECG enables a more detailed representation of infarct properties. Existing inverse MI inference methods overlook realistic scar morphology and cardiac repolarization, reducing sensitivity to subtle ECG variations and interpretability of infarct-induced electrophysiological changes. In this paper, we propose a novel framework for noninvasive MI localization using cardiac digital twins. To bridge the domain gap between simulation and reality, we introduce an anatomy-aware stochastic infarct synthesis strategy to synthesize realistic, irregular scars with border zones, mimicking ischemic transmural progression. We then construct a virtual cohort to simulate QRS-T waveforms, capturing both depolarization and repolarization dynamics. Furthermore, we design a Physiology and Anatomy Aware Network (PAA-Net) that jointly encodes 3D myocardial geometry and multi-lead ECGs to infer infarct areas with varying localizations, sizes, spatial extents, and transmuralities. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms existing methods in inverse inference, achieving Dice scores of 0.7391 and 0.5503 for scar and border zone segmentation, respectively, while further enhancing the interpretability of the ECG-infarct relationship. Our code will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [49] [Diffusion-guided Generalizable Enhancer for Urban Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22420)
*Henry Che,Jingkang Wang,Yun Chen,Ze Yang,Sivabalan Manivasagam,Raquel Urtasun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GenRe is a diffusion-guided enhancer that fixes deficiencies in pretrained 3D Gaussian representations for urban scenes within minutes, producing robust, high-fidelity renderings that generalize to large viewpoint shifts without costly per-scene optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural rendering approaches degrade under large viewpoint shifts, limiting closed-loop simulation. Current diffusion-based methods require costly per-scene optimization and fail to generalize beyond limited synthesized views.

Method: GenRe takes any pretrained 3D Gaussian representation and applies diffusion-guided distillation across diverse scenes to fix deficiencies efficiently (within minutes), producing a generalizable enhancer without per-scene optimization.

Result: Outperforms existing methods in both quality and efficiency; benefits downstream tasks like robust sensor simulation for autonomous driving.

Conclusion: GenRe enables scalable, robust, and efficient urban scene reconstruction that generalizes to challenging unseen viewpoints (e.g., lane changes), making it suitable for closed-loop autonomous driving simulation.

Abstract: Urban scene reconstruction from real-world observations has emerged as a powerful tool for self-driving development and testing. While current neural rendering approaches achieve high-fidelity rendering along the recorded trajectories, their quality degrades significantly under large viewpoint shifts, limiting the applicability for closed-loop simulation. Recent works have shown promising results in using diffusion models to enhance quality at these challenging viewpoints and distill improvements back into 3D representations. However, they often require costly per-scene optimization, and the distilled representations remain fragile and fail to generalize beyond limited synthesized views. To address these limitations, we propose GenRe, a novel diffusion-guided generalizable enhancer for urban scene reconstruction. GenRe takes as input any pretrained 3D Gaussian representation and fixes the deficiencies within a few minutes. By learning to distill generative priors across diverse scenes, GenRe produces robust and high-fidelity representation efficiently that generalizes reliably to challenging unseen viewpoints (e.g., lane change). Experiments show that GenRe outperforms existing methods in both quality and efficiency and benefits various downstream tasks, enabling robust and scalable sensor simulation for autonomous driving.

</details>


### [50] [Broken Memories: Detecting and Mitigating Memorization in Diffusion Models with Degraded Generations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22050)
*Yuanmin Huang,Mi Zhang,Chen Chen,Feifei Li,Geng Hong,Xiaoyu You,Min Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion models memorize training data, causing privacy risks. This paper identifies memorization-induced numerical instability ("broken" artifacts), proposes stability regions for detection, and develops an on-the-fly mitigation framework that achieves >0.999 AUC detection and 0.0% memorization rate with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address privacy and copyright risks from diffusion models memorizing training data, by detecting and mitigating memorization without compromising image quality or requiring prompt modifications.

Method: Identifies memorization via internal numerical instability (visual "broken" artifacts); introduces empirical stability regions based on latent update norms for quantitative characterization; proposes a step-wise, on-the-fly detection and adaptive mitigation framework.

Result: On Stable Diffusion 1.4: AUC > 0.999 for detection, 0.0% memorization rate after mitigation, ~0.01s overhead per image.

Conclusion: Memorization in diffusion models can be effectively detected via stability analysis and mitigated on-the-fly without sacrificing semantic fidelity or quality, offering a practical privacy-preserving solution.

Abstract: While diffusion models excel at generating high-quality images, their tendency to memorize training data poses significant privacy and copyright risks. In this work, we for the first time identify that memorization induces internal numerical instability, often manifesting as visually ``broken'' artifacts. Inspired by stability analysis in numerical methods, we introduce empirical stability regions based on latent update norms to quantitatively characterize stable behavior during generation. Leveraging this, we propose a principled, on-the-fly framework for step-wise detection and adaptive mitigation. Our approach suppresses memorization without altering prompts or guidance, thereby preserving semantic fidelity and image quality. Extensive experiments on Stable Diffusion 1.4 demonstrate that our method achieves an AUC $>0.999$ detection performance and a $0.0\%$ memorization rate after mitigation with negligible overhead ($\approx0.01$s per image).

</details>


### [51] [Pre-VLA: Preemptive Runtime Verification for Reliable Vision-Language-Action and World-Model Rollouts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22446)
*Zhen Sun,Yongjian Guo,Haoran Sun,Luqiao Wang,Wei Lu,Jiachi Ji,Shengzhe Ji,Junwu Xiong,Zhijun Meng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pre-VLA is a runtime verification architecture that preemptively assesses action quality before execution or world-model rollouts, using a multimodal backbone with dual-branch heads to predict safety and advantage scores, improving success rates and efficiency in embodied intelligence tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-quality actions from VLA models and world models can cause physical failures or wasted computation in rollouts, motivating a need for preemptive action quality assessment before deployment.

Method: Pre-VLA uses an efficient multimodal backbone with modality-aware pooling and a lightweight dual-branch head to predict safety confidence and advantage scores. It is trained with a multi-task objective (Focal classification, advantage regression, soft-threshold calibration). During deployment, a dual-mode preemptive resampling scheduler filters low-quality actions and triggers adaptive resampling.

Result: On LIBERO benchmark, Pre-VLA improves average closed-loop success rate from 30.79% to 37.62% over RynnVLA-002, reduces task execution steps, achieves 183.9 ms average forward verification per action chunk, and mitigates error accumulation in world-model rollouts.

Conclusion: Pre-VLA demonstrates that preemptive action validity assessment effectively enhances the reliability and efficiency of VLA models and world models in embodied tasks, reducing failures and computational waste.

Abstract: While large vision-language-action (VLA) models and generative world models (WM) have advanced long-horizon embodied intelligence, their practical deployment remains challenged by uncertainty in learning-based action generation. Low-quality actions may cause physical failures during execution or lead to misleading world-model rollouts with redundant rendering costs. To address this issue, we propose Pre-VLA, a unified runtime verification architecture that performs preemptive action validity assessment before physical execution or world-model imagination. Pre-VLA leverages an efficient multimodal backbone with modality-aware pooling and a lightweight dual-branch head to predict both safety confidence and critic-derived advantage scores for candidate action chunks. To handle severe class imbalance and unstable boundary decisions, we train Pre-VLA with a multi-task objective combining Focal classification, advantage regression, and soft-threshold calibration. During deployment, a dual-mode preemptive resampling scheduler filters low-quality actions and triggers adaptive resampling under a limited computation budget. Experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that Pre-VLA improves the average closed-loop success rate across four suites from 30.79\% to 37.62\% over RynnVLA-002, reduces task execution steps, achieves 183.9 ms average forward verification time per action chunk, and mitigates error accumulation in world-model rollouts.

</details>


### [52] [EasyVFX: Frequency-Driven Decoupling for Resource-Efficient VFX Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22051)
*Yue Ma,Xu Ye,Qinghe Wang,Yucheng Wang,Hongyu Liu,Yinhan Zhang,Xinyu Wang,Yuanpeng Che,Shanhui Mo,Paul Liang,Fangneng Zhan,Qifeng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EasyVFX is a resource-efficient framework for high-fidelity visual effects synthesis that uses frequency-domain decomposition (decoupling high-frequency spatial details from low-frequency motion dynamics) with a Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts architecture and a Test-Time Training strategy, achieving professional-grade VFX with significantly reduced computational requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generating high-fidelity VFX typically requires massive datasets and prohibitive computational power due to the intricate coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics. The authors aim to democratize professional-grade VFX by reducing these resource barriers.

Method: (1) Frequency-domain decomposition that decouples high-frequency (spatial appearance) and low-frequency (global motion dynamics) components; (2) Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) architecture with soft routing assigning specialized experts to distinct spectral bands; (3) Test-Time Training strategy with a Frequency-constraint Loss for fast adaptation (~100 steps on a single GPU).

Result: EasyVFX produces structurally consistent and visually stunning effects with fewer GPU resources, requiring only about 100 steps on a single GPU for test-time adaptation to unseen effects.

Conclusion: Frequency-aware learning is a key catalyst for democratizing professional-grade VFX, as it transforms high-dimensional learning problems into manageable sub-tasks, lowering optimization barriers and reducing data dependency.

Abstract: Generating high-fidelity visual effects (VFX) typically demands massive datasets and prohibitive computational power due to the intricate coupling of spatial textures and temporal dynamics. In this paper, we introduce EasyVFX, a resource-efficient framework that achieves realistic VFX synthesis under stringent constraints. Our core philosophy lies in frequency-domain decomposition: we observe that the complexity of VFX can be significantly mitigated by decoupling high-frequency components, which represent intricate spatial appearances, from low-frequency components that encapsulate global motion dynamics. This spectral disentanglement transforms a high-dimensional learning problem into manageable sub-tasks, thereby lowering the optimization barrier and reducing data dependency. Building upon this insight, we propose a two-stage training paradigm. First, we design a Frequency-aware Mixture-of-Experts (Freq-MoE) architecture. By utilizing a soft routing mechanism, our model assigns specialized experts to distinct spectral bands, enabling them to cultivate robust priors for appearance and motion dynamics. This specialization allows the model to acquire foundational VFX knowledge with fewer GPU resources. Second, we introduce a Test-Time Training strategy powered by a novel Frequency-constraint Loss. This allows the pre-trained model to swiftly adapt to specific, unseen effects through localized optimizations, requiring only about 100 steps on a single GPU. Experimental results demonstrate that EasyVFX produces structurally consistent and visually stunning effects, proving that frequency-aware learning is a key catalyst for democratizing professional-grade VFX.

</details>


### [53] [Distributed Image Compression with Multimodal Side Information at Extremely Low Bitrates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22061)
*Guojun Xu,Mingyang Zhang,Jianwen Xiang,Cheng Tan,Yanchao Yang,Junwei Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes MDIC, a multimodal distributed image compression framework that uses text-to-image diffusion and fine-grained alignment to enhance reconstruction quality at extremely low bitrates (<0.1 bpp).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DIC methods fail to exploit global context and object-level details from side information, resulting in local blurring and loss of fine details at extremely low bitrates.

Method: MDIC introduces (1) a text-to-image diffusion-based decoder conditioned on textual side information for global semantics, and (2) a feature-mask generator supervised by multimodal fine-grained alignment to extract visual details and compensate for lost category information from compressed VQ-VAE embeddings.
