Conclusion: High-fidelity synthetic data can serve as privacy-preserving resources for genetic education and counseling, and expand accessible computer vision tools in children's healthcare.

Abstract: Children with rare genetic diseases often exhibit distinctive facial phenotypes, yet developing computer vision systems for early diagnosis remains challenging due to extreme data scarcity, privacy constraints, and limited data sharing in pediatric settings. These challenges not only hinder automated diagnosis but also restrict the availability of visual resources for clinical genetic counseling. While prior work has shown that synthetic data can augment real datasets and preserve phenotype-level semantics, it remains unclear whether synthetic data alone is sufficient for learning in ultra-low-resource pediatric settings. In this work, we study the synthetic-only regime for pediatric rare disease recognition. Under a controlled experimental setup, models are trained exclusively on phenotype-aware synthetic facial images at increasing scales. We find that synthetic-only training achieves performance comparable to real-data-only baselines at sufficient scale across multiple backbones, suggesting that high-fidelity synthetic data can approximate clinically meaningful distributions. These findings together further enable the use of synthetic pediatric facial images as privacy-preserving resources for genetic education and counseling, supporting clinician training and patient communication. Our results highlight the potential of computer vision to improve data efficiency and expand accessible visual tools in children's healthcare.

</details>


### [135] [DecQ: Detail-Condensing Queries for Enhanced Reconstruction and Generation in Representation Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22777)
*Tianhang Wang,Yitong Chen,Wei Song,Zuxuan Wu,Min Li,Jiaqi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DecQ introduces lightweight detail-condensing queries to frozen vision foundation models in latent diffusion, improving reconstruction (PSNR +3.63dB) and generation (FID 1.41) with minimal extra computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Frozen VFMs provide robust semantic features for latent diffusion but lack spatial reconstruction capacity, while fine-tuning disrupts pretrained semantics. This creates a trade-off between reconstruction quality and generative fidelity.

Method: DecQ adds lightweight detail-condensing queries that extract fine-grained information from intermediate VFM features via condenser modules, feeding them into the decoder and generating them jointly with patch tokens during diffusion.

Result: With only 8 additional queries and 3.9% extra computation, DecQ improves PSNR from 19.13dB to 22.76dB, achieves 3.3× faster convergence, and obtains FID of 1.41 (no guidance) / 1.05 (with guidance).

Conclusion: DecQ effectively resolves the reconstruction-generation trade-off by aggregating multi-level VFM features via lightweight queries, boosting both reconstruction quality and generative performance.

Abstract: Representation Autoencoders (RAEs) leverage frozen vision foundation models (VFMs) as tokenizer encoders, providing robust high-level representations that facilitate fast convergence and high-quality generation in latent diffusion models. However, freezing the VFM inherently constrains its spatial reconstruction capacity, limiting fine-grained generation and image editing; in contrast, incorporating reconstruction-oriented signals via fine-tuning disrupts the pretrained semantic space and degrades generative fidelity. To address this trade-off, we propose DecQ, a simple yet effective framework for RAEs. Specifically, DecQ introduces lightweight detail-condensing queries that extract fine-grained information from intermediate VFM features through condenser modules. These queries are incorporated into the decoder to support reconstruction and are jointly generated with patch tokens during generative modeling. By aggregating information from both shallow and deep layers, DecQ effectively mitigates the reconstruction--generation trade-off, improving both reconstruction quality and generative performance. Our experiments demonstrate that: (1) with only 8 additional queries and 3.9% extra computation, DecQ improves reconstruction over the frozen DINOv2-based RAE, increasing PSNR from 19.13 dB to 22.76 dB; and (2) for generative modeling, DecQ achieves 3.3$\times$ faster convergence than RAE, attaining an FID of 1.41 without guidance and 1.05 with guidance.

</details>


### [136] [Sensor2Sensor: Cross-Embodiment Sensor Conversion for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22809)
*Jiahao Wang,Bo Sun,Yijing Bai,Vincent Casser,Songyou Peng,Zehao Zhu,Meng-Li Shih,Xander Masotto,Shih-Yang Su,Kanaad V Parvate,Tiancheng Ge,Linn Bieske,Dragomir Anguelov,Mingxing Tan,Chiyu Max Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Sensor2Sensor is a generative model that translates monocular dashcam videos into high-fidelity multi-modal AV sensor logs (multi-view cameras + LiDAR), using 4D Gaussian Splatting to create paired training data, enabling the use of vast in-the-wild video data for autonomous driving system development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Proprietary AV fleet data is limited in scale, diversity, and coverage of long-tail scenarios, while abundant in-the-wild dashcam data is incompatible with ADS that require structured multi-modal sensor inputs. There is a critical need to bridge this data gap.

Method: Sensor2Sensor addresses the lack of paired training data by first converting real AV logs into dashcam-style videos via 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) reconstruction and novel-view rendering, then uses a diffusion architecture to perform generative conversion from monocular video to multi-modal sensor outputs.

Result: Comprehensive quantitative evaluations on fidelity and realism of generated sensor data; demonstrated practical utility by converting challenging in-the-wild internet and dashcam footage into realistic multi-modal data formats.

Conclusion: Sensor2Sensor successfully unlocks vast external data sources for AV development by enabling structured, multi-modal sensor data generation from unstructured monocular video, addressing the scale and diversity limitations of proprietary AV datasets.

Abstract: Robust training and validation of Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS) require massive, diverse datasets. Proprietary data collected by Autonomous Vehicle (AV) fleets, while high-fidelity, are limited in scale, diversity of sensor configurations, as well as geographic and long-tail-behavioral coverage. In contrast, in-the-wild data from sources like dashcams offers immense scale and diversity, capturing critical long-tail scenarios and novel environments. However, this unstructured, in-the-wild video data is incompatible with ADS expecting structured, multi-modal sensor inputs for validation and training. To bridge this data gap, we propose Sensor2Sensor, a novel generative modeling paradigm that translates in-the-wild monocular dashcam videos into a high-fidelity, multi-modal sensor suite (AV logs) comprising multi-view camera images and LiDAR point clouds. A core challenge is the lack of paired training data. We address this by converting real AV logs into dashcam-style videos via 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) reconstruction and novel-view rendering. Sensor2Sensor then utilizes a diffusion architecture to perform the generative conversion. We perform comprehensive quantitative evaluations on the fidelity and realism of the generated sensor data. We demonstrate Sensor2Sensor's practical utility by converting challenging in-the-wild internet and dashcam footage into realistic, multi-modal data formats, further unlocking vast external data sources for AV development.

</details>


### [137] [MotiMotion: Motion-Controlled Video Generation with Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22818)
*Lee Hsin-Ying,Hanwen Jiang,Yiqun Mei,Jing Shi,Ming-Hsuan Yang,Zhixin Shu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MotiMotion is a framework that reformulates motion-controlled image-to-video generation as a reasoning-then-generation problem, using a training-free vision-language reasoner to refine trajectories and hallucinate plausible secondary motions, plus a confidence-aware control scheme, outperforming existing methods on a new MotiBench benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current motion-controlled image-to-video models rigidly follow sparse, imprecise, and causally incomplete user-provided trajectories, leading to unnatural or implausible outcomes, especially due to missing secondary causal consequences.

Method: (1) A training-free vision-language reasoner refines image-space coordinates of primary trajectories and hallucinates plausible secondary motions. (2) A confidence-aware control scheme modulates guidance strength, closely following high-confidence plans while correcting artifacts under low-confidence inputs with internal generative priors.

Result: Both VLM-based evaluation and a human study on MotiBench show MotiMotion produces videos with more plausible object behaviors and interaction, and is preferred over existing approaches.

Conclusion: Reformulating motion control as reasoning-then-generation with commonsense-aware reasoning and confidence-aware control significantly improves the plausibility and naturalness of generated videos.

Abstract: Current motion-controlled image-to-video generation models rigidly follow user-provided trajectories that are often sparse, imprecise, and causally incomplete. Such reliance often yields unnatural or implausible outcomes, especially by missing secondary causal consequences. To address this, we introduce MotiMotion, a novel framework that reformulates motion control as a reasoning-then-generation problem. To encourage causally grounded and commonsense-consistent interactions, we leverage a training-free vision-language reasoner to refine image-space coordinates of primary trajectories and to hallucinate plausible secondary motions. To further improve motion naturalness, we propose a confidence-aware control scheme that modulates guidance strength, enabling the model to closely follow high-confidence plans while correcting artifacts under low-confidence inputs with its internal generative priors. To support systematic evaluation, we curate a new image-to-video benchmark, MotiBench, consisting of interaction-centric scenes where new events are triggered by motion. Both VLM-based evaluation and a human study on MotiBench demonstrate that MotiMotion produces videos with more plausible object behaviors and interaction, and is preferred over existing approaches.

</details>


### [138] [Cambrian-P: Pose-Grounded Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22819)
*Jihan Yang,Zifan Zhao,Xichen Pan,Shusheng Yang,Junyi Zhang,Bingyi Kang,Hu Xu,Saining Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Camera pose is a lightweight yet powerful signal for video MLLMs. The authors introduce Cambrian-P, which adds per-frame learnable camera tokens and a pose regression head, achieving 4.5-6.5% gains on spatial reasoning benchmarks and SOTA streaming pose estimation, while also improving general video QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video MLLMs treat video frames as isolated 2D snapshots, ignoring camera pose information (position and orientation) that defines the spatial coordinate frame relating observations across frames. This limits the models' ability to perceive persistent 3D scenes as humans do.

Method: Cambrian-P augments a video MLLM with (1) per-frame learnable camera tokens that encode pose information, (2) a pose regression head for supervision, and (3) a carefully designed sampling scheme. Training uses both annotated and pseudo-annotated poses from in-the-wild video.

Result: Achieves 4.5-6.5% gains on VSI-Bench spatial reasoning, generalizes across eight additional spatial/general video QA benchmarks, achieves SOTA streaming pose estimation on ScanNet, and pseudo-annotated pose training surprisingly improves general video QA.

Conclusion: Camera pose is a fundamental signal for video models that reason about the physical world, benefiting both spatial reasoning and general video understanding tasks.

Abstract: Camera pose matters. The position and orientation of each viewpoint define a shared spatial coordinate frame that relates observations across video frames. Yet this signal is largely absent from multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) for video understanding, which process frames as isolated 2D snapshots, instead of the persistent scene humans perceive. We revisit pose as a lightweight supervisory signal and introduce Cambrian-P, a video MLLM augmented with per-frame learnable camera tokens and a pose regression head. With a carefully designed sampling scheme, the model achieves substantial gains of 4.5-6.5% on spatial reasoning benchmarks such as VSI-Bench, generalizes across eight additional spatial and general video QA benchmarks, and, as a byproduct, achieves state of the art streaming pose estimation on ScanNet. Surprisingly, training on pseudo-annotated poses from in-the-wild video further improves general video QA benchmarks, showing pose helps beyond spatial reasoning. Together, these results position camera pose as a fundamental signal for video models that reason about the physical world.

</details>


### [139] [Which Way Did It Move? Diagnosing and Overcoming Directional Motion Blindness in Video-LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22823)
*Jongseo Lee,Hyuntak Lee,Sunghun Kim,Sooa Kim,Jihoon Chung,Jinwoo Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Video-LLMs suffer from directional motion blindness—failing to perceive basic left/right/up/down motion despite good temporal video understanding. The root cause is a "direction binding gap" where motion information exists in hidden states but doesn't bind to correct verbal answers. The authors propose DeltaDirect, a projector-level objective using adjacent-frame feature deltas to predict 2D motion vectors, which dramatically improves motion direction accuracy from 25.9% to 85.4% on synthetic benchmarks and by 21.9 points on real-world benchmarks without real training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and fix the surprising failure of Video-LLMs to perceive basic motion direction (left/right/up/down), despite their success at complex temporal video understanding tasks.

Method: The authors (1) diagnose the "direction binding gap" by tracing motion information through the Video-LLM pipeline, (2) introduce MoDirect dataset for motion direction instruction tuning/evaluation, and (3) propose DeltaDirect, a projector-level objective that predicts normalized 2D motion vectors from adjacent-frame feature deltas.

Result: DeltaDirect improves motion direction accuracy from 25.9% to 85.4% on MoDirect-SynBench and by 21.9 points on MoDirect-RealBench over vanilla baselines without real-world tuning data, while preserving standard video-understanding performance.

Conclusion: Directional motion blindness in Video-LLMs can be effectively addressed through a diagnosis-driven, projector-level objective (DeltaDirect) that binds motion direction signals to verbal outputs, achieving strong gains on both synthetic and real-world motion direction tasks without sacrificing general video understanding.

Abstract: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) have made rapid progress on temporal video understanding, yet many fail at a basic perceptual primitive: signed image-plane motion direction. On simple videos of a single object moving left, right, up, or down, most Video-LLMs perform near chance, with above-chance cases largely attributable to prediction biases rather than genuine direction understanding. We call this failure directional motion blindness. We localize the failure by tracing motion direction information through the Video-LLM pipeline. Motion direction remains linearly accessible from the vision encoder, projector, and LLM hidden states, but the readout fails to bind this signal to the correct verbal answer option, revealing a direction binding gap. Although synthetic motion direction instruction tuning reduces this gap on the source domain, motion direction concept vector analysis shows that visual complexity weakens the signal magnitude and limits out-of-domain generalization. We introduce MoDirect, a dataset family for motion direction instruction tuning and evaluation, and DeltaDirect, a diagnosis-driven, projector-level objective that predicts normalized 2-D motion vectors from adjacent-frame feature deltas. On MoDirect-SynBench, instruction tuning with DeltaDirect improves motion direction accuracy from 25.9% to 85.4%. On MoDirect-RealBench, DeltaDirect improves real-world motion direction accuracy by 21.9 points over the vanilla baseline without real-world tuning data, while preserving standard video-understanding performance. Code: https://github.com/KHU-VLL/DeltaDirect

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [140] [Temporal Contrastive Transformer for Financial Crime Detection: Self-Supervised Sequence Embeddings via Predictive Contrastive Coding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21490)
*Danny Butvinik,Yonit Marcus,Nitzan Tal,Gabrielle Azoulay*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The Temporal Contrastive Transformer (TCT) learns temporal transaction embeddings via self-supervised contrastive learning. Embeddings alone achieve AUC 0.8644, but add no improvement over strong domain features (AUC 0.9205 vs 0.9245). The model captures behavioral patterns but needs further development for practical fraud detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a self-supervised representation learning framework that captures contextual temporal dynamics in financial transaction sequences, aiming to reduce reliance on manual feature engineering for fraud detection.

Method: Proposes Temporal Contrastive Transformer (TCT), trained with self-supervised contrastive learning to encode behavioral patterns over time. Learned embeddings are fed into a gradient boosting classifier for downstream fraud detection.

Result: Embeddings alone achieve AUC 0.8644. Combined with domain-engineered features, no improvement over baseline (AUC 0.9205 vs 0.9245), indicating learned representations largely overlap with existing features.

Conclusion: TCT captures relevant temporal behavioral signals but does not yet provide additive value over strong feature engineering. The work represents an intermediate stage, motivating further research on architecture, training objectives, and integration strategies.

Abstract: We introduce the Temporal Contrastive Transformer (TCT), a representation learning framework designed to capture contextual temporal dynamics in sequences of financial transactions. The model is trained using a self-supervised contrastive objective to produce embeddings that encode behavioral patterns over time, with the goal of supporting downstream fraud detection tasks. We evaluate TCT in a realistic setting by using the learned embeddings as input features to a gradient boosting classifier. Experimental results show that embeddings alone achieve meaningful predictive performance (AUC 0.8644), indicating that the model captures non-trivial temporal structure. However, when combined with domain-engineered features, no measurable improvement is observed over the baseline (AUC 0.9205 vs. 0.9245), suggesting that the learned representations largely overlap with existing feature abstractions. These findings position TCT as a promising representation learning approach that captures relevant behavioral signal, while highlighting the challenges of achieving additive value over strong domain features. The results reflect an intermediate stage in the development of temporal representation learning for financial crime detection and motivate further research on model architecture, training objectives, and integration strategies. At this early stage, achieving performance comparable to a strong feature-engineered baseline is itself a meaningful outcome, indicating that learned representations approximate domain-specific features without manual engineering. While not yet production-ready, these results point to a promising direction for reducing reliance on feature engineering in financial crime detection.

</details>


### [141] [Teaching Language Models to Forecast Research Success Through Comparative Idea Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21491)
*Srujan P Mule,Aniketh Garikaparthi,Manasi Patwardhan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Small language models (8B parameters) can effectively forecast which research ideas will succeed empirically, achieving 77.1% accuracy after SFT and 71.35% with interpretable reasoning via RLVR, outperforming GPT-5 (61.1%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LMs automate hypothesis generation, evaluating hundreds of AI-generated ideas without exhaustive experimentation becomes a critical bottleneck. The authors aim to determine if LMs can learn to forecast empirical success of research ideas before experiments are run.

Method: Constructed a dataset of 11,488 idea pairs from PapersWithCode; used SFT and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to train 8B models for comparative empirical forecasting (predicting which idea achieves better benchmark performance).

Result: SFT achieved 77.1% accuracy (vs. GPT-5's 61.1%), RLVR achieved 71.35% with interpretable justifications. Models showed robustness to surface-level heuristics and transferred to out-of-distribution and cross-domain tests.

Conclusion: Compute-efficient small LMs can serve as effective, objective verifiers for research ideas, offering a scalable path for autonomous scientific discovery without requiring exhaustive experimentation.

Abstract: As language models accelerate scientific research by automating hypothesis generation and implementation, a new bottleneck emerges: evaluating and filtering hundreds of AI-generated ideas without exhaustive experimentation. We ask whether LMs can learn to forecast the empirical success of research ideas before any experiments are run. We study comparative empirical forecasting: given a benchmark-specific research goal and two candidate ideas, predict which will achieve better benchmark performance. We construct a dataset of 11,488 idea pairs grounded in objective outcomes from PapersWithCode. While off-the-shelf 8B-parameter models struggle (30% acc.), SFT dramatically boosts performance to 77.1%, outperforming GPT-5 (61.1%). By framing evaluation as a reasoning task via Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), we train models to discover latent reasoning paths, achieving 71.35% acc. with interpretable justifications. Through additional ablations and out-of-distribution tests, we show robustness to surface-level heuristics and transfer to both a cross-domain time-split test set and an independently constructed test set. Our results demonstrate that compute-efficient small language models can serve as effective, objective verifiers, offering a scalable path for autonomous scientific discovery.

</details>


### [142] [The Attribution Impossibility: No Feature Ranking Is Faithful, Stable, and Complete Under Collinearity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21492)
*Drake Caraker,Bryan Arnold,David Rhoads*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that no feature ranking method can be simultaneously faithful, stable, and complete under collinearity. It characterizes the attribution design space into two families (faithful-complete unstable methods vs. ensemble stable methods like DASH), quantifies the impossibility across model classes, provides practical diagnostics, and mechanically verifies 305 theorems in Lean 4 — the first formally verified impossibility in XAI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formally prove and quantify the fundamental impossibility of feature ranking being simultaneously faithful, stable, and complete when features are collinear, and to characterize the complete design space of attribution methods.

Method: The paper proves the impossibility theorem analytically, quantifies it for four model classes (gradient boosting, Lasso, random forests), resolves it via ensemble averaging (DASH framework), characterizes the attribution design space into exactly two families, and mechanically verifies 305 theorems in Lean 4 from 16 axioms.

Result: Attribution ratio diverges as 1/(1-ρ²) for gradient boosting, is infinite for Lasso, and converges for random forests. DASH achieves Pareto-optimal Cramer-Rao variance bound. 68% of 77 public datasets exhibit attribution instability. SHAP-based fairness audits are provably unreliable under collinearity.

Conclusion: The impossibility is fundamental and quantitative; only two families of methods exist (faithful-complete unstable or ensemble stable with ties); switching to conditional SHAP does not escape the impossibility; practical diagnostics (Z-test workflow, single-model screening) are provided; the results are mechanically verified in Lean 4 as the first formally verified impossibility in explainable AI.

Abstract: No feature ranking can be simultaneously faithful, stable, and complete when features are collinear. For collinear pairs, ranking reduces to a coin flip. We prove this impossibility, quantify it for four model classes, resolve it via ensemble averaging (DASH), and machine-verify it with 305 Lean 4 theorems. We characterize the complete attribution design space: exactly two families of methods exist -- faithful-complete methods (unstable, with rankings that flip up to 50% of the time) and ensemble methods like DASH (stable, reporting ties for symmetric features) -- and no method lies outside this dichotomy. The impossibility is quantitative: the attribution ratio diverges as 1/(1-rho^2) for gradient boosting, is infinite for Lasso, and converges for random forests. DASH (Diversified Aggregation of SHAP) is provably Pareto-optimal among unbiased aggregations, achieving the Cramer-Rao variance bound with a tight ensemble size formula. In a survey of 77 public datasets, 68% exhibit attribution instability. Switching to conditional SHAP does not escape the impossibility when features have equal causal effects. The framework includes practical diagnostics -- a Z-test workflow and single-model screening tool -- and has direct consequences for fairness auditing: SHAP-based proxy discrimination audits are provably unreliable under collinearity. The design space theorem, diagnostics, and impossibility are mechanically verified in Lean 4 (305 theorems from 16 axioms, 0 sorry) -- to our knowledge, the first formally verified impossibility in explainable AI.

</details>


### [143] [Don't Collapse Your Features: Why CenterLoss Hurts OOD Detection and Multi-Scale Mahalanobis Wins](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21493)
*Rahul D Ray*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GOEN is a pipeline for OOD detection that combines multi-scale features, L2 normalization, Mahalanobis distance, and calibration with hard OOD examples. It reveals CenterLoss degrades OOD detection despite improving classification accuracy, achieving state-of-the-art OOD AUROC of 0.9483 on CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current OOD detection methods rely on feature representations optimized for classification accuracy, which neglect the specific requirements of epistemic uncertainty. The authors aim to show that better classification geometry does not automatically lead to better OOD detection.

Method: GOEN combines multi-scale features, L2 normalization, Mahalanobis distance, and a calibration head trained with hard OOD examples. The study systematically ablates components, notably removing CenterLoss to create GOEN-NoCenterLoss.

Result: GOEN-NoCenterLoss achieves average OOD AUROC of 0.9483 on CIFAR-10, outperforming deep ensembles (0.8827), KNN (0.8967), and ODIN (0.8870). CenterLoss degrades OOD AUROC from 0.9483 to 0.9366 despite improving classification accuracy.

Conclusion: Overly tight feature clusters compress inter-class margins and distort covariance structure needed for effective OOD detection. The assumption that better classification geometry leads to better epistemic uncertainty is challenged.

Abstract: The ability to detect out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs is fundamental to safe deployment of machine learning systems. Yet, current methods often rely on feature representations that are optimised solely for classification accuracy, neglecting the distinct requirements of epistemic uncertainty. We introduce GOEN (Geometry-Optimised Epistemic Network), a simple pipeline that combines multi-scale features, L2 normalisation, Mahalanobis distance, and a calibration head trained with real hard OOD examples. Through systematic ablation we uncover a counter-intuitive finding: CenterLoss, a popular regulariser for feature compactness, significantly degrades OOD detection performance, reducing average OOD AUROC from 0.9483 to 0.9366 despite improving classification accuracy. The best variant, GOEN-NoCenterLoss, achieves an average OOD AUROC of 0.9483, surpassing all baselines including deep ensembles (0.8827), KNN (0.8967), and ODIN (0.8870) on CIFAR-10 benchmarks, while maintaining competitive in-distribution accuracy. Our results challenge the prevailing assumption that better classification geometry automatically leads to better epistemic uncertainty. Instead, we show that overly tight feature clusters compress inter-class margins and distort the covariance structure needed for effective OOD detection. GOEN is efficient, training in under 20 minutes on a single GPU, and provides a practical blueprint for building AI systems that reliably recognise their own limitations.

</details>


### [144] [Double descent for least-squares interpolation on contaminated data: A simulation study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21494)
*Tino Werner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether the double descent phenomenon occurs in linear regression with contaminated training data, finding that heavily overparametrized least-squares interpolation achieves strong generalization, even surpassing robust estimators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand if double descent (where test error decreases after a certain model complexity) occurs in robust statistics settings with contaminated data, challenging classical statistical theory about overfitting.

Method: Compares the non-robust least-squares interpolation estimator against several robust alternatives in a linear regression setting with contaminated training data.

Result: Large overparametrization enables a double descent phenomenon, where the least-squares interpolator achieves very good generalization performance, outperforming robust alternatives.

Conclusion: Overparametrized least-squares interpolation can be highly effective even with contaminated data, challenging the necessity of robust estimators in such settings.

Abstract: Overparametrized models can exhibit an excellent generalization performance, although they should be prone to overfitting according to classical statistical theory. The discovery of the "double descent", indicating that the generalization error decreases after a certain model complexity has been reached, opened a new line of research. Robust statistics considers statistical estimation on contaminated data, which, due to assumptions that do not hold on real data, let data points appear as outliers w.r.t. the assumed "ideal" distribution, potentially severely distorting any classical estimator. We address the question whether a double descent phenomenon can be observed in a linear regression setting with contaminated training data. We compare the performance of the highly non-robust least-squares interpolation estimator with several robust alternatives. It turns out that large overparametrization indeed allows for a double descent phenomenon, resulting in a very good generalization performance of the least-squares interpolator, surpassing that of the robust alternatives.

</details>


### [145] [HealthCraft: A Reinforcement Learning Safety Environment for Emergency Medicine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21496)
*Brandon Dent*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HealthCraft is a reinforcement-learning environment for evaluating frontier LLMs in emergency medicine, using FHIR-based simulation, 24 MCP tools, and safety-critical reward rubrics. On 205 tasks, Claude Opus 4.6 achieves Pass@1 24.8% with 27.5% safety failures; GPT-5.4 achieves 12.6% with 34.0% safety failures. Multi-step performance collapses to near zero, and infrastructure bugs can flip which model appears stronger.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static medical-QA benchmarks fail to capture trajectory-level safety collapse, tool misuse, and capitulation under clinical pressure in emergency medicine.

Method: Adapted from Corecraft, built on FHIR R4 world state (14 entity types, 3,987 seed entities), exposes 24 MCP tools, uses dual-layer rubric with zero-reward on safety violations. 195 tasks (extended to 205) with 2,255 binary criteria (515 safety-critical). Includes deterministic LLM-judge overlay and supports Megatron+SGLang+GRPO training loops.

Result: Claude Opus 4.6: Pass@1 24.8% (safety failure 27.5%); GPT-5.4: Pass@1 12.6% (safety failure 34.0%). Multi-step workflow performance: Claude 1.0%, GPT-5.4 0.0%. Infrastructure bugs between v2-v8 flipped model rankings.

Conclusion: Frontier LLMs show severe safety and performance gaps in realistic emergency medicine trajectories. Infrastructure fidelity critically affects evaluation outcomes. The reward signal is not yet training-safe due to gameability (e.g., restraint criteria pass@0.929 prevalence).

Abstract: Frontier language models are being deployed into clinical workflows faster than the infrastructure to evaluate them safely. Static medical-QA benchmarks miss the failure modes that matter in emergency medicine: trajectory-level safety collapse, tool misuse, and capitulation under sustained clinical pressure. We present HealthCraft, the first public reinforcement-learning environment that rewards trajectory-level safety under realistic emergency-medicine conditions, adapted from Corecraft. It is built on a FHIR R4 world state with 14 entity types and 3,987 seed entities, exposes 24 MCP tools, and defines a dual-layer rubric that zeroes reward whenever any safety-critical criterion is violated. We release 195 tasks across six categories, graded against 2,255 binary criteria (515 safety-critical); a post-hoc 10-task negative-class slate extends this to 205 tasks and 2,337 criteria. V8 results on two frontier models show Claude Opus 4.6 at Pass@1 24.8% [21.5-28.4] and GPT-5.4 at 12.6% [10.2-15.6], with safety-failure rates of 27.5% and 34.0%. On multi-step workflows - the closest proxy to real emergency care - performance collapses to near zero (Claude 1.0%, GPT-5.4 0.0%) despite partial competence on individual steps. Six infrastructure bugs fixed between pilots v2 and v8 re-ordered which model "looks stronger," evidence that infrastructure fidelity is part of the measurement. A deterministic LLM-judge overlay bounds evaluator noise, and a 60-run negative-class smoke pilot shows the reward signal is not drop-in training-safe: restraint criteria pass at 0.929 prevalence, a gameability an eval harness can tolerate but a training reward cannot. We scaffold coupling to a Megatron+SGLang+GRPO loop per Corecraft Section 5.2 and leave training-reward ablations as future work. Environment, tasks, rubrics, and harness are released under Apache 2.0.

</details>


### [146] [Predicting Performance of Symbolic and Prompt Programs with Examples](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21515)
*Chengqi Zheng,Keya Hu,Shuzhi Liu,Tao Wu,Kevin Ellis,Yewen Pu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies performance prediction for LLM prompts and symbolic programs using a coin-flip model. It finds that symbolic programs are "all or nothing" while prompt programs have diffuse priors with many nearly-correct programs, explaining why few passing tests certify symbolic but not prompt programs. They propose RAP, which retrieves similar tasks/programs to construct a proxy prior for performance prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM prompting is unreliable—succeeding on a few test cases but failing at deployment. The authors aim to predict program performance (for both symbolic and prompt programs) on unseen tasks using limited in-domain examples, to address this reliability gap.

Method: Uses a coin-flip Bernoulli model where success probability = program performance. Compiles empirical performance priors from diverse programs/tasks. Develops RAP (Retrieved Approximate Prior) that retrieves similar tasks and prompt programs from a corpus to construct a proxy prior for performance prediction.

Result: Symbolic programs show all-or-nothing performance priors; prompt programs have diffuse priors with many nearly-correct programs. This explains why few passing tests certify symbolic but not prompt programs. RAP achieves solid performance predictions.

Conclusion: Performance prediction is feasible via Bayesian coin-flip modeling with appropriate priors. The fundamental difference in prior distributions between symbolic and LLM prompt programs explains their different certifiability. RAP offers a practical retrieval-based approach for predicting prompt program performance.

Abstract: LLM prompting is widely used for naturally stated tasks, yet it is unreliable it may succeed on a few test cases but fail at deployment time. We study performance prediction: given a program, either symbolic (e.g. Python) or a prompt executed on an LLM, and a few in-domain examples, predict its performance on unseen tasks from the same domain. We use a simple coin-flip model, treating each pass/fail program execution as a Bernoulli random variable, whose success probability is the programs unknown performance. In this model, performance depends entirely on: 1) the observed execution outcomes on test cases, and 2) a prior over performances. We compile empirical performance priors from a corpus of diverse programs and tasks, and find that performance for symbolic programs (e.g., Python) are all or nothing, while prompt programs have a diffuse prior with many nearly-correct programs. This difference explains why a few passing tests can certify symbolic programs but not prompt programs. Building on this insight, we develop RAP (Retrieved Approximate Prior), which retrieves similar tasks and prompt programs from an existing corpus to construct a proxy prior, which is then used to predict performance. We show RAP achieves solid performances.

</details>


### [147] [TONIC: Token-Centric Semantic Communication for Task-Oriented Wireless Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21553)
*Sige Liu,Kezhi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TONIC is a token-centric semantic communication framework that uses utility-aware unequal error protection at the transmitter and confidence-aware gating with Transformer-based completion at the receiver, outperforming baselines on image classification tasks over various wireless channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional wireless communication focuses on bit-level fidelity, which mismatches the token-level task relevance required by downstream foundation models, calling for a communication design that directly accounts for token importance and model requirements.

Method: The transmitter converts source samples into tokens, estimates token-level task relevance, and applies utility-aware unequal error protection under a fixed budget. The receiver uses token-level confidence to gate unreliable decisions (turning errors into erasures), then a Transformer-based completion model restores masked tokens for final inference.

Result: TONIC consistently outperforms separation-based schemes, pixel-domain DeepJSCC, and token-domain baselines under matched communication budgets over AWGN, Rayleigh, and Rician channels for image classification.

Conclusion: A token-centric, modular framework combining semantic-aware protection and confidence-aware gating with a Bayes-risk interpretation provides an interpretable and effective alternative to black-box end-to-end learning for task-oriented wireless communication.

Abstract: Tokens are becoming the basic units through which foundation models represent and process information for understanding and inference. However, traditional wireless communication, centered on bit-level fidelity, faces a mismatch between what is transmitted reliably and what downstream models actually consume. This mismatch calls for a communication design that directly accounts for token-level task relevance and downstream model requirements, rather than treating all transmitted bits as equally important. In this paper, we propose TONIC, a token-centric semantic communication framework for task-oriented wireless systems. The transmitter converts each source sample into a sequence of tokens, estimates token-level task relevance, and allocates protection through utility-aware unequal error protection under a fixed channel-use budget. At the receiver, token-level confidence is used to gate unreliable decisions, turning harmful substitutions into recoverable erasures before a Transformer-based completion model restores the masked tokens for final task inference. Our framework combines transmitter-side semantic-aware protection with receiver-side confidence-aware gating in a modular and interpretable architecture, rather than relying solely on fully black-box end-to-end learning. We further establish a utility-aware Bayes-risk interpretation for the receiver-side gating rule and study its interaction with unequal protection and completion. Experimental results on image classification show that TONIC consistently outperforms separation-based schemes, the pixel-domain DeepJSCC baseline, and token-domain baselines under matched communication budgets over AWGN, Rayleigh, and Rician channels.

</details>


### [148] [Harnesses for Inference-Time Alignment over Execution Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21516)
*Boyuan Wang,Bochao Li,Minghan Wang,Yuxin Tao,Fang Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies harness engineering for LLM agents through the lens of inference-time trajectory alignment, revealing that harnesses involve two mechanisms (task decomposition and guided execution) which can both help and hurt performance, and showing that partial harnesses specifying only initial steps can outperform fully structured workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Harness engineering is an important inference-time technique for LLM agents, but more elaborate harnesses are not uniformly better—increasing decomposition or guidance can sometimes improve execution but also reduce final task success. The authors aim to understand when and why harness design helps or hurts.

Method: The authors study harness design through inference-time trajectory alignment, separating harness into task decomposition (structuring tasks into sub-goals) and guided execution (reshaping local action distributions). They quantify how workflow granularity, retry budgets, and guidance-induced action reweighting shape performance limits, revealing failure modes including over-decomposition, over-pruning, and hallucinated execution. Predictions are validated through controlled synthetic experiments and real terminal agent benchmarks.

Result: The analysis reveals concrete failure modes (over-decomposition, over-pruning, hallucinated execution). The authors further show that effective harnesses can be partial—specifying only initial steps and leaving remaining execution to the agent can achieve higher pass rates than fully structured workflows.

Conclusion: Harness design should be approached carefully, as more structure is not always better. Partial harnesses that specify only initial steps can outperform fully structured workflows, offering a practical insight for LLM agent deployment.

Abstract: Harness engineering has emerged as an important inference-time technique for large language model (LLM) agents, aiming to improve long-term performance through task decomposition and guided execution. However, more elaborate harnesses are not uniformly better: increasing decomposition or guidance can sometimes improve execution, but can also reduce final task success. We study harness design through the lens of inference-time trajectory alignment. This perspective separates harness into two mechanisms: task decomposition, which structures a task into sub-goals, and guided execution, which reshapes local action distributions during execution. This decomposition allows us to quantify how workflow granularity, retry budgets, and guidance-induced action reweighting shape the performance limits of harness design. It further reveals concrete failure modes, including over-decomposition, over-pruning, and hallucinated execution. We validate these predictions through controlled synthetic experiments and real terminal agent benchmarks. Inspired by the theory, we further show that effective harnesses can be partial: specifying only the initial steps and leaving the remaining execution to agent can achieve higher pass rate than fully structured workflows.

</details>


### [149] [Correcting Class Imbalance in Prior-Data Fitted Networks for Tabular Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21742)
*Samuel McDowell,Nathan Stromberg,Lalitha Sankar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies class imbalance in Prior-data Fitted Networks (PFNs) and finds that thresholding and downsampling are effective mitigation techniques, leveraging PFNs' unique calibration and limited-data performance properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Class imbalance degrades PFN classification performance for rare classes, and existing techniques are either inapplicable (loss-based) or unproven due to PFNs' in-context learning dynamics.

Method: The authors adapted several classical class imbalance techniques (e.g., thresholding, downsampling) and evaluated their performance on PFN classification tasks.

Result: Thresholding works exceptionally well due to PFNs' calibration characteristics; downsampling performs comparably due to PFNs' strong limited-data performance, with additional computational benefits.

Conclusion: Thresholding and downsampling are effective strategies for mitigating class imbalance in PFNs, with downsampling offering reduced inference cost.

Abstract: Prior-data fitted networks (PFNs) have achieved exceptional performance on tabular classification tasks. However, like other classifiers, their performance can suffer under the effect of class imbalance, resulting in poor performance for rare classes. Several techniques exist which attempt to mitigate the deleterious effect of class imbalance on classification performance, but the in-context learning (ICL) dynamic of PFNs means that loss-based strategies are impossible, and other techniques are unproven. We have adapted several classical techniques addressing class imbalance and analyzed their performance on PFN classification. We observe that thresholding performs exceptionally well because of the calibration characteristics of PFNs, and downsampling performs comparably because of PFNs exceptional limited-data performance, with the additional benefit of reduced computation cost for inference.

</details>


### [150] [A Reproducible Log-Driven AutoML Framework for Interpretable Pipeline Optimization in Healthcare Risk Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21528)
*Rui Huang,Lican Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces yvsoucom-iterkit, a deterministic AutoML framework that encodes pipelines as traceable logs. Testing over 18,000 configurations on diabetes and stroke datasets reveals a redundant search space where performance is driven by a few components (augmentation, model choice, imbalance handling). Ensemble models achieve strong results (Weighted-F1 up to 0.94) with lower variability than SVM, suggesting AutoML can focus on high-impact components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate disease risk prediction is challenged by heterogeneous features, limited samples, and severe class imbalance, motivating a need for reproducible and interpretable automated ML frameworks.

Method: A deterministic, log-driven AutoML framework (yvsoucom-iterkit) that encodes each pipeline as a traceable log entity for component attribution, interaction, similarity, and cross-seed robustness analysis. Tested on Pima Indians Diabetes and Stroke datasets with over 18,000 pipeline configurations.

Result: Augmentation (0.454), model choice (0.198), and imbalance handling (0.101) drive Pima performance; imbalance handling dominates Stroke (0.406). Strong component redundancy observed (e.g., biMax-biMean RMS=0.0252). Ensemble models achieve Weighted-F1 0.89/0.94 on Pima/Stroke; Macro-F1 lower on Stroke (0.67). Ensembles show lower cross-seed variability (0.023-0.026) than SVM.

Conclusion: Effective AutoML optimization can focus on a reduced set of high-impact components, and the search space is partially redundant with structured performance patterns.

Abstract: Accurate and reproducible disease risk prediction remains challenging due to heterogeneous features, limited samples, and severe class imbalance. This study introduces yvsoucom-iterkit, a deterministic and log-driven automated machine learning framework that formulates pipeline optimization as a fully reproducible, configuration-level system. Each pipeline is encoded as a traceable log entity, enabling analysis of component attribution, interactions, similarity, and cross-seed robustness. Experiments on the Pima Indians Diabetes and Stroke datasets across more than 18,000 pipeline configurations reveal a structured and partially redundant search space, where performance is governed by a small subset of interacting components. Random Forest importance analysis identifies augmentation (0.454), model choice (0.198), and imbalance handling (0.101) as key drivers on Pima, while imbalance handling dominates Stroke (0.406). Component similarity analysis shows strong redundancy, with feature selection variants (biMax-biMean) exhibiting low RMS distance (0.0252), mixup closely matching no augmentation (0.0279), and TomekLinks aligning with no imbalance handling (0.0325), whereas Gaussian noise shows greater divergence from no augmentation (0.10). The framework achieves strong and stable performance using ensemble models (Weighted-F1 0.89, Macro-F1 0.88 on Pima; Weighted-F1 0.94 on Stroke), while Macro-F1 remains lower on Stroke (0.67) due to class imbalance. Cross-seed analysis reveals a performance-robustness trade-off, with ensembles showing lower variability (0.023-0.026) than SVM. These results indicate that effective AutoML optimization can focus on a reduced set of high-impact components.

</details>


### [151] [Optimal Guarantees for Auditing Rényi Differentially Private Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21938)
*Benjamin D. Kim,Lav R. Varshney,Daniel Alabi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a hypothesis-testing based framework for black-box auditing of Rényi differential privacy (RDP) claims, using Donsker-Varadhan variational estimators to directly estimate Rényi divergence. It provides non-asymptotic confidence intervals, establishes matching minimax optimal sample-complexity guarantees, and demonstrates strong empirical improvements over prior black-box auditing methods on DP-SGD across MNIST and CIFAR-10.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a theoretically rigorous and optimal auditing framework for verifying RDP claims made by machine learning algorithms, addressing the gap between existing auditing methods and information-theoretic limits.

Method: The paper formulates RDP auditing as a hypothesis testing problem and uses the Donsker-Varadhan variational estimator to estimate Rényi divergence between neighboring executions. It provides non-asymptotic confidence intervals via class-restricted DV estimators and proves matching minimax lower bounds for sample complexity.

Result: The framework achieves information-theoretically optimal sample complexity (up to logarithmic factors). Empirically, it produces strong improvements on RDP lower bounds compared to prior state-of-the-art black-box methods, particularly at small and moderate Rényi orders where accurate auditing is most challenging.

Conclusion: The proposed auditing framework is the first to achieve optimal guarantees for auditing RDP via DV estimators, with both theoretical optimality and strong empirical performance on DP-SGD across multiple datasets and privacy regimes.

Abstract: We study black-box auditing for machine learning algorithms that claim R \ 'enyi differential privacy (RDP) guarantees. We introduce an auditing framework, based on hypothesis testing, that directly estimates Rényi divergence between neighboring executions using the Donsker-Varadhan (DV) variational estimator. Our analysis yields explicit and non-asymptotic confidence intervals for RDP auditing via class-restricted DV estimators, separating statistical estimation error from algorithmic privacy leakage. We prove matching minimax lower bounds showing that, up to logarithmic factors, our sample-complexity guarantees are information-theoretically optimal, thereby establishing the first optimal guarantees for auditing RDP via DV estimators. Empirically, we instantiate our framework for auditing DP-SGD in a fully black-box setting. Across MNIST and CIFAR-10, and over a wide range of privacy regimes, our auditors produce a strong overall improvement on empirical RDP lower bounds compared to prior state-of-the-art black-box methods especially at small and moderate Rényi orders where accurate auditing is most challenging.

</details>


### [152] [DualOptim+: Bridging Shared and Decoupled Optimizer States for Better Machine Unlearning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21539)
*Xuyang Zhong,Qizhang Li,Yiwen Guo,Chen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualOptim+ is a new optimization framework for machine unlearning in LLMs that uses base and delta states to balance forgetting and retaining objectives, with a quantized 8-bit variant, achieving superior trade-offs across various tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve machine unlearning in large language models by better managing the conflict between forgetting and retaining objectives during optimization.

Method: DualOptim+ introduces a base state for shared representations and delta states for objective-specific residuals, adaptively bridging them based on directional gradient conflicts. Also introduces a quantized 8-bit variant.

Result: Consistently achieves superior trade-off between forgetting and retaining objectives across fictitious unlearning, real-world unlearning, safety alignment, and multi-task learning tasks.

Conclusion: DualOptim+ effectively balances conflicting objectives in LLM unlearning, and its 8-bit quantized variant maintains performance while reducing memory overhead.

Abstract: We propose DualOptim+, a novel optimization framework for improving machine unlearning in large language models. It introduces a base state to capture common representations shared by forgetting and retaining objectives and delta states to preserve objective-specific residuals. This architecture allows the optimizer to adaptively bridge shared and decoupled states based on the directional conflict between forgetting and retaining gradients. We further introduce DualOptim+ 8bit, a quantized variant that reduces memory overhead without compromising performance. Extensive experiments across fictitious and real-world unlearning, safety alignment, and multi-task learning tasks demonstrate that DualOptim+ consistently achieves a superior trade-off between different objectives. Codes are available at https://github.com/CityU-MLO/DualOptimPlus.

</details>


### [153] [Discovering Entity-Conditioned Lag Heterogeneity: A Lag-Gated Neural Audit Framework for Panel Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21542)
*Andi Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AC-GATE is a model that discovers entity-specific lag patterns in temporal panel data by conditioning lag-weight distributions on observable entity-level proxies, enabling directly auditable lag summaries without post-hoc explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current temporal panel methods lack directly auditable, entity-specific lag summaries; they typically treat lags as post-hoc explanations rather than structural model outputs.

Method: Proposes AC-GATE (Adaptive-Conditioning Encoder with Scale-Invariant Lag Gate), which instantiates conditional Moderated Distributed Lag by using entity-level proxies to condition lag-weight distributions over historical observations.

Result: AC-GATE recovers heterogeneous lag structures in synthetic data and produces non-degenerate, externally structured effective lags in two real-world country-level panels.

Conclusion: The approach enables auditable entity-specific lag discovery, validated through a layered audit protocol separating predictive calibration from lag discovery.

Abstract: Country-level temporal panels are widely used in empirical analysis. Researchers often need to audit how different entities respond to historical signals over different time horizons. Current approaches typically do not provide directly auditable entity-specific lag summaries. We formulate entity-conditioned heterogeneous lag discovery as a temporal panel mining task and propose AC-GATE, an Adaptive-Conditioning Encoder with a Scale-Invariant Lag Gate. It instantiates conditional Moderated Distributed Lag by using observable entity-level proxies to condition lag-weight distributions over historical observations, thereby making effective lags structural outputs of the model rather than post-hoc explanations. The evaluation is based on a layered audit protocol that separates predictive calibration from lag discovery. A synthetic panel with known ground-truth lags is used for mechanism recovery testing, and two real-world country-level panels are used for external audit and stress testing. The results show that AC-GATE can recover heterogeneous lag structure in synthetic data, and generates non-degenerate, externally structured effective lags in real data.

</details>


### [154] [Bandit Convex Optimization with Gradient Prediction Adaptivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22191)
*Shuche Wang,Adarsh Barik,Vincent Y. F. Tan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies whether optimistic gradient predictions can improve worst-case regret in bandit convex optimization. The authors prove that under single-point feedback, accurate predictions cannot overcome the Ω(√T) lower bound due to gradient estimation variance. They propose TP-VR-OPT for two-point feedback, achieving O(√(d·𝔼[S_T])) regret, which is optimal up to √d. They also develop adaptive variants and extend to non-stationary environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether optimistic gradient predictions can improve worst-case regret guarantees in bandit convex optimization (BCO) in a prediction-adaptive manner, especially when the cumulative prediction error S_T is small.

Method: The paper proposes Two-Point Variance-Reduced Optimistic Gradient Descent (TP-VR-OPT) with a variance-reduced gradient estimator whose variance scales with prediction error rather than gradient norm, enabling prediction-adaptive regret guarantees in the two-point feedback setting.

Result: 1) A negative result: under single-point feedback, Ω(√T) regret lower bound persists even when S_T=o(T). 2) TP-VR-OPT achieves O(√(d·𝔼[S_T])) regret in two-point feedback. 3) Matching lower bound of Ω(√(𝔼[S_T])) showing optimality up to √d. 4) Adaptive variants without prior knowledge of 𝔼[S_T] or T. 5) Extension to non-stationary environments with dynamic regret guarantees.

Conclusion: The paper provides a fundamental characterization of prediction-adaptive regret in BCO, showing that two-point feedback is necessary to benefit from accurate gradient predictions, and achieves near-optimal regret bounds.

Abstract: Bandit convex optimization (BCO) is a fundamental online learning framework with partial feedback, where the learner observes only the loss incurred at the chosen decision point in each round. In this work, we investigate whether optimistic gradient predictions can improve worst-case regret guarantees in a prediction-adaptive manner. Specifically, given gradient predictions $m_t$, we seek regret bounds that scale with the cumulative prediction error $S_T=\sum_{t=1}^T \|\nabla f_t(x_t)-m_t\|^2.$ We first establish a negative result: under the single-point feedback protocol, an unavoidable $Ω(\sqrt{T})$ regret lower bound persists even when $S_T=o(T)$, showing that the variance of gradient estimation fundamentally obscures the benefit of accurate predictions. To overcome this barrier, we propose \emph{Two-Point Variance-Reduced Optimistic Gradient Descent} (TP-VR-OPT) for the two-point feedback setting. The key idea is a novel variance-reduced gradient estimator whose variance scales with the prediction error rather than the gradient norm. This yields a regret bound of $O\big(\sqrt{d\,\mathbb{E}[S_T]}\big),$ where $d$ is the decision dimension. Complementing this result, we establish an information-theoretic lower bound that scales as $Ω(\sqrt{\mathbb{E}[S_T]})$, providing a fundamental characterization of the best achievable prediction-adaptive regret and showing that TP-VR-OPT is optimal up to a factor of $\sqrt d$. We further develop adaptive variants that eliminate the need for prior knowledge of $\mathbb{E}[S_T]$ or the horizon $T$, and extend our framework to non-stationary environments, establishing dynamic regret guarantees that adapt simultaneously to the cumulative prediction error and the comparator path length.

</details>


### [155] [Provable Joint Decontamination for Benchmarking Multiple Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21543)
*Zhenlong Liu,Hao Zeng,Hongxin Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JECS is a conformal selection method for multi-model benchmark decontamination that provides provable global contamination rate control by aggregating per-model p-values via the per-item maximum and applying adaptive BH to envelope-rescaled values.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmark data contamination inflates LLM evaluation scores and makes cross-model comparisons unreliable. Existing score-based methods lack theoretical guarantees, while conformal approaches only work for single models and can produce model-specific benchmarks that undermine fair comparison.

Method: JECS computes conformal p-values per model, aggregates them via per-item maximum, reconstructs a conservative envelope of the max-p null distribution from right-tail observations above a data-driven threshold, then applies adaptive Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) to envelope-rescaled values for benchmark selection with GCR control.

Result: Extensive experiments across various models and benchmarks demonstrate JECS achieves higher power than the max-p baseline while consistently maintaining target GCR control.

Conclusion: JECS provides a principled, theoretically-grounded solution for multi-model benchmark decontamination with provable guarantees, enabling fairer cross-model comparisons.

Abstract: Benchmark data contamination has become a central challenge in LLM evaluation: when evaluation examples appear in the training data of one or more audited models, reported performance can be inflated and cross-model comparisons become unreliable. A broad line of training-data detection work designs scores to quantify how strongly a model memorizes a given data point, but these score-based methods lack theoretical guarantees. Recent conformal approaches provide provable false-identification control for a single model; however, applying them separately to each model can produce model-specific benchmarks, undermining fair comparison across models. In this work, we formalize multi-model benchmark decontamination as a joint selection problem and propose Joint Envelope Conformal Selection (JECS), a conformal procedure that enables global contamination rate (GCR) control under stated assumptions. Specifically, JECS computes per-model conformal p-values, aggregates them by the per-item maximum, and reconstructs a conservative envelope of the max-p null distribution from right-tail observations above a data-driven threshold. By applying the adaptive Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) procedure to the envelope-rescaled values, we select a benchmark with provable GCR control. Extensive experiments across various models and benchmarks demonstrate that JECS achieves higher power than the max-p baseline while consistently maintaining the target GCR control.

</details>


### [156] [The Value of Covariance Matching in Gaussian DDPMs and the Lanczos Sampler](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22723)
*Md Sahil Akhtar,Aymane El Gadarri,Vivek F. Farias,Adam D. Jozefiak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that matching full posterior covariance in Gaussian DDPMs reduces path-space KL divergence from Ω(1/T) to O(1/T²), and introduces LGS — a training-free, matrix-free method using Lanczos iterations to sample from the optimal reverse covariance efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard isotropic reverse covariances suffer an unavoidable Ω(1/T) path-KL error. The authors aim to break this barrier and make full covariance matching practical for improved DDPM sampling.

Method: Lanczos Gaussian sampler (LGS) — a training-free, matrix-free method that samples from the optimal reverse covariance using only covariance-vector products (via Jacobian-vector products of the posterior mean), with exponential error decay in Lanczos steps.

Result: Full covariance matching reduces path KL from Ω(1/T) to O(1/T²). LGS with just three Lanczos steps improves sample quality over strong diagonal-covariance baselines like OCM-DDPM on standard image benchmarks.

Conclusion: Full covariance matching is both theoretically valuable and practically accessible for fast DDPM sampling, breaking the prior path-KL barrier with an efficient, training-free algorithm.

Abstract: A central error measure in Gaussian DDPMs is the path-space KL divergence between the exact reverse chain and the learned Gaussian reverse process. This quantity is especially relevant for procedures such as classifier guidance, which perturb the entire reverse trajectory rather than only the terminal sample. Prior analyses show that standard isotropic reverse covariances suffer an unavoidable $Ω(1/T)$ path-KL error as the number of denoising steps $T$ grows. We show that matching the full posterior covariance breaks this barrier, yielding an order-wise improvement that reduces the path KL to $O(1/T^2)$. To make full covariance matching practical, we introduce the Lanczos Gaussian sampler (LGS), a training-free, matrix-free method for sampling from the optimal reverse covariance using only covariance-vector products, which are available through Jacobian-vector products of the posterior mean. LGS avoids dense covariance storage and auxiliary covariance models. We prove that LGS approximation error decays exponentially in the number of Lanczos steps, where each Lanczos step requires a single Jacobian-vector product. Empirically, using only just three such steps improves sample quality over strong diagonal-covariance baselines, including OCM-DDPM, across standard image benchmarks. This identifies full covariance matching as both theoretically valuable and practically accessible for fast DDPM sampling.

</details>


### [157] [Tabular foundation models for robust calibration of near-infrared chemical sensing data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21544)
*Robin Reiter,Denis Cornet,Fabien Michel,Lauriane Rouan,Gregory Beurier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates TabPFN, a tabular foundation model, as a calibration strategy for NIR spectroscopy by benchmarking it on 66 datasets. Preprocessing-optimized TabPFN achieves the best overall rank in regression, while raw-spectra TabPFN excels in classification. However, classical methods remain competitive on outliers and extrapolated samples, suggesting foundation models can complement but not replace traditional chemometric workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: NIR spectroscopy calibration faces challenges including high-dimensional collinear spectra, small sample sizes, preprocessing dependence, outliers, and extrapolation issues. The authors explore whether tabular foundation models (specifically TabPFN) can offer a new, effective calibration strategy to address these limitations.

Method: TabPFN was benchmarked on 66 NIR datasets (54 regression, 12 classification) and compared against PLS/PLS-DA, Ridge, CatBoost, and 1D-CNNs. A unified validation framework was used where preprocessing and model selection were performed exclusively on calibration data before external test evaluation.

Result: In regression, preprocessing-optimized TabPFN achieved the best overall average rank, significantly outperforming PLS, CatBoost, raw-spectra TabPFN, and CNN-1D, while being statistically comparable to Ridge. In classification, raw-spectra TabPFN provided the best average rank. However, TabPFN's advantage decreased on spectral outliers and extrapolated samples.

Conclusion: Tabular foundation models like TabPFN can complement established chemometric workflows for NIR chemical sensing, particularly in small- to medium-sized calibration settings. However, spectroscopy-specific priors and uncertainty-aware deployment strategies are still needed, as classical methods remain competitive for outliers and extrapolation.

Abstract: Near-infrared spectroscopy is increasingly used as a rapid, non-destructive chemical sensing technology for the analysis of food, pharmaceutical, biological, and environmental samples. However, the practical deployment of NIR sensors still depends on calibration models able to handle high-dimensional, collinear spectra, limited sample sizes, preprocessing dependence, spectral outliers, and extrapolation beyond the calibration domain. Here, we evaluate whether tabular foundation models can provide a new calibration strategy for NIR chemical sensing. We benchmark TabPFN on 66 NIR datasets covering 54 regression and 12 classification tasks, and compare direct inference on raw spectra with preprocessing-optimized inference against PLS/PLS-DA, Ridge, Catboost, and one-dimensional convolutional neural networks. The study uses a unified validation framework in which preprocessing and model selection are performed exclusively on calibration data before external test evaluation. In regression, preprocessing-optimized TabPFN achieves the best overall average rank and significantly outperforms PLS, CatBoost, TabPFN on raw spectra, and CNN-1D, while remaining statistically comparable to Ridge. In classification, TabPFN applied directly to raw spectra provides the best average rank, with performance close to the optimized variant. Robustness analyses show that TabPFN provides strong average predictive performance but that its advantage decreases on spectral outliers and extrapolated samples, where classical chemometric models remain competitive. These results suggest that tabular foundation models can complement established chemometric workflows for NIR chemical sensing, especially in small- to medium-sized calibration settings, while highlighting the need for spectroscopy-specific priors and uncertainty-aware deployment strategies.

</details>


### [158] [PeakFocus: Bridging Peak Localization and Intensity Regression via a Unified Multi-Scale Framework for Electricity Load Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21550)
*Wangzhi Yu,Peng Zhu,Qing Zhao,Yiwen Jiang,Dawei Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PeakFocus is a unified framework for Electricity Load Peak Forecasting that jointly predicts peak timing and intensity via a peak-aware pipeline, multi-scale mixing locator, and location-aware decoder, outperforming baselines on public and industrial datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ELPF methods have three key limitations: (1) a two-stage predict-then-locate paradigm that severs temporal localization from intensity regression; (2) multi-scale representation conflicts causing peak misjudgment and timing misalignment; (3) lack of explicit peak timing context during intensity regression leading to intensity smoothing.

Method: PeakFocus introduces three components: (i) Unified Peak-Aware Pipeline (UPAP) with triple hybrid loss for joint supervision and tolerance-based evaluation; (ii) Multi-Scale Mixing Peak Locator (MSM-PL) using coarse-to-fine feature cascading to resolve peak misjudgment and timing misalignment; (iii) Location-Aware Decoder (LAD) injecting peak timing context into intensity regression to counteract smoothing.

Result: Extensive experiments on the public Electricity (ELC) dataset and the industrial-scale World Large-scale Electricity Load (WLEL) dataset demonstrate that PeakFocus outperforms baselines in both timing precision and intensity estimation.

Conclusion: PeakFocus effectively addresses the three limitations of existing ELPF methods by unifying peak timing localization and intensity regression, handling multi-scale conflicts, and providing explicit timing guidance, achieving superior forecasting performance.

Abstract: Electricity load peak forecasting (ELPF), simultaneously predicting peak timing and intensity, is a prerequisite for effective grid scheduling and risk management. However, existing methods face three limitations. First, they adopt a two-stage predict-then-locate paradigm, which severs the link between temporal localization and intensity regression. Second, they still struggle with the multi-scale representation conflict, leading to peak misjudgment and timing misalignment. Third, the lack of explicit peak timing context during intensity regression causes intensity smoothing because predictions are dominated by global smoothing trends. To address these limitations, we propose PeakFocus, a unified framework for ELPF. (i) A Unified Peak-Aware Pipeline (UPAP) utilizes a triple hybrid loss to jointly supervise temporal localization and intensity regression, alongside a tolerance-based evaluation protocol. (ii) A Multi-Scale Mixing Peak Locator (MSM-PL) exploits coarse-grained features to mitigate peak misjudgment caused by local fluctuations, and injects them into fine-grained features via a cascade mechanism to resolve timing misalignment. (iii) A Location-Aware Decoder (LAD) injects peak timing context into the intensity regression process, providing explicit guidance to counteract intensity smoothing and improve peak intensity estimation. Extensive experiments on the public Electricity (ELC) dataset and our industrial-scale World Large-scale Electricity Load (WLEL) dataset show that PeakFocus outperforms baselines in both timing precision and intensity estimation.

</details>


### [159] [Expectation Consistency Loss: Rethink Confidence Calibration under Covariate Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21552)
*Jinzong Dong,Zhaohui Jiang,Bo Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Expectation Consistency Condition (ECC) and Expectation Consistency Loss (ECL) for confidence calibration of classification models under covariate shifts, providing theoretical foundations and a practical unsupervised domain adaptation loss that outperforms existing methods across simulated and real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing confidence calibration methods assume i.i.d. data and struggle under covariate shifts, with previous approaches suffering from class-wise/calibration limitations and unstable importance weighting when density ratios are large or unbounded.

Method: Derives a necessary and sufficient condition (Expectation Consistency Condition) for calibration under covariate shifts, then proposes an unsupervised domain adaptation loss (ECL) compatible with canonical, class-wise, and top-label calibration, with proven sample complexity matching ECE and a mini-batch trainable scheme.

Result: Validated on both simulated and real-world covariate shift datasets, demonstrating effectiveness of the proposed ECL method.

Conclusion: Covariate shifts do not necessarily cause miscalibration; the proposed ECL provides a theoretically grounded, stable, and effective calibration approach under covariate shifts.

Abstract: Confidence calibration for classification models is vital in safety-critical decision-making scenarios and has received extensive attention. General confidence calibration methods assume training and test data are independent and identically distributed, limiting their effectiveness under covariate shifts. Previous calibration methods under covariate shift struggle with class-wise or canonical calibrations and often rely on unstable importance weighting when density ratios are large or unbounded. Given the above limitations, this paper rethinks confidence calibration under covariate shifts. First, we derive a necessary and sufficient condition for confidence calibration under covariate shifts, named Expectation consistency condition, which reveals covariate shifts do not necessarily lead to uncalibrated confidence and provides a weaker condition for confidence calibration than global covariate distribution alignment. Then, utilizing Expectation consistency condition, this paper proposes an unsupervised domain adaptation loss to calibrate confidence of the target domain, named Expectation consistency loss (ECL), which is compatible with canonical calibration, class-wise calibration, and top-label calibration. Third, we prove that computing ECL loss has the same sample complexity as Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and provide a theoretically grounded mini-batch trainable scheme for ECL loss. Finally, we validate the effectiveness of our method on both simulated and real-world covariate shift datasets.

</details>


### [160] [Beyond Single Slot: Joint Optimization for Multi-Slot Guaranteed Display Advertising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21556)
*Zhaoqi Zhang,Jiaming Deng,Miao Xie,Linyou Cai,Qianlong Xie,Xingxing Wang,Siqiang Luo,Gao Cong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A joint optimization framework for multi-slot guaranteed display advertising allocation, addressing slot-level redundancy, contract imbalance, and exposure concentration through offline bipartite matching with contract roulette mechanism, achieving 28.99% ARPU increase in Meituan online tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GD advertising methods assume single-slot scenarios, limiting optimization across multi-slot page views. Key challenges include slot-level redundancy, contract imbalance, and exposure concentration that need to be addressed.
