Method: Formulates allocation as offline bipartite matching with contract roulette mechanism for slot exclusivity and Page View constraints for impression control, plus a scalable allocation optimization algorithm for large-scale deployment.

Result: Online A/B tests on Meituan platform show 28.99% increase in Average Revenue Per User under 70% traffic, with DID analysis indicating improved contract stability.

Conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates strong applicability and effectiveness for real-world multi-slot guaranteed display advertising, improving merchant ROI, platform revenue efficiency, and contract fulfillment robustness.

Abstract: Guaranteed display advertising is crucial for platform monetization, yet existing methods often operate under a single-slot assumption, limiting their ability to optimize allocation across multi-slot page views. In this paper, we propose a novel joint optimization framework for multi-slot GD allocation, addressing key challenges such as slot-level redundancy, contract imbalance, and exposure concentration. Our approach formulates the allocation as an offline bipartite matching problem with a contract roulette mechanism for slot exclusivity and Page View constraints for impression control, and incorporates a scalable allocation optimization algorithm for efficient large-scale deployment. Extensive online tests on the Meituan advertising platform demonstrate that our method significantly improves merchant ROI, platform revenue efficiency, and contract fulfillment robustness. Specifically, online A/B tests show a 28.99% increase in Average Revenue Per User under 70% traffic, and DID analysis further indicates improved contract stability, demonstrating the strong applicability and effectiveness of our framework in real-world advertising deployments.

</details>


### [161] [From Parameters to Data: A Task-Parameter-Guided Fine-Tuning Pipeline for Efficient LLM Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21558)
*Hao Chen,Qi Zhang,Liyao Li,Zhanming Shen,Wentao Ye,Lirong Gao,Ningtao Wang,Xing Fu,Xiaoyu Shen,Junbo Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: P2D leverages task-sensitive attention heads as a dual mechanism for both data selection and model pruning, achieving 8.3 pp gains and 7.0x speedup with only 10% heads on 10% data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM adaptation to specialized domains is costly; prior work treats data selection and parameter-efficient fine-tuning as separate processes, but they may be intrinsically coupled.

Method: Proposes P2D framework with Strong Map Hypothesis (sparse attention heads unlock specific data patterns). Identifies critical heads via lightweight proxy, uses them as functional filter for data curation, and performs structural pruning simultaneously.

Result: Updating 10% attention heads on 10% data yields 8.3 pp performance improvement and 7.0x end-to-end speedup over strong baselines.

Conclusion: Precise parameter-data synchronization eliminates redundancy, establishing a new paradigm for efficient LLM alignment.

Abstract: Adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to specialized domains typically incurs high data and computational overhead. While prior efficiency efforts have largely treated data selection and parameter-efficient fine-tuning as isolated processes, our empirical analysis suggests they may be intrinsically coupled. We posit the Strong Map Hypothesis: a sparse subset of attention heads plays a dominant role in task-specific adaptation, acting as keys that unlock specific data patterns. Building on this observation, we propose From Parameters to Data (P2D), a unified framework that leverages these task-sensitive attention heads as a dual compass for both sample mining and structural pruning. To rigorously quantify the total pipeline cost, we introduce the Alignment Efficiency Ratio (AER) metric for both selection latency and training time. Mechanistically, P2D identifies critical heads via a lightweight proxy and uses them as a functional filter to curate high-affinity data, establishing a synergistic pipeline. Empirically, by updating merely 10% of attention heads on 10% of the data, P2D achieves an 8.3 pp performance gain over strong baselines and delivers a 7.0x end-to-end time speedup. These results validate that precise parameter-data synchronization eliminates redundancy, offering a new paradigm for efficient alignment.

</details>


### [162] [Energy-Gated Attention: Spectral Salience as an Inductive Bias for Transformer Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21842)
*Athanasios Zeris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Energy-Gated Attention (EGA), a modification to standard transformer attention that gates value aggregation by the spectral energy of key token embeddings, discovering dominant spectral modes through a learned linear projection. EGA achieves ~0.1 validation loss improvement on TinyShakespeare and Penn Treebank with minimal overhead (<0.26% parameters). The learned energy threshold converges to ~0.35, corresponding to the ~36% of tokens carrying above-average spectral energy in English text, matching the fraction of content words.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard transformer attention treats all tokens as equally salient regardless of informational content. The authors draw an analogy from turbulent fluid dynamics where coherent structures carry disproportionate energy, proposing that informationally dense tokens (morphological boundaries, syntactic heads, discourse markers) concentrate spectral energy and should attract proportionally more attention than background tokens (function words, fillers).

Method: Energy-Gated Attention (EGA) modifies standard attention by gating value aggregation using the spectral energy of key token embeddings, computed via a single learned linear projection that identifies the dominant spectral mode. The paper ablates across three wavelet families (fixed Morlet, Daubechies db2/db4, parametric Morlet) and a learned adaptive approach.

Result: EGA achieves +0.103 validation loss improvement on TinyShakespeare and +0.101 on Penn Treebank with only 12,480 additional parameters (<0.26% overhead) and no measurable computational cost. The learned energy threshold converges to ~0.35 regardless of initialization, corresponding to ~36% of tokens carrying above-average spectral energy in English text.

Conclusion: Fixed structured bases are suboptimal for identifying informationally dense tokens; the optimal energy direction is data-adaptive and non-sinusoidal. Learned wavelet packets are identified as a promising open direction for future work.

Abstract: Standard transformer attention computes pairwise similarity between queries and keys, treating all tokens as equally salient regardless of their intrinsic informational content. In turbulent fluid dynamics, coherent structures -- the energetically dominant, spatially organized patterns that persist amid background chaos -- carry a disproportionate fraction of total energy and govern all transport. We propose that tokens play an analogous role in transformer attention: informationally dense positions (morphological boundaries, syntactic heads, discourse markers) concentrate spectral energy and should attract proportionally more attention than background tokens (function words, repeated patterns, low-information filler). We propose Energy-Gated Attention (EGA): a simple modification that gates value aggregation by the spectral energy of key token embeddings, computed by a single learned linear projection that discovers the dominant spectral mode of the embedding field. On TinyShakespeare, EGA achieves +0.103 validation loss improvement with only 12,480 additional parameters (<0.26% overhead) and no measurable computational cost. The result is consistent on Penn Treebank (+0.101), demonstrating dataset independence. A systematic ablation across three wavelet families (fixed Morlet, Daubechies db2/db4, and a parametric Morlet) establishes that fixed structured bases are suboptimal -- the optimal energy direction is data-adaptive and non-sinusoidal -- while identifying learned wavelet packets as a promising open direction. The learned energy threshold converges to tau ~= 0.35 independently of initialization, corresponding to the fraction (~36%) of tokens carrying above-average spectral energy in English text, a stable linguistic property consistent with the fraction of content words in running English text.

</details>


### [163] [AutoMCU: Feasibility-First MCU Neural Network Customization via LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21560)
*Penglin Dai,Zijie Zhou,Xincao Xu,Junhua Wang,Xiao Wu,Lixin Duan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AutoMCU is an LLM-based multi-agent system that automatically customizes neural networks for microcontroller deployment by filtering infeasible designs early via hardware toolchain feedback, achieving competitive accuracy with significantly reduced customization time (1-2 hours vs. hundreds of GPU hours).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying neural networks on MCUs is challenging due to tight memory, storage, and computation constraints. Existing approaches (model compression, HW-NAS) rely on proxy metrics, have high search costs, and fail to fully bridge the gap between architecture design and verified deployment.

Method: AutoMCU is a feasibility-first LLM-based multi-agent system with two key mechanisms: 1) hardware-in-the-loop architecture generation that eliminates infeasible candidates early using vendor toolchain feedback under RAM/Flash constraints, and 2) state-isolated multi-agent scheduling for stable coordination of proposal, training, evaluation, and deployment stages.

Result: On CIFAR-10/100 under strict MCU constraints, AutoMCU achieves competitive accuracy while reducing customization time to ~1-2 hours vs. hundreds of GPU hours for HW-NAS baselines. Comparisons with ColabNAS and GENIUS on NAS-Bench-201 show effectiveness and stability. Real deployments on STM32 MCUs validate practical applicability.

Conclusion: AutoMCU effectively addresses MCU deployment challenges by combining LLM-based multi-agent coordination with early hardware feasibility filtering, significantly reducing search cost while maintaining accuracy and ensuring real deployability.

Abstract: Deploying neural networks on microcontroller units (MCUs) is critical for edge intelligence but remains challenging due to tight memory, storage, and computation constraints. Existing approaches, such as model compression and hardware-aware neural architecture search (HW-NAS), often depend on proxy metrics, incur high search cost, and do not fully bridge the gap between architecture design and verified deployment. This paper presents AutoMCU, a feasibility-first large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent system for automated neural network customization under MCU constraints. Given natural-language task requirements and hardware specifications, AutoMCU iteratively generates structured architecture candidates, filters infeasible designs through vendor toolchain feedback before training, evaluates feasible models under a controlled protocol, and verifies deployability through backend-grounded deployment analysis. AutoMCU includes two key mechanisms: 1) hardware-in-the-loop architecture generation for early elimination of undeployable candidates under RAM and Flash constraints, and 2) state-isolated multi-agent scheduling for stable coordination of proposal, training, evaluation, and deployment stages. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 under strict MCU constraints show that AutoMCU achieves competitive accuracy while reducing customization time to about 1--2 hours, compared with hundreds of GPU hours for representative MCU-oriented HW-NAS baselines. Comparisons with ColabNAS and the LLM-based NAS method GENIUS on NAS-Bench-201 further demonstrate the effectiveness and stability of AutoMCU. Real-device deployments on multiple STM32 microcontrollers validate its practical applicability to MCU-scale edge intelligence.

</details>


### [164] [Objective-Induced Bias and Search Dynamics in Multiobjective Unsupervised Feature Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21561)
*Mathieu Cherpitel,Thomas Bäck,Martijn R. Tannemaat,Anna V. Kononova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates how different objectives (accuracy, silhouette score, PCA reconstruction loss) combined with subset-size optimization affect multiobjective unsupervised feature selection, finding that PCA loss yields compact subsets with high predictive performance while silhouette-based methods favor trivial low-cardinality solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how choice of evaluation objective, direction of subset-size regularisation, and initialisation strategy affect multiobjective unsupervised feature selection, which is under-studied despite being critical for formulation behavior.

Method: Controlled experiments on a synthetic dataset with known informative, redundant, and irrelevant features, comparing 6 formulations combining 3 evaluation objectives (accuracy, silhouette score, PCA reconstruction loss) with subset-size minimisation or maximisation.

Result: Silhouette-based formulations bias toward trivial low-cardinality solutions and are weak proxies for predictive performance; PCA loss produces compact subsets with test accuracy comparable to supervised accuracy optimization.

Conclusion: Objective design is central to effective multiobjective unsupervised feature selection; the proposed PCA loss objective is a strong alternative.

Abstract: Unsupervised feature selection is commonly formulated as a multiobjective optimisation problem that jointly optimises subset quality and subset size. Yet the behaviour of this formulation depends critically on the choice of evaluation objective, the direction of subset-size regularisation, and the initialisation strategy. We study these factors in a controlled setting using a synthetic dataset with known informative, redundant, and irrelevant feature types. Six formulations are compared by combining three evaluation objectives: accuracy, silhouette score, and PCA reconstruction loss with subset-size minimisation or maximisation. The results show that formulation strongly affects both search dynamics and the quality of the resulting Pareto front. Silhouette-based formulations exhibit a strong bias toward trivial low-cardinality solutions and remain weak proxies for predictive performance. In contrast, the proposed PCA loss objective produces compact subsets with test accuracy comparable to subsets obtained by directly optimising supervised accuracy. Overall, the study shows that objective design is central to effective multiobjective unsupervised feature selection.

</details>


### [165] [Embedding-Based Federated Learning with Runtime Governance for Iron Deficiency Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21563)
*Fan Zhang,Simon Deltadahl,Majid Lotfian Delouee,Daniel Kreuter,Joseph Taylor,Allerdien Visser,BloodCounts Consortium,James H. F. Rudd,Nicholas S. Gleadall,Suthesh Sivapalaratnam,Folkert Asselbergs,Martijn C. Schut,Michael Roberts*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper deploys a personalized federated learning pipeline for iron deficiency prediction across two heterogeneous clinical sites, showing that FedMAP (personalized aggregation) outperforms FedAvg and local-only training by handling non-IID data distributions effectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most healthcare FL studies never reach real-world deployment. This work addresses the gap by deploying FL across two clinically distinct environments with non-IID data, demonstrating practical feasibility and the need for personalized aggregation.

Method: Uses a frozen domain-specific haematology foundation model (DeepCBC) for local feature extraction, trains only a compact downstream classifier via FL, employs FedMAP (personalized aggregation) instead of FedAvg, and enforces runtime governance via the FLA³ platform.

Result: FedAvg underperformed local-only training due to sample-size bias. FedMAP raised ROC-AUC from 0.9470→0.9594 at AUMC and 0.8558→0.8671 at NHSBT, achieving a macro ROC-AUC of 0.9133 and best balanced accuracy overall.

Conclusion: Personalized aggregation (FedMAP) is essential for clinical federations where client sample sizes and task relevance diverge substantially, as standard FedAvg fails under such non-IID conditions.

Abstract: Recent reviews find that the vast majority of published healthcare federated learning (FL) studies never reach real-world deployment. We developed an embedding-based FL pipeline for iron deficiency prediction from routine full blood count (FBC) data and deployed it across real institutional environments at Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC) and NHS Blood and Transplant (NHSBT), two clinical environments that differ markedly in iron deficiency prevalence, ferritin distribution, and subject populations. A frozen domain-specific haematology foundation model, DeepCBC, performs site-local representation extraction, restricting federated training to a compact downstream classifier and substantially reducing recurrent communication relative to full-encoder federation. The two clinical datasets are structurally not independent and identically distributed (non-IID), with heterogeneity arising from distinct population differences rather than sampling artefacts. Runtime governance is enforced by FLA$^3$, a healthcare-oriented FL platform providing study-scoped execution, policy-based authorisation, and signed audit logging. Standard sample-size-weighted aggregation (FedAvg) reduced the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC-AUC) at both sites relative to local-only training, as the global update was biased towards the larger AUMC distribution. FedMAP, a personalised aggregation method, raised ROC-AUC from 0.9470 to 0.9594 at AUMC and from 0.8558 to 0.8671 at NHSBT relative to local-only training, achieving the highest macro ROC-AUC of 0.9133 and the best macro balanced accuracy overall. These results support personalised aggregation in clinical federations where client sample size and task relevance diverge substantially.

</details>


### [166] [Leveraging Self-Paced Curriculum Learning for Enhanced Modality Balance in Multimodal Conversational Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21565)
*Phuong-Anh Nguyen,The-Son Le,Duc-Trong Le,Cam-Van Thi Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A plug-and-play Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL) framework is proposed for Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations (MERC), using dual-level difficulty measurers (utterance and conversation) to dynamically schedule training from easy to hard examples, achieving consistent improvements on IEMOCAP (+1.2% to +6.6% F1) and MELD (up to +10.4% F1) across architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations suffers from modality misalignment and imbalanced learning, which limits effective utilization of multimodal information.

Method: A plug-and-play SPCL framework with dual-level Difficulty Measurer (utterance-level for modality-specific difficulty and conversation-level for dialogue structure/emotional dependencies) and a Learning Scheduler that dynamically guides training from easier to harder instances.

Result: On IEMOCAP, weighted F1-score improved by ~+1.2% to +6.6% over baselines; on MELD, gains reached up to +10.4%.

Conclusion: SPCL effectively alleviates modality imbalance and improves model robustness as a lightweight, generalizable plug-and-play module for MERC.

Abstract: Multimodal Emotion Recognition in Conversations (MERC) is a crucial task for understanding human interactions, where multimodal approaches integrating language, facial expressions, and vocal tone have achieved significant progress. However, modality misalignment and imbalanced learning remain major challenges, limiting the effective utilization of multimodal information. To address this issue, we propose a plug-and-play framework based on Self-Paced Curriculum Learning (SPCL) for MERC. We introduce a dual-level Difficulty Measurer that captures both utterance-level and conversation-level challenges. The utterance-level score models fine-grained modality-specific difficulty, while the conversation-level score captures broader dialogue structures, including emotional dependencies and modality coherence. Based on these scores, the Learning Scheduler dynamically guides training from easier to more difficult instances. By integrating SPCL into existing MERC architectures, our method alleviates modality imbalance and improves model robustness. Extensive experiments on the IEMOCAP and MELD datasets demonstrate consistent improvements across different architectures and modality settings. On IEMOCAP, SPCL improves weighted F1-score by approximately +1.2% to +6.6% over baseline models, while on MELD, gains reach up to +10.4%. These results highlight the effectiveness and generalizability of SPCL as a lightweight plug-and-play module for multimodal emotion recognition.

</details>


### [167] [Calibration, Uncertainty Communication, and Deployment Readiness in CKD Risk Prediction: A Framework Evaluation Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21566)
*Michael O. Eniolade*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Five ML models achieved perfect AUROC (1.00) on CKD prediction using a small internal dataset, but failed catastrophically when tested on external MIMIC-IV data—AUROC dropped to ~0.48-0.58, calibration error surged to ~0.68-0.76, and conformal coverage collapsed far below the 90% target. None scored above 4/16 on deployment readiness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CKD risk prediction models focus heavily on discrimination (AUROC), but neglect calibration and uncertainty quantification, which are essential for clinical decision-making. This gap leaves clinicians with potentially misleading probability outputs.

Method: Five classifiers (logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, SVM+Platt, GNB) were trained on the UCI CKD dataset (N=400). Models were evaluated on calibration quality, conformal prediction coverage, and an eight-criterion deployment framework. A distributional stress-test was applied using MIMIC-IV (N=97) to test robustness under prevalence shift and feature missingness.

Result: All models achieved AUROC 1.00 on UCI with near-perfect calibration after isotonic regression. On MIMIC-IV, AUROC dropped to 0.48-0.58, ECE rose to 0.68-0.76, conformal coverage fell from 0.80-0.98 to 0.21-0.25, and no model exceeded 4/16 on deployment readiness.

Conclusion: Near-perfect internal performance does not transfer to external data. Calibration stability and conformal coverage should be mandatory evaluation steps before deploying any clinical prediction model.

Abstract: Machine learning models for chronic kidney disease (CKD) risk prediction often post strong discrimination scores on internal test sets. Calibration and uncertainty quantification get far less attention, leaving clinicians without reliable information about whether the probability outputs are accurate.
  We trained five classifiers on the UCI CKD dataset (400 patients, 62.5% CKD prevalence): logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, SVM with Platt scaling, and Gaussian naive Bayes. We evaluated each across calibration quality, conformal prediction coverage, and an eight-criterion deployment readiness framework. A distributional stress-test applied the best-calibrated variant of each model to the open-access MIMIC-IV demo cohort (97 patients, 23.7% CKD) to assess behaviour under prevalence shift and feature missingness. We measured calibration before and after Platt scaling and isotonic regression using Expected Calibration Error and Brier Score, and quantified uncertainty through split conformal prediction targeting 90% marginal coverage.
  All five models reached AUROC 1.00 on the UCI test set. Isotonic recalibration reduced internal ECE to 0.000-0.022. On MIMIC-IV, AUROC fell to 0.48-0.58, ECE rose to 0.68-0.76, and conformal coverage dropped from 0.80-0.98 to 0.21-0.25 against a 90% target. No model scored above 4 out of 16 on the deployment readiness checklist.
  Near-perfect internal performance did not transfer. Calibration stability and conformal coverage should be evaluated on external data before any clinical prediction model moves toward deployment.

</details>


### [168] [Equilibrium Propagation and Hamiltonian Inference in the Diffusive Fitzhugh-Nagumo Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21568)
*Jack Kendall*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends Equilibrium Propagation to skew-gradient systems and shows equivalence between deep Energy-Based Models and Hamiltonian neural networks, using Fitzhugh-Nagumo neuron networks as a case study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between Equilibrium Propagation (used in Energy-Based Models) and Hamiltonian neural networks, enabling credit assignment in biologically plausible neural network models.

Method: Extends Equilibrium Propagation to skew-gradient systems; shows stationary solutions of Fitzhugh-Nagumo models are described by self-adjoint operators; uses topology of deep residual networks to derive layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence relations.

Result: Demonstrates equivalence between deep Energy-Based Models and Hamiltonian neural networks; shows equilibrium propagation and Hamiltonian Echo Backpropagation can both be applied to Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks.

Conclusion: Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks and deep Energy-Based Models share a unified Hamiltonian framework for inference, enabling alternative learning rules for biologically plausible deep learning.

Abstract: In this work, we extend the Equilibrium Propagation framework to skew-gradient systems and show an equivalence between deep Energy-Based Models and Hamiltonian neural networks. We focus on networks of diffusively coupled Fitzhugh-Nagumo neurons as a prototypical example. We show that since stationary solutions of the Fitzhugh-Nagumo model are described by self-adjoint operators, the methods of equilibrium propagation for performing credit assignment can be applied. Furthermore, for Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks with the topology of a deep residual network, we show that the steady state solutions admit a (spatial) Hamiltonian, and thus the methods of Hamiltonian Echo Backpropagation can be applied. We end by deriving an explicit layer-wise Hamiltonian recurrence relation governing inference for stationary solutions of both deep Fitzhugh-Nagumo networks and deep Energy-Based Models.

</details>


### [169] [ConTact: Contact-First Antibody CDR Design via Explicit Interface Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21600)
*Mansoor Ahmed,Spencer VonBank,Nadeem Taj,Sujin Lee,Naila Jan,Murray Patterson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ConTact is a contact-then-act architecture for antibody CDR design that explicitly separates contact prediction from amino acid selection, achieving superior structural quality and epitope awareness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CDR design methods conflate contact reasoning with amino acid selection, forcing models to learn contacts implicitly through uniform message passing, which dilutes antigen signal across all positions.

Method: Three-stage cascade: (1) learning surface complementarity fingerprints, (2) predicting CDR-antigen contacts, (3) injecting contact-gated antigen features into sequence head. Uses distance-biased cross-attention and contact-weighted cross-entropy loss.

Result: On CHIMERA-Bench: 7% RMSD improvement over next-best baseline, 10% F1 score over GNN baselines, competitive AAR of 0.38 for CDR-H3 design.

Conclusion: Explicitly decoupling contact prediction from sequence selection improves both structural quality and antigen awareness in computational antibody design.

Abstract: Computational antibody CDR design methods condition on antigen structure to generate binding loops, yet existing architectures conflate two fundamentally distinct sub-problems: identifying which CDR positions will contact the antigen, and selecting amino acids at those positions. This conflation forces models to learn contact reasoning implicitly through uniform message passing, diluting antigen signal across all positions equally. We introduce ConTact, a contact-then-act architecture that explicitly decomposes CDR design into three cascaded stages: learning surface complementarity fingerprints, predicting CDR-antigen contacts, and injecting contact-gated antigen features into the sequence head. A distance-biased cross-attention module encodes geometric priors favoring spatial neighbors, while a contact-weighted cross-entropy loss concentrates gradient signal on binding-critical positions. On CHIMERA-Bench dataset, ConTact achieves the best structural quality (7% RMSD improvement over the next-best baseline), best epitope awareness (10% F1 score over GNN baselines), and competitive sequence recovery (AAR 0.38) among several CDR-H3 design baselines.

</details>


### [170] [When Are Teacher Tokens Reliable? Position-Weighted On-Policy Self-Distillation for Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21606)
*Xiaogeng Liu,Xinyan Wang,Yingzi Ma,Yechao Zhang,Chaowei Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies that teacher-token reliability in on-policy self-distillation for reasoning is trajectory-structured (not entropy-based), and proposes PW-OPSD which weights token supervision by position, improving math reasoning benchmarks (AIME) by +1.0-1.1 points across multiple models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing entropy-based on-policy distillation methods treat high teacher entropy as unreliable, but in reasoning tasks, high entropy can reflect either uncertainty or benign solution diversity. The authors aim to find a better predictor of teacher-token reliability that accounts for trajectory structure.

Method: (1) Introduces a branch-viability diagnostic that forces alternative next-tokens from the teacher prompt and tests if continuations recover correct answers. (2) Proposes PW-OPSD, which applies increasing position weights to token-level supervision while keeping the student rollout, privileged teacher pass, and clipped forward-KL target unchanged.

Result: Within-sequence position score is the strongest predictor of teacher-token reliability (AUROC 0.83), outperforming local uncertainty scores (max 0.57). PW-OPSD improves AIME 2024/2025 Avg@12 by +1.0 and +1.1 points, with consistent gains on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B and Olmo-3-7B-Think.

Conclusion: Teacher-token reliability in reasoning distillation is trajectory-structured and can be effectively leveraged via position weighting without additional teacher computation.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) trains a student on its own rollouts using a privileged teacher, but its standard objective weights all generated tokens equally, implicitly treating the privileged teacher target as equally reliable at every student-visited prefix. Existing entropy-based OPD methods relax this uniformity by modulating token-level supervision with teacher entropy, but high teacher entropy in reasoning has an ambiguous reliability meaning: it can reflect either non-viable uncertainty or benign solution diversity. To identify this phenomenon, we introduce a branch-viability diagnostic. Specifically, we record next-token alternatives from the privileged-answer teacher prompt, force each alternative after the student prompt plus its on-policy spine prefix, and test whether the resulting student-template continuation recovers the correct answer. On Qwen3-4B, we find that an oriented within-sequence position score is the strongest tested predictor of teacher-token reliability, reaching an area-under-ROC-curve (AUROC) of 0.83; local uncertainty scores are at most 0.57. Motivated by this trajectory-level structure, we propose Position-Weighted On-Policy Self-Distillation (PW-OPSD), which applies an increasing position weight while keeping the same student rollout, privileged teacher pass, and clipped forward-KL target as OPSD. In our comprehensive evaluations with different random seeds, the diagnostic-derived PW-OPSD improves AIME 2024 and AIME 2025 Avg@12 by +1.0 and +1.1 points, and a generalization evaluation on two larger-scale models from different families, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B and Olmo-3-7B-Think, also demonstrates consistent aggregate Avg@12 improvements. These results show that teacher-token reliability in reasoning distillation is trajectory-structured and can be utilized without additional teacher computation.

</details>


### [171] [AgForce Enables Antigen-conditioned Generative Antibody Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21610)
*Mansoor Ahmed,Murray Patterson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Antibody design models often ignore antigen input due to three failure modes (antigen blindness, vocabulary collapse, marginal distribution convergence). The authors propose AgForce, a GNN-based encoder-decoder with framework dropout, gated bottlenecks, hyperbolic cross-attention, MDN sequence head with Potts coupling & aMCL, and antigen cycle consistency. AgForce achieves superior binding quality and sequence recovery on CHIMERA-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing antibody design methods claim to condition on antigen structure but systematically fail to use antigen information; the paper identifies and formalizes three failure modes (antigen blindness, vocabulary collapse, convergence to marginal distributions) that prevent antigen-specific CDR generation.

Method: AgForce uses a GNN encoder and specialized decoders: (1) framework dropout, gated bottlenecks, and hyperbolic cross-attention to block antibody shortcut paths; (2) MDN sequence head with Potts-like pairwise coupling and annealed Multiple Choice Learning (aMCL) replacing cross-entropy; (3) antigen cycle consistency head that forces sequence predictions to encode antigen identity.

Result: On CHIMERA-Bench, AgForce achieves 8% better amino acid recovery over the best sequence baseline, surpasses baselines on all interface metrics, and nearly doubles the effective vocabulary of GNN methods.

Conclusion: AgForce demonstrates that explicitly addressing antigen blindness and vocabulary collapse through architectural innovations and a multi-component training objective can produce truly antigen-conditioned antibody designs with superior binding quality and sequence recovery.

Abstract: Antibody design methods condition on antigen structure to generate complementarity-determining regions (CDR), yet a systematic evaluation of baseline methods reveals that they largely ignore the antigen input. We identify three failure modes that explain this behavior. Antigen blindness arises because models derive predictions from antibody framework context rather than antigen information, producing nearly identical CDRs regardless of the target. Vocabulary collapse reduces predicted amino acids to three to five per position, far below the ground truth distribution in native sequences. Moreover, any model trained with standard per-position cross-entropy converges to the positional marginal distribution, making it provably unable to produce antigen-specific sequence predictions. We propose a novel encoder-decoder architecture called AgForce, that uses a graph neural network (GNN) as the encoder and specialized decoders for sequence-structure co-design. Specifically, we apply framework dropout, gated bottlenecks, and hyperbolic cross attention that prevent the antibody shortcut path. In the decoder, a Mixture Density Network (MDN) sequence head with Potts-like pairwise coupling and annealed Multiple Choice Learning (aMCL) replaces the cross-entropy objective with a multi-component distribution whose optimal solution differs from the positional marginal. An antigen cycle consistency head routes gradients through the sequence decoder, forcing predicted distributions to encode antigen identity. AgForce achieves the best binding quality and sequence recovery simultaneously on the CHIMERA-Bench dataset, improving amino acid recovery by 8% over the strongest sequence baseline while surpassing the baselines across all interface metrics, and nearly doubling the effective vocabulary of GNN methods. The source code is available at: https://github.com/mansoor181/ag-force.git

</details>


### [172] [$\textit{BlockFormer}$ : Transformer-based inference from interaction maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21617)
*Eloïse Touron,Pedro L. C. Rodrigues,Julyan Arbel,Nelle Varoquaux,Michael Arbel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a transformer-based data-driven approach to infer parameters from interaction maps (like Hi-C data), using synthetic training data and successfully applying it to centromere localization across diverse species.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formulate inference from interaction maps (e.g., centromere identification from Hi-C) as a generic inverse problem, addressing challenges like variable numbers/sizes of entities and leveraging shared structures across maps.

Method: A transformer architecture that handles variability in entity counts/sizes, combined with a custom simulator that generates abundant synthetic data for training.

Result: The method accurately recovers centromere genomic positions across a wide range of species with varying genome sizes.

Conclusion: The data-driven, transformer-based approach effectively solves the inverse problem for interaction maps, demonstrating strong generalization across species for centromere localization.

Abstract: Inference from interaction maps, such as centromere identification from genome-wide chromosome conformation capture techniques -- notably Hi-C -- can be formulated as a generic inverse problem: infer a set of parameters given a map summarizing pairwise interactions between entities through blocks of variable numbers and sizes. In this work, we introduce a data-driven approach that leverages shared structure between these maps, such as global alignment between localized patterns, while handling the variability in number and size of entities arising in real-world data. Our approach relies on a transformer architecture capable of handling such variability and a custom simulator to generate abundant, yet computationally cheap synthetic data for training. Applied to the problem of centromere localization, the method accurately recovers their genomic positions across a wide range of species of various genome sizes.

</details>


### [173] [Alike Parts: A Feature-Informed Approach to Local and Global Prototype Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21646)
*Jacek Karolczak,Jerzy Stefanowski*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a framework that integrates feature importance at two levels to improve prototype-based explanations: (1) "alike parts" for local explanations that highlight shared important features between an instance and its nearest prototype, and (2) augmenting global prototype selection with a feature diversity term. Experiments on six datasets show the approach maintains or improves prediction fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prototype-based explanations lack feature-level granularity, making it hard for users to understand which specific features drive the similarity between an instance and its prototype.

Method: The framework introduces (1) "alike parts" - a local explanation method using feature importance scores to highlight shared relevant feature subsets between a classified instance and its nearest prototype, and (2) augmenting the global prototype selection objective with a feature importance term that encourages diversity in feature attributions across prototypes.

Result: Experiments on six benchmark datasets show the augmented selection process maintains or increases prediction fidelity of the surrogate model, indicating feature diversity does not compromise model fidelity.

Conclusion: Adding feature-level granularity and diversity to prototype-based explanations is feasible without sacrificing prediction performance, improving both interpretability and model fidelity.

Abstract: Prototype-based explanations offer an intuitive, example-based approach to support the interpretability of machine learning black box classifiers but often lack feature-level granularity. We introduce a framework that integrates feature importance at two levels to address this gap. First, for local explanations, we propose \textit{alike parts}: a method that uses feature importance scores to highlight the most relevant, shared feature subsets between a classified instance and its nearest prototype, guiding user attention. Second, we augment the global prototype selection objective function with a feature importance term to actively promote diversity in the feature attributions of the selected prototypes. Experiments on six benchmark datasets show that this augmented selection process maintains or, in some cases, increases the prediction fidelity of the surrogate model, suggesting that feature diversity does not compromise model fidelity.

</details>


### [174] [Dropout Universality: Scaling Laws and Optimal Scheduling at the Edge-of-Chaos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21648)
*Lucas Fernandez Sarmiento*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a mean-field theory showing that dropout perturbs critical signal propagation at the edge of chaos, identifies two universality classes (smooth vs. ReLU-like activations) with distinct critical exponents, and uses this framework to derive optimal front-loaded dropout schedules that reduce test loss at no extra cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how dropout affects signal propagation in deep networks at critical initialization (edge of chaos), and to derive optimal dropout allocation strategies from first principles.

Method: Mean-field theory analysis of dropout as a perturbation of critical dynamics; derivation of critical/crossover scaling laws; identification of universality classes via analytic structure of correlation maps; empirical validation in MLPs and Vision Transformers.

Result: Smooth and ReLU-like activations form distinct universality classes with different exponents; a universal two-parameter scaling collapse exists; the theory predicts front-loaded dropout schedules under fixed budget, which substantially reduce held-out test loss.

Conclusion: Dropout at criticality can be understood analytically via mean-field theory, leading to practical, theoretically-grounded scheduling strategies that improve generalization without extra computation.

Abstract: We develop a mean-field theory of dropout as a perturbation of critical signal propagation at the edge of chaos. Dropout shifts the perfect-alignment fixed point, making the depth scale for information propagation finite even at critical initialization. We derive critical and crossover scaling laws for correlation decay and establish that smooth activations and kinked, ReLU-like activations constitute distinct universality classes, with different critical exponents and a universal two-parameter scaling collapse in detuning and dropout strength. The distinction traces to the analytic structure of the correlation map: smooth activations admit a Taylor expansion near perfect alignment, while kinked activations develop a branch point with universal non-analyticity. As a corollary, the framework yields saturated dropout profiles under fixed budget; a rank-flow tie-breaker then selects front-loaded schedules, substantially reducing held-out test loss at no extra computational cost, with accuracy gains as a consistent secondary effect. We test the predictions in MLPs and Vision Transformers and discuss CNN/ResNet extensions.

</details>


### [175] [EntmaxKV: Support-Aware Decoding for Entmax Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21649)
*Gonçalo Duarte,Miguel Couceiro,Marcos V. Treviso*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EntmaxKV introduces an entmax-native sparse decoding framework that exploits sparsity before KV pages are loaded, enabling exact sparse decoding by recovering the entmax support, achieving up to 3.36× (softmax) and 5.43× (entmax) speedup at 1M context length.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context decoding is bottlenecked by KV-cache memory traffic; existing sparse decoding methods use softmax attention whose dense tails make truncation discard nonzero probability mass, whereas α-entmax produces exact zeros, enabling exact sparse decoding if the support is recovered.

Method: EntmaxKV combines query-aware page scoring, support-aware candidate selection, sparse entmax attention, and a Gaussian-aware entmax selector that estimates the entmax threshold from lightweight page statistics to adapt the budget to the score distribution.

Result: EntmaxKV drops less probability mass, retains more support tokens, achieves lower output error than softmax-based sparse decoding at matched KV budgets, and closely matches full-cache entmax using a small fraction of KV cache, with up to 3.36× (softmax) and 5.43× (entmax) speedup at 1M context length.

Conclusion: EntmaxKV demonstrates that entmax-native sparse decoding can be both efficient and exact (error vanishes when support is recovered), outperforming softmax-based approaches and enabling practical long-context inference.

Abstract: Long-context decoding is increasingly limited by KV-cache memory traffic since each generated token attends over a cache whose size grows linearly with context length. Existing sparse decoding methods reduce this cost by selecting subsets of tokens or pages, but are designed for softmax attention, whose dense tails make any truncation discard nonzero probability mass. In contrast, $α$-entmax produces exact zeros, turning sparse decoding from dense-tail approximation into support recovery: if the selected candidates contain the entmax support, sparse decoding remains exact. While recent entmax kernels enable efficient training, they do not address the autoregressive decoding bottleneck, where dense inference still streams the full KV cache before sparsity is known. In this work, we introduce EntmaxKV, an entmax-native sparse decoding framework that exploits sparsity before KV pages are loaded. EntmaxKV combines query-aware page scoring, support-aware candidate selection, and sparse entmax attention. We analyze truncation error through the dropped probability mass $δ$, showing that output error is controlled by $δ$ and vanishes when the entmax support is recovered. We further introduce a Gaussian-aware entmax selector that estimates the entmax threshold from lightweight page statistics, adapting the selected budget to the score distribution. Empirically, EntmaxKV drops less probability mass, retains more support tokens, and achieves lower output error than softmax-based sparse decoding at matched KV budgets. On long-context and language modeling benchmarks, it closely matches full-cache entmax while using a small fraction of the KV cache, achieving up to $3.36\times$ (softmax) and $5.43\times$ (entmax) speedup over full attention baselines at 1M context length. Code available at: https://github.com/deep-spin/entmaxkv.

</details>


### [176] [Amplifying, Not Learning: Fine-Tuned AI Text Detectors Amplify a Pretrained Direction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21653)
*Alexander Smirnov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AI text detectors work by amplifying a pretrained typicality axis (AI vs human writing) rather than learning a true boundary; this axis can be manipulated with simple interventions, achieving state-of-the-art results, but fails on non-native ESL writing (inverting the axis).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether AI text detectors learn a genuine AI-vs-human discrimination boundary or simply amplify a pre-existing typicality axis from pretrained representations, and to test the falsifiable predictions of the "typicality" hypothesis.

Method: Projection onto centroid(AI)-centroid(HC3) axis on raw encoders; full fine-tuning vs. frozen probes; closed-form Jacobian predictor for axis manipulation; three operationally distinct probes (caps_rate residualisation, signed-epsilon ablation, text-pair predictor) for observer-invariance; calibration analysis of LoRA vs full-FT bias gap.

Result: Raw projection achieves 86-106% of fine-tuned discrimination ceiling; axis inverts on ESL writing (AUROC 0.06-0.20); 24-example frozen probe matches fine-tuning; interventions achieve universal R²=1.000 and lift TPR from 0.000 to 0.904 at 1% FPR; three probes agree at cos 0.74/0.81/1.00 across architectures.

Conclusion: AI text detectors do not construct a true AI-vs-human boundary but amplify a pretrained typicality axis; most of the bias gap between LoRA and full-FT is calibration shift, not learned representation; the typicality reading uniquely predicts ESL inversion.

Abstract: AI text detectors amplify a pretrained typicality axis; they do not construct an AI-vs-human boundary. On raw encoders before any task supervision, projecting onto centroid(AI)-centroid(HC3) achieves NYT-vs-HC3 AUROC 0.806/0.944/0.834 across three architectures (86-106% of the fine-tuned discrimination ceiling: on RoBERTa-base, raw projection exceeds fine-tuning); on RoBERTa-base, full fine-tuning reduces discrimination below raw on both fluent-formal populations tested. The same axis inverts on non-native ESL writing (AUROC 0.06-0.20) -- a falsifiable prediction unique to the typicality reading. A 24-example frozen probe matches full fine-tuning (0.900 vs 0.895). A closed-form Jacobian predictor parameterises axis-manipulating interventions with R^2 = 1.000 universal, lifts ELECTRA-CE deployment TPR from 0.000 to 0.904 at FPR = 1%, and transfers to three independently-trained third-party RoBERTa detectors at 16/16 oracle-equivalence (57% NYT-FPR reduction on the OpenAI detector). Scope: encoder family; mechanism magnitude HC3-anchored; population-level shared axis with per-text mechanisms varying across architectures. Three operationally distinct probes -- text-surface caps_rate residualisation, geometric signed-epsilon ablation, closed-form text-pair predictor -- agree at cos 0.74/0.81/1.00 across three architectures, confirming observer-invariance. Under matched-TPR-0.90 evaluation, the published intervention zoo (CC, dealign-f2c) is calibration-equivalent across 27 cells (|Delta AUROC| <= 0.0081), and >= 97% of the LoRA->full-FT bias gap on ELECTRA is calibration shift, not learned representation -- the central claim's prediction confirmed.

</details>


### [177] [Value-Gradient Hypothesis of RL for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21654)
*Arip Asadulaev,Daniil Ognev,Karim Salta,Martin Takac*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper develops a value-gradient perspective to explain why critic-free RL methods (PPO, GRPO) work for LLM post-training, showing that actor updates approximate value gradients under certain conditions, and proposes a criterion for when RL is most effective based on value gradient signal and reachable reward headroom.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why critic-free reinforcement learning methods (PPO, GRPO) are effective for fine-tuning LLMs, and to determine when they provide the largest gains, as this mechanism remains understudied.

Method: The authors develop a value-gradient perspective: (1) Under differentiable rollout and additive-noise parameterization, they show actor updates are value-gradient-like in expectation. (2) For discrete transformer policies, they prove autodifferentiation through attention produces empirical costates approximating the value signal, with error controlled by sampling gap and policy entropy.

Result: They decompose RL impact into value gradient signal and reachable reward headroom, leading to a criterion for predicting when RL will be most effective along a pretraining trajectory.

Conclusion: Critic-free RL works because it implicitly computes value gradients, and its effectiveness can be predicted by analyzing value gradient signal strength and reward reachability.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning substantially improves pretrained language models, but it remains understudied why critic-free methods such as PPO and GRPO work as well as they do, and when they should provide the largest gains. We develop a value-gradient perspective of critic-free RL for LLM post-training. First, under a differentiable rollout and additive-noise parameterization, we show that the actor update is value-gradient-like in expectation: the backward pass propagates costates whose conditional expectation equals the value gradient. Second, for discrete transformer policies, we show that autodifferentiation through attention produces empirical costates that approximate this value signal, with an error controlled by the sampling gap and policy entropy. These results motivate a decomposition of RL impact into value gradient signal and reachable reward headroom, yielding a criterion for when RL should be most effective along a pretraining trajectory.

</details>


### [178] [Hierarchical Variational Policies for Reward-Guided Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21661)
*Kushagra Pandey,Farrin Marouf Sofian,Jan Niklas Groeneveld,Felix Draxler,Stephan Mandt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework that amortizes test-time adaptation of diffusion models into a lightweight policy, enabling few-step sampling with strong quality-speed tradeoffs for inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time adaptation of pretrained diffusion models for downstream tasks (e.g., inverse problems) requires expensive test-time guidance or optimization, which is computationally costly.

Method: Formulates test-time adaptation as a hierarchical variational model where control is amortized into a lightweight stochastic policy. Supports few-step diffusion sampling with large step sizes for fast inference while maintaining quality via structured per-step control. Also extends to a semi-amortized regime combining cheap amortized proposals with limited test-time optimization.

Result: On 4x super-resolution, achieves better perceptual quality with more than 5x faster inference than the best-performing baseline. The semi-amortized variant achieves state-of-the-art perceptual quality across several inverse problems.

Conclusion: The proposed fully amortized sampler achieves a strong quality-speed tradeoff, matching or exceeding test-time scaling baselines with significantly less compute, and the semi-amortized extension achieves SOTA perceptual quality.

Abstract: Adapting pretrained diffusion models to downstream objectives such as inverse problems often requires expensive test-time guidance or optimization. We propose a principled framework for generating high-quality reward-aligned samples at substantially reduced inference cost. Our approach formulates test-time adaptation as a hierarchical variational model, where control is amortized into a lightweight yet expressive stochastic policy. This formulation naturally supports few-step diffusion sampling: large step sizes enable fast inference, while the learned policy maintains sample quality by providing structured per-step control. The resulting fully amortized sampler achieves a strong quality--speed tradeoff, matching or exceeding recent test-time scaling baselines while requiring significantly less compute. For example, on 4x super-resolution, our method achieves better perceptual quality with more than 5x faster inference compared to the best-performing baseline. We further extend our approach to a semi-amortized regime that combines cheap amortized proposals with limited test-time optimization, achieving state-of-the-art perceptual quality across several challenging inverse problems.

</details>


### [179] [Representation Gap: Explaining the Unreasonable Effectiveness of Neural Networks from a Geometric Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21692)
*David Perera,Victor Moura,Lais Isabelle Alves dos Santos,Michel F. C. Haddad,Flavio Figueiredo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Representation Gap, a metric related to generalization error with better asymptotic dynamics, and shows it's governed by the intrinsic dimension of the task, providing a precise asymptotic equivalent for equivariant diffusion models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Characterizing asymptotic generalization error of neural networks using efficiently estimable parameters is crucial but relies heavily on heuristics and intuition; the authors aim to mitigate this by introducing a better-behaved metric.

Method: The authors introduce the Representation Gap metric, use optimal quantization and point-process theory for equivariant diffusion models, derive asymptotic equivalents, and link the dynamics to intrinsic dimension.

Result: The Representation Gap's asymptotic dynamics are governed by a single parameter (intrinsic dimension), which is easy to interpret, efficient to estimate, and links to equivariances of common architectures; the asymptotic law extends to broader tasks and algorithms.

Conclusion: The asymptotic law and intrinsic dimension estimation are accurate on synthetic datasets (where ground truth is known) and produce results consistent with literature on realistic datasets.

Abstract: Characterizing precisely the asymptotic generalization error of neural networks using parameters that can be estimated efficiently is a crucial problem in machine learning, which relies heavily on heuristics and practitioners' intuition to make key design choices. In order to mitigate this issue, we introduce the Representation Gap, a metric closely related to the generalization error, but admitting better-behaved asymptotic dynamics. Focusing on equivariant diffusion models and leveraging results from optimal quantization and point-process theory, we derive a precise asymptotic equivalent of the Representation Gap and show that it is governed by a single parameter, the \textit{intrinsic dimension} of the task, which is easy to interpret, efficient to estimate, and can be linked to the equivariances of common neural network architectures. We show that this asymptotic dynamic also extends to a broader range of tasks and training algorithms. Finally, we demonstrate empirically that our asymptotic law and intrinsic dimension estimation are accurate on a wide range of synthetic datasets, where these quantities are known, as well as on more realistic datasets, where we obtain results consistent with the related literature.

</details>


### [180] [X-Token: Projection-Guided Cross-Tokenizer Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21699)
*Sharath Turuvekere Sreenivas,Adithyakrishna Venkatesh Hanasoge,Mingyu Yang,Ali Taghibakhshi,Saurav Muralidharan,Ashwath Aithal,Pavlo Molchanov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: X-Token proposes two complementary loss functions (P-KL and H-KL) to address common-token failure and over-conservative matching in cross-tokenizer knowledge distillation, achieving SOTA results on Llama-3.2-1B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing logit-based cross-tokenizer distillation suffers from two key shortcomings: (1) uncommon-token failure - critical tokens fall into unmatched subsets and get suppressed (e.g., multi-digit numerals), and (2) over-conservative matching - strict 1-to-1 token matching excludes near-equivalent tokens across surface forms.

Method: X-Token introduces two complementary loss functions: P-KL removes partitioning and aligns distributions via a sparse projection matrix W (initialized from tokenizer string rules) to address uncommon-token failure; H-KL retains hybrid form while relaxing matching to align each student token with its top-ranked teacher mapping under W. Both share the same matrix W and support multi-teacher distillation.

Result: On Llama-3.2-1B, X-Token outperforms GOLD by +3.82 average points with Qwen3-4B teacher and +0.5 with Phi-4-Mini teacher. Two-teacher setup (Phi-4-mini + Llama-3B) improves by +1.3 points over single-teacher distillation.

Conclusion: The proposed X-Token effectively addresses the two key shortcomings of full-distribution logit-based cross-tokenizer distillation, achieving state-of-the-art performance and naturally extending to multi-teacher settings.

Abstract: Cross-tokenizer knowledge distillation allows a student model to learn from teachers with incompatible vocabularies. Prior work operates on hidden states or logits; the latter is preferred as a drop-in replacement requiring no auxiliary components. Logit-based methods either use only the correct-token probability, missing the full 'dark knowledge' in the teacher's distribution, or operate on the full output distribution, relying on strict token partitioning and/or unprincipled heuristic ranking. We identify two key shortcomings of full-distribution, logit-based methods: (i) an uncommon-token failure, where critical tokens fall into the unmatched subset (e.g., Llama's 1100 multi-digit numerals under digit-splitting Qwen supervision) and are suppressed during training, reducing GSM8k from 12.89 to 2.56 compared to same-tokenizer KD from a weaker teacher; and (ii) over-conservative matching, where strict 1-to-1 matching excludes near-equivalent tokens across surface forms. These failures require distinct remedies: eliminating the partition when critical tokens are misaligned, and refining it when alignment is reliable. We propose X-Token, an approach with two complementary loss formulations targeting these issues. P-KL removes partitioning and aligns the student's distribution with the teacher's via a sparse projection matrix W (initialized from tokenizer-level string rules) to address the uncommon-token failure. H-KL retains the hybrid form while relaxing matching to align each student token with its top-ranked teacher mapping under W. Both objectives share W and extend naturally to multiple teachers. Empirically, on Llama-3.2-1B, X-Token outperforms the current state of the art GOLD by +3.82 average points with a Qwen3-4B teacher and by +0.5 with a Phi-4-Mini teacher. Further, a two-teacher setup (Phi-4-mini + Llama-3B) improves over single-teacher distillation by +1.3 points.

</details>


### [181] [TBP-mHC: full expressivity for manifold-constrained hyper connections through transportation polytopes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21724)
*Anton Lyubinin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Transportation Birkhoff Polytope (TBP) and its recursive variant (RTBP) to construct exactly doubly stochastic mixing matrices with full Birkhoff polytope expressivity, avoiding iterative normalization and combinatorial explosion, achieving competitive language model pretraining performance with improved stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hyper-connection methods either suffer from training instability (HC), rely on expensive Sinkhorn normalization (mHC), face factorial complexity (mHC-lite), or restrict expressivity to structured submanifolds (KromHC). The goal is to achieve efficient, exactly doubly stochastic mixing with full expressivity.

Method: Proposes Transportation Birkhoff Polytope (TBP) parameterization and its Recursive variant (RTBP) to construct exactly doubly stochastic mixing matrices with (n-1)^2 degrees of freedom, bypassing iterative normalization and combinatorial costs while maintaining full Birkhoff polytope expressivity.

Result: Demonstrates competitive performance on language model pre-training with improved training stability and scalability compared to prior hyper-connection variants.

Conclusion: TBP/RTBP provides a principled, efficient parameterization for doubly stochastic mixing in hyper-connections, achieving full expressive power of the Birkhoff polytope without the drawbacks of prior approaches.

Abstract: Hyper-Connections (HC) improve residual networks by introducing learnable mixing across multiple residual streams, but unconstrained mixing leads to training instability. Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC) address this by enforcing approximate double stochasticity via Sinkhorn normalization, while mHC-lite ensures exact constraints through convex combinations of permutation matrices at the cost of factorial complexity. KromHC reduces this cost using Kronecker-product parameterizations, but restricts the mixing matrices to a structured submanifold of the Birkhoff polytope .
  We propose Transportation Birkhoff Polytope (TBP) parameterizations and their Recursive variants (RTBP), which construct exactly doubly stochastic mixing matrices with $(n-1)^2$ degrees of freedom. Our approach avoids iterative normalization and combinatorial explosion while preserving full expressivity of the Birkhoff polytope. Empirical results on language model pre-training' demonstrate competitive performance with improved stability and scalability.

</details>


### [182] [I-SAFE: Wasserstein Coherence Metrics for Structural Auditing of Scientific AI Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21731)
*Barbara Tarantino,Gennaro Auricchio,Paolo Giudici*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces I-SAFE, a post-hoc auditing framework that uses Wasserstein Coherence Metric and related measures to evaluate whether deep learning models in scientific domains (like drug-target interaction prediction) rely on scientifically meaningful patterns rather than shortcuts or biases, revealing that models with similar accuracy can have very different distributional response profiles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep learning models in scientific prediction tasks often achieve strong benchmark performance, but this may stem from exploiting shortcuts, dataset biases, or distributional regularities rather than learning scientifically meaningful structure. Standard accuracy-based evaluation cannot distinguish between these cases, motivating the need for a principled auditing framework.

Method: The I-SAFE framework applies structurally guided perturbations to inputs based on external domain priors, then evaluates the coherence of output distributions using three metrics: Quantile-Based Metric (QBM) for location-level coherence, Wasserstein Coherence Metric (WCM) for ordinal coherence, and a translation-invariant WCM for shape coherence. It is demonstrated on drug-target interaction prediction using Davis kinase benchmark and KLIFS annotations.

Result: Applied to three DTI models (DeepConvDTI, DeepDTA, TAPB) that achieve comparable predictive accuracy, I-SAFE reveals substantially different distributional response profiles across models—a distinction entirely invisible to accuracy-based evaluation.

Conclusion: The I-SAFE framework provides a model-agnostic, post-hoc auditing tool applicable to any domain with structured inputs and available external priors, enabling researchers to differentiate between models that rely on domain-relevant structure versus those exploiting spurious correlations.

Abstract: Deep learning models are increasingly used in scientific prediction tasks where strong benchmark performance is often interpreted as evidence of scientifically meaningful behavior. This interpretation is fragile, as models may exploit shortcut features, dataset-specific regularities, or distributional biases that are predictive on held-out data but not aligned with domain-relevant structure. To address this limitation, we introduce the \textsc{I-SAFE} (Interventional Secure, Accurate, Fair and Explainable) framework, a post-hoc distributional auditing framework for scientific AI models centered on the Wasserstein Coherence Metric (WCM). Given a trained black-box predictor and an external structural prior encoding domain knowledge about task-relevant input structure, \textsc{I-SAFE} evaluates raw model outputs under structurally guided perturbations of the input. The proposed audit measures output-distribution coherence through three complementary metrics: a Quantile-Based Metric (QBM) for location-level coherence, the WCM for ordinal coherence, and a translation-invariant WCM variant for shape coherence. We instantiate \textsc{I-SAFE} on drug--target interaction (DTI) prediction using the Davis kinase benchmark, KLIFS (Kinase--Ligand Interaction Fingerprints and Structures) binding-pocket annotations, and three sequence-based DTI models: DeepConvDTI, DeepDTA, and TAPB. Although the models operate in a comparable predictive regime, \textsc{I-SAFE} reveals substantially different distributional response profiles, a distinction invisible to accuracy-based evaluation. The framework is model-agnostic and applicable to any domain where inputs admit a structured decomposition and an external prior is available.

</details>


### [183] [Quantitative coronary calcification analysis for prediction of myocardial ischemia using non-contrast CT calcium scoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21745)
*Juhwan Lee,Sadeer Al-Kindi,Ammar Hoori,Tao Hu,Hao Wu,Justin N. Kim,Robert Gilkeson,Sanjay Rajagopalan,David L. Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper developed a machine learning framework combining Agatston score, calcium-omics features, and age to predict myocardial ischemia from routine non-contrast CT calcium scoring scans, achieving 98.9% precision and 87.7% F1 score in a cohort of 987 patients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve cardiovascular risk stratification by developing a more accessible method for predicting myocardial ischemia using routine non-contrast CTCS scans, which are more widely available than stress perfusion imaging.

Method: Analyzed 1,375 patients with both CTCS and PET stress perfusion imaging; extracted 74 variables (clinical, Agatston, calcium-omics); used XGBoost with SHAP for feature selection; trained models with 5-fold cross-validation.

Result: The final model (Agatston score + 8 calcium-omics features + age) achieved precision 98.9±3.0%, sensitivity 79.2±8.4%, F1 87.7±5.3%. Calcium-omics features significantly improved performance vs. clinical-only or clinical+Agatston models (p<0.05). Number of calcified arteries showed strongest logistic regression association (OR: 3.63).

Conclusion: Machine learning using calcium-omics from routine non-contrast CTCS provides incremental predictive value for myocardial ischemia beyond conventional risk factors and Agatston scoring, enabling more accessible cardiovascular risk stratification.

Abstract: Non-contrast computed tomography calcium scoring (CTCS) is widely recognized as an effective tool for cardiovascular risk stratification. This study aimed to develop a novel machine learning framework for predicting myocardial ischemia from routine non-contrast CTCS scans using quantitative coronary calcium assessment. This study analyzed 1,375 patients who underwent both non-contrast CTCS and regadenoson stress cardiac positron emission tomography myocardial perfusion imaging within one year at University Hospitals Cleveland Medical Center. A total of 74 variables, including clinical variables, Agatston score, and calcium-omics features, were evaluated. Relevant features were identified using XGBoost with Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP). Predictive models were trained and evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation. Among 987 patients, 89 (9%) were positive for myocardial ischemia. The final model incorporated the Agatston score, eight calcium-omics features, and age. The proposed model achieved a precision of 98.9+/-3.0%, sensitivity of 79.2+/-8.4, and F1 score of 87.7+/-5.3%. The addition of calcium-omics features significantly improved predictive performance compared with models using clinical variables alone or clinical variables with the Agatston score (p<0.05). Interestingly, the number of calcified arteries, despite being the lowest-ranked feature based on SHAP analysis, showed the strongest association with myocardial ischemia in logistic regression analysis (odds ratio: 3.63, 95% confidence interval: 2.80-4.77, p<0.00001). We developed a machine learning approach for predicting myocardial ischemia using routinely acquired non-contrast CTCS scans. Calcium-omics features provided incremental predictive value beyond conventional risk factors and Agatston scoring and may support more accessible cardiovascular risk stratification.

</details>


### [184] [Models Can Model, But Can't Bind: Structured Grounding in Text-to-Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21751)
*Zhiqi Gao,Albert Ge,Alexander Berenbeim,Nathaniel D. Bastian,Frederic Sala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies a "binding limit" where LLMs fail to ground optimization problem data as instance size grows, and proposes BIND, an inference-time method that externalizes numeric data to structured files, significantly improving accuracy across models and matching larger model performance with lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based text-to-optimization approaches suffer accuracy degradation as problem instance size increases, even for simple formulations, due to models struggling with data binding (grounding coefficients/parameters) rather than formulation modeling.

Method: Introduces Text2Opt-Bench (12 categories of solver-verified problems up to thousands of variables) and BIND, an inference-time approach that externalizes numeric data to structured files so models bind data programmatically instead of transcribing from prompts.

Result: BIND improves GPT-5-Nano from 59.1% to 82.4% (matching pass@5 at lower cost), GPT-5 from 86.2% to 95.8%. A 1.5B binding specialist matches a 7B end-to-end baseline, outperforming SFT and RL across three optimization categories.

Conclusion: The binding capability is separable from modeling and can be effectively addressed through externalization, enabling smaller models to match larger ones by focusing on the binding bottleneck separately.

Abstract: Text-to-optimization requires two separable capabilities: modeling -- choosing the right optimization structure -- and binding -- grounding every coefficient, index, and parameter in the concrete problem data. We study this via Text2Opt-Bench, a scalable benchmark of solver-verified optimization problems spanning 12 categories, from textbook linear programs to stochastic and multi-objective formulations with up to thousands of variables. Across 10+ models, we find that accuracy collapses as instance data grows, even when the formulation itself is simple. We call this the effective binding limit. We address this via a simple inference-time approach, BIND, which externalizes numeric data to structured files so the model binds data programmatically rather than transcribing from the prompt. BIND improves GPT-5-Nano from 59.1% to 82.4% accuracy, matching pass@5 (82.0%) at lower token cost than pass@1, and GPT-5 from 86.2% to 95.8%. Furthermore, we validate our hypothesis by finetuning a model exclusively on binding and show that it outperforms end-to-end SFT and RL across three structurally distinct optimization categories, with a 1.5B binding specialist alone matching a 7B end-to-end baseline.

</details>


### [185] [PEARL: Unbiased Percentile Estimation via Contrastive Learning for Industrial-Scale Livestream Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21752)
*Blake Gella,Wei Wu,Yuhao Yin,Zexi Huang,Zikai Wang,Emily Liu,Junlin Zhang,Wentao Guo,Qinglei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PEARL is a nonparametric contrastive percentile approximation framework that mitigates behavioral intensity imbalance in recommender systems by modeling relative preference signals instead of absolute engagement magnitudes, achieving significant improvements in both offline experiments and large-scale production deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recommender systems suffer from behavioral intensity imbalance—heterogeneous user engagement patterns cause systematic distortion where highly active users' signals dominate while others are underrepresented, degrading recommendation quality and robustness.

Method: PEARL uses (1) nonparametric contrastive percentile approximation from real interaction samples to model relative preferences, (2) prediction-based bootstrapping for percentile smoothing handling sparse/discrete feedback, (3) generalized value-weighted formulation, and (4) co-training strategy for representation learning.

Result: Offline: effectively mitigates behavioral bias and improves performance across multiple ranking targets. Online (billions-scale platform): +2.10% Watch Duration, +0.80% Consumption Amount, +1.49% Interaction Rate, -6.91% Report Rate.

Conclusion: PEARL provides a theoretically grounded, computationally efficient solution for debiasing recommendation under behavioral intensity imbalance, with demonstrated real-world scalability and effectiveness.

Abstract: Recommender systems trained on user interaction data are susceptible to behavioral intensity imbalance--a systematic distortion arising from heterogeneous engagement patterns across users. This imbalance skews feedback signals such that observed interactions no longer faithfully reflect true preferences, causing models to disproportionately amplify signals from highly active users while underrepresenting others, which ultimately degrades recommendation quality and robustness at scale. To address this issue, we propose a nonparametric contrastive percentile approximation framework, PEARL, that models relative preference signals instead of absolute engagement magnitudes. Building upon relative advantage debiasing, PEARL leverages real contrastive interaction samples to approximate percentile relationships directly, without relying on auxiliary distribution estimation models. We provide theoretical justification demonstrating that such pairwise comparisons yield unbiased estimates of percentile-based preference signals. For broader applicability, we introduce a prediction-based bootstrapping mechanism for percentile smoothing to handle sparse and discrete feedback, alongside a generalized value-weighted formulation and a co-training strategy to enhance both modeling flexibility and representation learning. Extensive offline experiments demonstrate that PEARL effectively mitigates behavioral bias and consistently improves recommendation performance across multiple ranking targets. Deployed in a production livestream platform with a combined user base of billions, online A/B testing confirms substantial real-world gains: +2.10% Watch Duration, +0.80% Consumption Amount, +1.49% Interaction Rate, and -6.91% Report Rate.

</details>


### [186] [stable-worldmodel: A Platform for Reproducible World Modeling Research and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21800)
*Lucas Maes,Quentin Le Lidec,Luiz Facury,Nassim Massaudi,Ayush Chaurasia,Francesco Capuano,Richard Gao,Taj Gillin,Dan Haramati,Damien Scieur,Yann LeCun,Randall Balestriero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents stable-worldmodel (swm), an open-source platform that standardizes world model research by providing a unified data layer, clean baseline implementations, and comprehensive evaluation benchmarks with systematic variations for testing generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
