Motivation: World model research is fragmented across disparate codebases, data pipelines, and evaluation protocols, hindering reproducibility and fair comparison. Three key bottlenecks exist: fragile one-off codebases, slow video data loading, and lack of standardized generalization benchmarks.

Method: SWM provides: (1) a high-performance Lance-based data layer supporting MP4, HDF5, and LeRobot formats, (2) clean, tested implementations of modern world model baselines and planning solvers, and (3) a broad suite of environments/tasks with controllable visual, geometric, and physical factors for systematic evaluation.

Result: A unified, scalable framework that dramatically reduces research overhead and enables standardized evaluation of dynamics understanding, control performance, representation quality, and out-of-distribution generalization.

Conclusion: SWM accelerates trustworthy progress toward reliable world models by standardizing the full pipeline under a single framework.

Abstract: World models are central to building agents that can reason, plan, and generalize beyond their training data. However, research on world models is currently fragmented, with disparate codebases, data pipelines, and evaluation protocols hindering reproducibility and fair comparison. Current practice is further limited by three key bottlenecks: fragile one-off codebases, slow video data loading, and the lack of standardized generalization benchmarks. We present stable-worldmodel (swm), an open-source platform for standardized and reproducible world modeling research and evaluation. It delivers (1) a high-performance Lance-based data layer with native support and conversion tools for MP4, HDF5, and LeRobot datasets, (2) clean, well-tested implementations of modern world model baselines and planning solvers, and (3) a broad suite of environments and tasks extended with controllable visual, geometric, and physical factors of variation for systematic in-silico evaluation of dynamics understanding, control performance, representation quality, and out-of-distribution generalization. By unifying the full pipeline under a single, scalable framework, \texttt{swm} dramatically reduces research overhead and accelerates trustworthy progress toward reliable world models.

</details>


### [187] [Machine learning prediction of obstructive coronary artery disease using opportunistic coronary calcium and epicardial fat assessments from CT calcium scoring scans](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21762)
*Juhwan Lee,Ammar Hoori,Tao Hu,Justin N. Kim,Mohamed H. E. Makhlouf,Michelle C. Williams,David E. Newby,Robert Gilkeson,Sanjay Rajagopalan,David L. Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A machine learning framework using calcium-omics and fat-omics features from non-contrast CT scans predicts obstructive coronary artery disease with 85.3% accuracy, outperforming traditional methods and even detecting disease in zero-calcium-score patients.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an advanced ML framework using quantitative analysis of coronary calcium and epicardial fat from CTCS images to predict obstructive CAD, potentially reducing dependence on contrast-enhanced or invasive diagnostic procedures.

Method: 1,324 SCOT-HEART patients; extracted 24 clinical variables, 189 calcium-omics, and 211 epicardial fat-omics features; CatBoost + SHAP feature selection; CatBoost gradient boosting for predictive modeling.

Result: 14 predictive features identified (top 2 from fat-omics, 12 from calcium-omics); sensitivity 83.1%, specificity 93.8%, accuracy 85.3%, F1 score 73.9%. Effective even in zero-calcium-score patients with obstructive CAD.

Conclusion: This approach improves clinical decision-making and may reduce reliance on contrast-enhanced or invasive diagnostics, especially for low-to intermediate-risk patients.

Abstract: Non-contrast computed tomography calcium scoring (CTCS) is a cost-effective imaging modality widely used to detect coronary artery calcifications. This study aimed to develop an advanced machine learning framework that utilizes quantitative analyses of coronary calcium and epicardial fat from CTCS images to predict obstructive coronary artery disease (CAD). The study population consisted of 1,324 patients from the SCOT-HEART clinical trial who underwent both CTCS and coronary CT angiography. We extracted and analyzed a broad range of features, including 24 clinical variables, 189 calcium-omics, and 211 epicardial fat-omics features from the CTCS images. Feature selection was conducted using the CatBoost algorithm combined with SHapley Additive exPlanation (SHAP) values. Predictive modeling utilized the CatBoost gradient boosting method, focusing on the most informative features. From an initial set of 424 candidate features, 14 were identified as most predictive through the CatBoost-SHAP method. The top two predictive features originated from fat-omics, with the remaining 12 features derived from calcium-omics. The optimized model achieved robust predictive capabilities, demonstrating a sensitivity of 83.1+/-4.6%, specificity of 93.8+/-1.7%, accuracy of 85.3+/-2.0%, and an F1 score of 73.9+/-3.3%. Inclusion of calcium-omics and fat-omics data significantly improved predictive performance. Notably, the model also showed reliable predictive accuracy in patients with diverse coronary calcium scores, including cases with obstructive CAD despite a zero-calcium score. This innovative approach holds promise for improving clinical decision-making and potentially reducing dependence on contrast-enhanced or invasive diagnostic procedures, particularly within low-to intermediate-risk patient groups.

</details>


### [188] [On the Sample Complexity of Discounted Reinforcement Learning with Optimized Certainty Equivalents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21763)
*Oliver Mortensen,Mohammad Sadegh Talebi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies sample complexity bounds for risk-sensitive reinforcement learning under Optimized Certainty Equivalent (OCE) risk measures in finite MDPs, characterizing when the problem is PAC-learnable and providing tight bounds for CVaR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the sample complexity requirements for learning optimal policies under risk-sensitive criteria (beyond risk-neutral RL), particularly characterizing which OCE risk measures admit PAC-learnable solutions and establishing tight theoretical bounds.

Method: The authors analyze a model-based approach for recursive OCE risk measures, characterize utility functions u for which OCE objectives are PAC-learnable, and derive both upper and lower bounds on sample complexity.

Result: PAC-learnability occurs iff u has full domain (ℝ); otherwise not learnable. Tight bounds in state-action space size SA are established. For CVaR_τ, correct dependence on τ is 1/τ² (improving prior art by 1/τ factor), though suboptimal in horizon dependence.

Conclusion: The work provides exact characterization of learnable OCE risk measures, tight sample complexity bounds for value and policy learning, and identifies a gap in horizon dependence for CVaR to be addressed in future work.

Abstract: We study risk-sensitive reinforcement learning in finite discounted MDPs, where a generative model of the MDP is assumed to be available. We consider a family or risk measures called the optimized certainty equivalent (OCE), which includes important risk measures such as entropic risk, CVaR, and mean-variance. Our focus is on the sample complexities of learning the optimal state-action value function (value learning) and an optimal policy (policy learning) under recursive OCE. We provide an exact characterization of utility functions $u$ for which the corresponding OCE defines an objective that is PAC-learnable. We analyze a simple model-based approach and derive PAC sample complexity bounds. We establish that whenever $u$ does not have full domain $\text{dom}(u)\neq \mathbb{R}$, the corresponding problem is not PAC-learnable. Finally, we establish corresponding lower bounds for both value and policy learning, demonstrating tightness in the size $SA$ of state-action space, and for a more restricted class of utilities, we derive lower bounds that makes the dependence on the effective horizon $\frac{1}{1-γ}$ explicit. Specifically, for $\text{CVaR}_τ$ we show that the correct dependence on $τ$ is $\frac{1}{τ^2}$, thus improving by a factor of $\frac{1}τ$ over state-of-the-art although our bound has a suboptimal dependence on $\frac{1}{1-γ}$.

</details>


### [189] [Position: The Time for Sampling Is Now! Charting a New Course for Bayesian Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21765)
*Emanuel Sommer,David Rügamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This position paper argues that sampling-based inference (SAI) for Bayesian neural networks has reached computational parity with optimization-based methods and is poised to supersede them, offering better uncertainty quantification, prediction performance, and downstream capabilities, while calling for the community to overcome misconceptions and focus on posterior exploration and sample distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The practical adoption of sampling-based inference (SAI) in Bayesian neural networks remains limited due to persistent misconceptions about its feasibility and efficiency.

Method: Position paper arguing that SAI has achieved computational parity with optimization-based methods, identifying misconceptions as key barriers, and proposing two core challenges: sufficient posterior landscape exploration and high-fidelity posterior sample distillation.

Result: SAI can yield superior prediction performance via model averaging, enable diverse downstream tasks, and provide landscape insights; computational parity with optimization methods is claimed.

Conclusion: Overcoming misconceptions and addressing posterior exploration and sample distillation will unlock SAI's full potential as a central tool in Bayesian deep learning.

Abstract: The practical adoption of sampling-based inference (SAI) in Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) remains limited, partly due to persistent misconceptions about the feasibility and efficiency of sampling. This position paper argues that SAI has achieved computational parity with optimization-based methods and is at the verge of superseding such methods for effective and efficient inference in BNNs. This development should be in the interest of the whole community, promoting BNNs as a principled paradigm with its long-standing yet unfulfilled promise of providing principled uncertainty quantification for neural networks. SAI can even do more -- yielding superior prediction performance through model averaging, serving as the foundation for a plethora of possible downstream tasks, and providing crucial insights into the landscape of BNNs. In order to make such a change happen and unfold the potential of sampling, overcoming current misconceptions is a necessary first step. The next step is to realign research efforts toward addressing remaining challenges in SAI. In particular, the community must focus on two core problems: sufficient exploration of the posterior landscape and high-fidelity distillation of posterior samples for efficient downstream inference. By addressing conceptual and practical obstacles, we can unlock the full potential of SAI and establish it as a central tool in Bayesian deep learning.

</details>


### [190] [Beyond Euclidean Proximity: Repairing Latent World Models with Horizon-Matched Trajectory Reachability Metrics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22164)
*Liangyu Li,Shengzhi Wang,Qingwen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRM replaces raw latent distance with learned reachability metrics for terminal-cost planning in latent world models, dramatically improving success rates (e.g., 7%→97% on TwoRoom).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raw latent Euclidean distance between terminal and goal states misweights decision-relevant variables (e.g., XY position vs. irrelevant latent dimensions), leading to poor candidate ranking in latent MPC.

Method: Trains a small pairwise head on logged trajectory structure with horizon-aware supervision (broad, balanced temporal separations) to produce a terminal-ranking metric that replaces or augments raw latent proximity.

Result: TwoRoom: 7.0%→97.0% (LeWM), 32.7%→84.0% (PLDM). Shuffled controls stay at 0.0%. Short-horizon TRM reaches only 35.0%. PushT: improves SCSA ranking and selected final distance.

Conclusion: TRM is a planner-facing repair for terminal-cost interfaces; mechanistic evidence explains when learned reachability metrics should replace or augment raw latent proximity.

Abstract: Latent world models can contain the state needed for control, yet their terminal-cost interface can expose the planner to the wrong decision-relevant information. In common latent MPC, candidate sequences are ranked by Euclidean distance between predicted terminal and goal latent states; this assumes that raw latent distance weights reachability-relevant variables correctly. We propose trajectory reachability metrics (TRM), a post-hoc terminal-ranking method for fixed latent world models. TRM trains a small pairwise head from logged trajectory structure and uses it as a replacement or hybrid cost; the encoder, dynamics, sampler, optimizer, and evaluation manifests remain fixed. The key design choice is horizon-aware supervision: the metric is trained on broad, balanced temporal separations to match the long-horizon terminal candidate ranking problem. On a hard TwoRoom benchmark, raw latent planning with LeWorldModel (LeWM) reaches 7.0% success, while full-horizon TRM reaches 97.0%; shuffled temporal-label controls stay at 0.0%. The same recipe improves a PLDM baseline from 32.7% to 84.0% across three seeds, and a short-horizon TRM variant reaches only 35.0% with the 100,000 pair budget. In TwoRoom, we provide mechanistic evidence for why TRM works: XY position is linearly decodable (R^2=0.998), yet raw latent MSE misranks candidates; the XY-probe rowspace accounts for less than 1% of terminal-goal latent MSE but carries most candidate-quality signal; and SCSA audits show that TRM improves the ordering and selected endpoint seen by the planner. On PushT go50/go75, TRM-style task-state metrics improve SCSA ranking and selected final distance more cleanly than closed-loop success, motivating auxiliary hybrid costs in continuous manipulation. TRM is the planner-facing repair, and audits explain when terminal reachability metrics should replace or augment raw latent proximity.

</details>


### [191] [Memory-R2: Fair Credit Assignment for Long-Horizon Memory-Augmented LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21768)
*Sikuan Yan,Ahmed Bahloul,Ercong Nie,Susanna Schwarzmann,Riccardo Trivisonno,Volker Tresp,Yunpu Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Memory-R2 introduces LoGo-GRPO, a training framework combining local and global group-relative optimization to address credit assignment challenges in memory-augmented LLM agents trained with RL across multi-session environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training memory-augmented LLM agents with RL in multi-session environments is unfair because different rollouts modify memory states differently, violating assumptions behind group-relative methods like GRPO that require shared environments for fair comparison.

Method: LoGo-GRPO combines (1) global objective for end-to-end learning from long-horizon rewards, (2) local rerollouts for fair group comparisons from the same intermediate memory state, (3) shared-parameter co-learning of fact extractor and memory manager via role-specific prompts on the same LLM backbone, and (4) progressive curriculum training (8→16→32 sessions).

Result: Not specified in abstract (framework proposed, empirical results not detailed here).

Conclusion: Memory-R2 provides an effective training paradigm for memory-augmented LLM agents in long-horizon multi-session settings.

Abstract: Memory-augmented LLM agents enable interactions that extend beyond finite context windows by storing, updating, and reusing information across sessions. However, training such agents with reinforcement learning in multi-session environments is challenging because memory turns the agent's past actions into part of its future environment. Once different rollouts write, update, or delete different memories, they no longer share the same intermediate memory state, making trajectory-level comparisons fundamentally unfair. This violates a key assumption behind group-relative methods such as GRPO, where rollouts are compared as if they were sampled from the same effective environment. Consequently, trajectory-level rewards provide noisy or biased credit signals for long-horizon memory operations. To address this challenge, we introduce Memory-R2, a training framework for long-horizon memory-augmented LLM agents. Its core algorithm, LoGo-GRPO, combines local and global group-relative optimization. The global objective preserves end-to-end learning from long-horizon trajectory-level rewards, while local rerollouts compare different memory-operation outcomes from the same intermediate memory state, yielding fairer group comparisons and more precise supervision for memory construction. Beyond credit assignment, Memory-R2 jointly optimizes memory formation and memory evolution with a shared-parameter co-learning design, where a fact extractor and a memory manager are instantiated from the same LLM backbone through role-specific prompts. To stabilize multi-step RL over long memory horizons, we adopt a progressive curriculum that increases the training horizon from 8 to 16 to 32 sessions. Together, these components provide an effective training paradigm for memory-augmented LLM agents in long-horizon multi-session settings.

</details>


### [192] [An Evidence Hierarchy for Bayesian Object Classification via OSINT-Aided Heterogeneous Sensor Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22259)
*Jan Nausner,Michael Hubner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a context-aware, domain knowledge-enhanced fusion approach for CBRNE threat detection, using an evidence hierarchy (direct, indicative, contextual), OSINT integration, and Bayesian classification with informed priors, achieving up to 95% accuracy in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Individual sensors have limited capability (only detect subsets of threats, varying reliability, indirect indications, high clutter rates), and there is limited availability of high-quality datasets for learning-based models, necessitating a better fusion approach.

Method: A three-part method: (1) establishing a novel evidence hierarchy (direct, indicative, contextual information); (2) incorporating OSINT-based contextual information into fusion; (3) using all evidence levels to build a Bayesian threat classification with domain knowledge-informed priors.

Result: Evaluated in simulated scenarios, the approach demonstrates robustness to clutter and prior mismatch, achieving overall classification accuracy up to 95%.

Conclusion: Context-aware and domain knowledge-enhanced fusion significantly improves CBRNE threat classification robustness and accuracy, mitigating sensor-related shortcomings.

Abstract: Heterogeneous sensor fusion is vital for detecting, localizing, and classifying CBRNE threats. However, individual sensors are often only capable of detecting a subset of relevant threats with varying reliability or can even provide only indirect threat indications, making threat classification challenging. Furthermore, high clutter rates on the sensor side present a great challenge for fusion systems. Additionally, the limited availability of high quality datasets hinders the advancement of learning-based detection and classification models in smart sensors. To mitigate these sensor related shortcomings, a context-aware and domain knowledge-enhanced fusion process is proposed. First, a novel evidence hierarchy is established that enables modeling of direct, indicative, and contextual information. Second, contextual information about the environment is introduced into the fusion process, by collecting, processing, and exploiting OSINT inputs. Third, all levels of the evidence hierarchy are used to craft a Bayesian threat type classification mechanism with domain knowledge-informed priors. The proposed methodology is evaluated in simulated scenarios, and the results demonstrate the benefit of the proposed fusion approach in terms of robustness to clutter and prior mismatch, with an overall classification accuracy of up to 95%.

</details>


### [193] [Manifold-Guided Attention Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21770)
*Ian Li,Kapilesh Guruprasad,Raunak Sengupta,Ninad Satish,Loris D'Antoni,Rose Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MAGS is a trajectory-aware activation steering method that monitors attention head outputs and applies corrective projections when they deviate from a learned correctness manifold, yielding consistent improvements across reasoning, coding, and molecular generation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs make reasoning errors despite having the right knowledge; existing activation steering uses fixed correction vectors that disrupt correct steps. A trajectory-aware method is needed.

Method: For key attention heads, learn a low-dimensional correctness subspace from contrastive correct/incorrect traces. During inference, monitor head proximity to this manifold and apply targeted projection correction when deviation exceeds a learned threshold.

Result: Outperforms unsteered baselines and static steering across MATH-500, GSM8K, HumanEval, MBPP, and SMILES benchmarks.

Conclusion: Correctness manifolds are a general geometric feature of LLM attention heads; trajectory-aware steering is more effective than static steering.

Abstract: Large language models frequently produce errors in reasoning tasks despite possessing the underlying knowledge required for correct reasoning. One possible approach to improve reasoning consistency is through activation steering. However, existing activation steering approaches apply fixed, pre-computed correction vectors, ignoring where the model currently sits along its generation trajectory; the result is indiscriminate perturbation that disrupts already-correct steps as freely as erroneous ones. We propose Manifold-Guided Attention Steering (MAGS), a trajectory-aware inference-time intervention grounded in a geometric observation: the output activations of specific attention heads diverge from a low-dimensional correctness manifold at the point of error, and this deviation compounds through subsequent steps. For each identified attention head, we learn a low-dimensional subspace from contrastive pairs of correct and incorrect traces that capture the directions along which error behavior deviates from correct behavior. During inference, we monitor each head's proximity to this manifold and apply a targeted projection correction when deviation exceeds a learned threshold, steering the attention output back toward the correct subspace before the error propagates. MAGS consistently outperforms both unsteered baselines and static steering approaches across benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning (MATH-500, GSM8K), code generation (HumanEval, MBPP), and molecular generation (SMILES), suggesting that correctness manifolds are a general feature of LLM attention geometry.

</details>


### [194] [Provable Robustness against Backdoor Attacks via the Primal-Dual Perspective on Differential Privacy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21780)
*Aman Saxena,Jan Schuchardt,Yan Scholten,Stephan Günnemann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a framework that connects randomized smoothing to differential privacy profiles, enabling tight, modular certification of robustness against combined training-time (backdoor) and test-time (evasion) attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing randomized smoothing guarantees handle either poisoning attacks (training-time) or evasion attacks (test-time) separately, but not both jointly. Backdoor attacks involve perturbations at both stages, requiring a unified certificate.

Method: The framework connects randomized smoothing to the dual view of differential privacy via privacy profiles, enabling a numerical procedure for composing heterogeneous mechanisms (training & inference). It instantiates for DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation with inference-time smoothing.

Result: Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in providing joint robustness guarantees against both training-time and inference-time attacks.

Conclusion: A principled, general framework for using composite mechanisms to certify robustness under complex threat models that better capture real-world adversarial capabilities.

Abstract: Randomized smoothing is a powerful tool for certifying robustness to adversarial perturbations, including poisoning attacks via randomized training and evasion attacks via randomized inference. Extending these guarantees to backdoor attacks, where training and test data are jointly perturbed, remains challenging because training- and test-time randomized mechanisms must be analyzed within a single robustness certificate. We address this by connecting randomized smoothing to the dual view of differential privacy through privacy profiles, which provide a numerical procedure for composing heterogeneous mechanisms. The resulting framework enables tight, modular, end-to-end certification of complex, composed mechanisms while leveraging existing analyses of differentially private mechanisms. We instantiate the framework for DP-SGD and Deep Partition Aggregation with inference-time smoothing, deriving joint robustness guarantees against both training-time and inference-time attacks. Experiments on MNIST and CIFAR-10 demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework. Overall, we provide a principled and general framework for using composite mechanisms to certify robustness under complex threat models that better capture the capabilities of real-world adversaries.

</details>


### [195] [MMD-Balls as Credal Sets: A PAC-Bayesian Framework for Epistemic Uncertainty in Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21783)
*Ahanaf Hasan Ariq*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper develops a PAC-Bayesian framework for test-time adaptation that uses MMD to quantify distribution shift, interprets MMD-balls as credal sets from imprecise probability theory, and provides formal generalization bounds and uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TTA methods lack formal guarantees connecting the magnitude of distribution shift to prediction reliability, making it unclear when adaptation is truly needed or beneficial.

Method: The authors establish: (i) a PAC-Bayesian bound with MMD-dependent shift penalty under RKHS-Lipschitz loss; (ii) finite-sample version via MMD concentration; (iii) uniform worst-case risk bound with lower-upper risk decomposition; and (iv) geodesic preservation bounds for kernel-guided adaptation.

Result: The framework provides formal generalization bounds explicitly parameterized by MMD, separates epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty via credal set interpretation, and yields a principled decision criterion for when adaptation is warranted.

Conclusion: Bridging PAC-Bayesian learning with imprecise probability theory offers a theoretically grounded approach to TTA that quantifies when and why adaptation improves reliability under distribution shift.

Abstract: Test-time adaptation (TTA) methods improve model performance under distribution shift but lack formal guarantees connecting shift magnitude to prediction reliability. We develop a PAC-Bayesian framework yielding generalization bounds explicitly parameterized by the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between source and target distributions. Our principal contribution is interpreting MMD-balls around the source distribution as credal sets in Walley's imprecise probability theory, yielding natural epistemic uncertainty quantification. We establish: (i) a PAC-Bayesian bound with an MMD-dependent shift penalty under an RKHS-Lipschitz loss assumption; (ii) a finite-sample version via MMD concentration; (iii) a uniform worst-case risk bound over all distributions in the credal set, with a lower-upper risk decomposition; and (iv) geodesic preservation bounds explaining why kernel-guided adaptation protects local feature geometry. The credal set interpretation separates epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty and provides a principled decision criterion for when adaptation is warranted.

</details>


### [196] [Understanding Multimodal Failure in Action-Chunking Behavioral Cloning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22493)
*Lorenzo Mazza,Massimiliano Datres,Ariel Rodriguez,Sebastian Bodenstedt,Gitta Kutyniok,Stefanie Speidel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper analyzes why behavioral cloning fails with multimodal action-chunking policies, identifying key failure mechanisms in latent-variable and action-space generative models, supported by experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavioral cloning struggles when the same observation has multiple valid actions (multimodality). Understanding how different policy parameterizations handle this is crucial for improving imitation learning.

Method: The authors analyze two types of policies: (1) latent-variable policies, examining the trade-off between posterior-prior regularization and mode information preservation, and (2) action-space generative policies, analyzing how Lipschitz smoothness constraints limit multimodal coverage.

Result: Excessive posterior-prior regularization removes action-conditioned information in latent-variable policies, while insufficient regularization leads to poor prior coverage. For generative policies, small Lipschitz constants prevent assigning probability to many well-separated modes, requiring sharp transitions or off-support bridges.

Conclusion: Both policy types have fundamental limitations in handling multimodality, with insights validated on synthetic and robotic benchmarks.

Abstract: Behavioral cloning becomes difficult when the same observation admits several valid actions. We study this problem for action-chunking policies and show that different multimodal parameterizations fail in different ways. For latent-variable policies, posterior-prior regularization makes deployment-time sampling more reliable, but excessive regularization removes the action-conditioned information needed to distinguish demonstrated modes. Reducing this regularization can preserve mode information, but then success depends on whether the prior covers the relevant latent regions. For action-space generative policies, multimodality is constrained by the smoothness of the base-to-action transport: a map with small Lipschitz constant cannot assign substantial probability to many well-separated modes. Covering many modes therefore requires either sharp transitions in base space or off-support bridge regions in action space. Experiments on synthetic multimodal tasks and robotic simulation benchmarks support these mechanisms.

</details>


### [197] [Three Costs of Amortizing Gaussian Process Inference with Neural Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21798)
*Robin Young*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper theoretically analyzes the approximation gap between Gaussian Processes (GPs) and Latent Neural Processes (LNPs), decomposing the KL divergence into three interpretable sources: label contamination, information bottleneck, and amortization error. It provides convergence rates linking architecture design to kernel smoothness and input dimension, and offers architectural recommendations to improve LNP performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify the cost of amortizing GP inference with neural processes, identifying the sources of approximation error between the exact GP posterior and the learned neural process predictives.

Method: The paper bounds the KL divergence between GP and LNP predictives, decomposing it into three interpretable terms: label contamination (using label values for label-independent quantities), information bottleneck (finite-dimensional representation limiting context geometry resolution), and amortization error (shared encoder across contexts). It analyzes convergence rates for different kernels.

Result: The bottleneck truncation decays as O(e^{-c d^{2/d_x}}) for squared-exponential kernels and O(d^{-2ν/d_x}) for Matérn kernels. Label contamination is O(1) generally, with noise component decaying as O(1/n).

Conclusion: The analysis yields architectural recommendations: predict variance from context locations alone (GP-amortization regime) and replace mean aggregation with second-order pooling to close the dominant amortization gap.

Abstract: Neural processes amortize Gaussian process inference, replacing the exact $O(n^3)$ posterior with a learned $O(n)$ map from context sets to predictive distributions. For a class of latent neural processes, we bound the Kullback--Leibler (KL) divergence between the GP and LNP predictives, decomposing it into three interpretable sources, namely label contamination as the neural process uses label values to estimate a quantity that is label-independent in the exact GP, an information bottleneck because the finite-dimensional representation cannot resolve the full context geometry, and amortization error from a single encoder network shared across all contexts. The bottleneck truncation term decays in the representation dimension $d$ as $O(e^{-cd^{2/d_x}})$ for squared-exponential kernels on $\mathbb{R}^{d_x}$ where $c > 0$ is a kernel-dependent constant and as $O(d^{-2ν/d_x})$ for Matérn-$ν$ kernels, directly linking architecture sizing to kernel smoothness and input dimension. The label contamination term is $O(1)$ in general, with only the observation-noise component decaying as $O(1/n)$, identifying a persistent cost of routing uncertainty estimation through a label-dependent representation. These results characterize the costs of amortization within the analyzed class and yield architectural recommendations to predict variance from context locations alone in the GP-amortization regime, and replace mean aggregation with second-order pooling to close the dominant amortization gap.

</details>


### [198] [MoSA: Motion-constrained Stress Adaptation for Mitigating Real-to-Sim Gap in Continuum Dynamics via Learning Residual Anisotropy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22597)
*Jiaxu Wang,Junhao He,Jingkai Sun,Yi Gu,Yunyang Mo,Jiahang Cao,Qiang Zhang,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoSA is a physics-informed framework that learns residual stress operators (capturing mild anisotropy/heterogeneity) on top of isotropic physical priors, using microplane-constrained redistribution and motion-constrained supervision, to improve real-to-sim dynamics learning and enable better sim-to-real transfer for robot manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world objects exhibit mild anisotropy and heterogeneity beyond what isotropic homogeneous physics models can capture; after calibrating the near-isotropic backbone, these residual effects become the key bottleneck for closing the real-to-sim gap, while pure black-box neural modeling discards physical priors and suffers from poor data efficiency and overfitting.

Method: MoSA uses an isotropic model as a physics prior and learns residual stress operators via a physics-informed cascaded network with microplane-constrained redistribution, plus motion constraints supervising temporal and spatial derivatives of the deformation field.

Result: Achieves superior accuracy, generalization, and robustness in learned dynamics, learns physically meaningful residual anisotropy, and enables more reliable sim-to-real transfer in robot manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: Targeting residual effects (anisotropy/heterogeneity) via physics-constrained learning significantly improves real-to-sim dynamics modeling and downstream sim-to-real transfer.

Abstract: Learning real-world dynamics from visual observations is crucial for various domains. A common strategy is to calibrate simulators by estimating physical parameters, yet accuracy is ultimately bounded by the underlying physical models, which often assume materials are homogeneous and isotropic. Even if reasonable, real-world objects typically exhibit mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. After the near-isotropic backbone is well calibrated, these residual effects become the key bottleneck for further closing the real-to-sim gap. Although neural networks can fit dynamics end-to-end, such black-box modeling discards strong physical priors, leading to poor data efficiency and overfitting. Therefore, we propose MoSA, a motion-constrained stress adaptation framework that targets these residual effects to further improve real-to-sim dynamics learning. MoSA uses an isotropic model as a physics prior and learns residual stress operators to capture mild anisotropy and heterogeneity. It progressively adapts stresses via microplane-constrained redistribution in a physics-informed cascaded network. We further impose motion constraints by supervising temporal and spatial derivatives of the deformation field. Experimentally, our learned dynamics achieves superior accuracy, generalization, and robustness, while learning physically meaningful residual anisotropy. Finally, we validate MoSA in a robot manipulation setting, showing that better real-to-sim dynamics modeling translates into more reliable sim-to-real transfer. Project Page is available at https://mercerai.github.io/MoSA/.

</details>


### [199] [Why Semantic Entropy Fails: Geometry-Aware and Calibrated Uncertainty for Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21801)
*Zheyuan Zhang,Kaiwen Shi,Han Bao,Zehong Wang,Tianyi Ma,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies two critical gaps (anisotropic and calibration gaps) in current entropy-based uncertainty estimators used in post-training, and proposes GCPO (Geometric-aware Calibrated Policy Optimization) which integrates geometry-aware measures and reward-based calibration to better regulate gradient variance and learning signal quality, consistently improving performance across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current critic-free models for post-training use response-level uncertainty signals to regulate group-based optimization (e.g., GRPO), but these approaches are empirically unstable and lack a principled understanding of how uncertainty signals influence optimization dynamics. The authors aim to provide a theoretical foundation for uncertainty signals in regulating gradient variance and learning signal quality.

Method: The paper first provides a principled formulation interpreting uncertainty signals as mechanisms for gradient variance and learning signal regulation. It identifies two gaps in entropy-based estimators: the anisotropic gap and the calibration gap. Then proposes GCPO, combining geometry-aware measures (capturing semantic disagreement) with reward-based calibration to align uncertainty with actual learning signal strength.

Result: Experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that GCPO more faithfully tracks gradient variability and consistently improves post-training performance compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: Designing uncertainty signals that are aligned with optimization dynamics is crucial for robust post-training. The paper offers a principled perspective for improving post-training through geometry-aware, calibrated uncertainty estimation.

Abstract: Post-training has become central to improving reasoning and alignment in large language models, where critic-free models enable scalable learning from model-generated outputs but lack principled mechanisms to distinguish informative from noisy signals. Recent approaches leverage response-level measures as uncertainty signals to regulate group-based optimization methods such as GRPO. Yet their empirical success remains unstable and unclear in how they influence optimization dynamics. In this paper, we provide, to our knowledge, the first principled formulation that interprets uncertainty signals as mechanisms for characterizing and regulating gradient variance and learning signal quality. Based on both empirical and theoretical analysis, we identify two critical gaps of current entropy-based estimators: The anisotropic gap and The calibration gap. Motivated by this analysis, we propose Geometric-aware Calibrated Policy Optimization (GCPO), a novel framework integrating geometry-aware measures to capture semantic disagreement with reward-based calibration to align uncertainty with learning signal strength. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our approach more faithfully tracks gradient variability and consistently improves post-training performance. Our results highlight the importance of designing uncertainty signals that are aligned with optimization dynamics, offering a principled perspective for robust post-training.

</details>


### [200] [Same Architecture, Different Capacity: Optimizer-Induced Spectral Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21803)
*Nandan Kumar Jha,Brandon Reagen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Optimizers fundamentally shape how effectively a Transformer's FFN width translates into spectral representation capacity; Muon achieves near-linear spectral scaling (β=1.02) on hard learning regimes while AdamW shows weak scaling (β=0.44), revealing optimization as a primary axis of representation scaling independent of architecture and validation loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scaling laws ignore optimizer effects on representation structure; this paper aims to show that optimizers significantly influence how well models utilize spectral capacity in FFN representations, particularly in hard-to-learn regimes.

Method: Measured soft and hard spectral ranks of FFN eigenspectra across different optimizers (AdamW, Muon, low-rank Dion variants), holding architecture and width schedule fixed; compared spectral scaling exponents and hard-soft rank asymmetry; also contrasted optimizer effects against architectural interventions (attention rank, positional encoding).

Result: Muon achieves linear spectral scaling (β=1.02) on rare-token representations vs AdamW's weak scaling (β=0.44); AdamW can match Dion's perplexity under extended training while showing different spectral geometry; optimizer-induced spectral shifts often exceed those from architectural modifications.

Conclusion: Optimization should be treated as a first-class axis of representation scaling, motivating optimizer-architecture co-design rather than treating optimizers as fixed training details.

Abstract: Scaling laws have made language-model performance predictable from model size, data, and compute, but they typically treat the optimizer as a fixed training detail. We show that this assumption misses a fundamental axis of representation scaling: how effectively the optimizer converts added FFN width into utilized spectral capacity. Using eigenspectra of feed-forward network representations, measured through soft and hard spectral-ranks, we find that \emph{the same Transformer architecture realizes markedly different spectral scaling laws when trained with different optimizers}. Holding architecture and width schedule fixed, AdamW exhibits weak hard-rank scaling ($β$=0.44) on rare-token (TAIL) representations where learning is known to be hardest, whereas Muon achieves linear scaling ($β$=1.02) in the same regimes, a $2.3\times$ increase in the scaling exponent. This difference is not reducible to validation loss: AdamW configurations can match low-rank Dion variants in perplexity, under extended training, while exhibiting sharply different spectral geometry, demonstrating that matched loss does not imply matched representation structure. Hard--soft rank asymmetry further reveals that optimizers differ not only in how much capacity is realized, but also in how that capacity is structured across eigenmodes. To disentangle optimizer effects from architectural ones, we compare against architectural interventions (e.g., attention rank and positional encoding), and find that optimizer-induced spectral shifts often exceed the architectural effects. These results suggest optimization as a first-class axis of representation scaling, motivating optimizer--architecture co-design.

</details>


### [201] [Symbolic Density Estimation for Discrete Distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21813)
*Ziwen Liu,Meng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces symbolic density estimation (SDE), an unsupervised framework that automatically discovers closed-form probability mass functions for discrete distributions by combining structural priors, evolutionary search, and validity-aware inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The catalog of interpretable discrete probability distributions has grown slowly through manual derivations, motivating the need for an automated framework to discover closed-form densities.

Method: SDE composes elementary analytic operations within a structured search space, integrating domain-specific structural priors, evolutionary search, and validity-aware inference. It extends to zero-inflation and finite mixture models.

Result: The method recovers all benchmark distribution families with accurate parameter estimates, and identifies concise, interpretable mixture models that improve goodness-of-fit over standard models on real data.

Conclusion: SDE offers a promising automated approach for discovering interpretable discrete distributions, with demonstrated accuracy and practical utility.

Abstract: Discrete probability laws underpin statistical modeling, yet the catalog of interpretable distributions has expanded only gradually through centuries of case-by-case mathematical derivations. We introduce symbolic density estimation (SDE), an unsupervised framework that automatically recovers closed-form probability mass functions by composing elementary analytic operations within a structured search space. Our method integrates domain-specific structural priors with evolutionary search and a validity-aware inference stage, and it extends to richer distribution families such as zero inflation and finite mixtures. To support systematic evaluation and future research, we contribute a benchmark dataset spanning a broad collection of commonly used discrete distributions. The proposed algorithm recovers all benchmark families with accurate parameter estimates. A real data application shows that it identifies concise and interpretable mixture models that improve goodness-of-fit over standard models.

</details>


### [202] [Beyond Scalar Objectives: Expert-Feedback-Driven Autonomous Experimentation for Scientific Discovery at the Nanoscale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21820)
*Ralph Bulanadi,Jefferey Baxter,Arpan Biswas,Hiroshi Funakubo,Dennis Meier,Jan Schultheiß,Rama Vasudevan,Yongtao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces deep-kernel pairwise learning (DKPL), a method that incorporates human expert judgments into autonomous microscopy experiments, overcoming the limitations of traditional Bayesian optimization that relies on predefined scalar descriptors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional BO-based autonomous experimentation relies on predefined scalar descriptors, which can be challenging to determine and may miss subtle scientifically important phenomena that experts can recognize through interdisciplinary insight.

Method: DKPL enables experts to directly compare and evaluate experimental outputs based on interdisciplinary knowledge, then learns a latent utility function from these expert pairwise judgments to guide subsequent autonomous microscopy experiments.

Result: DKPL successfully learned physically meaningful nanoscale structures and prioritized high-information measurement regions. It distinguished high vs low characteristic domain-wall angles in bismuth ferrite and discovered both head-to-head and tail-to-tail domain-wall character in erbium manganite.

Conclusion: This work establishes a pathway toward expert-guided self-driving laboratories capable of addressing scientific problems beyond the limits of scalar-metrics-driven learning by integrating expert knowledge into autonomous experiments.

Abstract: Self-driving laboratories or autonomous experimentation are emerging as transformative platforms for accelerating scientific discovery. Bayesian optimization (BO) is among the most widely used machine learning frameworks for these purposes, but these BO-based frameworks rely on predefined scalar descriptors to guide experimentation. In many situations, the determination of an appropriate scalar descriptor can be challenging, and may fail to capture subtle yet scientifically important phenomena apparent to experts with interdisciplinary insight. To overcome this limitation, here we develop deep-kernel pairwise learning (DKPL), an approach for autonomous microscopy experiments which incorporates human expertise and interdisciplinary scientific knowledge into an active learning loop. Instead of relying on explicit scalar objectives, DKPL enables experts to directly evaluate which experimental output is more promising using interdisciplinary knowledge. DKPL then learns a latent utility function from these expert judgements to guide subsequent autonomous microscopy experiments. We demonstrate DKPL's performance in learning physically meaningful nanoscale structures while effectively prioritizing high-information measurement regions using an experimental model dataset with known ground truth. We further apply DKPL to analyze the character of ferroelectric domain walls, where we find DKPL capable of distinguishing between high and low characteristic domain-wall angles in bismuth ferrite, and able to discover both head-to-head and tail-to-tail domain-wall character in erbium manganite. This development establishes an approach to integrate expert knowledge into autonomous microscopy experiments and demonstrates a pathway toward expert-guided self-driving laboratories capable of addressing scientific problems beyond the limits of scalar-metrics-driven learning.

</details>


### [203] [On-Policy Consistency Training Improves LLM Safety with Minimal Capability Degradation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21834)
*Andy Han,Kristina Fujimoto,Avidan Shah,Kiet Nguyen,Kai Xu,Chen Yueh-Han,Ilia Sucholutsky,Rico Angell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes On-Policy Consistency Training (OPCT), a new method for training AI models to be safer by having them learn from their own responses during training, rather than from fixed examples. OPCT significantly reduces sycophancy (8.1% vs 15.4%), improves jailbreak defense (~99% vs 87%), and avoids capability regressions (e.g., no 28-point drop on MATH-500) compared to standard supervised fine-tuning approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing alignment methods produce models that misbehave in various ways (sycophancy, jailbreaks, lacking safety warnings). Consistency training via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) causes models to memorize surface forms and generalize poorly, leading to capability regressions. The authors aim to develop a better consistency training approach that improves generalization and preserves capabilities.

Method: OPCT computes the training objective over the model's own responses to prompts, supervised by itself conditioned on corresponding contrastive prompts. Unlike prior SFT-based consistency training that uses offline, pre-generated supervision signals, OPCT uses on-policy (model's own) responses.

Result: Across three model families and three safety axes (sycophancy, jailbreaking, safety awareness), OPCT outperforms SFT. Sycophancy rate: 8.1% (OPCT) vs 15.4% (baseline) vs 11.2% (SFT). Jailbreak defense: ~99% (OPCT) vs 87% (SFT). Safety awareness: OPCT outperforms SFT on 2/3 models. OPCT avoids capability regressions (e.g., no 28-point drop on MATH-500 that SFT exhibits).

Conclusion: Consistency training is best implemented as OPCT rather than SFT, especially when generalization beyond the training distribution is desired. The on-policy approach yields better safety outcomes while preserving model capabilities.

Abstract: Aligned models can misbehave in several ways: they are often sycophantic, fall victim to jailbreaks, or fail to include appropriate safety warnings. Consistency training is a promising new alignment paradigm to mitigate such failures by training invariants into the model using contrastive input pairs. Existing consistency training procedures generate the supervision signal once, offline, and use supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to update the model. Unfortunately, the resulting models tend to merely memorize the surface forms of the training distribution and thus generalize poorly and regress in their capabilities. We introduce On-Policy Consistency Training (OPCT), a new consistency training approach where the objective is computed over the model's own responses to prompts, supervised by itself conditioned on corresponding contrastive prompts. We evaluate OPCT on three safety axes: sycophancy, jailbreaking, and safety awareness. Across three model families, OPCT outperforms its SFT counterpart on all safety desiderata. It nearly halves the sycophancy rate relative to baseline (8.1% vs. 15.4%, compared to 11.2% for SFT). Under an adaptive per-target attacker, OPCT holds jailbreak defense success near 99% on held-out jailbreak behaviors, whereas SFT achieves 87% on average. On safety awareness, OPCT outperforms SFT in two out of three models, and matches it on the other. OPCT also largely avoids the capability regressions that SFT induces, such as a 28-point drop on MATH-500. Our results suggest that consistency training is best implemented as OPCT rather than as SFT, especially when generalization beyond the training distribution is desired.

</details>


### [204] [Geometry-Adaptive Explainer for Faithful Dictionary-Based Interpretability under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21849)
*Sungjun Lim,Heedong Kim,Andrew Lee,Kyungwoo Song*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies that dictionary-based explainers (like sparse autoencoders) suffer faithfulness degradation under distribution shift because the model's active subspace rotates away from the trained dictionary. The authors formalize this as a "faithfulness gap," propose a Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE) that realigns the dictionary with OOD activations using only unlabeled data and no gradient updates, and prove it improves over unadapted explainers with theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dictionary-based mechanistic interpretability methods have been widely used to explain model behavior, but their faithfulness under out-of-distribution (OOD) shifts has not been systematically studied. The paper aims to understand and fix the degradation of explainers when the input distribution changes.

Method: The authors formalize the "faithfulness gap" as a geometric misalignment between the ID-trained dictionary and the OOD-active subspace. They propose GAE, which realigns the dictionary via a transformation that requires only unlabeled OOD activations and no gradient updates. Theoretical analysis bounds the excess loss by second-moment shift.

Result: GAE matches or surpasses all training-based baselines in causal faithfulness across multiple models and OOD settings.

Conclusion: The faithfulness gap under distribution shift can be effectively mitigated by geometry adaptation without retraining, offering a practical and theoretically grounded solution for robust mechanistic interpretability.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability aims to explain a model's behavior by identifying causally responsible internal structures. Dictionary-based explainers such as sparse autoencoders and transcoders are a primary tool, but their faithfulness under out-of-distribution (OOD) shift has received little systematic attention. We show that distribution shift rotates the subspace that the model actively uses, misaligning the explainer's dictionary trained on in-distribution (ID) activations. We formalize this misalignment as the faithfulness gap, a geometric distance between the ID dictionary and the OOD-active subspace, and show that it controls OOD faithfulness degradation. To reduce this gap, we propose the Geometry-Adaptive Explainer (GAE), which realigns the explainer's dictionary with the OOD-active subspace while preserving the original feature structure. This requires only unlabeled OOD activations and no gradient updates. We prove that GAE improves over the unadapted ID explainer, with excess loss bounded quadratically by the second-moment shift. Empirically, GAE even matches or surpasses all training-based baselines in causal faithfulness across multiple models and OOD settings.

</details>


### [205] [OPPO: Bayesian Value Recursion for Token-Level Credit Assignment in LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21851)
*Yu Li,Rui Miao,Tian Lan,Zhengling Qi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPPO improves LLM reasoning by accumulating oracle signals along trajectories to compute token-level advantages without a value network, outperforming GRPO, DAPO, and SDPO on math, science, and code benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods like GRPO assign a single trajectory-level advantage to all tokens, diluting signals at key reasoning steps and adding noise to uninformative ones. Prior distillation methods provide per-token signals but apply them in isolation, ignoring trajectory-level context.

Method: OPPO accumulates oracle signals (likelihood ratios) along a trajectory to compute a running estimate of success probability at each position via Bayesian update. This yields token-level advantages without a value network or extra rollouts. Two variants: self-oracle (student as scorer) and teacher-oracle (frozen stronger model).

Result: OPPO improves over GRPO, DAPO, and SDPO by up to +6.0 points on AMC'23 and +5.2 points on AIME'24 across seven benchmarks, with gains increasing monotonically with response length.

Conclusion: OPPO provides a principled, critic-free token-level credit assignment mechanism for RL-based LLM reasoning improvement, with theoretical guarantees on variance reduction and practical gains over strong baselines.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become the standard recipe for improving LLM reasoning, but the dominant algorithm GRPO assigns a single trajectory-level advantage to every token, diluting the signal at pivotal reasoning steps and injecting noise at uninformative ones. Critic-free alternatives derived from on-policy distillation supply per-token signals through oracle-conditioned likelihood ratios, yet apply each signal in isolation from the trajectory-level evidence accumulated up to that position. We propose Oracle-Prompted Policy Optimization (OPPO), which rests on a single observation: the oracle signal used by prior distillation-style methods for local discrimination is also the natural Bayesian update of the model's belief about eventual success. Accumulating the signal along a trajectory yields, in closed form and at the cost of one extra forward pass, a running estimate of the success probability at every position, together with a token-level advantage that requires no learned value network and no additional rollouts. A first-order analysis factorizes the advantage into the per-token discrimination signal used by distillation methods modulated by a state weight that concentrates credit on genuinely pivotal tokens, with a directional variance-reduction guarantee. The framework admits two estimators differing only in which model scores the evidence: a \textit{self-oracle} that reuses the student and recovers the on-policy distillation reward as a strict special case, and a \textit{teacher-oracle} that delegates scoring to a stronger frozen model. On two base LLMs across seven mathematics, science, and code reasoning benchmarks, OPPO improves over GRPO, DAPO, and SDPO by up to $+6.0$ points on AMC'23 and $+5.2$ points on AIME'24, with gains that widen monotonically with response length.

</details>


### [206] [The Illusion of Reasoning: Exposing Evasive Data Contamination in LLMs via Zero-CoT Truncation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21856)
*Yifan Lan,Yuanpu Cao,Hanyu Wang,Lu Lin,Jinghui Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Zero-CoT Probe (ZCP), a black-box method to detect evasive data contamination in LLMs by truncating Chain-of-Thought reasoning and comparing performance on original vs. isomorphically perturbed benchmarks, introducing a Contamination Confidence metric for quantifying contamination severity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data contamination undermines objective evaluation of LLMs, and malicious publishers use evasive strategies (e.g., paraphrasing benchmarks) to evade detection. Existing methods struggle to detect such stealthy contamination reliably.

Method: ZCP truncates the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) process to expose latent shortcut mappings, then compares zero-CoT performance on the original benchmark vs. an isomorphically perturbed reference dataset to isolate memorization from genuine reasoning. A Contamination Confidence metric quantifies both likelihood and severity.

Result: Extensive experiments on both previously identified contaminated models and specially fine-tuned contaminated models show ZCP robustly detects both direct and evasive data contamination.

Conclusion: ZCP effectively detects contamination even when models use evasive strategies, and the Contamination Confidence metric provides a more nuanced assessment beyond binary classification.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated impressive reasoning abilities across a wide range of tasks, but data contamination undermines the objective evaluation of these capabilities. This problem is further exacerbated by malicious model publishers who use evasive, or indirect, contamination strategies, such as paraphrasing benchmark data to evade existing detection methods and artificially boost leaderboard performance. Current approaches struggle to reliably detect such stealthy contamination. In this work, we uncover a critical phenomenon: a model's generated reasoning steps actively mask its underlying memorization. Inspired by this, we propose the Zero-CoT Probe (ZCP), a novel black-box detection method that deliberately truncates the entire Chain-of-Thought (CoT) process to expose latent shortcut mappings. To further isolate memorization from the model's intrinsic problem-solving capabilities, ZCP compares the model's zero-CoT performance on the original benchmark against an isomorphically perturbed reference dataset. Furthermore, we introduce Contamination Confidence, a metric that quantifies both the likelihood and severity of contamination, moving beyond simple binary classifications. Extensive experiments on both previously identified contaminated models and specially fine-tuned contaminated models demonstrate that ZCP robustly detects both direct and evasive data contamination. The code for ZCP is accessible at https://github.com/Yifan-Lan/zero-cot-probe.

</details>


### [207] [When to Switch, Not Just What: Transition Quality Prediction in Clash Royale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21868)
*Heeyun Heo,Huy Kang Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analyzing 926,334 Clash Royale matches, the paper finds switching strategies after losses is inversely linked to win rates. The authors propose TQP, a three-stage recommendation pipeline (Who→When→What) that accounts for switching costs and individual differences, achieving +10.4% SwitchGap at 5.4% recommendation rate, with loss-triggered switchers benefiting most.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Players often switch strategies after losing streaks, but this behavior is paradoxically associated with lower win rates. Existing recommendation systems suffer from the "Zero Switching Cost Assumption"—evaluating strategies by expected quality alone without considering the behavioral cost of switching or individual differences in switching propensity.

Method: TQP (Transition Quality Predictor) is a three-stage pipeline: (1) PersonaGate suppresses recommendations for players who benefit from consistency; (2) TimingGate identifies opportune moments to switch using subtype- and state-matched baselines; (3) ScoreFusion ranks strategies by combining adoptability signals with predicted transition quality (delta win rate). Also introduces SwitchGap as a new evaluation metric.

Result: The pipeline achieves +10.4 percentage points SwitchGap at a 5.4% recommendation rate. Loss-triggered switchers—the lowest-performing group—benefit the most from subtype-conditioned guidance.

Conclusion: Accounting for switching costs and individual differences in strategy recommendation systems is crucial. The Who→When→What framework outperforms conventional approaches that ignore transition dynamics.

Abstract: In competitive games, players frequently switch strategies after losing streaks, yet our analysis of 926,334 match records from 34,619 Clash Royale players reveals a counterintuitive pattern: switching frequency is inversely associated with the win rate, with effects that vary substantially across players and situational contexts. We attribute this to a limitation common in many prior recommendation systems, which evaluate strategies by expected quality while overlooking the behavioral cost of switching and individual differences in switching propensity. We refer to this implicit premise as the Zero Switching Cost Assumption. To address this, we reformulate strategy recommendation as a transition-level decision problem and instantiate it as TQP (Transition Quality Predictor), a three-stage pipeline structured as Who -> When -> What. PersonaGate suppresses recommendations for players whose strategic consistency is empirically associated with superior outcomes. TimingGate identifies moments when switching is likely to yield a net benefit over staying, using a subtype- and state-matched baseline to control for natural win-rate recovery. ScoreFusion ranks candidate strategies by combining an adoptability signal with predicted transition quality (delta WR). We further introduce SwitchGap, an evaluation metric that measures a policy's discriminative quality without treating observed player choices as optimal ground truth. This property is particularly important because the most frequent switchers record the lowest win rates. The full pipeline achieves a SwitchGap of +10.4 percentage points at a recommendation rate of 5.4%, and loss-triggered switchers, despite being the lowest-performing group, benefit the most from subtype-conditioned guidance.

</details>


### [208] [Noise Schedule Design for Diffusion Models: An Optimal Control Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21911)
*Seo Taek Kong,Weina Wang,R. Srikant*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a principled optimal control framework for designing noise schedules in diffusion models, linking Fisher information to sampling error bounds, and deriving tunable schedule generalizations that achieve state-of-the-art FID scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theoretical results show \(\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/n)\) sampling error bounds, but these hold only for specific noise schedules that are not used in practice. There is a need to bridge theory and practice by developing a principled method for noise schedule design.

Method: The problem is recast as an optimal control problem where the state is Fisher information (evolving via an ODE) and the control is the noise schedule. The objective function is an upper bound on KL sampling error. Sufficient conditions on schedules are derived, and under parametric assumptions, closed-form schedules generalizing exponential/sigmoid forms are obtained.

Result: Sufficient conditions for achieving \(\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/n)\) sampling error are derived. The proposed generalized schedules (tunable parameters) achieve superior FID scores on image generation benchmarks compared to standard schedules.

Conclusion: Noise schedule design can be systematically optimized via optimal control, yielding both theoretical guarantees and practical improvements in diffusion model performance.

Abstract: We develop a principled framework for analyzing and designing noise schedules in diffusion models. We show that one can recast this design problem as an optimal control problem, whose state is the Fisher information of the diffusion process which evolves according to an ODE and the control input is the noise schedule. The objective of the optimal control problem is a functional involving the Fisher information, which is shown to be an upper bound on the Kullback-Leibler sampling error. By solving this optimal control problem, we obtain sufficient conditions on noise schedules under which state-of-the-art $\tilde{\mathcal{O}} (d/n)$ sampling error is achievable, where $d$ is the data dimension and $n$ is the number of discretization steps. While existing theoretical work also prove that $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(d/n)$ sampling error bounds are achievable, these results hold for specific noise schedules, which do not include the schedules used in practice. Under a further parametric assumption on the data distribution, we show that one can obtain closed-form expressions for the noise schedules. These noise schedules generalize standard empirical schedules such as exponential and sigmoid schedules by allowing additional parameters that can be tuned. Systematically tuning the parameters of these schedules yields new schedules that achieve superior FID scores on image generation benchmarks.

</details>


### [209] [CausalGuard: Conformal Inference under Graph Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21928)
*Vikash Singh,Weicong Chen,Debargha Ganguly,Yanyan Zhang,Nengbo Wang,Sreehari Sankar,Mohsen Hariri,Alexander Nemecek,Chaoda Song,Shouren Wang,Biyao Zhang,Van Yang,Erman Ayday,Jing Ma,Vipin Chaudhary*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalGuard is a structure-weighted conformal framework that aggregates graph-conditional doubly robust pseudo-outcomes to provide valid and efficient treatment effect estimation with distribution-free finite-sample coverage guarantees, demonstrating superior performance over graph-agnostic baselines on five benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating treatment effects from observational data requires a valid adjustment set, but the true causal graph is unknown. Graph misspecification leads to under-coverage, while graph-agnostic conformal methods regain coverage only through large padding intervals. A method that leverages graph structure flexibly while maintaining coverage is needed.

Method: CausalGuard proposes candidate DAGs from LLM-derived edge priors, prunes them via conditional-independence tests, and reweights them by BIC. It then aggregates graph-conditional doubly robust pseudo-outcomes using these posterior weights, and calibrates a composite nonconformity score to provide distribution-free finite-sample marginal coverage.

Result: Across five benchmarks, CausalGuard achieves mean coverage above the 90% nominal level for the target estimand and reduces interval width compared to graph-agnostic conformal baselines. Stress tests show it suppresses invalid collider adjustment and remains stable under misspecified priors when candidate sets are data-supported.

Conclusion: CausalGuard offers a principled way to incorporate uncertain graph structure into conformal inference, providing valid coverage without excessive padding. It is robust to prior misspecification as long as the candidate set retains data-supported structures.

Abstract: Estimating treatment effects from observational data requires choosing an adjustment set, but valid adjustment depends on an unknown causal graph. Graph misspecification can cause under-coverage, while graph-agnostic conformal wrappers may regain nominal coverage only through large padding. We introduce CausalGuard, a structure-weighted conformal framework that calibrates after aggregating graph-conditional doubly robust pseudo-outcomes. Candidate DAGs are proposed from an LLM-derived edge prior, pruned by conditional-independence tests, and reweighted by Bayesian Information Criterion. A composite nonconformity score then calibrates the posterior-weighted pseudo-outcome. CausalGuard provides distribution-free finite-sample marginal coverage for this aggregated pseudo-outcome; under causal identification, overlap, conditional-mean nuisance stability, and concentration on target-aligned valid adjustment strategies, its conditional mean converges to the true Conditional Average Treatment Effect. Across five benchmarks, CausalGuard attains mean coverage above the nominal 90% level for the directly evaluable target and reduces width when graph-agnostic conformal baselines require large padding. Stress tests show that CausalGuard suppresses invalid collider adjustment and remains stable under misspecified priors when the retained candidate set is data-supported.

</details>


### [210] [SCI-Defense: Defending Manipulation Attacks from Generative Engine Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21948)
*Xucheng Yu,Haibo Jin,Huimin Zeng,Haohan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCI-Defense is a three-component defense framework (PPL, SIS, ICD) against GEO attacks on LLM-based ranking systems, achieving perfect precision/recall on product descriptions but struggling with web passages lacking persuasive signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based ranking systems are vulnerable to Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) attacks where adversaries inject semantic signals into product descriptions to artificially boost rankings, and existing defenses (PPL-only filters, SafetyClf, paraphrasing) achieve zero recall against semantic manipulation.

Method: Proposes SCI-Defense with three components: Perplexity detection (PPL), Semantic Integrity Scoring (SIS) evaluating four dimensions (Authority Attribution, Narrative Purposiveness, Comparative Claims, Temporal Claims), and Inter-Candidate Detection (ICD).

Result: On Amazon product descriptions: Precision=1.000, FPR=0.000; Recall: 1.000 (String), 0.952 (Reasoning), 0.830 (Review attacks). On MS MARCO: perfect recall on String attacks but near-zero recall on Review attacks due to lack of persuasive signals.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that existing defenses are ineffective against semantic GEO attacks, and reveals that Specification Amplification and Use-Case Saturation attacks exploit structural defense blind spots, pointing to future research directions.

Abstract: LLM-based ranking systems are vulnerable to Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) attacks, where adversaries inject semantic signals into product descriptions to artificially boost rankings. We propose SCI-Defense, a three-component defense framework combining Perplexity detection (PPL), Semantic Integrity Scoring (SIS), and Inter-Candidate Detection (ICD). SIS evaluates four manipulation dimensions: Authority Attribution (AA), Narrative Purposiveness (NP), Comparative Claims (CA), and Temporal Claims (TC). Evaluated on 600 Amazon product descriptions across 6 categories, SCI-Defense achieves Precision=1.000 and FPR=0.000, with Recall of 1.000, 0.952, and 0.830 against String, Reasoning, and Review attacks respectively. On 600 MS MARCO web passages, String attacks are blocked with perfect recall while Review attacks yield near-zero recall, as web passages lack the persuasion-oriented signals that SIS targets in product descriptions. We demonstrate that existing defenses -- PPL-only filters, SafetyClf content classifiers, and paraphrasing -- achieve zero recall against semantic manipulation attacks. We further demonstrate new attacks such as Specification Amplification and Use-Case Saturation can expose semantic relevance manipulation as a structural defense blind spot that suggests directions for future research.

</details>


### [211] [Dynamic Mixture of Latent Memories for Self-Evolving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21951)
*Dianzhi Yu,Vireo Zhang,Hongru Wang,Yanyu Chen,Minda Hu,Wanghan Xu,Siki Chen,Philip Torr,Zhenfei Yin,Irwin King*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoLEM is a generative mixture of latent memory framework using dynamic MoE that enables continual learning without catastrophic forgetting by keeping the base model frozen and internalizing knowledge into additional expert modules with lightweight autoencoders for routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continual learning methods either update model parameters (causing catastrophic forgetting) or use external memory (failing to improve intrinsic capabilities). The authors aim to enable genuine self-evolution without forgetting.

Method: MoLEM uses multiple experts as independent memory carriers with key-query matching routing. The base model stays frozen; knowledge is internalized into additional modules. Each training stage has a lightweight autoencoder for routing selection; unmatched inputs fall back to pretrained model.

Result: After full continual-learning sequence across math, science, and code domains, MoLEM improves average accuracy by 10.40% over the Vanilla pretrained baseline. No competing methods consistently exceed this baseline across different training orders.

Conclusion: MoLEM effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting in continual learning while genuinely enhancing the model's intrinsic capabilities through latent memory injection.

Abstract: Achieving self-evolution in intelligent agents requires the continual accumulation of new knowledge across changing task sequences without forgetting previously acquired abilities. Existing approaches either internalize knowledge by updating model parameters, which induces catastrophic forgetting, or rely on external memory, which fails to genuinely enhance the model's intrinsic capabilities. We propose MoLEM, a generative mixture of latent memory framework based on a dynamic mixture-of-experts (MoE). We treat multiple experts as independent carriers to generate memory. A router selects and weights experts through key-query matching, and the aggregated latent memory is injected into the reasoning process. The base model for reasoning remains entirely frozen, with all experiential knowledge internalized into the additional modules, avoiding catastrophic forgetting. For continual learning, each training stage is paired with a lightweight autoencoder that selects the appropriate routing group at inference, and inputs that match no stage fall back to the pretrained model. Experiments train the framework on continual-learning sequences spanning math, science, and code domains.
