In this way, MDL-GBTRSC connects interpretable local representation learning with affinity graph construction in a unified spectral clustering framework. Experiments on real and synthetic datasets show that MDL-GBTRSC achieves the best average ARI and NMI under the adopted fixed-configuration protocol compared with classical spectral clustering baselines and representative granular-ball, micro-cluster, and anchor-based methods.

</details>


### [265] [Asymmetric Virtual Memory Paging for Hybrid Mamba-Transformer Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22416)
*An Xuan Nguyen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AVMP is a virtual memory system for hybrid LLMs that physically separates KV caches and SSM states into distinct pools behind a unified address space, dynamically migrating capacity between pools to avoid out-of-memory errors and improve throughput by up to 13.3x.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current inference engines handle hybrid language models (e.g., Jamba) poorly because they either pad SSM states to attention page sizes (wasting up to 7.3x capacity) or use static dual pools that cannot adapt to shifting prompt distributions between requests.

Method: Asymmetric Virtual Memory Paging (AVMP) separates KV caches and SSM states into physically distinct memory pools behind a unified virtual address space, and migrates capacity between pools on allocation failure, keeping behavior deterministic.

Result: On an RTX 3060 12GB across 270 synthetic cells and 60 ShareGPT cells: OOM events drop 7.6%, request throughput improves 1.83x to 13.3x on synthetic workloads and 2.36x on ShareGPT, with all gains holding under 95% confidence intervals.

Conclusion: AVMP provides two mechanisms—shorter OOM recovery on capacity-pressured workloads and faster allocation calls on KV-heavy workloads—indicating that physically separating the two cache types with dynamic migration is an effective approach for hybrid model inference.

Abstract: Hybrid language models like Jamba mix attention layers with State Space Models (SSMs), creating two memory cache types with opposite profiles: Key-Value (KV) caches grow linearly with sequence length, while SSM states stay fixed per layer. Current inference engines handle this poorly. Unified pools pad SSM states to attention page sizes, wasting up to 7.3x capacity. Static dual pools cannot adapt when prompt distributions shift between requests. We present Asymmetric Virtual Memory Paging (AVMP). The allocator separates the two cache types into physically distinct pools behind a unified virtual address space, and migrates capacity between pools when one runs out. Migration triggers only on allocation failure, keeping behavior deterministic. We evaluate AVMP across 270 synthetic cells plus 60 cells of ShareGPT trace replay on an RTX 3060 12GB. Out-of-Memory events drop 7.6% and request throughput improves 1.83x to 13.3x across synthetic workloads and 2.36x on ShareGPT. All gains hold under paired-bootstrap 95% confidence intervals. A phase-time breakdown reveals two distinct mechanisms: shorter OOM recovery on capacity-pressured workloads, and faster allocation calls on KV-heavy workloads. Implementation is pure Python; Triton integration is future work.

</details>


### [266] [AMUSE: Anytime Muon with Stable Gradient Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22432)
*Jueun Kim,Baekrok Shin,Jihun Yun,Beomhan Baek,Minhak Song,Chulhee Yun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies that Muon accelerates training along low-curvature directions but amplifies noise in high-curvature directions, and proposes AMUSE—a method combining Muon's rapid progress with Schedule-Free averaging via a time-varying interpolation coefficient—that improves the performance-iteration trade-off without learning rate schedules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing optimizers (AdamW with schedules, Schedule-Free, Muon) each have limitations: Muon shows strong empirical gains but its mechanism is poorly understood, and it introduces oscillatory behavior in high-curvature directions. The authors aim to understand Muon's failure modes and design a better optimizer.

Method: AMUSE (Anytime MUon with Stable Gradient Evaluation) integrates Muon and Schedule-Free averaging using a time-varying interpolation coefficient: it initially leans toward the fast Muon sequence for rapid adaptation, then gradually shifts toward the averaged sequence to dampen valley-wall oscillations.

Result: Across vision tasks and LLM pretraining, AMUSE consistently improves the performance-iteration Pareto frontier compared to (Schedule-Free) AdamW and Muon.

Conclusion: By combining Muon's bulk-progress speed with Schedule-Free's oscillation suppression, AMUSE achieves better anytime performance without requiring learning rate schedules, outperforming both baselines.

Abstract: Modern deep learning commonly relies on AdamW with prescribed learning rate schedules, but recent works challenge both components: Schedule-Free optimization removes explicit schedules via iterate averaging, and Muon improves the update geometry by orthogonalizing momentum for matrix parameters. Despite Muon's strong empirical performance, its underlying mechanism remains partially understood. We study Muon through the river-valley loss landscape, where useful training progress occurs along a flat, low-curvature bulk subspace (the river), while high-curvature dominant directions form steep valley walls that induce oscillations. We empirically show that while Muon's orthogonalization accelerates river progress by increasing the bulk component, it also amplifies dominant-direction noise, causing oscillatory trajectories. Building on this, we propose Anytime MUon with Stable gradient Evaluation (AMUSE), which integrates Muon's rapid bulk progress with the stabilizing effect of Schedule-Free averaging. AMUSE uses a time-varying interpolation coefficient that initially evaluates gradients near the fast Muon sequence for rapid adaptation, then gradually shifts toward the stable averaged sequence to suppress valley-wall oscillations. As a result, AMUSE requires no learning rate schedules and supports anytime training. Across vision tasks and large language model pretraining, AMUSE consistently improves the performance-iteration Pareto frontier over (Schedule-Free) AdamW and Muon.

</details>


### [267] [Don't Forget the Critic: Value-Based Data Rehearsal for Multi-Cyclic Continual Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22454)
*Benjamin Poole,Andrew Quinn,Li Yang,Minwoo Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Qreg+NWLU, a method that applies continuous data rehearsal with immediate Q-value regularization to improve learning efficiency, reduce forgetting, and enable knowledge transfer in multi-cyclic continual RL with Deep Q-Networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing data rehearsal methods in Continual RL are confined to policy gradient frameworks and only regularize actors, ignoring value function approximation. Also, prior evaluations rarely consider multi-cyclic environments where tasks repeat, which exacerbates forgetting and plasticity issues.

Method: Qreg+NWLU introduces two modifications to Deep Q-Networks: (1) continuous data rehearsal that dynamically collects and updates stored Q-values throughout training, and (2) "No-Wait" regularization applied immediately rather than after the first task.

Result: The proposed modifications yield improvements in learning efficiency, forgetting mitigation, and knowledge transfer compared to Qreg and conventional CRL methods in value function approximation settings.

Conclusion: Data rehearsal can be effectively extended to value function approximation (DQN) with simple modifications, and multi-cyclic settings are important benchmarking scenarios for CRL research.

Abstract: Data rehearsal has emerged as a leading approach for mitigating catastrophic forgetting in Continual Reinforcement Learning (CRL). However, existing work remains confined to policy gradient frameworks, regularizing only actors due to the performance degradation incurred by critic regularization. This actor-centric approach overlooks the potential of data rehearsal for value function approximation. Moreover, existing evaluations in CRL rarely consider multi-cyclic environments where task sequences repeat, a critical real-world scenario that exacerbates forgetting and plasticity. We investigate data rehearsal for Deep Q-Networks using Q-value regularization in multi-cyclic settings and propose Qreg+NWLU which introduces two simple modifications: (1) continuous data rehearsal that dynamically collects and updates stored Q-values throughout training, and (2) "No-Wait" regularization that applies immediately rather than after the first task. Together, these modifications yield improvements in learning efficiency, forgetting mitigation, and knowledge transfer over Qreg and conventional CRL methods within value function approximation settings.

</details>


### [268] [BioFormer: Rethinking Cross-Subject Generalization via Spectral Structural Alignment in Biomedical Time-Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22468)
*Guikang Du,Haoran Li,Xinyu Liu,Zhibo Zhang,Xiaoli Gong,Jin Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BioFormer uses spectral drift alignment and sample conditional normalization to explicitly suppress subject-specific variability in biomedical time-series, achieving 6% F1 improvement over 12 baselines across 6 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods implicitly suppress subject-specific variability in BTS through adversarial learning or model building, but rarely model it explicitly. The authors propose spectral drift as a new perspective to characterize this variability.

Method: Proposes BioFormer with two key components: (1) Frequency-Band Alignment Module (FBAM) generating band-wise modulation factors from spectral distribution to adjust amplitude/phase, and (2) Sample Conditional Layer Normalization that infers normalization from intrinsic signal statistics.

Result: Outperforms 12 baselines across 6 datasets with absolute 6% F1-score improvements.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling spectral drift as subject-specific variability and aligning spectral structure is an effective approach for cross-subject generalization in biomedical time-series.

Abstract: Cross-subject generalization in biomedical time-series refers to training on data from some subjects and testing on unseen subjects.The key challenge is to suppress subject specific variability in BTS representations.Most existing methods implicitly suppress the variability through model building or subject adversarial learning, but rarely model it explicitly.We introduce spectral drift as a new perspective to characterize subject specific variability.Specifically, BTS signals under the same label often share consistent oscillatory structure, yet exhibit subject-dependent magnitude or phase shifts in specific frequency components, which we interpret as subject-specific variability. Building on this insight, we propose BioFormer.At its core is a Frequency-Band Alignment Module(FBAM) that generates band-wise modulation factors from the spectral distribution and adaptively adjusts amplitude and phase to align spectral structure, thereby mitigating variability.We further pair FBAM with Sample Conditional Layer Normalization, which infers normalization parameters from intrinsic signal statistics rather than subject identity, stabilizing cross-subject representations.Extensive experiments on six datasets demonstrate that BioFormer outperforms 12 baselines, yielding absolute F1-score improvements of 6%.

</details>


### [269] [Lost in Tokenization: Fundamental Trade-offs in Graph Tokenization for Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22471)
*Maya Bechler-Speicher,Gilad Yehudai,Gil Harari,Clayton Sanford,Amir Globerson,Joan Bruna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that tokenization choice (spectral, random-walk, adjacency) fundamentally determines transformer expressivity for graphs, showing different tokenizations require vastly different depths for the same computation, with some being lossy or ill-conditioned, and that shallow transformers cannot convert between tokenization families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformers are widely used for graph learning, but the impact of tokenization choice on what structural information is exposed to the model has been an underexplored yet crucial design decision.

Method: The authors theoretically analyze three tokenization families (spectral, random-walk, adjacency), proving expressivity bounds, depth separations, and limitations in converting between tokenizations, supplemented by controlled experiments.

Result: Different tokenizations induce distinct depth regimes; random-walk tokenization is lossy regardless of walk length; spectral tokenization is lossless but ill-conditioned for local tasks; shallow transformers cannot convert between tokenization families.

Conclusion: Tokenization is a fundamental expressivity component for graph transformers; different tasks favor different structural views, and combining complementary tokenizations can leverage distinct signals.

Abstract: Transformers have become a central architecture for graph learning, but their application to graphs requires first choosing a tokenization: a graph-to-token map that determines which structural information is exposed at the input. In this work, we show that this choice is a fundamental component of transformer expressivity. We examine three tokenizations that serve as building blocks for many existing graph tokenizations: spectral, random-walk, and adjacency tokenizations. We prove that different tokenizations induce distinct depth regimes: the same graph computation may be realizable by a shallow transformer under one tokenization, while requiring substantially larger depth under another. For example, we prove that random-walk tokenization is lossy for any walk length, making it impossible in general to recover the graph from it, and that while spectral tokenization is lossless, it is ill-conditioned for local tasks. We further show that although both random-walk and spectral tokenizations are derived from adjacency information, it is impossible for a limited-depth transformer to convert between tokenization families in general. In particular, we establish lower bounds and impossibility results showing that unfavorable tokenizations may preclude the efficient recovery of more suitable structural representations. Finally, we complement our theory with controlled experiments on synthetic and real-world tasks, validating the predicted separations and showing that different tasks favor different structural views, and combining complementary tokenizations allows the transformer to leverage distinct signals from each representation.

</details>


### [270] [Winner-Take-All bottlenecks enforce disentangled symbolic representations in multi-task learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22472)
*Julian Gutheil,Simon Hitzginger,Robert Legenstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that Winner-take-all (WTA) bottlenecks in deep neural networks can extract symbolic, categorical latent factors in multi-task learning, with theoretical proofs and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While WTA networks are common in both biological brains (cortical circuits) and deep learning (softmax/attention), their role in extracting latent factors from highly non-linearly entangled data has remained unclear. The authors aim to bridge this gap.

Method: The authors theoretically prove that under well-defined conditions, a WTA bottleneck within a deep neural network enforces extraction of categorical latent factors in multi-task learning. They also empirically validate on two datasets with architectures not fully meeting theorem assumptions.

Result: The emergent representations are highly symbolic—single neurons or populations encode abstract features (e.g., object, color, position). Empirical results show advantages for generalization even when assumptions are partially violated.

Conclusion: The proposed model provides insights into generalization of WTA-like networks and serves as a potential interface between symbolic and subsymbolic AI systems.

Abstract: Winner-take-all (WTA) networks constitute a central circuit motif in cortical networks of the brain. In addition, WTA-like activations are abundant in modern deep learning models in the form of the softmax activation for example in attention layers of transformers. While their role in the extraction of latent factors has been studied for relatively simple generative models, their role in the context of highly non-linearly entangled latent factors has remained elusive. In this article, we show that a WTA bottleneck within a deep neural network can enforce under certain well-defined conditions the extraction of categorical latent factors of the data in a multi-task learning setup. In particular, we prove that the representation that emerges in the WTA bottleneck is highly symbolic, where a single neuron or a population of neurons encodes the presence of a single abstract feature such as a specific object, color, or position. We furthermore show empirically on two datasets, that this also holds for architectures and setups that do not fully comply with the assumptions of our theorem and demonstrate the advantages of the acquired symbolic representation for generalization. Our proposed model provides insights into the generalization capabilities of deep neural networks with WTA-like components and may serve as an interface between symbolic and subsymbolic AI systems.

</details>


### [271] [Structured-Sparse Attention for Entity Tracking with Subquadratic Sequence Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22476)
*Hangyue Zhao,Paul Caillon,Erwan Fagnou,Alexandre Allauzen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a blockwise evaluation method for resolvent-style attention operators in entity tracking. By exploiting the structured nature of learned attention (local block-diagonal with light cross-block residue), they achieve subquadratic complexity ($O(n^{4/3}d)$) while maintaining accuracy, with 12-29% wall-clock time reduction and up to 2.4× speedup over compact dense Transformers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Entity tracking requires maintaining latent states over long sequences. Existing task-specific attention operators can compress deep Transformers into few layers via multi-hop state propagation, but their dense evaluation remains computationally expensive.

Method: They identify that learned attention in entity tracking exhibits strong structure: most mass concentrates in local block-diagonal neighborhoods with light cross-block residue. They derive a blockwise evaluation of a resolvent-style operator that keeps within-block interactions exact and routes cross-block interactions through a reduced system, achieving subquadratic complexity $O(n^{4/3}d)$.

Result: The method matches dense operator accuracy on controlled tracking benchmarks, reduces wall-clock time by 12-29%, and is up to 2.4× faster than compact dense Transformer at comparable exact-match accuracy.

Conclusion: The blockwise evaluation is effective for entity tracking, but performance collapses when simultaneously evolving properties exceed the number of attention heads, indicating a key limitation.

Abstract: Entity tracking requires maintaining and updating latent states for entities and attributes over long sequences. Recent task-specific attention operators can compress deep Transformer stacks into a few layers by performing multi-hop state propagation within a single layer, but their dense evaluation remains expensive. We show that in this setting, learned attention is strongly structured: most mass concentrates in local block-diagonal neighborhoods with a light cross-block residue. Exploiting this, we derive a blockwise evaluation of a resolvent-style operator that keeps within-block interactions exact and routes cross-block interactions through a reduced system. The resulting evaluation is subquadratic in sequence length $O(n^{4/3}d)$ (and $O(n^{7/3})$ when $d\approx n$). On controlled tracking benchmarks, our method matches the dense operator's accuracy while reducing wall-clock time by $12-29\%$ under a standardized measurement protocol, and is up to $2.4 \times$ faster than a compact dense Transformer at comparable exact-match accuracy. We further provide ablations over block size and model capacity, and identify a limitation: performance collapses when the number of simultaneously evolving properties exceeds the number of attention heads.

</details>


### [272] [Implicit Regularization of Mini-Batch Training in Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22480)
*Clement Wang,Antoine Vialle,Robin Vaysse,Thomas Bonald*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Random Node Sampling (RNS) matches or outperforms full-graph GNN training on 8/10 datasets, explained by backward error analysis showing mini-batch SGD implicitly regularizes by gradient variance; RNS yields lower variance and better implicit objective than structure-aware samplers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work assumed structure-aware samplers are necessary for GNN mini-batch training due to topology changes and boundary effects; this paper challenges that assumption by testing the simplest baseline—RNS.

Method: Empirical comparison of RNS against full-graph training on 10 datasets; backward error analysis of graph mini-batch SGD to derive implicit regularization proportional to gradient variance.

Result: RNS matches/outperforms full-graph training on 8/10 datasets with lower time/memory; its expected loss is closer to full-graph loss and per-batch gradients have lower variance.

Conclusion: Graph sampler choice is a form of implicit regularization; RNS is theoretically grounded and a strong scalable alternative to complex samplers.

Abstract: Mini-batch training of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) is fundamentally different from training on i.i.d. data: sampling a subgraph alters the topology and introduces boundary effects, leading prior work to develop structure-aware samplers that preserve local connectivity and reduce embedding variance. Surprisingly, we demonstrate that the simplest possible scheme, Random Node Sampling (RNS), training on the induced subgraph of uniformly sampled nodes, matches or outperforms full-graph training on 8 of 10 datasets at a fraction of the wall-clock time and memory. To explain this, we apply backward error analysis to graph mini-batch Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) and show that it implicitly minimizes the sampled loss plus a regularizer proportional to the mini-batch gradient variance, a quantity directly shaped by the sampler. Although RNS discards local structure, it produces mini-batches whose expected loss is closer to the full-graph loss, and whose per-batch gradients have lower variance, yielding a better implicit objective. Our analysis reframes the choice of graph sampler as a form of implicit regularization, and identifies RNS as a strong, theoretically grounded method for scalable GNN training.

</details>


### [273] [When Stronger Triggers Backfire: A High-Dimensional Theory of Backdoor Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22481)
*Donald Flynn,Hadas Yaron Goldhirsh,Jonathan P. Keating,Inbar Seroussi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies backdoor poisoning attacks in high-dimensional settings, finding that stronger training triggers can counterintuitively improve clean test accuracy while attack success peaks at an intermediate trigger strength, with theoretical proofs for linear models and experimental validation on CIFAR-10 and ResNet-18.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the counterintuitive behavior of backdoor poisoning attacks in high dimensions, where stronger training triggers can paradoxically help the defender, and to provide a theoretical framework explaining this phenomenon.

Method: The authors study regularized generalized linear models on Gaussian-mixture data in the proportional regime (p/n → κ), varying training trigger strength α against a fixed test trigger. They prove closed-form results for squared loss and extend to general convex GLM losses via a Gaussian-proxy fixed-point system.

Result: Three key findings: (i) clean test accuracy increases with α, (ii) attack success peaks at finite α then declines, and (iii) the most damaging trigger direction is the minimum eigenvector of data covariance. A finite-sample noise floor proportional to κ is identified as the mechanism behind (i).

Conclusion: The counterintuitive high-dimensional effects are theoretically proven and empirically validated, showing that classical n ≫ p analysis misses these phenomena. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and ResNet-18 confirm the theory extends beyond convex settings.

Abstract: Backdoor poisoning attacks behave counter-intuitively in high dimensions: stronger training triggers can help the defender. We study regularised generalised linear models on Gaussian-mixture data in the proportional regime ($p/n \to κ$), varying the training trigger strength $α$ against a fixed test trigger. Three phenomena emerge: (i) clean test accuracy increases with $α$; (ii) attack success peaks at a finite $α$ and then declines; and (iii) the most damaging trigger direction is the minimum eigenvector of the data covariance. We prove all three results in closed form for the squared loss, and extend (i) and (ii) to general convex GLM losses via a Gaussian-proxy fixed-point system. We identify a finite-sample noise floor proportional to $κ$ as the mechanism behind (i), invisible to classical $n \gg p$ analysis. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and Gaussian surrogates match the theory closely; ResNet-18 experiments show the same phenomena beyond the convex setting.

</details>


### [274] [Represented Is Not Computed: A Causal Test of Candidate Algorithmic Intermediates in a Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22488)
*Ishita Darade,Sushrut Thorat*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Transformer trained on base-digit extraction represents the algorithmic intermediates (floor division and modulo) of the closed-form solution, but causal analysis reveals these intermediates are not transmitted along the localized causal route to the output, showing probe-based and causal conclusions can diverge sharply.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how Transformers integrate structured prompt components and whether their internal representations align with explicit algorithmic steps, using arithmetic as a cleaner domain where candidate algorithms can be precisely defined.

Method: Train a Transformer on base-digit extraction; evaluate accuracy; use linear probes to decode algorithmic intermediates; apply causal intervention tests to trace information flow; perform sparse circuit search to identify functional subgraphs.

Result: The model achieves 99.83% accuracy. Probes find staged arithmetic computation. However, causal tests show the localized route from D to output does not transmit the probed intermediates. Sparse circuit search finds separate N, B, D routes that combine late.

Conclusion: Probe-based conclusions about internal algorithms can diverge from causal mechanistic evidence, even when explicit algorithmic hypotheses are available; representation does not imply causal utilization.

Abstract: Structured prompts require integrating components according to task-relevant relations. How a network implements this integration is often hard to judge in language or vision, where those relations are rarely specified precisely enough to define a candidate internal algorithm. Arithmetic offers a cleaner setting. We study a Transformer trained on base-digit extraction: given $N$, $B$, and $D$, it must report the coefficient of $B^D$ in the base-$B$ expansion of $N$. The closed-form solution, $\lfloor N/B^D \rfloor \bmod B$, provides explicit candidate algorithmic intermediates. Across three seeds, the model reaches 99.83% exact-answer accuracy on held-out number-base intersections, establishing reliable task competence. Linear probes decode the intermediates, making staged arithmetic computation plausible. Causal tests then separate representation from use: within the localized route from the stream with $D$ as input to the output positions, behavior depends on early $D$-selective communication, independent of $N$ and $B$. Relatedly, a sparse circuit search finds mostly separate $N$, $B$, and $D$ routes that combine late rather than the staged route suggested by the probes. Thus, the model represents the intermediates that make the closed-form solution plausible, but the identified localized causal route does not transmit them to the output stream. This case shows that probe-based conclusions can diverge sharply from causal observations, even when explicit algorithmic hypotheses are available.

</details>


### [275] [The Signal in the Noise: OOD Detection Through Goodness-of-Fit Testing in Factorised Latent Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22496)
*Philipp Bomatter,Jack Geary,Henry Gouk*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes SITN, a method for out-of-distribution detection using continuous normalizing flows, leveraging their diffeomorphic and mass-preserving properties to detect OOD samples by identifying atypical noise representations, outperforming likelihood-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deep generative models assign unreliable likelihoods for OOD detection, making them ineffective despite being a natural choice. This paper aims to overcome this limitation.

Method: The method (SITN) exploits the diffeomorphic and mass-preserving properties of continuous normalizing flows to analyze noise-level representations of samples, identifying OOD data as those mapped to atypical noise vectors under the prior.

Result: SITN achieves effective OOD detection on standard benchmarks and synthetic perturbations, with minimal computational overhead, strict false positive rate control, and no need for OOD data access.

Conclusion: The proposed SITN method addresses the unreliability of likelihood-based OOD detection in deep generative models and avoids the complexity bias inherent in such approaches.

Abstract: Deep generative models offer a natural foundation for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, yet prior work has shown that their assigned likelihoods are notoriously unreliable indicators for in- vs out-of-distribution data. In this paper, we address this problem by leveraging the diffeomorphic and mass-preserving properties of continuous normalising flows. Our analysis shows that OOD samples are mapped to noise samples that are highly atypical under the noise prior in ways not captured by the likelihood. Based on this observation, we propose a new method -- Signal in the Noise (SITN) -- for OOD detection on the single-sample level. SITN requires no access to OOD data, incurs minimal computational overhead, and provides strict control of false positive rates. Comprehensive evaluations through standard benchmarks and synthetic perturbations highlight the method's effectiveness and the absence of the complexity bias inherent to likelihood-based methods.

</details>


### [276] [The Neural Compiler: Program-to-Network Translation for Hybrid Scientific Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22498)
*Lucas Sheneman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The Neural Compiler translates symbolic equations (written in a Scheme-like language) into frozen, differentiable PyTorch modules that encode known physics exactly, enabling hybrid scientific ML models where learned components handle unknown parts. It matches hand-coded implementations numerically, recovers physical constants with <1% error using few parameters (vs. PINNs with >8500 params and 7-93% error), and enables zero-error composability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific ML needs to combine known physics with data-driven learning, but existing approaches either ignore known structure, use soft penalties that don't enforce exactness, or require manual PyTorch coding for each equation—which is error-prone and unscalable.

Method: The Neural Compiler translates programs in a first-order Scheme-like expression language into frozen, differentiable PyTorch modules that exactly match the source program to floating-point precision, with gradients via autograd. It supports 51 primitive operations including vector/matrix algebra for PDE discretizations.

Result: Evaluated on six domains (Feynman equations, Lotka-Volterra, damped pendulum, 1D heat equation, 3D vector mechanics, compositional generalization). Compiled modules match hand-coded PyTorch numerically with no accuracy loss. With 1-4 trainable params, recover physical constants <1% error; PINN baselines (8500+ params) show 7-93% error. Compiled modules compose with zero error vs. accumulating errors in neural approximations.

Conclusion: The key value is systematic composability—generating correct, differentiable modules from symbolic specifications without manual rewriting. The string-in, module-out interface also provides a natural target for LLMs to translate scientific descriptions into executable modules.

Abstract: Scientific machine learning often requires combining known physics with unknown parameters or correction terms learned from data. Existing approaches either ignore known structure, encode it as a soft penalty, or require hand-written PyTorch code for each equation. We present The Neural Compiler, a system that translates programs written in a first-order Scheme-like expression language into frozen, differentiable PyTorch modules. These modules match the source program to floating-point precision and provide gradients through autograd. In hybrid models, the compiled module encodes known physics exactly while learned components model the unknown remainder. We evaluate the compiler across six experiment domains: Feynman physics equations, Lotka-Volterra dynamics, a damped pendulum, a one-dimensional heat equation, three-dimensional vector mechanics, and compositional generalization. Compiled modules match hand-coded PyTorch implementations numerically for single equations, showing no accuracy loss from compilation. With only 1 to 4 trainable parameters, compiled models recover physical constants to less than 1 percent error in most cases, while standard PINN baselines with more than 8500 parameters show 7 to 93 percent error. Compiled modules also compose with zero error, while neural approximations can accumulate large errors in deep composition chains. The main value of the compiler is not improved accuracy over hand-coded equations, but systematic composability: it generates correct, differentiable modules from symbolic specifications without rewriting each equation by hand. The system supports 51 primitive operations, including vector and matrix algebra, enabling PDE discretizations and hybrid scientific models. This string-in, module-out interface also provides a natural target for large language models that translate scientific descriptions into executable differentiable modules.

</details>


### [277] [Generative Modeling by Value-Driven Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22507)
*Pablo Moreno-Muñoz,Adrian Müller,Gergely Neu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new generative modeling framework using discrete-time stochastic control and linear programming to produce value-driven transport (VDT) policies with straight transport paths, enabling fast, robust simulation and easy integration of conditional generation, classifier-free guidance, and unpaired data translation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a generative modeling approach that overcomes limitations of existing flow, diffusion, and Schrödinger bridge methods by producing straight, efficiently simulable transport paths while maintaining flexibility for various downstream tasks.

Method: Formulates measure transport as a discrete-time stochastic control problem, recast as a linear program whose dual yields the optimal value function encoding the control policy. A simulation-free primal-dual algorithm is developed to compute approximate optimal value functions and VDT policies.

Result: VDT policies produce straight transport paths that are fast and robust to simulate. The method supports conditional generation, classifier-free guidance, and unpaired data-to-data translation. Experimental results show strong performance and scalability potential.

Conclusion: The proposed VDT framework offers a principled, flexible, and efficient alternative to existing generative models, with promising scalability and competitive empirical performance.

Abstract: We propose a new framework for generative modeling based on a discrete-time stochastic control formulation of measure transport. Adapting classic results from control theory, we formulate our problem as a linear program whose dual variables correspond to the \emph{optimal value function} of the control problem, which directly encodes the optimal control policy. Exploiting this LP formulation, we develop an efficient simulation-free primal-dual algorithm for computing approximately optimal value functions and the associated \emph{value-driven transport} (VDT) policies which approximate the true optimal policy. We show that well-trained VDT policies enjoy numerous favorable properties in comparison with other state-of-the-art methods based on flows, diffusions, or Schrödinger bridges: they lead to straight transport paths which can be simulated quickly and robustly, and can be enhanced in all the same ways as diffusion and flow-based models (e.g., conditional generation, classifier-free guidance, unpaired data-to-data translation are all easy to incorporate). We evaluate our methodology in a range of experiments, with results that indicate strong performance and good potential for scalability.

</details>


### [278] [Stabilising Explainability Fragility in Cybersecurity AI: The Impact and Mitigation of Multicollinearity in Public Benchmark Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22529)
*Ioannis J. Vourganas,Anna Lito Michala*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that multicollinearity (high correlation among features) causes instability in AI explanations (SHAP, LIME) for intrusion detection systems, proposes formal theorems, a new metric (Explainability Fragility Score), and two mitigation methods (CAA-Filtering and SHARP).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite widespread use of post-hoc explainability tools (SHAP, LIME) in intrusion detection, the impact of multicollinearity on explanation robustness has been unexplored, threatening trustworthiness in security-critical contexts.

Method: The paper introduces a formal theorem proving multicollinearity inflates attribution variance, validates it on UNSW-NB15 across four model families (linear, tree-based, kernel, neural), proposes Explainability Fragility Score, CAA-Filtering (post-hoc stabilization), and SHARP (training-time regularization).

Result: Multicollinearity significantly inflates explanation instability; the proposed methods (CAA-Filtering and SHARP) mitigate fragility while maintaining stable predictive performance, validated using Kendall's τ across bootstrapped explanations.

Conclusion: Multicollinearity mitigation should be incorporated into IDS pipelines for trustworthy XAI; the paper provides a set of practical guidelines for practitioners in security-critical AI systems.

Abstract: This paper investigates a unexplored yet impactful vulnerability in AI explainability used in intrusion detection (IDS): multicollinearity-induced instability. Despite extensive reliance on post-hoc explainability tools such as SHAP or LIME, the impact of correlated features on explanation robustness is not evaluated. We introduce a formal theorem stating that multicollinearity inflates attribution variance. This demonstrates that explanations and feature importances are non-identifiable under multicollinearity. A suite of comprehensive experiments validates the theorem on a representative benchmark dataset, UNSW-NB15. Four widely used families of models are evaluated, including linear, tree-based, kernel, and neural, across full and pruned feature sets based on VIF and correlation thresholding. We propose the novel metric of Explanability Fragility Score and two novel methods to mitigate it with variable integration complexity. CAA-Filtering focuses on stabilising explanations by grouping attributions of trained models. SHARP is a novel training-time regularisation framework that penalises attribution instability, enabling controllable and monotonic improvement of explainability stability. The findings support stable predictive performance, using Kendall's τ to quantify instability across bootstrapped explanations. This work has direct implications for the trustworthiness and reproducibility of XAI in security-critical contexts, and motivates incorporating multicollinearity mitigations into the IDS pipelines, providing a set of guidelines for practitioners.

</details>


### [279] [Disentanglement Beyond Generative Models with Riemannian ICA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22531)
*Edmond Cunningham*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RICA replaces the global generative assumptions of traditional ICA with local Riemannian geometry, introducing a disentanglement tensor for pointwise disentanglement that depends on the Hessian of data log likelihood and Ricci curvature, enabling local disentanglement analysis without requiring a global generative model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap between theoretical disentanglement frameworks (ICA, which assumes independent latent variables) and modern representation learning where pretrained encoders exhibit disentangled properties without such generative assumptions. No general theory exists to interpret these features as independent factors of variation.

Method: Riemannian ICA (RICA) replaces global generative models with local geometric structure. It formalizes factors of variation through radial curves mapping to axis-aligned lines in latent space using Riemannian geometry. The key contribution is the disentanglement tensor, encoding pointwise disentanglement via the Hessian of data log likelihood and Ricci curvature.

Result: In controlled source recovery with known ground-truth sources, RICA recovers sources across several manifolds, while ICA baselines' success depends on the coordinate representation of observations.

Conclusion: RICA provides a theoretical basis for studying local disentanglement without assuming a global generative model, bridging the gap between ICA theory and modern representation learning practice.

Abstract: There is a gap between the theoretical foundations of disentanglement and the practice of modern representation learning. Existing theoretical frameworks, particularly Independent Component Analysis (ICA) and its nonlinear variants, assume a generative model with statistically independent latent variables underlying the data so that disentanglement amounts to identifying the latents that could have generated the data. This generative framework is interpretable and theoretically justified, but its strong assumptions make it difficult to apply to modern representation learning. Modern pretrained encoders often learn features that exhibit disentangled properties without making generative assumptions, yet there is no general theory for interpreting these features as independent factors of variation. We take a step toward such a theory by introducing Riemannian ICA (RICA), which replaces ICA's global generative model with local geometric structure. RICA is founded on the observation that in ICA, the factors of variation underlying a data point can be understood through radial curves emanating from the point that map to axis-aligned lines in the latent space. We formalize this perspective using Riemannian geometry and introduce our theory in a way that is consistent with the existing generative approach. Our main contribution is the disentanglement tensor, which encodes a second-order notion of disentanglement that we call pointwise disentanglement. This tensor depends on the Hessian of the data log likelihood as well as the Ricci curvature induced by the model. In a controlled source recovery setting with known ground-truth sources, RICA recovers sources across several manifolds, while the success of ICA baselines depends on the coordinates used to represent the observations. Our work provides a theoretical basis for studying local disentanglement without assuming a global generative model.

</details>


### [280] [Relational Linear Properties in Language Models: An Empirical Investigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22532)
*Giovanni Valer,Luigi Gresele,Marco Bronzini,Emanuele Marconato*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a KL-divergence based probing method to test relational linearity in language model representations, showing that it varies across models, layers, and paraphrased relation phrasings, while being more efficient than prior approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear properties in LM representations are ubiquitous but hard to test experimentally. The authors aim to provide a rigorous experimental method to test the relational linearity hypothesis—that object unembeddings can be linearly predicted from subject embeddings for a fixed relation.

Method: Proposes a probing method based on Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence to evaluate relational linearity across layers and paraphrased relational queries. It avoids Jacobian approximations used in prior work (e.g., Linear Relational Embeddings by Hernandez et al., 2024).

Result: Experiments across four datasets show: (1) relational linearity varies across models, (2) layer-wise patterns align with prior observations about linguistic information in representations, and (3) paraphrasing the relation affects linearity differently.

Conclusion: The proposed KL-divergence probing method is both effective and efficient for testing relational linearity, revealing nuanced dependencies on model architecture and linguistic phrasing.

Abstract: Linear properties are ubiquitous in the representations of language models; however, testing them experimentally remains a challenging task. This work focuses on relational linearity: the hypothesis that, for a fixed relation (e.g., "plays"), the unembedding of an object (e.g., "trumpet") can be predicted from the embedding of its subject (e.g.,"Miles Davis") by a linear map. We present an experimental method to test the formulation of relational linearity by Marconato et al. (2025). Specifically, we introduce a probing method, based on Kullback-Leibler divergence, to evaluate this property and examine its variation across layers and paraphrased relational queries. It is also more efficient than previous work; for example, it avoids the crude Jacobian approximations used in Linear Relational Embeddings by Hernandez et al. (2024). Our findings across four datasets show that relational linearity varies across models, exhibits layer-wise patterns consistent with prior observations about linguistic information in model representations, and is differently affected by changes in how the relation is phrased.

</details>


### [281] [F-TIS: Harnessing Diverse Models in Collaborative GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22537)
*Nikolay Blagoev,Oğuzhan Ersoy,Wendelin Boehmer,Lydia Yiyu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Filtered Truncated Importance Sampling (F-TIS), a GRPO-style training method that enables heterogeneous LLMs to collaborate in decentralized RL post-training by handling off-policy samples, achieving convergence comparable to on-policy training with up to 12% better generalization on out-of-distribution tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current GRPO training assumes homogeneous models for on-policy sampling, but decentralized systems involve heterogeneous models with different capabilities. Prior work shows off-policy samples hurt GRPO convergence, motivating the need for a method that enables heterogeneous collaboration without performance degradation.

Method: The authors propose Filtered Truncated Importance Sampling (F-TIS), a GRPO-style training paradigm that uses importance sampling with filtering and truncation to effectively leverage off-policy samples from heterogeneous models while maintaining communication efficiency.

Result: F-TIS achieves identical final model convergence to purely on-policy training across various heterogeneous setups, and in some cases yields up to 12% improvement in generalization on out-of-distribution tasks.

Conclusion: F-TIS enables practical decentralized LLM RL training with heterogeneous models, overcoming the off-policy convergence issue while even improving generalization in certain scenarios.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning methods such as GRPO have seen great popularity in LLM post-training. In GRPO, models produce completions to a set of prompts, which are rewarded, and the policy is updated towards the relatively high reward completions. Due to the auto-regressive nature of models, the generation phase of such style of training can be extremely time consuming. As a solution, prior work has sought to distribute the inference step across many nodes, working parallel. These works assume primarily homogeneous models in the training in order to keep samples as close to on-policy as possible. This assumption may be impractical in decentralized systems, where parties with various computes and preferences may wish to collaborate on the same task. Thus, decentralized training requires an approach that can handle heterogeneous models - different models collaborating on the same tasks. However, this leads to highly off-policy samples presented during training, which prior work has identified that off-policy samples can hurt GRPO convergence. To enable heterogeneity, we propose Filtered Truncated Importance Sampling (F-TIS) - a GRPO-style training paradigm that can use off-policy samples to improve local model's learning. Our framework allows various models to collaborate in the same RL training run while being communication efficient. We extensively evaluate F-TIS in various heterogeneous setups and we show that it exhibits identical final model convergence to purely on-sample training. Furthermore, we observe in some setups better generalization on out-of-distribution tasks than on-policy training, increasing model's performance by up to 12\%.

</details>


### [282] [ImplicitTerrainV2: Wavelet-Guided Spatially Adaptive Neural Terrain Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22556)
*Haoan Feng,Xin Xu,Leila De Floriani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ImplicitTerrainV2 is a compact, efficient neural terrain representation that uses wavelet-guided frequency control, derivative-aware supervision, and post-training compression to achieve 66.25 dB PSNR on Swiss terrain tiles with 3.2x fewer parameters and 1.23 bpp storage, outperforming prior work while supporting continuous off-grid queries and derivative evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Raster DEMs rely on interpolation and finite-difference operators, while prior terrain INRs lack frequency control, neglect gradient structure, and are too large/costly for practical deployment.

Method: Combines a wavelet complexity field (WCF) for spatially-adaptive frequency masks, complexity-aware adaptive sampling, gradient matching supervision for derivative fidelity, and post-training mixed-precision quantization with entropy coding.

Result: 66.25 dB PSNR (5.70 dB improvement), 3.2x fewer parameters, 55s training per tile, 1.23 bpp storage with only 0.28 dB drop.

Conclusion: The neural format competes with established DEM codecs in rate-distortion while offering off-grid queries, closed-form derivatives, and resolution-independent reconstruction for downstream GIS applications.

Abstract: Digital elevation models (DEMs) underpin terrain analysis in Geographic Information Systems (GIS), but in their common raster form, they rely on interpolation for off-grid sampling and finite-difference operators for derivative-based analysis. Implicit neural representations (INRs) offer a continuous alternative, but prior terrain INRs lack explicit frequency control, neglect the gradient structure of terrain, and remain too large and costly to train for practical deployment. We present ImplicitTerrainV2, which advances terrain INRs toward a compact, efficient neural terrain data format by combining a spectral control mechanism with wavelet-guided spatial adaptivity, derivative-aware supervision, and post-training model compression. At its core, a wavelet complexity field (WCF) derives spatially-adaptive frequency masks from analytically computed wavelet coefficients, localizing high-frequency capacity to complex terrain regions. The same field guides complexity-aware adaptive sampling that concentrates training in high-complexity regions, while gradient matching applies extra supervision to enforce the smooth manifold structure of terrain DEMs for improved derivative fidelity. Post-training mixed-precision quantization and entropy coding reduce storage to 1.23 bpp with a 0.28 dB PSNR drop. On 50 Swiss terrain tiles, ImplicitTerrainV2 reaches 66.25 dB end-to-end PSNR, improving over the prior work by 5.70 dB while using 3.2x fewer parameters and training in 55 s per tile on a single GPU. Our compressed neural format is competitive with several established DEM codecs in rate-distortion performance, while additionally supporting off-grid point queries, closed-form derivative evaluation, and resolution-independent reconstruction, which may benefit many downstream GIS applications.

</details>


### [283] [Neural Flow Operators can Approximate any Operator: Abstract Frameworks and Universal Approcimations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22557)
*Shuang Chen,Juncai He,Xue-Cheng Tai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an abstract neural flow framework for both neural networks and neural operators, featuring two continuous-depth models with composition and separation structures, proving well-posedness and universal approximation (including the first such result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces), and showing that time discretizations recover ResNet-type and plain architectures respectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a unified theoretical framework that bridges continuous-depth neural flows with discrete architectures (ResNets and plain networks), while establishing universal approximation guarantees for both finite-dimensional and infinite-dimensional settings.

Method: The authors propose two continuous-depth neural flow models: (1) a composition structure that via time discretization recovers ResNet-type architectures, and (2) a separation structure that via splitting-based discretization yields plain architectures. They prove well-posedness and universal approximation, including the first such result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces, and extend these to convolutional neural flow models.

Result: Established well-posedness and universal approximation properties for the proposed neural flows. Achieved the first universal approximation result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces. Showed that discretization of composition structure recovers ResNet architectures, and discretization of separation structure recovers plain architectures.

Conclusion: The neural flow framework provides a unified theoretical foundation connecting continuous-depth models to both residual and plain architectures for neural networks and neural operators, with strong approximation guarantees.

Abstract: We introduce an abstract neural flow framework for neural networks and neural operators. The framework contains two continuous-depth models, namely neural flows with composition and separation structures, and covers both finite-dimensional function approximation and infinite-dimensional operator approximation. We prove well-posedness and universal approximation properties for the corresponding neural flows, including, to the best of our knowledge, the first universal approximation result for flow-based models between infinite-dimensional spaces. We also obtain universal approximation results for convolutional neural flow models. Through suitable time discretizations, the composition structure recovers ResNet-type architectures, while the separation structure, via a splitting-based discretization, yields plain architectures. This gives a unified flow-based route to both residual and plain architectures for neural networks and neural operators with fully connected or convolutional linear layers.

</details>


### [284] [Regret-Based $(ε,δ)$-optimal Stopping Criteria for Bayesian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22561)
*Haowei Wang,Jingyi Wang,Qiyu Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes theoretically sound stopping criteria for Bayesian optimization (GP-UCB) by deriving tighter instantaneous regret bounds, ensuring an ε-optimal solution with high probability upon termination, validated through experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current BO practice terminates after a fixed budget, incurring unnecessary cost with no optimality guarantee; a theoretically sound stopping criterion is needed.

Method: Derives provably tighter instantaneous regret bounds for GP-UCB at any iteration, then uses these bounds to construct stopping criteria that guarantee an ε-optimal solution with probability 1-δ.

Result: Tighter regret bounds are established theoretically, and numerical experiments show the stopping criteria are effective and efficient.

Conclusion: The proposed stopping criteria provide a theoretically grounded and practical way to terminate GP-UCB with guaranteed solution quality, reducing unnecessary evaluations.

Abstract: Bayesian optimization (BO) is a widely used iterative black-box optimization method that utilizes Gaussian process (GP) surrogate models. In practice, BO is typically terminated after a fixed evaluation budget is exhausted, which can incur unnecessary cost and provides no optimality guarantee on solution quality. Recent research in developing a practical stopping criterion has made empirical progress, yet a theoretically sound stopping criterion remains a work in progress. In this work, we present provably tighter instantaneous regret bounds for GP upper confidence bound (GP-UCB) at any given iteration. Then, we propose stopping criteria for GP-UCB based on this tighter bound that ensures an $ε$-optimal solution with high probability $1-δ$ upon termination. Numerical experiments are performed to validate and demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our stopping criteria.

</details>


### [285] [GraphFlow: A Graph-Based Workflow Management for Efficient LLM-Agent Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22566)
*Ao Li,Shangpeng Yang,Fahao Chen,Tianheng Xu,Peng Li,Zhou Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphFlow introduces wGraph, a unified graph-based workflow representation for LLM agents, enabling dynamic workflow generation and efficient KV cache management, achieving ~5% performance gain and 4× memory reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing workflow-assisted agent systems rely on predefined templates and shallow matching, limiting their ability to capture deep semantic relationships and generalize to unseen tasks.

Method: (1) wGraph: a unified graph where nodes represent atomic operations, serving as a shared substrate. (2) GraphFlow system with adaptive workflow generation (constructs workflows from wGraph based on task semantics/constraints) and workflow state management (exploits wGraph structure to manage KV caches, reducing redundant computation).

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods by ~4.95 percentage points on average across five benchmarks, with ~4× memory footprint reduction.

Conclusion: GraphFlow demonstrates that graph-based workflow representation with dynamic instantiation and efficient state management significantly improves both performance and efficiency for LLM agent serving.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents demonstrate strong reasoning and execution capabilities on complex tasks when guided by structured instructions, commonly referred to as workflows. However, existing workflow-assisted agent serving systems typically rely on predefined templates and shallow matching mechanisms, which limit their ability to capture deep semantic relationships and generalize to previously unseen tasks. To address these limitations, we propose a new workflow management paradigm that represents workflows using a unified graph, termed wGraph, where each node corresponds to an atomic operation. wGraph serves as a shared substrate from which task-specific workflows are dynamically instantiated. Building on wGraph primitives, we introduce GraphFlow, a system that efficiently integrates workflows into agent serving through two key designs. First, adaptive workflow generation dynamically constructs workflows from wGraph based on task semantics and constraint requirements. Second, workflow state management exploits wGraph structure to efficiently manage Key-Value (KV) caches, reducing redundant computation during agent serving. Extensive experiments across five benchmark datasets show that GraphFlow consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, yielding an average performance improvement of approximately 4.95 percentage points, while achieving an approximately 4$\times$ reduction in memory footprint.

</details>


### [286] [A Tutorial on Diffusion Theory: From Differential Equations to Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22586)
*Jiayi Fu,Yuxia Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This tutorial presents diffusion models through the lens of differential equations, deriving forward and reverse ODE/SDE formulations, connecting score matching to noise prediction, and unifying DDPM/DDIM under the reverse SDE/ODE framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified and rigorous mathematical understanding of diffusion models from the differential equations perspective, bridging the gap between score-based generative models and denoising diffusion probabilistic models.

Method: The paper derives forward conditional Gaussian processes in both ODE and SDE forms, then marginalizes them to obtain forward transport from data distribution to Gaussian prior. It then derives reverse-time dynamics (reverse SDE and probability-flow ODE) governed by the marginal score function, linking score matching to the standard noise-prediction objective. Sampling methods including DPM-Solver, classifier guidance, and classifier-free guidance are discussed.

Result: The framework shows that DDPM and DDIM share the same training objective; DDPM sampling corresponds to discrete reverse-SDE sampling, while DDIM sampling corresponds to reverse-ODE sampling.

Conclusion: Diffusion models can be fully understood through differential equations, providing a principled foundation for training (score matching/noise prediction) and sampling (SDE vs. ODE discretization, guided sampling) paradigms.

Abstract: This tutorial develops diffusion models from the viewpoint of differential equations. We begin with the conditional Gaussian forward process and show that this path admits both an ordinary differential equation (ODE) representation and a stochastic differential equation (SDE) representation. Averaging the conditional process over the data distribution then yields marginalized forward ODE and SDE formulations that transport the data distribution $p_0=p_{\mathrm{data}}$ to a Gaussian prior $p_1=\mathcal{N}(0,I)$. We next derive the corresponding reverse-time dynamics, namely the reverse SDE and the reverse probability-flow ODE, both of which are governed by the marginal score $\grad\log p_t(x)$. This leads to a training objective for score estimation and shows that the standard noise-prediction objective is equivalent to score matching up to an additive constant independent of the model parameters. We then discuss sampling methods for the learned reverse dynamics, including DPM-Solver, as well as guided sampling through classifier guidance and classifier-free guidance. Finally, we compare DDPM and DDIM with the reverse SDE/ODE framework and show that they share the same training objective, while DDPM sampling corresponds to discrete reverse-SDE sampling and DDIM sampling corresponds to reverse-ODE sampling.

</details>


### [287] [Do Deep Ensembles Actually Capture Uncertainty in Graph Neural Networks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22593)
*Pedro C. Vieira,Pedro Ribeiro,Viacheslav Borovitskiy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep ensembles provide surprisingly little improvement over single models for graph neural networks, mainly due to "epistemic collapse" where independently trained networks converge to overly similar predictions, reducing their ability to capture uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether the widely accepted effectiveness of deep ensembles for uncertainty quantification in deep learning transfers to graph-structured data, specifically message-passing graph neural networks.

Method: Benchmarked standard deep ensembles across seven diverse datasets with varying tasks and complexities, analyzing aleatoric-epistemic decomposition to understand uncertainty sources.

Result: Ensembles provide marginal gains over single models; improvements mainly come from stabilizing optimization noise in point predictions rather than better uncertainty estimates. Epistemic collapse occurs because independently trained networks converge to overly similar predictions.

Conclusion: Deep ensemble success does not seamlessly transfer to graph machine learning; the lack of diversity in predictions neutralizes their key advantage in capturing epistemic uncertainty.

Abstract: While deep ensembles are widely considered to be the default method for uncertainty quantification in deep learning, their effectiveness for graph-structured data is often simply assumed based on successes in domains like computer vision. We investigate standard deep ensembles specifically for message-passing graph neural networks. Benchmarking across seven datasets representing varied tasks and complexities, we reveal that ensembles provide surprisingly little improvement over a single model. Instead, the observed marginal gains stem primarily from stabilizing optimization noise in point predictions rather than yielding meaningfully better uncertainty estimates. Through an aleatoric-epistemic decomposition, we identify epistemic collapse: independently trained networks consistently converge to overly similar predictions. Because disagreement is the fundamental mechanism through which ensembles capture epistemic uncertainty, this lack of diversity neutralizes their key advantage. Analyzing this phenomenon further, we suggest this collapse is driven by functional rather than weight-space convexity, where distinct parameter solutions induce almost identical behavior. Our results suggest that deep ensemble success does not seamlessly transfer to graph machine learning.

</details>


### [288] [Factored Diffusion Policies:Compositionally Generalized Robot Control with a Single Score Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22596)
*Sayan Mitra,Ege Yuceel,Noah Giles,Abhishek Pai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Factored diffusion policies use a single shared diffusion network with per-factor null-token dropout to compose diffusion scores additively across task factors, reducing training data requirements from product to sum of factor cardinalities, with theoretical guarantees and strong results in drone racing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic tasks factor into multiple combinable factors (object, obstacles, color, etc.). Collecting expert demonstrations for every combination grows combinatorially, making it infeasible to cover all factor-value combinations.

Method: A single shared diffusion network trained with per-factor null-token dropout, where the score decomposes additively across factors at inference. A trajectory-tube certificate links the score-level bound through reverse-time sampling ODE and a contracting tracking controller into a closed-loop state-trajectory tube.

Result: On state-based multi-gate drone racing: 90% held-out gate pass rate (matching oracle) vs. 3% for K-network composition baseline. On vision-based single-gate traversal: zero-shot transfer with +11.7pp success-rate gain and 2.4X crash-rate reduction.

Conclusion: Factored diffusion policies dramatically reduce the training data budget while providing theoretical generalization guarantees, outperforming compositional baselines and enabling zero-shot transfer across unseen task variations.

Abstract: Robotic tasks are typically specified by a tuple of factors, such as the object to be grasped, the obstacles to be avoided, the color of the target, and so on. Collecting expert demonstrations for every combination of factor values grows combinatorially. We present factored diffusion policies: a single shared diffusion network trained with per-factor null-token dropout, whose score decomposes additively across factors at inference. Under approximate conditional independence between factors given the action-observation pair, this composition approximates the true joint score with a bounded uniform error, reducing the training-task budget from a product of factor cardinalities to a sum. A trajectory-tube certificate chains this score-level bound through the reverse-time sampling ODE and a contracting tracking controller into a closed-loop state-trajectory tube whose radius factors into an ODE-sensitivity constant and a per-factor score-error budget. Unlike compositional-diffusion methods for control that combine separately trained networks, we use one shared network. Drone racing experiments confirm both the generalization bound and the certificate. On state-based multi-gate racing, the factored policy passes 90% of held-out gates -- matching an oracle -- while a K-network composition baseline collapses to 3%; on vision-based single-gate traversal, it transfers zero-shot to an unseen venue with +11.7pp success-rate gain and 2.4X crash-rate reduction.

</details>


### [289] [Benchmarking Machine Learning Architectures for Antimicrobial Stewardship in Pediatric ICUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22611)
*Niklas Raehse,Luregn J. Schlapbach,Daphné Chopard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper benchmarks machine learning models for predicting antimicrobial stewardship opportunities in pediatric ICUs, finding that simpler tabular models with coarse temporal resolution often outperform complex sequence/graph models in calibration, and that prediction success depends more on target prevalence and dataset characteristics than model complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship in PICUs is critical due to diagnostic uncertainty driving broad-spectrum antibiotic use, increasing antimicrobial resistance and long-term harms. Prior ML work focused on adult populations and static tabular data, leaving pediatric AMS prediction underexplored.

Method: The authors conducted a systematic benchmarking study comparing tabular, sequence-based, and graph-based temporal models at multiple temporal resolutions on a public dataset and a private institutional PICU cohort, using four proxy targets (IV-to-oral switching, de-escalation, discontinuation, short-course therapy) under a unified evaluation framework.

Result: Predictive performance was driven primarily by target prevalence and dataset characteristics, not model complexity. Sequence models improved precision-recall trade-off at coarse (24h) resolution but poorer calibration. Finer temporal modeling offered limited benefit. Multi-task learning produced marginal improvements.

Conclusion: The study highlights the importance of target design, temporal representation, and calibration in clinical ML, recommending simpler tabular models for reliable decision support in pediatric AMS.

Abstract: Antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) is critical in pediatric intensive care units (PICUs), where diagnostic uncertainty often drives broad-spectrum antibiotic use, increasing antimicrobial resistance and potential long-term harms. Machine learning offers a promising approach for identifying patient-level opportunities for stewardship interventions from electronic health record data, yet prior work has focused largely on adult populations and static tabular representations. We present a systematic benchmarking study of AMS intervention prediction in the PICU across a public dataset and a private institutional cohort. We define four clinically relevant proxy targets for reducing antibiotic exposure: intravenous-to-oral switching, de-escalation, discontinuation, and short-course therapy. Under a unified evaluation framework, we compare tabular, sequence-based, and graph-based temporal models at multiple temporal resolutions. We find that predictive performance is driven primarily by target prevalence and dataset characteristics rather than model complexity. Sequence models improve the precision-recall trade-off over tabular approaches at coarse (24-hour) resolution, while finer temporal modeling provides limited additional benefit. However, these gains come at the cost of poorer calibration, with simpler tabular models yielding more reliable probability estimates. Multi-task learning produces only marginal improvements, suggesting limited shared structure across stewardship targets. Our findings highlight the importance of target design, temporal representation, and calibration in clinical machine learning, and provide practical guidance for developing reliable decision support systems for pediatric AMS.

</details>


### [290] [Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization for LLM-Guided Program Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22613)
*Halil Alperen Gozeten,Xuechen Zhang,Emrullah Ildiz,Ege Onur Taga,Tara Javidi,Samet Oymak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization (EMO) for LLM-guided program discovery, proposing a two-stage framework (EMO-STA) that first evolves a shared archive across task families, then adapts to target tasks, showing improved efficiency and generalization over single-task evolution across eight task families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-guided evolutionary search methods optimize each task independently, failing to exploit reusable structure across related tasks, leading to redundant computation and missed opportunities for knowledge sharing.

Method: EMO-STA is a two-stage framework: (1) evolve a shared archive of executable programs across a task family, (2) adapt selected shared candidates to target tasks using strategies like warm-starting, best average, or best per-task adaptation.

Result: EMO-STA outperforms matched-compute single-task evolution across most settings in eight task families; STA Best-Local excels in-distribution, STA Best-Shared generalizes to unseen tasks; balanced shared/adaptation budgets are optimal; shared evolution mitigates overfitting in low-data settings.

Conclusion: Multi-task evolution with shared archives improves both compute efficiency and generalization, especially under limited data, by favoring programs that generalize across tasks rather than exploiting task-specific artifacts.

Abstract: Recent LLM-guided evolutionary search methods have shown that iterative program mutation can discover strong algorithms, but they typically optimize each task independently, even when related tasks share reusable structure. We introduce Evolutionary Multi-Task Optimization (EMO) for LLM-guided program discovery, and propose EMO-STA (Shared-Then-Adapt), a two-stage framework that first evolves a shared archive of executable programs across a task family and then adapts selected shared candidates to each target task. Within EMO-STA, we explore multiple adaptation strategies, including warm-starting from the shared archive, adapting the best average shared program, and adapting the shared program that performs best on each target task. Across eight task families spanning continuous optimization, geometric construction, modeling, and algorithmic optimization, EMO-STA improves over matched-compute single-task evolution in most settings, with STA Best-Local providing the strongest in-distribution adaptation and STA Best-Shared yielding robust transfer to unseen tasks. Compute-allocation experiments show that allocating a substantial fraction of the family-level budget to shared evolution is consistently beneficial, with roughly balanced shared and adaptation budgets often being optimal. Beyond compute efficiency, we show that shared evolution can mitigate overfitting in low-evidence settings (e.g. few training data), including ARC tasks and time-series feature engineering, by favoring programs that generalize across all tasks rather than exploiting task-specific brittle artifacts.

</details>


### [291] [Two is better than one: A Collapse-free Multi-Reward RLIF Training Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22620)
*Shourov Joarder,Diganta Sikdar,Ahsan Habib Akash,Binod Bhattarai,Prashnna Gyawali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RLVR improves LLM reasoning but needs external supervision. RLIF uses internal signals but suffers from reward hacking and entropy collapse. The paper proposes a multi-reward RLIF framework with answer-level (cluster voting) and completion-level (token self-certainty) rewards, plus GDPO normalization and KL-Cov regularization, achieving stable unsupervised reasoning close to supervised methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RLIF methods rely on a single internal reward, leading to reward hacking, entropy collapse, and degraded reasoning structure. The paper aims to address these issues with a multi-reward framework.

Method: Two complementary internal rewards: (1) answer-level reward via cluster voting, (2) completion-level reward via token-wise self-certainty. GDPO-based normalization mitigates reward-scale imbalance. KL-Cov regularization targets low-entropy token distributions to preserve exploration and prevent collapse.

Result: On math reasoning and code-generation benchmarks, the method improves stability and robustness over prior unsupervised RL approaches, achieving performance close to supervised RLVR methods.

Conclusion: Complementary internal rewards with targeted regularization can support stable long-horizon reasoning without external ground-truth supervision.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has substantially improved the reasoning ability of LLMs, but often depends on external supervision from human annotations or gold-standard solutions.
