Reinforcement learning from internal feedback (RLIF) has recently emerged as a scalable unsupervised alternative, using signals extracted from the model itself. However, existing RLIF methods typically rely on a single internal reward, which can lead to reward hacking, entropy collapse, and degraded reasoning structure. We propose a multi-reward RLIF framework that decomposes the training signal into two complementary components: an answer-level reward based on cluster voting and a completion-level reward based on token-wise self-certainty. To combine these signals robustly, we apply GDPO-based normalization to reduce reward-scale imbalance. We further introduce KL-Cov regularization, which targets low-entropy token distributions responsible for disproportionate entropy reduction, preserving exploration and preventing late-stage collapse. Across mathematical reasoning and code-generation benchmarks, our method improves stability and robustness over prior unsupervised RL approaches, while achieving performance close to supervised RLVR methods. These results show that complementary internal rewards, combined with targeted regularization, can support stable long-horizon reasoning without relying on external ground-truth supervision. Code will be released soon.

</details>


### [292] [A note on convergence of Wasserstein policy optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22622)
*David Šiška,Yufei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical note proving linear convergence of Wasserstein Policy Optimization in continuous state-action spaces under entropy-regularised MDPs, using mean-field analysis and log-Sobolev inequalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite WPO's empirical success, its theoretical convergence properties in continuous state/action spaces were not fully established. This paper aims to fill that theoretical gap.

Method: The authors leverage mean-field analysis for convergence of gradient flows via log-Sobolev inequalities. They assume sufficiently regular solutions to the gradient flow equation, demonstrate monotonic energy dissipation, and establish a local log-Sobolev inequality to prove linear convergence.

Result: The value function converges linearly to the global optimum under the entropy-regularised MDP framework.

Conclusion: WPO converges linearly in continuous settings, providing theoretical justification for its empirical performance.

Abstract: Wasserstein Policy Optimization (WPO) is a recently proposed reinforcement learning algorithm that leverages Wasserstein gradient flows to optimize stochastic policies in continuous action spaces. Despite its empirical success, the theoretical convergence properties of WPO in environments with continuous state and action spaces have yet to be fully established. In this note, we argue that WPO within the framework of entropy-regularised Markov Decision Processes converges linearly. This is done by leveraging recent advances in mean-field analysis for convergence of gradient flows using log-Sobole inequalities. Assuming existence of sufficiently regular solution to the gradient flow equation we demonstrate monotonic energy dissipation along the flow and establish a local log-Sobolev inequality. Ultimately, these properties allow us to argue that the value function should converge linearly to the global optimum.

</details>


### [293] [The Double Dilemma in Multi-Task Radiology Report Generation: A Gradient Dynamics Analysis and Solution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22635)
*Erjian Zhang,Yatong Hao,Liejun Wang,Zhiqing Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes CAME-Grad, a plug-and-play optimizer for multi-task radiology report generation that addresses the failure of linear scalarization via conflict-averse direction rectification and magnitude-enhanced energy injection, achieving consistent improvements across eight RRG methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-task RRG methods use coarse linear scalarization strategies that fail to balance discriminative clinical supervision constraints with the smoothness needs of report generation, leading to gradient dynamics issues characterized as a "Double Dilemma" of drift term deviation and diffusion term decay.

Method: CAME-Grad uses (1) conflict-averse direction rectification to ensure geometric validity, (2) magnitude-enhanced energy injection to avoid local optima, and (3) adaptive gradient fusion to balance theoretical optimal direction with task-specific inductive bias.

Result: CAME-Grad achieves average improvements of 2.3% on MIMIC-CXR and 1.9% on IU X-Ray across eight diverse RRG methods, demonstrating consistent and substantial gains as a universal optimizer.

Conclusion: The proposed backbone-agnostic optimizer effectively addresses the multi-task learning imbalance in RRG, offering a simple plug-and-play solution that generalizes well across different architectures.

Abstract: While multi-task learning based automatic radiology report generation (RRG) is widely adopted to ensure clinical consistency, most focus on architectural designs yet remain limited to coarse linear scalarization strategies. These strategies cannot effectively balance the hard constraints of discriminative clinical supervision with the smoothness requirements of report generation. To address these problems, we analyze the failure mechanism of linear scalarization from the perspective of gradient dynamics, utilizing the stochastic differential equation (SDE) framework to characterize it as a "Double Dilemma" of drift term deviation and diffusion term decay. Based on this, we propose a backbone-agnostic optimizer named Conflict-Averse Magnitude-Enhanced Gradient Descent (CAME-Grad). Through conflict-averse direction rectification and magnitude-enhanced energy injection, the algorithm not only ensures geometric validity, but also avoids local optimal solutions. Then, the adaptive gradient fusion mechanism is used to establish a dynamic balance between the theoretical optimal direction and the task-specific inductive bias. Experiments show that as a universal plug-and-play optimizer, CAME-Grad brings substantial and consistent improvements across eight diverse RRG methods, elevating overall clinical efficacy performance by an average of 2.3\% on MIMIC-CXR and 1.9\% on IU X-Ray. Our code is available at https://github.com/vpsg-research/CAME-Grad.

</details>


### [294] [Why SGD is not Brownian Motion: A New Perspective on Stochastic Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22644)
*Igor Ignashin,Anna Radovskaya,Andrew Semenov,Egor Lopatin,Stanislav Potapov,Aleksandr Kovalenko,Andrey Veprikov,Aleksandr Shestakov,Andrey Leonidov,Aleksandr Beznosikov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGD is reformulated as deterministic dynamics in a fluctuating loss landscape, leading to a discrete Fokker-Planck equation distinct from the standard Langevin model. Near flat directions, no stationary distribution exists, and variance grows over time with diffusion proportional to learning rate. Empirical evidence from vision and NLP models confirms this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The standard Langevin approximation of SGD relies on continuous-time limits and sqrt(eta) noise scaling that do not match discrete SGD updates at finite learning rates. This work aims to provide a more accurate formulation of SGD dynamics.

Method: The authors derive a master equation for parameter distribution directly from discrete SGD updates, obtaining a discrete Fokker-Planck equation that differs from the standard Langevin form at O(eta^2). They analyze SGD dynamics near critical points by decomposing behavior along the eigenbasis of the mean Hessian.

Result: Nearly-flat directions exhibit no stationary distribution; variance grows over time, producing effective diffusion with coefficient proportional to learning rate. Empirical results on vision and NLP neural networks confirm qualitative separation between confined and diffusive modes.

Conclusion: The discrete Fokker-Planck framework better captures SGD dynamics than the standard Langevin model, especially in flat regions of the loss landscape, with implications for understanding generalization and optimization in deep learning.

Abstract: Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is commonly modeled as a Langevin process, assuming that minibatch noise acts as Brownian motion. However, this approximation relies on a continuous-time limit and a sqrt(eta) noise scaling that does not match the discrete SGD update at finite learning rate. In this work, we propose an alternative formulation of SGD as deterministic dynamics in a fluctuating loss landscape induced by minibatch sampling. Starting directly from the discrete update, we derive a master equation for the parameter distribution and obtain a discrete Fokker--Planck equation that differs from the standard Langevin form at order eta^2. Using this framework, we analyze SGD dynamics near critical points of the loss. We show that the behavior decomposes along the eigenbasis of the mean Hessian into qualitatively distinct regimes. In particular, nearly-flat directions do not admit a stationary distribution: the variance grows over time, corresponding to effective diffusion along valleys with a coefficient proportional to the learning rate. We provide empirical evidence supporting these predictions on neural network models in computer vision and natural language processing, observing a clear qualitative separation between confined and diffusive modes.

</details>


### [295] [ChronoVAE-HOPE: Beyond Attention -- A Next-Generation VAE Foundation Model for Specialized Time Series Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22684)
*José Alberto Rodríguez,Luis Balderas,Miguel Lastra,Antonio Arauzo-Azofra,José M. Benítez*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ChronoVAE-HOPE is a next-generation Time Series Foundation Model that replaces quadratic attention with a dual-memory system (Titans + CMS) and uses a disentangled VAE to separate trend/seasonal components, achieving strong classification performance on UCR benchmarks after self-supervised pre-training on Monash archive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TSFMs suffer from quadratic attention cost and cannot disentangle structural components (trend vs. seasonality) in time series, limiting their adaptation to specialized classification tasks.

Method: ChronoVAE-HOPE uses a VAE framework with the HOPE Block featuring dual-memory (Titans for short-term, CMS for long-term), a disentangled latent space factorizing trend/seasonal components via separate encoder heads and decoder pathways, and self-supervised pre-training with MTSM + VAE reconstruction loss on Monash archive.

Result: Strong classification performance on UCR benchmark datasets, especially in settings with strict causal structure.

Conclusion: ChronoVAE-HOPE provides a robust, interpretable framework for adapting foundation models to time series classification through structured generative representations.

Abstract: Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) have become a new component of the state-of-the-art in general time series forecasting. However, adapting them to specialized classification tasks remains constrained by two interconnected challenges: the quadratic cost of standard attention mechanisms and the inability to disentangle the structural components underlying time series variability. This technical report introduces ChronoVAE-HOPE, a next-generation TSFM that reconciles massive generalization with structured latent representation for time series classification. The core of the proposal is a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) framework built upon the HOPE Block, which replaces quadratic attention with a dual-memory system: Titans modules for dynamic short-term retention and a Continuum Memory System (CMS) for the abstraction of long-term historical context. A key architectural novelty is the disentangled latent space, which factorizes representations into independent trend and seasonal components via dedicated encoder heads and separate decoder pathways. ChronoVAE-HOPE undergoes self-supervised pre-training on the Monash archive, combining a Masked Time Series Modeling (MTSM) auxiliary objective with a disentangled VAE reconstruction loss. The pre-trained encoder is subsequently frozen and used to generate fixed-length embeddings for downstream classification on the UCR benchmark datasets. Empirical results demonstrate strong performance across diverse temporal domains, particularly in settings characterized by strict causal structure. ChronoVAE-HOPE establishes a robust and interpretable framework for the adaptation of foundation models to time series classification through structured generative representations.

</details>


### [296] [Posterior Collapse as Automatic Spectral Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22691)
*Johannes Hirn*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Posterior collapse in β-VAEs acts as automatic spectral pruning, with latent modes decoupling in order of usefulness based on a β-controlled cutoff, revealing a cascade of collapses that can be predicted via Landau stability analysis and match PCA spectra in the linear Gaussian case.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and characterize posterior collapse in β-VAEs as a structured, interpretable phenomenon rather than a mere training failure.

Method: Landau stability analysis of the β-VAE loss; definition of a latent-rescaling-invariant order parameter to rank active modes; comparison to PCA in the linear Gaussian case.

Result: Collapse thresholds correspond to a utility-ranked pruning; in the linear Gaussian case, the collapse spectrum matches the PCA spectrum, and collapses follow a mean-field law. Predictions are validated on the WorldClim dataset.

Conclusion: Posterior collapse is an interpretable spectral pruning mechanism, and the framework provides a principled way to identify which latent variables to inspect or retain.

Abstract: We show that posterior collapse in $β$-VAEs implements automatic spectral pruning. A latent mode collapses if its contribution to reconstruction is below the cutoff set by $β$. Equilibrium solutions with different $β$ thus reveal a cascade of collapses as latent modes decouple from least to most useful.
  We derive this as a consequence of the loss via a Landau stability analysis. We define a latent-rescaling-invariant order parameter that ranks active latent modes and whose collapse thresholds identify which effective variables to inspect first.
  In the linear Gaussian case, the collapse spectrum, utility spectrum, and normalized PCA spectrum coincide, and each collapse follows a mean-field law. We test these predictions on the WorldClim dataset.

</details>


### [297] [Clipping Bottleneck: Stabilizing RLVR via Stochastic Recovery of Near-Boundary Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22703)
*Shuo Yang,Jinda Lu,Chiyu Ma,Kexin Huang,Haoming Meng,Qihui Zhang,Yuyang Liu,Bolin Ding,Guoyin Wang,Li Yuan,Jingren Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies "hard clipping" in RLVR (GRPO-style) as a bottleneck that discards useful near-boundary signals, and proposes Near-boundary Stochastic Rescue (NSR), a simple stochastic perturbation at the clipping threshold that recovers these signals. NSR improves training stability and performance over baselines like DAPO and GSPO on models up to 30B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR (specifically GRPO-style) methods use hard clipping that discards tokens just beyond the threshold, which may contain informative signals. This rigid clipping causes training instability and suboptimal convergence.

Method: Proposes Near-boundary Stochastic Rescue (NSR): a minimal, plug-and-play modification that stochastically retains slightly out-of-bound tokens to recover lost signals near the clipping boundary.

Result: NSR substantially improves training stability and delivers consistent gains over strong baselines like DAPO and GSPO, validated across 7B-30B models, both dense and MoE architectures.

Conclusion: Simply stochastically rescuing near-boundary signals effectively addresses the hard clipping bottleneck in RLVR, outperforming deterministic gradient decay approaches.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a central paradigm for scaling LLM reasoning, yet its optimization often suffers from training instability and suboptimal convergence. Through a systematic dissection of clipping-based GRPO-style objectives, we identify the rigid clipping decision induced by hard clipping as a key practical bottleneck in the studied RLVR setups. Specifically, our analysis suggests that informative signals can lie in the near-boundary region just beyond the clipping threshold, and are therefore discarded by the standard hard-clipping rule. Notably, once this bottleneck is precisely identified, even simple stochastic perturbations at the boundary can recover meaningful performance gains. Building on this finding, we propose Near-boundary Stochastic Rescue (NSR), a minimal, plug-and-play modification that stochastically retains these slightly out-of-bound tokens to recover lost signals. While NSR, via stochastic sampling, can be interpreted as inducing an implicit gradient decay in expectation, our ablations reveal that its stochastic, boundary-local rescue mechanism is consistently more effective than deterministic gradient decay. Validated by extensive experiments across model sizes from 7B to 30B and both dense and MoE architectures, as a plug-and-play solution, NSR substantially improves training stability and delivers consistent gains over strong baselines such as DAPO and GSPO.

</details>


### [298] [Abstraction for Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22711)
*Clarisse Wibault,Alexander Goldie,Antonio Villares,Maike Osborne,Jakob Foerster*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces relativised options and hierarchical representations to enable absolute abstraction in offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning, improving performance through experience reuse across similar contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MDPs exhibit redundancy due to symmetries and shared structure across state-goal pairs in GCRL; existing work focuses on temporal abstraction via hierarchy, but the potential for absolute abstraction through hierarchy is underexplored.

Method: The authors introduce relativised options and distinct representations for different hierarchy levels, enabling agents to reuse experience across similar contexts. Two simple algorithms are proposed for learning relativised options and abstracting from the absolute frame of reference.

Result: The proposed inductive biases (relativised options and hierarchical abstraction) significantly improve performance in offline GCRL tasks.

Conclusion: Hierarchy not only provides temporal abstraction (horizon reduction) but also enables absolute abstraction, allowing better generalization and experience reuse in offline GCRL.

Abstract: Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) often exhibit significant redundancy due to symmetries and shared structure across state-goal pairs in real-world Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (GCRL). While hierarchical policies have been motivated for horizon reduction via temporal abstraction in offline GCRL, we demonstrate that hierarchy also enables absolute abstraction. By introducing relativised options as well as distinct representations for different levels of the hierarchy, we demonstrate how an agent can reuse experience across similar contexts of the state-space. Based on this framework, we introduce two simple algorithms for learning relativised options and abstracting from the absolute frame of reference. Our experiments show that such inductive biases significantly improve performance in offline GCRL.

</details>


### [299] [Reading Task Failure Off the Activations: A Sparse-Feature Audit of GPT-2 Small on Indirect Object Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22719)
*Mahdi Nasermoghadasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper audits which sparse-autoencoder (SAE) features of GPT-2 small fire differently on failed vs. successful trials of the IOI task. While feature 17,491 (d=+2.93, "cryptographic keys") strongly correlates with failure on "the keys" prompts, causal ablation and robustness checks show it is not a sufficient cause. The main contribution is the audit pipeline, not any single feature.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify which sparse-autoencoder features correlate with GPT-2 small's failures on the IOI task, and to determine whether these correlations reflect causal mechanisms or mere spurious patterns.

Method: 1) Run GPT-2 small on 300 IOI prompts; 2) Compare SAE feature activations (24,576 features, layer-8 residual-stream) between failed/successful trials using significance (Holm-corrected) and effect size (Cohen's d); 3) Apply three controls: causal ablation, representation baseline (logistic regression), and seed-robustness check.

Result: 146 features clear significance threshold; 105 reach large effect size. Feature 17,491 ("cryptographic keys") is the strongest correlate (d=+2.93), but ablation does not restore accuracy (6.7%→4.4%). Logistic regression matches SAE features in predictive power (ROC AUC ~0.93). The "keys" failure is seed-robust, but feature 17,491 is top correlate in only 1/5 seeds.

Conclusion: The methodological contribution is the audit pipeline (cheap, model-agnostic, surfaces named correlates) rather than any specific feature. The work demonstrates that strong correlational signals in SAE features can fail causal and robustness tests, highlighting the need for such controls.

Abstract: We report a small, reproducible audit of which sparse-autoencoder (SAE) features of GPT-2 small fire differently on failed versus successful trials of the Indirect Object Identification (IOI) task. On 300 prompts, GPT-2 small reaches 79.7% accuracy; 146 of the 24,576 features in the layer-8 residual-stream SAE release of Bloom (2024) clear a Holm-corrected significance threshold and 105 reach a large effect size (|Cohen's d| > 0.8). The strongest single correlate of failure -- feature 17,491, d=+2.93, Neuronpedia label 'cryptographic keys' -- is essentially silent except when the prompt's transferred object is 'the keys,' on which GPT-2 small fails 93.3% of the time vs. 7.5% on the other seven objects (Fisher exact p = 8.79 x 10^-33). We put this correlate through three controls that a mechanistic claim should pass. (i) A causal ablation: zeroing feature 17,491 in the residual stream across all token positions of the 45 keys prompts does not restore accuracy (6.7% -> 4.4%); the feature is a correlate, not a sufficient cause at this layer. (ii) A representation baseline: a logistic regression on the raw 768-dimensional residual stream reaches 5-fold ROC AUC = 0.929, matching the top-100 SAE features (0.927); the SAE basis adds interpretability, not predictive power. (iii) A seed-robustness check: across five random seeds the keys-subset failure rate stays in 75.0--93.3% (the behavioural effect is real), but feature 17,491 is the top-|d| feature in only 1 of 5 runs. The methodological contribution is therefore the audit pipeline (cheap, model-agnostic, surfaces named correlates) rather than any single feature found through it. We release the code, the 300-prompt corpus, the 300x24,576 activation matrix, the ablation and baseline scripts, and the figures. The full pipeline runs on a laptop (Apple M3 Max, no discrete GPU).

</details>


### [300] [Multiple Neural Operators Achieve Near-Optimal Rates for Multi-Task Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22724)
*Adrien Weihs,Hayden Schaeffer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes multi-task operator learning using the Multiple Neural Operators (MNO) architecture, showing that shared representations across tasks do not increase overall learning cost compared to single-task learning, and that MNO and multi-task DeepONet have similar asymptotic approximation rates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether sharing representations across multiple operator learning tasks increases or preserves the approximation and statistical complexity, and to establish tight bounds for multi-task operator learning.

Method: The authors derive near-optimal upper bounds for approximation and generalization of Lipschitz multiple operator maps, establish lower bounds and minimax rates, and compare MNO with a multi-task DeepONet extension.

Result: Shared representations across tasks do not increase overall cost (same scaling laws as single operator learning). Both MNO and multi-task DeepONet achieve essentially the same asymptotic approximation rates in the worst case.

Conclusion: Multi-task operator learning is no more costly than single-task learning under the studied framework, and both MNO and DeepONet-based architectures perform comparably asymptotically.

Abstract: We study the approximation and statistical complexity of learning collections of operators in a shared multi-task setting, with a focus on the Multiple Neural Operators (MNO) architecture. For broad classes of Lipschitz multiple operator maps, we derive near-optimal upper bounds for approximation and statistical generalization. On the lower-bound side, we establish a curse of parametric complexity and prove corresponding minimax rates. Together, these results show that shared representations across tasks do not increase the overall cost: multi-task operator learning follows the same scaling laws as single operator learning. We also compare MNO with a multi-task extension of DeepONet based on concatenated task inputs and show that, from a worst-case approximation-complexity perspective, both architectures satisfy essentially the same asymptotic rates.

</details>


### [301] [Post-Training is About States, Not Tokens: A State Distribution View of SFT, RL, and On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22731)
*Dong Nie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper argues that the state distribution (i.e., the prompts and generated prefixes) on which supervision is applied during post-training is as important as the loss function itself. Through controlled experiments with Qwen3-0.6B-Base, they show that on-policy methods (RL and on-policy distillation) outperform off-policy SFT in both task improvement and retention, supporting a state-centric view of post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing analyses of post-training methods (SFT, RL, distillation) focus primarily on loss function differences (maximum likelihood, policy gradients, KL divergences). The authors argue that a complementary and understudied factor—the state distribution on which supervision is applied—deserves systematic investigation.

Method: The authors formalize post-training as state-distribution shaping and conduct controlled small-scale experiments using Qwen3-0.6B-Base on GSM8K, with TruthfulQA and MMLU as retention evaluations. They compare SFT (fixed dataset states), on-policy distillation (OPD) from a degraded SFT teacher, and lightweight on-policy RL.

Result: Three key findings: (1) mild SFT improves GSM8K with little forgetting, but stress SFT causes substantial retention loss; (2) OPD from a degraded SFT teacher surpasses that teacher on all three benchmarks; (3) lightweight on-policy RL improves GSM8K while preserving retention.

Conclusion: The source and locality of training states can be as important as the form of the supervision signal, supporting a state-centric view of post-training.

Abstract: Large language model post-training methods such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reinforcement learning (RL), and distillation are often analyzed through their loss functions: maximum likelihood, policy gradients, forward KL, reverse KL, or related objective-level variants. We study a complementary factor: the state distribution on which supervision is applied. For an autoregressive policy, a state is a prompt plus generated prefix. SFT trains on fixed dataset states, while RL and on-policy distillation (OPD) train on states induced by the current learner. We formalize post-training as state-distribution shaping and run a controlled smallscale study using Qwen3-0.6B-Base on GSM8K, with TruthfulQA and MMLU as retention evaluations. Our results show three phenomena. First, a mild SFT run improves GSM8K with little forgetting, while a stress SFT run causes substantial retention loss. Second, OPD from a degraded SFT teacher surpasses that teacher on GSM8K, TruthfulQA, and MMLU, despite using the teacher as its only supervision source. Third, a lightweight on-policy RL run improves GSM8K while preserving retention. These results support a state-centric view of post-training: the source and locality of training states can be as important as the form of the supervision signal.

</details>


### [302] [The Distillation Game: Adaptive Attacks & Efficient Defenses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22737)
*Youssef Allouah,Mahdi Haghifam,Sanmi Koyejo,Reza Shokri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper studies the trade-off between model usefulness and vulnerability to distillation attacks via a minimax game, proposing Product-of-Experts (PoE) defense and showing that adaptive students recover more capability than passive evaluation suggests, narrowing the gap between expensive defenses and simpler ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distillation attacks create a deployment trade-off where model outputs that increase utility also make imitation easier; existing evaluations may underestimate the true threat from adaptive adversaries.

Method: Formulates a minimax game between a utility-constrained teacher and an adaptive student; derives one-sided response rules including adaptive evaluation (student reweights high-value examples) and a teacher-side defense template (suppressing outputs useful for distillation). Proposes Product-of-Experts (PoE), a forward-pass-only defense combining teacher with proxy student during generation.

Result: Adaptive evaluation reveals a large passive-adaptive gap: on state-of-the-art defenses, adaptive students recover substantially more capability on GSM8K and MATH. The robustness gap between expensive defenses and PoE narrows considerably under adaptive evaluation, while PoE remains cheaper and preserves higher-quality reasoning traces.

Conclusion: Strong distillation remains difficult to stop; progress on antidistillation should be judged against adaptive students rather than passive ones.

Abstract: Distillation attacks create a deployment trade-off for model providers: the same outputs that make a model more useful can also make it easier to imitate. We study this trade-off through a minimax game between a utility-constrained teacher and an adaptive student. Our framework yields tractable one-sided response rules: an adaptive evaluation rule in which the student reweights high-value examples, and a teacher-side defense template that suppresses outputs most useful for distillation. From a cheap proxy for example value, we derive Product-of-Experts (PoE), a simple forward-pass-only defense that combines the teacher with a proxy student during generation. Empirically, adaptive evaluation reveals a large passive--adaptive gap: on state-of-the-art defenses, adaptive students recover substantially more capability than passive evaluation suggests on GSM8K and MATH. Under this stronger evaluation, the apparent robustness gap between expensive defenses and PoE narrows considerably, while PoE remains substantially cheaper and preserves higher-quality reasoning traces. Overall, our results suggest that strong distillation remains difficult to stop, and that progress on antidistillation should be judged against adaptive students rather than passive ones. Our code is available at: https://github.com/ysfalh/distillation-game.

</details>


### [303] [Proxy-Based Approximation of Shapley and Banzhaf Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22738)
*Santo M. A. R. Thies,Hubert Baniecki,R. Teal Witter,Eyke Hüllermeier,Maximilian Muschalik,Fabian Fumagalli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProxySHAP introduces a method that combines tree-based proxy models with residual correction to efficiently and accurately estimate Shapley and Banzhaf interaction indices, achieving state-of-the-art performance with polynomial-time computation and superior approximation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing estimators for higher-order Shapley/Banzhaf interactions face a speed-accuracy trade-off, limiting their practical utility in modern machine learning applications.

Method: ProxySHAP reconciles tree-based proxy models (for sample efficiency) with residual correction (for consistency). It generalizes interventional TreeSHAP to compute exact interaction indices for tree ensembles in polynomial time, and uses Maximum Sample Reuse (MSR) to correct proxy bias without exponential variance scaling.

Result: ProxySHAP achieves the lowest error across both small- and large-budget regimes, significantly outperforms ProxySPEX and KernelSHAP-IQ, and excels on downstream explainability tasks in large-scale applications with thousands of features.

Conclusion: ProxySHAP sets a new state-of-the-art for approximating interaction indices, effectively bridging speed and accuracy while providing theoretical guarantees on consistency and variance.

Abstract: Shapley and Banzhaf interactions capture the complex dynamics inherent in modern machine learning applications. However, current estimators for these higher-order interactions trade off between speed and accuracy. To overcome this limitation, we introduce ProxySHAP. ProxySHAP reconciles the high sample efficiency of tree-based proxy models with a principled path to consistency via residual correction. On a theoretical level, we derive a polynomial-time generalization of interventional TreeSHAP to compute exact interaction indices for tree ensembles, successfully bypassing exponential tree-depth dependencies in prior methods. Furthermore, we formally analyze the residual adjustment strategy, characterizing the specific conditions under which Maximum Sample Reuse (MSR) corrects proxy bias without its variance scaling exponentially with interaction size. Extensive benchmarking demonstrates that ProxySHAP sets a new state-of-the-art standard for approximation quality, including in large-scale applications with thousands of features. By achieving the lowest error in both small- and large-budget regimes, ProxySHAP significantly outperforms the prior best estimators ProxySPEX and KernelSHAP-IQ, while also delivering superior performance on downstream explainability tasks.

</details>


### [304] [Ternary Decision Trees with Locally-Adaptive Uncertainty Zones](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22740)
*William Smits*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces ternary decision trees that add an uncertainty zone around each split threshold, where instances receive blended predictions and are flagged as boundary-uncertain. Five methods for estimating the uncertainty zone width are proposed and evaluated, with the margin method achieving the best efficiency and performance across 72 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard decision trees use hard binary splits that assign identical confidence to all instances regardless of their proximity to decision boundaries, failing to distinguish between clear and ambiguous cases.

Method: Ternary decision trees augment split nodes with an uncertainty zone (half-width delta) around the optimal threshold. Five delta-estimation methods are proposed: quality-plateau, class-overlap, gain-ratio, node-bootstrap, and margin. Instances falling in the uncertainty zone receive blended predictions from both subtrees.

Result: All five methods with probabilistic routing significantly outperform standard CART on decided accuracy (p<0.001 across 72 datasets). Margin method achieves best efficiency (0.104 accuracy gain per flagging rate), winning on 42/72 datasets. On mammography, node-bootstrap achieves +0.71% decided accuracy by flagging 10.8% cases.

Conclusion: Ternary decision trees improve upon standard decision trees by providing calibrated uncertainty estimates without external noise specification. The margin method is recommended as a hyperparameter-free default, with node-bootstrap and quality-plateau best suited for tracking irreducible error on clean data.

Abstract: Decision trees partition the feature space using hard binary thresholds, assigning identical confidence to instances far from a decision boundary and to those directly on it. We introduce ternary decision trees, which augment each split node with an uncertainty zone of half-width delta centered on the optimal threshold. Instances in this zone receive predictions formed by weighted blending of both child subtrees and are flagged as boundary-uncertain, signaling that downstream applications may treat these predictions differently. Crucially, delta is computed locally at each node from statistics already available during standard CART split finding, requiring no external noise specification. We propose and evaluate five delta-estimation methods: quality-plateau (plateau width of the split criterion curve), class-overlap (empirical class-distribution overlap), gain-ratio (split quality relative to split entropy), node-bootstrap (threshold variance under node-level resampling), and margin (SVM-inspired distance to the nearest cross-class training example). Evaluated across 72 OpenML-CC18 datasets with 5-fold cross-validation, all five methods with probabilistic routing significantly outperform standard CART on decided accuracy (Wilcoxon signed-rank, p < 0.001). The margin method achieves the best efficiency (0.104 accuracy gain per unit of boundary-uncertain flagging rate), wins on 42 of 72 datasets, and requires zero additional hyperparameters. Analysis on three Breiman synthetic benchmarks reveals that margin is self-calibrating on clean data while node-bootstrap and quality-plateau best track theoretical irreducible error. Experiments on four medical and financial datasets demonstrate practical value: on mammography, node-bootstrap achieves +0.71% decided accuracy by flagging 10.8% of screening cases as boundary-uncertain.

</details>


### [305] [Cyber-Physical Anomaly Detection in IoT-Enabled Smart Grids Using Machine Learning and Metaheuristic Feature Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22749)
*Adis Alihodžić,Eva Tuba,Milan Tuba*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper uses machine learning with genetic algorithm-based feature selection on the MSU/ORNL dataset to distinguish cyber-physical attacks from natural events in smart grids. The Extra Trees model combined with GA reduces PMU features from 112 to ~27 while improving macro-F1 (0.9118→0.9212) and ROC-AUC (0.9791→0.9837), showing that many electrical measurements are redundant.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern smart grids face increased vulnerability to both physical incidents and cyber attacks. Operators need reliable methods to distinguish between natural events and malicious actions, motivating the need for accurate, efficient classification using limited measurement data.

Method: The approach combines machine learning classifiers (logistic regression, RBF-SVM, XGBoost, Random Forest, Extra Trees) with genetic algorithm (GA)-based feature selection, applied to the MSU/ORNL Power System Attack Dataset. Feature space is reduced from 112 PMU attributes to an average of 27.4.

Result: Tree-based ensemble models performed best. The GA + Extra Trees model reduced features from 112 to ~27.4, increasing macro-F1 from 0.9118 to 0.9212 and ROC-AUC from 0.9791 to 0.9837, demonstrating that many PMU measurements are redundant.

Conclusion: A compact subset of phasor-based features can provide accurate and interpretable anomaly detection in smart grids, and many synchronized electrical measurements are redundant for classification purposes.

Abstract: Modern smart grids rely on dense measurement infrastructures, communication links, and intelligent field devices. Although this improves supervision and control, it also increases vulnerability to cyber-physical disruptions. Operators must distinguish physical incidents, such as faults or line disturbances, from malicious actions, such as false data injection or unauthorized command execution. This chapter investigates this problem using the well-known MSU/ORNL Power System Attack Dataset. The proposed method combines machine learning with genetic-algorithm-based feature selection. The objective is twofold: to classify attack and natural events accurately, and to determine whether a reduced set of physically informative PMU/IED measurements can support reliable detection. Several baseline models are evaluated, including logistic regression, RBF-SVM, XGBoost, Random Forest, and Extra Trees. The results show that tree-based ensemble models are the most effective for the considered dataset, with Extra Trees providing the strongest full-feature baseline. After feature selection, the GA + Extra Trees model reduces the clean PMU feature space from 112 attributes to an average of 27.4 attributes over five runs, while increasing macro-F1 from 0.9118 to 0.9212 and ROC-AUC from 0.9791 to 0.9837. These results indicate that many synchronized electrical measurements are redundant. A compact subset of phasor-based features can still provide accurate and interpretable anomaly detection in smart grids.

</details>


### [306] [SeqLoRA: Bilevel Orthogonal Adaptation for Continual Multi-Concept Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22743)
*Javad Parsa,Enis Simsar,Amir Joudaki,Thomas Hofmann,André M. H. Teixeira*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SeqLoRA is a constrained continual learning framework using bilevel optimization to jointly learn both LoRA factors, enabling effective multi-concept composition in text-to-image diffusion models without expensive post-hoc fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning allows fast personalization of diffusion models, but composing multiple custom concepts leads to representation interference. Existing methods either require costly post-hoc fusion or use frozen adaptation subspaces that limit expressiveness and concept fidelity.

Method: SeqLoRA employs a constrained continual learning framework with bilevel optimization to jointly optimize both LoRA factors. The authors establish convergence guarantees, model residual activations as matrix sub-Gaussian processes for forgetting bounds, and prove learning the LoRA basis from data minimizes residual interference energy.

Result: SeqLoRA improves identity preservation and scalability across up to 101 concepts, avoids costly fusion, and reduces attribute interference in composed generations.

Conclusion: SeqLoRA effectively addresses the trade-off between expressiveness and concept fidelity in multi-concept composition by jointly learning LoRA factors through bilevel optimization, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical results.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning enables fast personalization of text-to-image diffusion models, but composing multiple custom concepts remains challenging due to representation interference. Existing modular methods either rely on expensive post-hoc fusion or freeze adaptation subspaces, which limit expressiveness and concept fidelity. To address this trade-off, we propose Sequential regularized LoRA (SeqLoRA), a constrained continual learning framework that jointly optimizes both LoRA factors via bilevel optimization. Theoretically, we establish strong convergence guarantees for our algorithm and model the residual layer activations as a matrix sub-Gaussian process to derive high-probability bounds on catastrophic forgetting. We further prove that learning the LoRA basis from data minimizes residual interference energy more effectively than frozen-basis methods. Experiments on multi-concept image generation demonstrate that SeqLoRA improves identity preservation and scalability across up to 101 concepts, while avoiding costly fusion and reducing attribute interference in composed generations.

</details>


### [307] [CogAdapt: Transferring Clinical ECG Foundation Models to Wearable Cognitive Load Assessment via Lead Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22774)
*Amir Mousavi,Mohammad Sadegh Sirjani,Erfan Nourbakhsh,Mimi Xie,Rocky Slavin,Leslie Neely,John Davis,John Quarles*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CogAdapt adapts clinical ECG foundation models to wearable cognitive load assessment using LeadBridge adapter (3-lead to 12-lead) and ProFine progressive fine-tuning, achieving macro-F1 of 0.626 and 0.768 on two datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time cognitive load assessment is needed for adaptive HCI but faces challenges of limited labeled data and poor cross-subject generalization, while existing ECG foundation models cannot be directly applied to wearables due to sensor configuration mismatch and task differences.

Method: CogAdapt consists of LeadBridge (a learnable adapter converting 3-lead wearable signals to 12-lead representations) and ProFine (progressive fine-tuning that gradually unfreezes encoder layers while preventing catastrophic forgetting).

Result: Evaluated on CLARE and CL-Drive datasets with leave-one-subject-out cross-validation, CogAdapt achieves macro-F1 scores of 0.626 and 0.768, substantially outperforming baselines trained from scratch.

Conclusion: Foundation model adaptation is promising for subject-independent cognitive load assessment from wearable sensors.

Abstract: Real-time cognitive load assessment is essential for adaptive human-computer interaction but remains challenging due to limited labeled data and poor cross-subject generalization. Recent ECG foundation models pre-trained on millions of clinical recordings offer rich representations, but cannot be directly applied to wearable devices due to sensor configuration mismatch and task differences. In this paper, we propose CogAdapt, a framework that adapts clinical ECG foundation models to wearable cognitive load assessment. CogAdapt introduces LeadBridge, a learnable adapter that transforms 3-lead wearable signals into anatomically consistent 12-lead representations, and ProFine, a progressive fine-tuning strategy that gradually unfreezes encoder layers while preventing catastrophic forgetting. Evaluations on two public datasets (CLARE and CL-Drive) under leave-one-subject-out cross-validation show that CogAdapt substantially outperforms baselines trained from scratch, achieving macro-F1 scores of 0.626 and 0.768. These results demonstrate the promise of foundation model adaptation for subject-independent cognitive load assessment from wearable sensors.

</details>


### [308] [Plug-in Losses for Evidential Deep Learning: A Simplified Framework for Uncertainty Estimation that Includes the Softmax Classifier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22746)
*Berk Hayta,Hannah Laus,Simon Mittermaier,Felix Krahmer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes simplified loss approximations for Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) that are easier to implement, showing that the approximation error decays with growing evidence, and validates on speech commands data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EDL provides single-pass uncertainty estimation via Dirichlet distributions, but its objectives are more complex than standard losses, complicating analysis and implementation. The authors aim to simplify EDL objectives.

Method: The authors approximate the first-order EDL risk minimization objective using a plug-in loss evaluated at the Dirichlet mean. They prove the approximation error decays with growing evidence under mild assumptions for loss functions like MSE and cross-entropy.

Result: The simplified objectives achieve comparable predictive accuracy and selective prediction performance to classical EDL on the Google Speech Commands dataset, while being simpler to implement. This is the first coverage-accuracy trade-off analysis for speech recognition via EDL.

Conclusion: The plug-in loss approximation for EDL is theoretically justified (error decays with evidence) and practically effective, providing a simpler pathway to uncertainty estimation that includes standard softmax as a special case.

Abstract: Real-world sensor-based learning systems require uncertainty estimation that is both reliable and computationally efficient. Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) provides single-pass uncertainty estimation by modeling the class probabilities via Dirichlet distributions, where the Dirichlet parameters are predicted by a learned neural network mapping. However, this approach can lead to computational challenges, as Dirichlet expected objectives are more complex than standard supervised learning losses, complicating their analysis and implementation. We address this issue by approximating the objective of the first-order empirical risk minimization problem induced by EDL with a plug-in loss evaluated at the Dirichlet mean and show that, under mild assumptions, the approximation error decays with growing evidence for a broad class of loss functions, including mean-squared error and cross-entropy loss. As a special case, our analysis provides justification for the use of softmax in the context of uncertainty estimation, since under a particular evidence-to-Dirichlet mapping, our framework includes the standard softmax classifier. We validate the proposed simplified objectives on the Google Speech Commands dataset and show that they achieve predictive accuracy and selective prediction performance comparable to classical EDL, while being simpler to implement using standard deep learning losses and training pipelines. To the best of our knowledge, this empirical analysis is the first to obtain coverage-accuracy trade-offs for speech recognition tasks through EDL.

</details>


### [309] [MambaGaze: Bidirectional Mamba with Explicit Missing Data Modeling for Cognitive Load Assessment from Eye-Gaze Tracking Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22775)
*Amir Mousavi,Mohammad Sadegh Sirjani,Erfan Nourbakhsh,Mimi Xie,Rocky Slavin,Leslie Neely,John Davis,John Quarles*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MambaGaze is a framework for real-time cognitive load assessment from eye-tracking data that handles missing data (blinks/tracking failures) via XMD encoding and captures long-range dependencies efficiently using bidirectional Mamba-2, achieving 73-76% accuracy (outperforming baselines by 4-12%) and real-time edge deployment at 43-68 FPS under 7.5W.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable adaptive human-centered AI for safety-critical applications (e.g., driver vigilance monitoring, flight deck assistance) by addressing two key challenges in real-time cognitive load assessment from eye-tracking: frequent data missingness (blinks/tracking failures) and efficient modeling of long-range temporal dependencies.

Method: 1) XMD encoding: augments raw features with observation masks and time-deltas to explicitly model data uncertainty. 2) Bidirectional Mamba-2: captures temporal dependencies with linear computational complexity. Evaluated via leave-one-subject-out on CLARE and CL-Drive datasets; deployed on NVIDIA Jetson platforms.

Result: 76.8% accuracy on CLARE; 73.1% on CL-Drive. Outperforms CNN, Transformer, ResNet, VGG baselines by 4-12 percentage points. Edge deployment: 43-68 FPS, power consumption below 7.5W.

Conclusion: MambaGaze is feasible for wearable real-time cognitive load monitoring, offering both accuracy and efficiency for safety-critical applications.

Abstract: Real-time cognitive load assessment from eye-tracking signals could potentially enable adaptive human-centered-AI such as safety-critical applications such as driver vigilance monitoring or automated flight deck assistance, yet two challenges persist: handling frequent data missingness from blinks and tracking failures, and efficiently modeling long-range temporal dependencies. We propose MambaGaze, a framework that addresses these challenges through 1) XMD encoding, which augments raw features with observation masks and time-deltas to explicitly model data uncertainty, and 2) bidirectional Mamba-2, which captures temporal dependencies with linear computational complexity. Experiments on CLARE and CL-Drive datasets under leave-one-subject-out evaluation show that MambaGaze achieves 76.8% and 73.1% accuracy, respectively, outperforming CNN, Transformer, ResNet, and VGG baselines by 4-12 percentage points. Edge deployment benchmarks on NVIDIA Jetson platforms demonstrate real-time inference at 43-68 FPS with power consumption below 7.5W, confirming feasibility for wearable cognitive load monitoring.

</details>


### [310] [SDPM: Survival Diffusion Probabilistic Model for Continuous-Time Survival Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22776)
*Stanislav R. Kirpichenko,Andrei V. Konstantinov,Lev V. Utkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDPM is a generative diffusion model for continuous-time survival analysis that avoids parametric assumptions and time discretization, achieving competitive predictive performance on real datasets and accurately recovering continuous survival distributions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing survival analysis methods often impose structural assumptions on the hazard function or discretize time, limiting flexibility and introducing approximation errors. The authors aim to develop a more flexible, continuous-time approach.

Method: SDPM models the conditional distribution P(T, δ | x) using a denoising diffusion model. It operates in a transformed target space with standardized log-times and a continuous Gaussian-mixture representation of the censoring indicator. Under conditionally independent censoring, generated samples are transformed into survival function estimates via the Kaplan-Meier estimator.

Result: SDPM achieves competitive predictive performance across C-index, integrated time-dependent AUC, and integrated Brier score on ten real datasets. On synthetic Cox-Weibull data, it recovers the underlying continuous survival distribution more accurately than a strong nonparametric baseline when sufficient samples are generated. Ablation study confirms the importance of target-space transformations.

Conclusion: SDPM is a flexible, generative approach to continuous-time survival analysis that avoids parametric assumptions and discretization errors, demonstrating strong predictive performance and accurate distribution recovery.

Abstract: Survival analysis aims to estimate a time-to-event distribution from data with censored observations. Many existing methods either impose structural assumptions on the hazard function or discretize the time axis, which may limit flexibility and introduce approximation errors. We propose the Survival Diffusion Probabilistic Model (SDPM), a generative approach to continuous-time survival analysis. SDPM models the conditional distribution of the survival outcome, represented by the pair of observed time and censoring indicator, $\mathbb{P}(T,δ\mid \mathbf{x})$, using a denoising diffusion model. Under the assumption of conditionally independent censoring, conditional samples generated by the model can be transformed into survival function estimates using the Kaplan-Meier estimator. This formulation avoids parametric assumptions on the event-time distribution and does not require a discretization of the output time space. The model operates in a transformed target space, using standardized log-times and a continuous Gaussian-mixture representation of the censoring indicator. We evaluate SDPM on ten real survival datasets and compare it with five strong baselines, including tree-based, boosting-based, and neural survival models. Results show that SDPM achieves competitive predictive performance across C-index, integrated time-dependent AUC, and integrated Brier score. A study on synthetic Cox-Weibull data demonstrates that SDPM can recover the shape of an underlying continuous survival distribution more accurately than a strong nonparametric baseline when sufficiently many samples are generated. An ablation study confirms the importance of the proposed target-space transformations, which improve event-rate calibration, reduce invalid generated times, and provide consistent gains in predictive discrimination. Codes implementing the proposed model are publicly available.

</details>


### [311] [Lumberjack: Better Differentially Private Random Forests through Heavy Hitter Detection in Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22756)
*Christian Janos Lebeda,David Erb,Tudor Cebere,Aurélien Bellet*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lumberjack is a differentially private random forest algorithm that constructs large trees and applies privacy-preserving pruning using a novel heavy hitter detection method with $O(\sqrt{\log h})$ error, achieving state-of-the-art utility on benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing differentially private random forests suffer from poor utility, making them impractical for sensitive tabular data applications. The authors aim to close the utility gap between DP and non-DP random forests.

Method: Lumberjack builds large random decision trees, then applies aggressive privacy-preserving pruning to retain only sufficiently populated nodes. It uses a novel $(\varepsilon,δ)$-DP heavy hitter detection algorithm for hierarchical data with error $O_{\varepsilon,δ}(\sqrt{\log h})$ for trees of height $h$.

Result: Lumberjack consistently outperforms prior DP random forest methods on benchmark datasets, establishing a new state of the art with substantial improvements in the privacy-utility trade-off for practical privacy budgets.

Conclusion: Carefully designed DP random forests can close much of the utility gap, suggesting a promising and underexplored direction for future research in privacy-preserving machine learning.

Abstract: Random forests are widely used in fields involving sensitive tabular data, but existing approaches to enforcing differential privacy (DP) typically degrade performance to the point of impracticality. In this paper, we introduce Lumberjack, a differentially private random forest algorithm that achieves substantially higher utility by constructing large random decision trees and then applying aggressive, privacy-preserving pruning to retain only sufficiently populated nodes. A key component of our approach is a novel $(\varepsilon,δ)$-DP heavy hitter detection algorithm for hierarchical data, whose error is $O_{\varepsilon,δ}(\sqrt{\log h})$ for trees of height $h$ and may be of independent interest. This favorable scaling enables the use of significantly deeper trees than in prior work, leading to improved expressiveness under privacy constraints. Our empirical evaluation on benchmark datasets shows that Lumberjack consistently outperforms prior DP random forest methods, establishing a new state of the art. In particular, our approach yields substantial improvements in the privacy-utility trade-off for practical privacy budgets. Our findings suggest that carefully designed DP random forests can close much of the utility gap, highlighting a promising and underexplored direction for future research.

</details>


### [312] [The Matching Principle: A Geometric Theory of Loss Functions for Nuisance-Robust Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22800)
*Vishal Rajput*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper unifies diverse ML robustness methods (adversarial training, domain adaptation, IRM, augmentation, etc.) as estimators of a single object: the covariance of label-preserving deployment nuisance. It provides closed-form optimality in linear-Gaussian models, introduces a Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI) for probing embedding sensitivity, and validates the theory with 13 pre-registered experiments from classical ML to 7B-scale LLMs, with 12 passing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robustness, domain adaptation, photometric invariance, compositional generalization, temporal robustness, alignment safety, and anisotropic regularization are treated as separate problems with separate methods. The paper argues they share one underlying statistical problem: estimating the covariance of label-preserving deployment nuisance.

Method: Proposes the "matching principle": regularize the encoder Jacobian along a matrix whose range covers the deployment nuisance covariance. Provides closed-form optimality proofs in linear-Gaussian models (Theorem A), necessity of range coverage (Theorem G), and consistency lemmas. Introduces TDI as a label-free probe for embedding sensitivity.

Result: 13 pre-registered experiments from classical ML through Qwen2.5-7B test predicted ordering (matched > isotropic > wrong-W). 12 pass; the sole exception (Office-31) was an eigengap failure predicted beforehand. At 7B scale, matched style-PMH improves selective honesty and preserves Style TDI where DPO degrades it.

Conclusion: The key contribution is naming the deployment nuisance covariance, specifying what the regularizer must do, and providing a closed-form falsifiable theory once that object is identified—not claiming universal leaderboard performance.

Abstract: Robustness, domain adaptation, photometric and occlusion invariance, compositional generalisation, temporal robustness, alignment safety, and classical anisotropic regularisation are usually treated as separate problems with separate method families. This paper argues that much of their shared structure is one statistical problem: estimate the covariance of label-preserving deployment nuisance, then regularise the encoder Jacobian along a matrix whose range covers that covariance (the matching principle). CORAL, adversarial training, IRM, augmentation, metric learning, Jacobian penalties, and alignment-style constraints are different estimators of that object, not independent robustness tricks.
  In the linear-Gaussian model we prove closed-form optimality (Theorem A), including cube-root water-filling within the matched range; necessity of range coverage for quadratic Jacobian penalties (Theorem G); the same range dichotomy at deep global minima; and two falsification controls (Lemma C; Corollaries E), with seven conditional consistency lemmas (D1-D7) for estimation under standard identifiability assumptions.
  We introduce the Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI), a label-free probe of embedding sensitivity when task accuracy or Jacobian Frobenius norm is insufficient.
  Thirteen pre-registered blocks from classical ML through Qwen2.5-7B test the predicted matched, then isotropic, then wrong-W ordering on geometry and deployment drift; twelve pass, and the sole exception (Office-31) is an eigengap failure named before the run. At 7B scale, matched style-PMH improves selective honesty and preserves Style TDI where standard DPO degrades it.
  The contribution is naming the deployment nuisance covariance, stating what the regulariser must do, and supplying a closed-form falsifiable theory once that object is identified, not universality on every leaderboard.

</details>


### [313] [Uniform Diffusion Models Revisited: Leave-One-Out Denoiser and Absorbing State Reformulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22765)
*Samson Gourevitch,Yazid Janati,Dario Shariatian,Umut Simsekli,Eric Moulines,Eric P. Xing,Alain Durmus*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies a mismatch between the plug-in ELBO and cross-entropy denoising objective in Uniform Diffusion Models (UDM), proposes leave-one-out posterior parameterization, derives exact conversions between denoiser/posterior/score, and introduces an absorbing-state reformulation of uniform diffusion that bridges the gap with Masked Diffusion Models (MDM).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to understand and resolve the empirical performance gap between masked diffusion models (MDM) and uniform diffusion models (UDM), showing the gap stems from parameterization/design choices rather than the marginal distributions themselves.

Method: 1) Identifies that UDM's standard plug-in bridge parameterization optimizes a leave-one-out posterior (predicting clean tokens without using their own noisy observation) rather than the denoising posterior. 2) Derives exact conversions between denoiser, leave-one-out posterior, and score. 3) Proposes inference improvements: informed predictor-corrector sampler and improved temperature sampling. 4) Introduces absorbing-state reformulation of uniform diffusion that decomposes UDM into masked-diffusion-like operations.

Result: Leave-one-out parameterizations consistently improve UDM generation. The absorbing construction matches or surpasses masked diffusion on language modeling benchmarks.

Conclusion: The empirical gap between masked and uniform diffusion models is driven more by parameterization and sampling design than by the choice of marginals themselves.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models are often trained through clean-data prediction, but the prediction can be used in different ways to define the reverse dynamics. In Masked Diffusion Models (MDM) these choices largely coincide, whereas in Uniform Diffusion Models (UDM) they do not. We show that the standard plug-in bridge parameterization for UDM is not optimized by the denoising posterior, but by a leave-one-out posterior that predicts each clean token without using its own noisy observation. This identifies a mismatch between the plug-in ELBO and the usual cross-entropy denoising objective. We characterize the leave-one-out target and derive exact conversions between the denoiser, the leave-one-out posterior, and the score. These conversions allow us to disentangle parameterization and training objective. Our results also lead to inference improvements without any additional training through an informed predictor-corrector sampler and improved temperature sampling based on the leave-one-out predictor.
  We further introduce an absorbing-state reformulation of uniform diffusion that preserves the UDM joint law while decomposing it into masked-diffusion-like sampling operations, with simpler denoising posteriors, carry-over unmasking, and a natural remasking mechanism. On language modeling, leave-one-out parameterizations consistently improve UDM generation, while the absorbing construction matches or surpasses masked diffusion. These results suggest that the empirical gap between masked and uniform diffusion is driven less by the choice of marginals themselves than by parameterization and sampling design. The code and models can be found at https://github.com/samsongourevitch/rev_udm.

</details>


### [314] [Vector Policy Optimization: Training for Diversity Improves Test-Time Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22817)
*Ryan Bahlous-Boldi,Isha Puri,Idan Shenfeld,Akarsh Kumar,Mehul Damani,Sebastian Risi,Omar Khattab,Zhang-Wei Hong,Pulkit Agrawal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VPO is an RL algorithm that trains LLMs to produce diverse responses by optimizing for vector-valued rewards, outperforming scalar RL baselines on test-time search tasks like code generation and evolutionary search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LLM post-training optimizes scalar rewards, producing low-entropy distributions that fail to provide the diversity needed for inference-time search procedures like AlphaEvolve.

Method: Vector Policy Optimization (VPO) replaces the GRPO advantage estimator to train LLMs on vector-valued rewards (e.g., per-test-case correctness), encouraging a set of solutions specialized to different reward trade-offs.

Result: VPO matches or beats scalar RL baselines on pass@k and best@k across four tasks, with larger gains as search budget increases, and enables solutions to problems unsolvable by GRPO models in evolutionary search.

Conclusion: Optimizing for diversity via vector rewards may need to become the default post-training objective as test-time search becomes more standardized.

Abstract: Language models must now generalize out of the box to novel environments and work inside inference-scaling search procedures, such as AlphaEvolve, that select rollouts with a variety of task-specific reward functions. Unfortunately, the standard paradigm of LLM post-training optimizes a pre-specified scalar reward, often leading current LLMs to produce low-entropy response distributions and thus to struggle at displaying the diversity that inference-time search will require. We propose Vector Policy Optimization (VPO), an RL algorithm that explicitly trains policies to anticipate diverse downstream reward functions and to produce diverse solutions. VPO exploits that rewards are often vector-valued in practice, like per-test-case correctness in code generation or, say, multiple different user personas or reward models. VPO is essentially a drop-in replacement for the GRPO advantage estimator, but it trains the LLM to output a set of solutions where individual solutions specialize to different trade-offs in the vector reward space. Across four tasks, VPO matches or beats the strongest scalar RL baselines on test-time search (e.g. pass@k and best@k), with the gap widening as the search budget grows. For evolutionary search, VPO models unlock problems that GRPO models cannot solve at all. As test-time search becomes more standardized, optimizing for diversity may need to become the default post-training objective.

</details>


### [315] [Remember to be Curious: Episodic Context and Persistent Worlds for 3D Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22814)
*Lily Goli,Justin Kerr,Daniele Reda,Alec Jacobson,Andrea Tagliasacchi,Angjoo Kanazawa*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces ReCuriosity, a curiosity-driven RL approach that uses an online 3D reconstruction as a persistent world model and a sequence-model policy for episodic context, enabling effective exploration in 3D environments without task rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard curiosity-driven RL fails in complex 3D environments because agents get trapped in local loops, revisiting forgotten states and generating false intrinsic rewards. This stems from a lack of spatial persistence (no permanent world model) and episodic context (no trajectory history).

Method: Uses an online 3D reconstruction as a persistent world model (continuously updated) paired with a policy parameterized as a sequence model over RGB observations to maintain episodic trajectory history. Trained purely via curiosity (no task rewards) on HM3D.

Result: Outperforms RL-based active mapping baselines on HM3D; generalizes zero-shot to Gibson and AI-generated worlds; enables efficient downstream task adaptation (apple picking, image-goal navigation) outperforming from-scratch baselines.

Conclusion: Effective curiosity in 3D environments requires persistent world models and episodic memory. The proposed approach bridges the gap between curiosity-driven exploration and real-world deployment.

Abstract: Exploration is a prerequisite for learning useful behaviors in sparse-reward, long-horizon tasks, particularly within 3D environments. Curiosity-driven reinforcement learning addresses this via intrinsic rewards derived from the mismatch between the agent's predictive model of the world and reality. However, translating this intrinsic motivation to complex, photorealistic environments remains difficult, as agents can become trapped in local loops and receive fresh rewards for revisiting forgotten states. In this work, we demonstrate that this failure stems from a lack of spatial persistence and episodic context. We show that effective curiosity requires a model of the world that is persistent and continuously updated, paired with an agent that maintains an episodic trajectory history to navigate toward novel regions. We achieve this using an online 3D reconstruction as a persistent model of the world, while the agent policy is parameterized as a sequence model over RGB observations to maintain episodic context. This design enables effective exploration during training while allowing the agent to navigate using solely RGB frames at deployment. Trained purely via curiosity on HM3D, our agent outperforms RL-based active mapping baselines and generalizes zero-shot to Gibson and AI-generated worlds. Our end-to-end policy enables efficient adaptation to downstream tasks, such as apple picking and image-goal navigation, outperforming from-scratch baselines. Please see video results at https://recuriosity.github.io/.

</details>


### [316] [Integrable Elasticity via Neural Demand Potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22820)
*Carlos Heredia,Daniel Roncel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ICDN is a demand-first neural model that learns log-demand as a smooth function of log-prices, enabling exact elasticity derivation. It outperforms a log-log benchmark on the Dominick's beer dataset with more stable and economically plausible elasticity estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a demand-first neural model that can derive elasticities exactly from the learned demand surface, improving upon traditional log-log models for multiproduct retail demand.

Method: ICDN models log-demand as a smooth, context-conditioned function of log-prices, allowing exact derivation of elasticities. It uses a neural network architecture that integrates context information and ensures integrability conditions.

Result: On the Dominick's beer dataset, ICDN improves out-of-sample generalization over a directed log-log benchmark and yields more stable, economically plausible elasticity estimates, especially for weakly identified cross-price effects.

Conclusion: The ICDN approach provides a robust framework for multiproduct demand modeling that generates more reliable elasticity estimates, particularly in settings where traditional methods struggle with cross-price effects.

Abstract: We propose the Integrable Context-Dependent Demand Network (ICDN), a demand-first neural model for multiproduct retail demand. The model learns log-demand as a smooth, context-conditioned function of log-prices, allowing elasticities to be derived exactly from the learned demand surface. On the Dominick's beer dataset, ICDN improves out-of-sample generalization over a directed log-log benchmark and yields more stable, economically plausible elasticity estimates, especially for weakly identified cross-price effects.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [317] [Rapid Adaptive Matched Filter for Detecting Radar Targets with Unknown Velocity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21685)
*Anatolii A. Kononov*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Doppler domain localized adaptive matched filter (DDL-AMF) for radar target detection in heterogeneous clutter with limited data, outperforming the existing DDL-GLR method without needing clutter spectrum parameters or prior measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve radar target detection in severely heterogeneous clutter environments with limited training data, where existing methods like RODI-based DDL-GLR suffer performance loss, especially when target Doppler frequency is unknown.

Method: The authors introduce a Region of Possible Target Detection (RPTD) concept that captures most target signal power in a small set of Doppler bins, and implement it within a Doppler Domain Localized Adaptive Matched Filter (DDL-AMF).

Result: The RPTD-based DDL-AMF outperforms the RODI-based DDL-GLR detector, achieves near-optimum performance under unknown target Doppler frequency and multimodal clutter spectra, and requires no clutter spectrum parameter information or pre-measurements.

Conclusion: The proposed DDL-AMF detector is more practical and robust than the DDL-GLR counterpart, enabling rapid adaptive detection with near-optimum performance in challenging clutter environments.

Abstract: This paper introduces a Doppler domain localized (DDL) implementation of the adaptive matched filter (AMF) for radar target detection in severely heterogeneous clutter environments with limited training data. The proposed detector uses the concept of a region of possible target detection (RPTD), a small set of Doppler bins that capture most of the target signal power. This RPTD-based DDL-AMF detector outperforms an earlier suggested DDL implementation of the generalized likelihood ratio (GLR) test, which employs the region of detection improvement (RODI) concept. Unlike the RODI-based DDL-GLR detector, the proposed DDL-AMF detector requires no information on clutter spectrum parameters and no measurements to determine the number and locations of RODIs. Moreover, the performance of the RODI-based DDL-GLR detector falls far below the optimum when the target Doppler frequency is unknown. In contrast, the RPTD-based DDL-AMF detector ensures rapid adaptive detection with near-optimum performance under unknown target Doppler frequency and multimodal clutter spectra.

</details>


### [318] [Rate-Splitting--Inspired Bistatic OFDM-ISAC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21729)
*Bruno F. Costa,Anup Mishra,Israel Leyva-Mayorga,Taufik Abrão,Petar Popovski*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a rate-splitting (RS) inspired framework for uplink bistatic ISAC over OFDM, where messages are split into robust and supplementary streams superposed with sensing signals, and a staged sensing-communication receiver is designed to manage interference, with joint power allocation for spectral efficiency maximization under sensing constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OFDM-based uplink bistatic ISAC suffers from challenging interference structures due to unequal direct/echo-path contributions and Doppler-induced ICI, which render orthogonal resource separation and fixed SIC strategies inadequate.

Method: Proposes an RS-inspired framework where each communication message is split into robust and supplementary streams superposed over a sensing signal, with a staged sensing-communication receiver. Tractable per-subcarrier SINR expressions are derived, and a joint power-allocation problem for SE maximization under sensing-performance/power constraints is formulated and solved via convex surrogates and fractional programming.

Result: Compared to NOMA-inspired baselines, the proposed framework provides more effective IFI management and improved robustness to Doppler-induced ICI.

Conclusion: The RS-inspired framework effectively addresses interference challenges in uplink bistatic ISAC, outperforming NOMA-based approaches in interference management and Doppler robustness.

Abstract: Achieving effective uplink bistatic ISAC over an OFDM waveform gives rise to challenging interference structures. These are mostly due to unequal direct- and echo-path contributions and Doppler-induced ICI, rendering orthogonal resource separation and fixed SIC strategies inadequate. To address this problem, we propose a RS-inspired framework where the transmitter splits each communication message into a robust and a supplementary stream, which are jointly superposed over a sensing signal.
