Result: TO-Agents produces preference-aligned designs in 60% of trials across two case studies, achieving up to 6x more successful trials than the ablated pipeline. The system identifies effective parameter levers, recovers from poor revisions, and expands design exploration.

Conclusion: Agentic topology optimization can shift designers from low-level parameter tuning toward higher-level specification of form and function, while highlighting safeguards needed for reliable autonomous engineering design.

Abstract: Topology optimization can generate efficient structures, but designers often must manually translate qualitative intent, such as desired visual style, product experience, or manufacturability into solver settings that are not directly tied to those preferences. We present TO-Agents, a multi-agent AI framework that connects natural-language design intent with iterative topology optimization. The framework converts a human-provided problem description into validated solver inputs, runs a topology optimization solver, renders the resulting 3D topology, and uses multi-view vision-language reasoning with an independent judge agent to critique each result and revise solver parameters. We evaluate the framework on two long-horizon design tasks: a cantilever beam benchmark and a phone-stand product design. In both tasks, the designer specifies an aesthetic preference for hierarchically branched structures inspired by natural tree morphologies, and the system performs four revision cycles across ten independent replicates. TO-Agents produces at least one preference-aligned design in 60% of trials for each case study, corresponding to up to 6x more successful trials than an ablated pipeline without visual or historical feedback. Judge scores and human evaluations show that the pipeline can identify effective parameter levers, recover from poor revisions, and expand design exploration. A manufacturing agent further post-processes top-ranked designs for additive manufacturing, enabling end-to-end intent-to-prototype design. We also identify failure modes, including overshooting, selective memory, misplaced tools, and incorrect parameter reasoning. These results suggest that agentic topology optimization can shift designers from low-level parameter tuning toward higher-level specification of form and function, while highlighting safeguards needed for reliable autonomous engineering design.

</details>


### [347] [The Shape of Testimony: A Scalable Framework for Oral History Archive Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21623)
*Itamar Trainin,Renana Keydar,Amit Pinchevski*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This study computationally analyzes over 1,600 Holocaust testimonies from USC Shoah Foundation and Yale Fortunoff Video Archive, generally confirming but complicating the structured vs. free-form dichotomy between the two collections.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critically examine the commonly accepted distinction between structured (USC) and free-form (Yale Fortunoff) Holocaust testimonies using large-scale computational methods rather than qualitative observation.

Method: Large-scale computational analysis of 1,600+ testimonies using discourse segmentation, topic modeling, and LLM-based analysis to quantify topic coherence, interviewer-survivor dynamics, and question type distributions.

Result: Generally corroborates structural differences between collections but reveals significant overlaps within individual interviews and across narrative patterns, complicating the simple dichotomy.

Conclusion: The structured vs. free-form dichotomy is oversimplified; the paper offers a scalable, replicable framework for comparative corpus analysis with broader applications in digital oral history.

Abstract: Researchers in Holocaust studies have often distinguished between two styles of oral survivor testimony: the USC Shoah Foundation's interviews tend to follow a structured, interviewer-guided format, whereas the Yale Fortunoff Video Archive generally favors a more free-form, open-ended style. This distinction has influenced both scholarly research and the development of later archives. In this study, we critically examine that claim by conducting a large-scale computational analysis of more than 1,600 testimonies from both collections. Leveraging discourse segmentation, topic modeling, and large language model (LLM) based analysis, we quantify the "structuredness" level of testimonies through topic coherence, interviewer-survivor dynamics, and the distribution of question types. Our results generally corroborate the structural differences identified in earlier research, while also revealing significant overlaps between the collections, both within individual interviews and across common narrative patterns. This complicates the simple "structured vs. free-form" dichotomy often applied to these oral histories. Beyond revisiting a foundational claim in Holocaust studies, our work provides a scalable, replicable framework for comparative corpus analysis. As a proof of concept, it suggests broader applications for digital oral history, narrative analysis, and the design of citizen-science annotation platforms.

</details>


### [348] [MindLoom: Composing Thought Modes for Frontier-Level Reasoning Data Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21630)
*Haiyang Shen,Taian Guo,Xuanzhong Chen,Mugeng Liu,Weichen Bi,Wenchun Jing,Sixiong Xie,Zhuofan Shi,Yudong Han,Chongyang Pan,Siqi Zhong,Jinsheng Huang,Ming Zhang,Yun Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MindLoom is a framework that synthesizes frontier-level reasoning data for LLMs by decomposing hard problems into atomic "thought modes" and recomposing them to generate new diverse, difficulty-controlled problems, achieving strong results across nine STEM and math benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for synthesizing reasoning data have limited visibility into structural factors governing problem difficulty, leading to narrow diversity and unstable difficulty control.

Method: MindLoom (1) decomposes verified solutions into thought mode chains, (2) trains a retrieval model to match problem states to compatible thought modes, (3) composes new problems by iteratively applying retrieved thought modes with distribution-aligned sampling, and (4) uses rollout-based judging for difficulty labeling and correct-response supply.

Result: Models fine-tuned on MindLoom data outperform base models, distillation, and external-data baselines across nine benchmarks covering five STEM disciplines and four math reasoning tasks. Ablations confirm each component's contribution, and analysis shows broad reasoning coverage with controllable difficulty.

Conclusion: MindLoom offers an effective framework for producing diverse, difficulty-controlled reasoning data from a small set of hard problems, improving LLM reasoning capabilities across multiple domains.

Abstract: Although LLMs have made substantial progress in reasoning, systematically producing frontier-level reasoning data remains difficult. Existing synthesis methods often have limited visibility into the structural factors that govern problem difficulty, which can result in narrow diversity and unstable difficulty control. In this work, we view the difficulty of a reasoning problem as arising from the accumulation of atomic knowledge-reasoning transformations, which we term thought modes. Building on this perspective, we propose MindLoom, a framework for synthesizing frontier-level reasoning data through compositional thought mode engineering. Given a collection of hard problems with verified solutions, MindLoom first decomposes those solutions into thought mode chains that reveal each problem's construction logic. It then trains a retrieval model that matches problem states to compatible thought modes, providing guidance on which reasoning challenges to introduce during synthesis. New problems are composed by iteratively applying retrieved thought modes to seed questions, with distribution-aligned sampling to encourage diverse reasoning coverage. Finally, a rollout-based judging stage labels generated questions by difficulty and supplies judged-correct responses for supervised fine-tuning. We evaluate MindLoom on nine benchmarks covering five STEM disciplines and four mathematical reasoning tasks across multiple model families and sizes. Models fine-tuned on MindLoom-generated data achieves favorable performances over base models, distillation, and external-data baselines across the reported benchmarks. Ablation studies indicate the contribution of each component, and further analysis suggests that MindLoom covers a broad range of reasoning patterns while maintaining useful difficulty control. We have open-sourced our implementation at https://github.com/EachSheep/MindLoom.

</details>


### [349] [AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0: Data Model Expansions and Content Evaluation Framework for Using Agentic AI to Improve Integration between AOPs and New Approach Methodologies (NAMs)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21645)
*Virginia K. Hench,J. Harry Caufield,Sierra A. T. Moxon,Jason M. O'Brien,Stephen W. Edwards*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, an evidence model prototype that expands the AOP-Wiki data model to improve quality, FAIRness, AI-readiness, and integration with new approach methodologies (NAMs), aiming to support computationally-generated and quantitative Adverse Outcome Pathways for regulatory science and One Health applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The AOP-Wiki's current data model and infrastructure cannot support continued growth and evolution of AOPs, limiting regulatory science and emerging biomedical/One Health applications. The authors aim to modernize the AOP-Wiki to better serve next-generation risk assessment.

Method: Development of AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, the third evidence model prototype, demonstrating data model expansions focused on internal quality improvement, evidence structuring for FAIRness and AI-readiness, and improved integration between the AOP framework and NAMs.

Result: A concrete prototype (EMOD 3.0) that expands the AOP-Wiki data model to support computationally-generated AOPs and quantitative AOPs (qAOPs), with enhanced FAIRness, AI-readiness, and NAM integration.

Conclusion: The expanded AOP-Wiki data model lays a foundation for supporting AI-generated AOPs and qAOPs, transforming the AOP-Wiki to better serve regulatory science, biomedical, and One Health contexts in the era of agentic AI.

Abstract: Adverse Outcome Pathways (AOP) are logic models that causally link biological mechanisms that can be measured in a lab to adverse outcomes, relevant to chemical regulatory endpoints. AOPs contextualize new approach methodologies (NAMs), in vitro and in silico methods used as alternatives to animal testing and the sequential events in an AOP serve as multi-scale models spanning biological scales. The AOP-Wiki serves as the global repository for AOPs. While the AOP-Wiki has played a central role in AOP expansion over the past decade, constraints within the current data model and application infrastructure limit the AOP-Wiki from supporting continued AOP growth and evolution. Yet, the transformative power of agentic AI has re-invigorated AOP-Wiki data modernization efforts at a time when core AOP principles can be harnessed to inform use of AI for aggregating and structuring AOP-relevant information. Seizing upon this momentum, we present AOP-Wiki EMOD 3.0, the third in a series of evidence model prototypes, which concretely demonstrates data model expansions and our vision for how the AOP-Wiki might be transformed to better serve regulatory science and emergent use of AOPs in biomedical and One Health contexts. We aim to lay a foundation to support computationally-generated AOPs and quantitative AOPs (qAOPs) by focussing on solutions for AOP-Wiki internal quality improvement, evidence structuring to enhance AOP FAIRness and AI-readiness, and improved integration between the AOP framework and NAMs to better serve next generation risk assessment.

</details>


### [350] [Investigating Concept Alignment Using Implausible Category Members](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21683)
*Sunayana Rane,Brenden M. Lake,Thomas L. Griffiths*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper probes AI concept understanding by testing implausible category membership (e.g., "Is an olive a vehicle?"), revealing misalignments with human judgments (e.g., treating "words" as vehicles/clothing, confusing vegetables/fruits, over-assigning weapons) that lead to safety issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard probing methods asking about plausible category members risk recalling training data patterns. The authors aim to characterize concept boundaries by asking about implausible members, revealing where AI systems lack the common-sense concept knowledge humans take for granted.

Method: Using categories from Rosch and Mervis's classic psychological study, the authors compare AI systems' vs. humans' assignments of objects to both correct and mismatched superordinate categories, analyzing cross-category assignment patterns.

Result: AI models show meaningful mismatches: treating "words" as vehicles/clothing, misclassifying vegetables as fruit, and over-assigning non-weapon items to "weapons." These concept boundary misalignments differ from human judgments in surprising ways.

Conclusion: Concept misalignment in AI systems translates into problematic downstream behaviors with implications for AI safety, highlighting the need for human-like concept understanding in reliable systems.

Abstract: Developing AI systems with a human-like understanding of everyday concepts is a key step towards developing safe, reliable systems whose behavior makes sense to humans. When probing concept understanding, asking questions about plausible category members (e.g., "Is a car a vehicle?") is likely to recall patterns in the model's vast training data. We pursue an alternative strategy, characterizing the boundaries of conceptual categories by asking about implausible category members (e.g., "Is an olive a vehicle?") to probe the kind of concept-level knowledge we take for granted in fellow humans. We characterize concept boundaries for a set of fundamental concepts by studying AI systems' assignments of objects to superordinate categories from a classic psychological study by Rosch and Mervis, as well as their assignments of the same objects to mismatched superordinate categories. We compare these assignments to those made by human participants on the full range of within-category and cross-category assignment tasks. Our results reveal a range of concepts for which which models differ in meaningful and surprising ways from humans, including treating "words" as belonging to categories like "vehicles" and "clothing," identifying several "vegetable" category members as "fruit," and assigning exemplars from non-weapon categories to the "weapons" category. We also demonstrate how these instances of concept misalignment translate into problematic downstream behavior with implications for AI safety.

</details>


### [351] [The Impact of AI Usage and Informativeness on Skill Development in Logical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21695)
*Shang Wu,Hongyu Yao,Catarina Belem,Shuyuan Fu,Mark Steyvers,Padhraic Smyth*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper examines how AI usage and informativeness affect human skill development in logical reasoning tasks, finding that heavy AI use weakens skills, low-information AI hinders learning, while high-information AI can complement reasoning when used appropriately.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite AI's growing integration into problem-solving, its effects on individual skill development remain unclear, motivating investigation into whether AI complements or substitutes human learning.

Method: Controlled logical reasoning task with on-demand AI assistance, comparing AI usage patterns and AI informativeness levels (low vs. high information) on learning outcomes.

Result: Heavy AI users underperform compared to peers; low-information AI harms both immediate and post-AI performance; high-information AI improves short-run performance without reducing post-AI outcomes on average, with heterogeneous effects.

Conclusion: AI can either complement or substitute human skill development depending on context (usage patterns and informativeness), suggesting regulatory implications for promoting skill development alongside AI.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is being increasingly integrated into human problem-solving, yet its effects on individual skill development remain unclear. We examine how both AI usage and informativeness can shape learning in the context of a controlled logical reasoning task with on-demand access to AI assistance. We find that greater AI usage is associated with weaker skill development: heavy AI users underperform relative to comparable peers, whereas light AI users perform similarly to matched users who do not use AI. We also find in our study that these patterns are mediated by AI informativeness. Low-information AI neither improves immediate performance nor preserves performance after AI assistance is removed, and is linked to weaker learning overall. On the other hand, high-information AI was found to improve short-run performance without reducing post-AI outcomes on average in our experiments, but with heterogeneous effects. Our findings in general suggest that AI can, depending on context, either complement human skill development by amplifying independent reasoning or can act as a substitute that undermines such reasoning, with the implication that regulating AI access and usage will be important for promoting skill development in the presence of AI assistance.

</details>


### [352] [Latent-space Attacks for Refusal Evasion in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21706)
*Giorgio Piras,Raffaele Mura,Fabio Brau,Maura Pintor,Luca Oneto,Fabio Roli,Battista Biggio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper reframes refusal suppression in LLMs as a latent-space evasion attack against linear probes, proposes a Controlled Latent-space Evasion attack that projects representations beyond the decision boundary, and achieves state-of-the-art attack success across 15 models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing refusal suppression methods via direction ablation lack a principled understanding of why they work; the authors aim to provide theoretical grounding for these methods.

Method: The authors recast refusal suppression as a latent-space evasion attack against linear probes, showing prior work's difference-in-means direction defines a probe and ablation equals projection onto its decision boundary. They propose CLE (Controlled Latent-space Evasion) that pushes representations past the boundary with optimized confidence.

Result: State-of-the-art attack success rate across 15 instruction-tuned, multimodal, and reasoning models, outperforming refusal-ablation baselines and specialized jailbreak attacks.

Conclusion: The evasion-attack perspective explains prior work's success, reveals its limitation (stopping at decision boundary), and motivates CLE which achieves superior refusal suppression.

Abstract: Safety-aligned language models are trained to refuse harmful requests, yet refusal behavior can be suppressed by steering their internal representations. Existing methods do so by ablating a refusal direction from model activations, aiming to remove refusal from the model's residual stream. Despite their empirical success, these methods lack a principled account of the latent-space transformation they induce and why it suppresses refusal. In this work, we recast refusal suppression as a latent-space evasion attack against linear probes trained to separate refused from answered prompts. Under this view, prior work's difference-in-means direction naturally defines such a probe, and its ablation is exactly a projection onto its decision boundary, i.e., a minimum-confidence evasion attack. This perspective not only explains the empirical success of prior work but also admits a key limitation: evasion stops at the decision boundary, motivating the need to push representations further into the compliant region, i.e., where the model answers. We leverage this by proposing a Controlled Latent-space Evasion attack that projects representations past the boundary with an optimized confidence. We achieve state-of-the-art attack success rate across 15 instruction-tuned, multimodal, and reasoning models, outperforming existing refusal-ablation baselines and specialized jailbreak attacks.

</details>


### [353] [AttuneBench: A Conversation-Based Benchmark for LLM Emotional Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21739)
*Kate M. Lubrano,Faisal Sayed,Ankita Rathod,Akshansh,Craver Corbyn Thomas-Smith,Mark E. Whiting,Karina Nguyen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AttuneBench is a benchmark using 200 real multi-turn conversations where participants chatted with LLMs and annotated emotions, model behaviors, and preferred responses. Testing 11 models revealed that emotion recognition, behavioral classification, preference prediction, and response quality are largely independent capabilities, with preference alignment being more discriminative than emotion-label accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EI benchmarks rely on synthetic prompts, single-turn cases, or third-party annotation, which do not directly measure how models infer and respond to a participant's emotional state over real, multi-turn conversations.

Method: The authors built AttuneBench using 200 genuine multi-turn human-model conversations with turn-by-turn annotations of emotional state, model behavior, and preferred responses from participants. They evaluated 11 models on emotion recognition, behavioral classification, preference prediction, and judged response quality.

Result: Model rankings across the four capabilities were largely independent. Preference alignment and response-quality judgments are substantially more model-discriminating than emotion-label accuracy.

Conclusion: Emotionally intelligent behavior decomposes into separable capabilities; accurately predicting what response a specific user wants in context is more discriminative than recognizing emotions alone. Single-turn or synthetic formats cannot capture these distinctions across turns.

Abstract: Emotional intelligence (EI), the ability to perceive, understand, and respond appropriately to others' emotional states, is central to human communication, and increasingly important to assess as LLMs assume conversational roles in everyday life. Existing EI benchmarks rely on synthetic prompts, single-turn cases, or third-party annotation. These approaches do not directly measure how models infer and respond to a participant's emotional state over the course of a real conversation. We introduce AttuneBench, a benchmark grounded in 200 genuine multi-turn human-model conversations in which participants conversed with anonymized LLMs and provided turn-by-turn annotations of their emotional state, the model's behavior, and their preferred responses. Across 11 evaluated models, we find that model rankings on emotion recognition, behavioral classification, preference prediction, and judged response quality are largely independent, indicating that emotionally intelligent behavior decomposes into separable capabilities. Preference alignment and response-quality judgments are substantially more model-discriminating than emotion-label accuracy. These results indicate that emotionally intelligent behavior requires predicting what kind of response a specific user wants in context, a distinction that aggregate scoring can obscure and that single-turn or synthetic formats cannot directly capture across turns. AttuneBench provides a framework for assessing each of these capabilities and for diagnosing model-specific strengths and failure modes in emotionally salient conversation.

</details>


### [354] [SMDD-Bench: Can LLMs Solve Real-World Small Molecule Drug Design Tasks?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21740)
*Kevin Han,Renfei Zhang,Kathy Wei,Hamed Mahdavi,Niloofar Mireshghallah,Amir Farimani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces SMDD-Bench, a benchmark of 502 multi-turn tasks across 5 drug design types to evaluate LLM agents on real-world small molecule drug discovery. The best model (GPT5.4) solves only 40.2% of tasks, highlighting significant room for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of LLM agents for small molecule drug design is inconsistent, overly simplistic, limited in scope, or restricted to single-turn QA. There is no standardized benchmark for real-world, multi-turn agentic performance in this domain.

Method: Created SMDD-Bench with 502 guaranteed-solvable task instances across 5 task types (2D Pharmacophore Identification, Interaction Point Discovery, Scaffold Hopping, Lead Optimization, Fragment Assembly), involving 102 unique protein targets. Tested 7 frontier LLMs (both open and closed source) on these tasks.

Result: The best-performing model (GPT5.4) solves only 40.2% of tasks, indicating that current LLM agents are far from fully autonomous computational drug design.

Conclusion: SMDD-Bench provides a much-needed standardized testbed to drive progress in training and evaluating LLM agents for fully autonomous drug design. A public leaderboard is available.

Abstract: LLM agents have incredible potential for scientific discovery applications. However, the performance of LLM agents on real-world, small molecule drug design (SMDD) tasks across diverse chemistries and targets is unclear. Current evaluation methods are either ad hoc, too simple for real-world discovery, limited in scale, or restricted to single-turn question answering. In effort to standardize the evaluation of LLM agents on small molecule design, we introduce SMDD-Bench, a challenging, multi-turn, long-horizon agentic benchmark consisting of 502 guaranteed-solvable task instances spanning 5 task types: 2D Pharmacophore Identification, Interaction Point Discovery, Scaffold Hopping, Lead Optimization, and Fragment Assembly. SMDD-Bench tasks span a wide region of chemical space and involve 102 unique protein targets. Completely solving the benchmark would require having strong chemical and biological reasoning and 3D intuition, understanding specialized tool use, and displaying planning expertise over a limited number of oracle calls. We benchmark 7 frontier open and closed source LLMs and find even the most performant LLM, GPT5.4, solves only 40.2\% of tasks. We hope SMDD-Bench provides a standardized testbed to invigorate the field towards training and evaluating LLM agents for fully autonomous computational drug design. We host a public leaderboard at smddbench.com .

</details>


### [355] [Who Uses AI? Platforms, Workforce, and AI Exposure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21743)
*Michelle Yin,Burhan Ogut*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper shows that AI-based occupation exposure measures are significantly biased by platform user base rather than actual workforce composition, leading to substantially different employment effect estimates depending on the data source used.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and quantify biases in AI conversation log-based occupation exposure measures, which have been increasingly used in labor economics research.

Method: The authors hold outcome, sample, controls, and estimator fixed while varying only the platform input; compare within-vendor consumer vs. enterprise channels; reweight to BLS workforce shares; and formalize non-classical measurement error with probability limits and partial-identification bounds.

Result: Post-ChatGPT employment coefficients vary by a factor of 1.9 across platforms; consumer vs. enterprise channels produce opposite-sign estimates; BLS reweighting attenuates estimates by 42-93%; bias understates substitution more than augmentation.

Conclusion: AI-based occupation exposure scores partly reflect platform user demographics rather than actual workforce composition, introducing substantial non-classical measurement error that systematically biases employment elasticity estimates.

Abstract: A growing literature uses artificial intelligence platform conversation logs to measure occupation exposure. We show that these scores partly measure platform user base rather than the workforce. Holding outcome, sample, controls, and estimator fixed while varying only the platform input changes the post-ChatGPT employment coefficient by a factor of 1.9, and within-vendor consumer-versus-enterprise channels produce estimates that disagree in sign. Reweighting to Bureau of Labor Statistics workforce shares attenuates estimates by 42 to 93 percent. We formalize the non-classical measurement error, derive probability limits and partial-identification bounds for employment elasticities. The bias understates substitution more than augmentation.

</details>


### [356] [A Causal Argumentation Method for Explainability of Machine Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21758)
*Henry Salgado,Meagan R. Kendall,Martine Ceberio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper integrates causality with argument-based reasoning (BAF) to explain why models make predictions, moving beyond standard XAI that only identifies which features are relevant.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current XAI methods identify which features are relevant to predictions but fail to explain why certain decisions were made.

Method: The approach first discovers causal relationships among variables, then translates them into a Bipolar Argumentation Framework (BAF) representing supportive and opposing feature interactions, using semi-stable semantics to find explaining extensions.

Result: The method was demonstrated on two benchmark datasets and compared against standard post-hoc explainability approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed causal-argumentative framework provides more explanatory power than standard XAI by explaining why predictions occur, not just which features matter.

Abstract: Explainable AI (XAI) methods identify which features are relevant to a model's predictions but often fail to clarify why certain decisions are made. In this work, we present a novel method that integrates causality with argument-based reasoning to explain why models may be making predictions. Our approach first identifies causal relationships among variables using causal discovery methods and then translates these into a Bipolar Argumentation Framework (BAF) to represent supportive and opposing interactions among features. By using semi-stable semantics, we find extensions of features that explain why certain outcomes may have been chosen. We demonstrate our method on two benchmark datasets and compare its results against standard post-hoc explainability approaches.

</details>


### [357] [What Counts as AI Sycophancy? A Taxonomy and Expert Survey of a Fragmented Construct](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21778)
*Meryl Ye,Lujain Ibrahim,Jessica Y. Bo,Myra Cheng,Ida Mattsson,Daniel Vennemeyer,Robert Kraut,Steve Rathje*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper reviews 70 studies on AI sycophancy to create a taxonomy distinguishing belief-directed vs. person-directed sycophancy and explicit vs. implicit forms, then surveys 106 experts (94.3% agree it's a problem but disagree on definitions). The field lacks consistent definitions, causing comparison and mitigation issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The term "AI sycophancy" lacks a consistent definition across research, industry, and policy, leading to incomparable evaluation results, non-transferable mitigation strategies, and systems that resist one form but exhibit others.

Method: (1) Literature review of 70 papers on AI sycophancy to develop a taxonomy; (2) Survey of 106 experts in AI sycophancy and related fields to assess agreement on which behaviors qualify as sycophantic.

Result: The taxonomy distinguishes belief-directed vs. person-directed sycophancy and explicit vs. implicit manifestations. Current research focuses on overt belief-directed sycophancy. Survey: 94.3% of experts agree sycophancy is a significant problem, but there is substantial disagreement on which specific behaviors qualify.

Conclusion: AI sycophancy is a broad family of behaviors requiring different measurements, interventions, and governance. The taxonomy provides a shared vocabulary to address these issues systematically.

Abstract: AI sycophancy has become a prominent concern in large language model (LLM) research. Yet the term lacks a consistent definition and has been applied to behaviors ranging from agreeing with a user's false claim to excessively praising the user to withholding corrective feedback. When researchers, companies, and policymakers use the same term to describe different behaviors, evaluation results become difficult to compare, mitigation strategies fail to transfer, and systems that are resistant to one form of sycophancy continue exhibiting other forms. To address this, we make two contributions. First, we reviewed 70 papers on AI sycophancy to develop a taxonomy of how the behavior has been defined and measured. The taxonomy distinguishes (1) whether a model is sycophantic toward a user's positions and beliefs, or toward the user's broader personal traits and emotions, and (2) whether this occurs through explicit, direct language or more implicit, subtle behaviors such as framing, omission, or tone. Mapping existing literature to our taxonomy reveals that current research has focused on overt forms of sycophancy toward users' beliefs, leaving more subtle and person-directed behaviors relatively understudied. Second, we surveyed 106 experts in AI sycophancy and related fields to examine whether researchers agree on which model behaviors are sycophantic. While experts are nearly unanimous in believing that sycophancy is a significant problem in current AI systems (94.3% agree), they disagree substantially on which specific behaviors qualify. Together, these findings demonstrate that AI sycophancy is a broad family of behaviors with different measurement challenges, intervention requirements, and governance implications. Our taxonomy provides a shared vocabulary for understanding and addressing these behaviors.

</details>


### [358] [Trace2Skill: Verifier-Guided Skill Evolution for Long-Context EDA Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21810)
*Zijian Du,Nathaniel Pinckney*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trace2Skill is a test-time scaling framework that improves hardware LLM agents on complex Verilog design problems without model fine-tuning, by evolving task-specific skills from rollout traces and using dense verifier feedback to boost pass rates on previously unsolvable tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Complex Verilog Design Problems (CVDP) are challenging for hardware LLM agents because they require localizing RTL, testbenches, and dependencies in large repos, making precise edits, and recovering from sparse hidden-verifier failures. Existing approaches (new models or more sampling) are insufficient.

Method: Trace2Skill treats agent's natural-language skill as an evolvable policy. It mines rollout traces for success/failure modes, converts them into diagnostics and oracle lessons, and uses an oracle-mutator-selector loop to produce task-specific skills. It also supports bounded runtime dense verifier feedback for guidance.

Result: Across hard CVDP tasks that defeat the seed agent and frontier coding agents, Trace2Skill with dense verifier feedback substantially improves task pass rates and achieves breakthrough passes on previously unsolved tasks, without fine-tuning data, specialized RTL training, or weight updates.

Conclusion: Trace2Skill provides a general test-time scaling strategy that can extend beyond digital design to other verifiable EDA tasks, offering a practical alternative to model fine-tuning for improving hardware agents.

Abstract: Complex Verilog Design Problems (CVDP) challenge hardware LLM agents because solving them requires localizing verifier-relevant RTL, testbenches, include paths, and build dependencies inside large repository snapshots, making precise edits, and recovering from sparse hidden-verifier failures. We present Trace2Skill, a test-time scaling framework that improves a hardware agent without RTL-specialized model fine-tuning. Rather than training a new model or only sampling more candidate solutions, Trace2Skill treats the agent's natural-language skill as an evolvable policy. It mines repeated rollout traces for success and failure modes, converts them into dense diagnostics and oracle lessons, and uses an oracle, mutator, and selector loop to produce task-specific skills that guide later search, editing, validation, and recovery. Because final pass/fail labels are often too coarse for hard failures, Trace2Skill also supports bounded runtime dense verifier feedback that returns sanitized functional observations while keeping hidden harnesses and reference solutions inaccessible to the agent. This feedback helps guide skill evolution and agent execution by connecting skill text, verifier evidence, and downstream behavior. Across hard CVDP tasks that defeat the seed CVDP agent, including tasks that also defeat frontier coding agents, Trace2Skill with dense verifier feedback substantially improves task pass rates and produces breakthrough passes on previously unsolved tasks, without requiring high-quality fine-tuning data, specialized RTL model training, or model weight updates. The same framework provides a general test-time scaling strategy that can extend beyond digital design to other verifiable EDA tasks.

</details>


### [359] [Implicit Safety Alignment from Crowd Preferences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21822)
*Qian Lin,Daniel S. Brown*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hierarchical framework (Safe Crowd Preference-based RL) that extracts safety-aligned skills from crowd preference data and composes them to safely solve downstream RL tasks without explicit safety rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLHF approaches that combine preference-learned rewards with task rewards have inherent limitations. The authors aim to discover and transfer shared safety criteria from crowd preferences to downstream tasks to regularize agent behavior.

Method: A hierarchical framework that extracts safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences using a high-level policy to compose these skills for safely solving downstream RL tasks.

Result: The approach substantially lowers safety costs without explicit safety rewards, achieving task performance comparable to oracle methods that use ground-truth safety signals, validated across safe RL environments and LLM-style tasks.

Conclusion: Shared safety criteria can be effectively discovered from crowd preferences and transferred to downstream tasks, outperforming direct reward combination approaches.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) can reveal implicit objectives such as safety considerations that go beyond task completion. In this work, we focus on the common safety criteria embedded in crowd preference datasets, where different users may express distinct preferences or objectives, yet follow similar safety principles. Our aim is to discover shared safety criteria from crowd preferences and then transfer them to downstream RL tasks to regularize agent behavior and enforce safety. We first show that direct reward combination-optimizing a preference-learned reward model together with downstream task rewards-has inherent limitations. Motivated by this, we propose Safe Crowd Preference-based RL, a hierarchical framework that extracts safety-aligned skills from crowd preferences and composes them via a high-level policy to safely solve downstream tasks. Experiments across safe RL environments and a preliminary LLM-style task with diverse user goals and shared safety constraints demonstrate that our approach substantially lowers safety costs without access to explicit safety rewards, while achieving task performance comparable to oracle methods trained with ground-truth safety signals.

</details>


### [360] [Toward AI VIS Co-Scientists: A General and End-to-End Agent Harness for Solving Complex Data Visualization Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21825)
*Haichao Miao,Zhimin Li,Kuangshi Ai,Kaiyuan Tang,Chaoli Wang,Peer-Timo Bremer,Shusen Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents an end-to-end agentic harness (VIS co-scientist) that autonomously designs custom visual analysis applications from just data and high-level task descriptions, validated on IEEE SciVis Contests across multiple scientific domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific data analysis often requires expertise outside core domains (data management, visualization design), creating barriers. The goal is to build an AI co-scientist that can autonomously execute long-horizon tasks, with this work focusing on the visualization component.

Method: An end-to-end agentic harness with multiple specialized agents and skills that coordinate exploratory analysis, planning, environment configuration, implementation, interface validation, and task completion evaluation. Each stage produces artifacts guiding downstream work and iterative refinement.

Result: The system autonomously produces functional single-page visual analysis applications with verified linked-view behavior, customized to domain experts' tasks, validated on IEEE SciVis Contests spanning multiple science and engineering fields.

Conclusion: This is an important step toward a general AI co-scientist, demonstrating that autonomous visualization solution design from high-level directions is feasible for real-world complex scientific data analysis scenarios.

Abstract: The ability to inspect, interpret, and communicate complex data is crucial for virtually any scientific endeavor, but often requires significant expertise outside the core domain ranging from data management and analysis to visualization design and implementation. We present an end-to-end agentic harness that, based on only the data and a high level description of the tasks, independently designs custom visual analysis applications (VIS apps). This represents an important step towards a general AI co-scientist envisioned by many as an autonomous system that can autonomously execute long horizon tasks based on high-level directions. Our proposed VIS co-scientist is an essential component of this broader AI co-scientist vision: a harness that can autonomously analyze data and design visualization solutions using a collection of agents and specialized skills that coordinate exploratory analysis, plan, configure the environment, implement, validate the interface, and most importantly evaluate the overall task completion. Each stage produces document and instruction artifacts that guide downstream work and enable iterative refinement. We validate this approach on IEEE SciVis Contests spanning multiple science and engineering fields. These contests serve as ideal proving grounds because they encode real-world complexity: ambiguous requirements, diverse data modalities, design trade-offs, and task-driven validation. Given only the data and target tasks, our system autonomously produces functional single-page VIS Apps with verified linked-view behavior, highly customized to domain experts' specified tasks and needs.

</details>


### [361] [FLUID: From Ephemeral IDs to Multimodal Semantic Codes for Industrial-Scale Livestreaming Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21832)
*Xinhang Yuan,Zexi Huang,Anjia Cao,Xudong Lu,Zikai Wang,Penghao Zhou,Chang Liu,Wentao Guo,Qinglei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FLUID is the first framework that fully removes item IDs from livestreaming recommendation, using a cross-domain multimodal encoder (LUCID) with discrete hierarchical codes and ID-free late-fusion design, achieving significant gains in a production-scale deployment over one billion users globally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Livestreaming rooms are short-lived (tens of minutes), causing persistent cold-start issues for ID-based collaborative filtering, as item IDs cannot accumulate sufficient interaction signals.

Method: FLUID uses a cross-domain multimodal encoder (short videos + livestreams) to produce discrete hierarchical codes (LUCID), then employs a late-fusion, ID-free design injecting slice-level and room-level LUCID as independent tokens, stabilized by staged warmup under online incremental training.

Result: Deployed on industrial recommender with 1B+ users globally; gains: +0.55% Quality Watch Duration, +2.05% Cold-Start Room Views, +0.05% Active Hours.

Conclusion: FLUID effectively solves the cold-start problem in livestreaming recommendation by retiring item IDs entirely, demonstrating strong real-world performance at scale.

Abstract: Modern recommender systems rely heavily on ID-based collaborative filtering: each item is represented by a unique ID embedding that accumulates collaborative signals from user interactions. Livestreaming recommendation, however, faces a unique challenge in this paradigm: a live room typically broadcasts for only tens of minutes, so its item ID remains poorly learned in a persistent cold-start state and ID-centric ranking models fail to generalize. We present FLUID, the first framework to fully retire the candidate-side item ID from a production-scale livestreaming ranker. FLUID couples a cross-domain multimodal encoder, jointly trained on short videos and livestreams to produce discrete hierarchical codes (LUCID), with a late-fusion, ID-free design that injects slice-level and room-level LUCID as independent tokens, stabilized by a staged warmup under online incremental training. Deployed on our industrial livestreaming recommenders with a cross-platform combined user base of over one billion globally, FLUID delivers significant online gains of +0.55% Quality Watch Duration, +2.05% Cold-Start Room Views, and +0.05% Active Hours.

</details>


### [362] [Planning in the LLM Era: Building for Reliability and Efficiency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21902)
*Michael Katz,Harsha Kokel,Kavitha Srinivas,Shirin Sohrabi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that LLM-based planning is shifting from direct plan generation to generating symbolic planners that can be verified and reused efficiently, discussing three categories of methods and future research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing interest in intelligent agents has highlighted planning as a key capability, but early LLM-based planning approaches (single-shot generation, hybrid search) are unsound, incomplete, and resource-inefficient. The field needs more reliable and resource-efficient planning methods.

Method: The paper categorizes planner-generation methods into three major categories, examines their limitations, and outlines research steps toward more reliable LLM-based planner generation.

Result: Identifies a shift from using LLMs for direct plan generation to using them for generating symbolic solvers at solution construction time, which can be verified and used efficiently at inference.

Conclusion: The shift toward generating verifiable, maintainable planners with minimal LLM dependence at inference time represents a broader realignment of the planning field, offering a path toward more reliable and resource-efficient AI agents.

Abstract: Growing attention to intelligent agents has put a spotlight on one of their central capabilities: planning. Early attempts to leverage large language models (LLMs) for planning relied on single-shot plan generation, followed by hybrid approaches that coupled LLMs with limited external search. These methods, unsound and incomplete by their very nature, often require substantial resources without yielding better solutions on unseen problems. As the limitations of LLMs become clearer, recent work has shifted toward using them at solution construction time -- generating symbolic solvers for a family of problems that can be verified and then used efficiently at inference time. This trend reflects the growing need for agents that are both reliable and resource-efficient. It also offers a path towards generating maintainable planners with minimal dependence on language models at inference time. In this paper, we argue that this shift reflects a broader realignment of the planning field in the LLM era. We examine three major categories of planner-generation methods, discuss their current limitations, and outline research steps towards a more reliable and efficient LLM-based generation of planners.

</details>


### [363] [AI-Enabled Serious Games: Integrating Intelligence and Adaptivity in Training Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21962)
*Priyamvada Tripathi,Bill Kapralos*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This chapter examines how AI approaches (LLMs, RL, agent-based architectures) can enable real-time instructional adaptation in serious games, distinguishing between instructional intelligence and adaptivity, while highlighting challenges like explainability, validation, and limited empirical evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Serious games face persistent challenges including static scenario design, authoring bottlenecks, limited learner modeling, and difficulty implementing real-time instructional adaptation, motivating the exploration of AI to address these limitations.

Method: The authors provide a historical synthesis of adaptive learning systems (from CAI to ITS, DDA, learning analytics, and AI-enabled architectures) and discuss how LLMs, RL, and agent-based architectures can contribute to integrated intelligence and adaptivity.

Result: AI introduces capabilities such as dynamic scenario variation, contextual feedback, adaptive pacing, and learner-state modeling that may help address persistent challenges in serious games.

Conclusion: While AI offers promising solutions for real-time adaptation, challenges remain including explainability, validation, computational cost, and limited empirical evidence on long-term learning outcomes in AI-enabled serious games.

Abstract: Serious games are widely used for learning and training across domains such as healthcare, defense, and education. Persistent challenges remain, however, including static scenario design, authoring bottlenecks, limited learner modeling, and difficulty implementing meaningful real-time instructional adaptation. Recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) introduce novel capabilities such as dynamic scenario variation, contextual feedback, adaptive pacing, and learner-state modeling that may help address some of these limitations. At the same time, integrating AI into serious games raises important questions related to validity, transparency, system control, and learner trust. This chapter examines how contemporary AI approaches may support real-time instructional adaptation in serious games. It distinguishes between instructional intelligence, defined as a system's capacity to infer learner knowledge and reason about pedagogically appropriate responses, and adaptivity, defined as the ability to modify instructional actions during interaction. A historical synthesis of adaptive learning systems is presented, tracing developments from early computer-assisted instruction through intelligent tutoring systems (ITS), dynamic difficulty adjustment (DDA), authoring platforms, learning analytics, and recent AI-enabled architectures. Building on this perspective, the chapter discusses how large language models (LLMs), reinforcement learning (RL), and agent-based architectures may contribute to more integrated forms of intelligence and adaptivity in serious games. It also highlights practical and research challenges associated with AI-enabled systems, including explainability, validation, computational cost, and the limited empirical evidence regarding long-term learning outcomes in AI-enabled serious games.

</details>


### [364] [Format-Constraint Coupling in Knowledge Graph Construction from Statistical Tables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21974)
*Jingxuan Qi,Zhiqiang Ye,Yuxiang Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper reveals a phenomenon called "format-constraint coupling" in which applying extraction schemas to CSV time-series matrices can catastrophically reduce knowledge graph fidelity, with joint effects exceeding independent effects by up to +1.180. The authors introduce CSVFidelity-Bench for evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how extraction schemas interact with serialization formats (specifically statistical CSV time-series matrices) and potentially harm knowledge graph fidelity, contrary to the expectation that schemas should improve it.

Method: Controlled experiments using 2x2 factorial designs across 6+ datasets, probing and token ablation for surface-form anchoring explanation, and testing across format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and LLM families.

Result: Schema-format coupling can reduce fact coverage below unconstrained baselines (4/6 datasets). Joint effects exceed sum of independent effects by up to +1.180. Direct graph access exposes fidelity gaps up to +47.6pp (p<0.0001) while standard retrieval masks them.

Conclusion: Extraction schemas must be carefully matched to formats to avoid catastrophic fidelity loss. The authors release CSVFidelity-Bench (15 datasets, 1,892 facts) to support fidelity-aware evaluation.

Abstract: An extraction schema should not reduce knowledge graph fidelity. On statistical CSV, however, it can. We study country-by-year time-series matrices, a common layout on open-data portals. In this setting, serialization format and schema constraints interact super-additively. Their joint effect exceeds the sum of independent effects by up to +1.180 (2x2 factorial, 6 datasets). Bootstrap 95% CIs are strictly positive on 4/6 datasets, with strongest evidence on wide Type-II matrices. More critically, a schema applied to a mismatched format can trigger catastrophic mismatch. Fact coverage falls below the unconstrained baseline on 4/6 datasets through entity inflation or extraction refusal. We call this observed pattern format-constraint coupling. Probing and token ablation support a surface-form anchoring explanation centred on column-name references. Controlled variants across format-schema pairings, GraphRAG hosts, and LLM families show the same direction within the measured scope; one LLM family shows only partial activation. The observation also has a diagnostic consequence. Three standard retrieval modes largely mask construction quality (delta <= 1pp), whereas direct graph access exposes gaps up to +47.6pp (p < 0.0001). To support fidelity-aware evaluation, we release CSVFidelity-Bench. It contains 15 datasets, 11 Type-II matrices, 4 Type-III tables, and 1,892 Gold Standard facts across 6 domains.

</details>


### [365] [Echo: Learning from Experience Data via User-Driven Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21984)
*Hande Dong,Xiaoyun Liang,Jiarui Yu,Jiayi Lin,Changqing Ai,Feng Liu,Wenjun Zhang,Rongbi Wei,Chaofan Zhu,Linjie Che,Feng Wu,Xin Shen,Dexu Kong,Xiaotian Wang,Qiuyuan Chen,Bingxu An,Yueting Lei,Qiang Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Echo is a framework that transforms noisy agent-environment interaction logs into high-quality training signals by harvesting user refinement sequences, achieving a 10% acceptance rate improvement in code completion (25.7% → 35.7%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static human data is expensive to scale and bounded by creator knowledge. Current AI agents produce massive streams of raw experience data, but this data is noisy, filled with trial-and-error, and has low information density, making it inefficient for direct model training.

Method: Echo operationalizes the transition from raw experience to learnable knowledge by systematically harvesting user-driven refinement sequences. When users transform flawed agent proposals into verified solutions (driven by outcome responsibility), these refinement sequences serve as high-quality training signals that distill crude agent attempts into learnable knowledge.

Result: Large-scale validation in a production code completion environment shows Echo increases the acceptance rate from 25.7% to 35.7%, breaking the static performance ceiling.

Conclusion: Echo effectively harnesses user refinement as a continuous alignment signal, enabling agents to learn from real-world experience and overcome the limitations of static human data.

Abstract: Static "human data" faces inherent limitations: it is expensive to scale and bounded by the knowledge of its creators. Continuous learning from "experience data" - interactions between agents and their environments - promises to transcend these barriers. Today, the widespread deployment of AI agents grants us low-cost access to massive streams of such real-world experience. However, raw interaction logs are inherently noisy, filled with trial-and-error and low information density, rendering them inefficient for direct model training.
  We introduce Echo, a generalized framework designed to operationalize the transition from raw experience to learnable knowledge, effectively "echoing" environmental feedback back into the training loop for model optimization. In today's agent ecosystem, user refinement serves as a primary source of such feedback: driven by responsibility for the outcome, users rigorously transform flawed agent proposals into verified solutions. These user-driven refinement sequences inherently distill agents' crude attempts into high-quality training signals. Echo systematically harvests these signals to continuously align the agent with real-world needs. Large-scale validation in a production code completion environment confirms that Echo effectively harnesses this pipeline, breaking the static performance ceiling by increasing the acceptance rate from 25.7% to 35.7%.

</details>


### [366] [ECPO: Evidence-Coupled Policy Optimization for Evidence-Certified Candidate Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21993)
*Miaobo Hu,Shuhao Hu,BoKun Wang,Yina Sa,Xin Wang,Xiaobo Guo,Daren Zha,Jun Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Evidence-Coupled Policy Optimization (ECPO) for ranking systems that not only order candidates but also provide verifiable evidence certificates, using skeleton-aligned trajectories and a constrained policy with three coupled rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ranking systems in decision-support settings need to expose independently checkable evidence alongside candidate ordering, but current approaches lack mechanisms to certify that cited evidence is sufficient to recover the ranking decision.

Method: ECPO learns an interpretable trajectory reward from skeleton alignment, argument consistency, and graph features, then optimizes a constrained policy with three coupled rewards: listwise ranking utility, span-level certificate validity, and an evidence-cycle reward using a label-free deterministic verifier.

Result: The method optimizes CertNDCG and decision-evidence coupling, and is evaluated against zero-shot, SFT, GRPO, RM-only scoring with deterministic evidence attachment, grammar/JSON-constrained decoding, validator retry, best-of-N RM selection, and post-hoc evidence rationalization across closed-, predicted-, and hybrid-roster settings.

Conclusion: The paper reframes the ranking goal from maximizing ordinary NDCG to maximizing CertNDCG (certified NDCG) with decision-evidence coupling, introducing a principled approach for evidence-certified candidate ranking.

Abstract: Ranking systems used in decision-support settings should not only order candidates but also expose evidence that can be independently checked. We study evidence-certified candidate ranking: given an intent_id, a predefined plan skeleton, a window-local candidate roster, and text-derived candidate trajectories with span provenance, a system must output a Top-K list together with doc_id:span evidence certificates whose cited spans are sufficient to recover the decision. We instantiate this task on MAVEN-ERE and RAMS with fixed upstream extraction, window-local randomized candidate identifiers, skeleton-aligned trajectory supervision, hard negatives, and audit references. We introduce Evidence-Coupled Policy Optimization (ECPO), a listwise policy-optimization objective whose action is the joint object of ranking and evidence certificate. ECPO first learns an interpretable trajectory reward from skeleton alignment, argument consistency, and optional graph features; it then optimizes a constrained policy with three coupled rewards: listwise ranking utility, span-level certificate validity, and an evidence-cycle reward computed by a label-free deterministic verifier that reconstructs candidate support from claim-stripped cited spans. This reframes the goal from maximizing ordinary NDCG alone to maximizing CertNDCG and decision-evidence coupling. The evaluation compares ECPO against zero-shot, SFT, and GRPO policies, RM-only scoring with deterministic evidence attachment, grammar/JSON-constrained decoding, validator retry, best-of-N RM selection, and post-hoc evidence rationalization under closed-roster, predicted-roster, and hybrid-roster settings.

</details>


### [367] [The Log is the Agent: Event-Sourced Reactive Graphs for Auditable, Forkable Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21997)
*Yohei Nakajima*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ActiveGraph inverts traditional LLM agent architecture by making an append-only event log the source of truth, with a deterministic working graph projected from it, enabling replay, forking, and full lineage tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent frameworks are built around language models with ad-hoc logging and memory layers. The authors aim to replace this with a log-centric design that provides determinism, replayability, and causal traceability that retrieval-summarization memory cannot offer.

Method: ActiveGraph uses an append-only event log as the source of truth, with a working graph as a deterministic projection. Behaviors (functions, LLM routines, logic on typed edges) react to graph changes and emit new events. Coordination happens solely through the shared graph, not direct component communication.

Result: Three properties emerge: (1) deterministic replay of any run from its log, (2) cheap forking without re-executing shared prefix, and (3) end-to-end lineage from high-level goals to individual model calls. A diligence example demonstrates full causal reconstruction from the log.

Conclusion: The architecture is positioned as well-suited for self-improving agents, extending BabyAGI lineage and prior graph-memory research, though this is discussed without claiming definitive demonstration.

Abstract: Most agent frameworks are built around the language model: a conversation loop comes first, then tools, then rules, and finally a logging layer bolted on for observability, with state persisted as retrievable "memory." We describe ActiveGraph, a runtime that inverts this arrangement. The append-only event log is the source of truth; the working graph is a deterministic projection of that log; and behaviors--ordinary functions, classes, LLM-backed routines, or logic attached to typed edges--react to changes in the graph and emit new events. No component instructs another; coordination happens entirely through the shared graph. This single design decision yields three properties that retrieval-and-summarization memory systems do not provide: deterministic replay of any run from its log, cheap forking that branches a run at any event without re-executing the shared prefix, and end-to-end lineage from a high-level goal down to the individual model call that produced each artifact. We present the architecture, a determinism contract that makes replay sound, and a worked diligence example whose full causal structure is reconstructable from the log alone. We discuss--without claiming to demonstrate--why this substrate is unusually well suited to self-improving agents, and how it extends the BabyAGI lineage and prior graph-memory research.

</details>


### [368] [Active Evidence-Seeking and Diagnostic Reasoning in Large Language Models for Clinical Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22047)
*Chen Zhan,Xihe Qiu,Xiaoyu Tan,Xibing Zhuang,Gengchen Ma,Yue Zhang,Shuo Li,Peifeng Liu,Xiaoxiao Ge,Liang Liu,Lu Gan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces an OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator to evaluate LLMs in interactive diagnostic settings, finding that multi-turn evidence seeking reduces diagnostic accuracy by 12.75% and evidence quality by 24.36% compared to static full-context benchmarks, suggesting static evaluations overestimate real clinical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of LLMs on static medical exams may not reflect real clinical diagnosis, which requires iterative evidence gathering under uncertainty. The authors aim to build a more realistic benchmark to assess active diagnostic inquiry.

Method: The authors developed an OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator with 468 controlled cases, tested 15 different models under a protocol comparing multi-turn interactive questioning against full-context static evaluation.

Result: Multi-turn evidence seeking caused a 12.75% drop in diagnostic accuracy and a 24.36% reduction in supporting-evidence quality. Error analysis identified premature diagnostic closure and inefficient questioning as key factors.

Conclusion: Static full-context benchmarks may overestimate LLM performance in interactive clinical settings, motivating the need for complementary interactive assessments to ensure safer clinical decision support.

Abstract: Large language models perform well on static medical examinations, yet clinical diagnosis often requires iterative evidence gathering under uncertainty. Building on prior interactive evaluation efforts, we introduce an OSCE-inspired standardized patient simulator and a controlled, reproducible benchmark for active diagnostic inquiry. Across 468 cases and 15 models in our protocol, we observe that multi-turn evidence seeking reduces diagnostic accuracy by 12.75% and lowers supporting-evidence quality by 24.36% relative to full-context evaluation; error analyses associate these drops with premature diagnostic closure and inefficient questioning. Together, these results suggest that static full-context benchmarks may overestimate performance in interactive evidence-seeking settings, motivating complementary interactive assessment for safer clinical decision support.

</details>


### [369] [Enhancing Visual Token Representations for Video Large Language Models via Training-Free Spatial-Temporal Pooling and Gridding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22078)
*Bingjun Luo,Tony Wang,Hanqi Chen,Xinpeng Ding*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ST-GridPool is a training-free visual token enhancement method for Video LLMs that uses Pyramid Temporal Gridding and Norm-based Spatial Pooling to improve spatiotemporal interactions and performance without costly retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs like LLaVA use simplistic pooling/interpolation that overlooks intricate dynamics of visual tokens, limiting efficient compression while preserving spatiotemporal interactions.

Method: Two components: (1) Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG) for multi-grained spatiotemporal interactions via hierarchical temporal gridding, and (2) Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP) that leverages token norms correlation with semantic richness to preserve high-information regions.

Result: ST-GridPool consistently improves Video LLM performance across various benchmarks without requiring costly retraining, serving as an efficient plug-and-play solution.

Conclusion: ST-GridPool offers an effective, training-free, and plug-and-play approach for enhancing visual token representations in Video LLMs, bridging the gap left by existing simplistic pooling methods.

Abstract: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly advanced video understanding tasks, yet challenges remain in efficiently compressing visual tokens while preserving spatiotemporal interactions. Existing methods, such as LLaVA family, utilize simplistic pooling or interpolation techniques that overlook the intricate dynamics of visual tokens. To bridge this gap, we propose ST-GridPool, a novel training-free visual token enhancement method designed specifically for Video LLMs. Our approach integrates Pyramid Temporal Gridding (PTG), which captures multi-grained spatiotemporal interactions through hierarchical temporal gridding, and Norm-based Spatial Pooling (NSP), which preserves high-information visual regions by leveraging the correlation between token norms and semantic richness. Extensive experiments on various benchmarks demonstrate that ST-GridPool consistently enhances performance of Video LLMs without requiring costly retraining. Our method offers an efficient and plug-and-play solution for improving visual token representations. Our code is available in https://github.com/bingjunluo/ST-GridPool.

</details>


### [370] [A Camera-Cooperative ISAC Framework for Multimodal Non-Cooperative UAVs Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22090)
*Wenfeng Wu,Luping Xiang,Kun Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a Camera-Cooperative ISAC (CC-ISAC) framework that combines visual and radar sensing to efficiently detect and track non-cooperative UAVs, achieving significant reductions in beam steering and tracking overhead while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-modal perception has inherent limitations, and competition between communication and sensing for shared resources poses challenges in detecting non-cooperative UAVs in ISAC systems.

Method: Proposes CC-ISAC with two modules: (1) Vision-to-Echo Data Alignment (V2EDA) using cross-attention to align visual and echo features, and (2) Multimodal Fusion-Based Estimation (MMFE) integrating historical and current multimodal data for robust state estimation.

Result: 71% reduction in beam steering overhead, 1.69-11.15% reduction in tracking overhead, while maintaining high angular estimation accuracy on the DeepSense 6G dataset.

Conclusion: CC-ISAC effectively mitigates resource contention between sensing and communication, enabling reliable UAV surveillance while freeing system resources for additional communication tasks.

Abstract: The detection of non-cooperative unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) presents significant challenges for Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) systems due to the inherent limitations of single-modal perception and the competition for shared communication and sensing resources. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Camera-Cooperative ISAC (CC-ISAC) framework that employs multimodal sensing to enable efficient UAV beam steering and tracking. The proposed framework employs cameras for coarse-grained airspace monitoring and utilizes ISAC for fine-grained, high-precision sensing, forming a complementary perception loop that enhances both sensing accuracy and resource efficiency. Within this framework, two key modules are developed: (1) a Vision-to-Echo Data Alignment (V2EDA) model that aligns visual and echo-domain features through cross-attention mechanisms, and (2) a Multimodal Fusion-Based Estimation (MMFE) model that integrates historical multimodal data with current observations for robust state estimation. Extensive evaluations conducted on the DeepSense 6G dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves an average reduction of 71% in beam steering overhead and 1.69-11.15% in tracking overhead while maintaining high angular estimation accuracy. The CC-ISAC framework effectively mitigates resource contention between sensing and communication, enabling reliable UAV surveillance while freeing substantial system resources for additional communication tasks, thereby representing a practical advancement in ISAC system design.

</details>


### [371] [Knowledge Graph Re-engineering Along the Ontological Continuum (extended version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22093)
*Enrico Daga,Valentina Tamma,Terry Payne*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces the "ontological continuum," a theoretical framework for describing, comparing, navigating, and transforming knowledge graphs across diverse modelling practices, using two orthogonal distinctions (semantics vs pragmatics, properties vs affordances). It takes an empirical stance, aims to formalize the framework (e.g., via FCA), demonstrates its utility with a provenance case study, and outlines five open research challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The diversity of KG modelling practices (from lightweight vocabularies to richly axiomatised ontologies) makes integration and reuse expensive and brittle, especially in neuro-symbolic AI where bridging neural and symbolic components requires principled reengineering of KGs. Without a principled understanding of the KG space, GenAI-based automation remains conceptually ungrounded.

Method: The paper proposes the "ontological continuum," a theoretical construct characterized by two orthogonal axes: semantics vs pragmatics, and properties vs affordances. The approach is empirical—derived from observing real-world KG engineering practices—and aims for formal explication (e.g., via Formal Concept Analysis).

Result: A case study on provenance knowledge demonstrates how a single concern manifests differently across the continuum. The paper also articulates five open research challenges to guide future work.

Conclusion: The ontological continuum provides a missing conceptualization to describe, compare, navigate, and transform KGs across modelling practices. The authors invite the community to adopt this as a shared research agenda.

Abstract: Knowledge graphs have become the primary vehicle for data integration and are critical to the success of modern AI, but the diversity of KG modelling practices, from lightweight vocabularies to richly axiomatised ontologies, makes integration and reuse expensive and brittle. This challenge is particularly acute in neuro-symbolic AI, where bridging neural and symbolic components depends on the ability to reengineer KGs to fit new requirements; GenAI now offers unprecedented automation capability, but without a principled understanding of the KG space, such automation remains conceptually ungrounded. We introduce the ontological continuum as that missing conceptualisation, a theoretical construct a theoretical construct whose characterisation framework is defined by two orthogonal distinctions: semantics vs pragmatics, and properties vs affordances; together these define a vocabulary to describe, compare, navigate, and transform KGs across the full range of modelling practices. The methodological stance is empirical: rather than prescribing how KGs should be modelled, the continuum aims to define a theory of the existent, derived from observation of real-world KG engineering practices and whose structure can be made formally explicit, for example, through Formal Concept Analysis (FCA). We ground the vision through a case study on provenance knowledge, showing how a single concern manifests differently across the continuum. We articulate five open research challenges and invite the community to develop the ontological continuum as a shared research agenda.

</details>


### [372] [MPDocBench-Parse: Benchmarking Practical Multi-page Document Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22100)
*Bangbang Zhou,Hangdi Xing,Yifan Chen,Jianjun Xu,Qi Zheng,Feiyu Gao,Zhibo Yang,Shuai Bai,Ming Yan,Jieping Ye,Hongtao Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MPDocBench-Parse is a new benchmark for multi-page document parsing with 433 annotated documents (3,246 pages), covering 15 document types in English/Chinese, designed to evaluate content fidelity, logical structure, and semantic continuity beyond existing single-page or task-specific benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing document parsing benchmarks are inadequate for realistic scenarios because they focus on specific tasks or assess only single-page, text-centric settings, lacking fine-grained evaluation of semantic continuity, hierarchical structure recovery, and visual content preservation.

Method: The authors constructed MPDocBench-Parse with 433 manually annotated documents (3,246 pages) across 15 document types in English and Chinese, with a comprehensive evaluation protocol covering text/table/formula recognition, truncated text/table merging, figure extraction, reading order, and heading hierarchy recovery.

Result: Existing models perform well on basic text extraction but show clear limitations in semantic continuity integration, visual content parsing, and hierarchical structure recovery.

Conclusion: MPDocBench-Parse provides a unified foundation for advancing document parsing toward more realistic, multi-page scenarios.

Abstract: Document parsing converts visually rich documents into machine-readable structured representations, forming a crucial foundation for information systems. Although many benchmarks have been proposed for document parsing, they remain inadequate for realistic scenarios. Existing benchmarks either focus on specific tasks or assess only single-page, text-centric settings, making them insufficient for practical multi-page parsing. Moreover, they lack fine-grained evaluation of semantic continuity, hierarchical structure recovery, and visual content preservation. To address these gaps, we propose MPDocBench-Parse, a benchmark for multi-page document parsing in real-world applications. It contains 433 manually annotated documents with 3,246 pages, covering 15 document types in English and Chinese, with diverse layout styles, and supports document-level end-to-end evaluation. We further design a comprehensive protocol for content fidelity and logical structure, covering text, table, and formula recognition, truncated text and table merging, figure extraction, reading order, and heading hierarchy recovery. Experiments show that, while existing models perform well on basic text extraction, they still suffer clear limitations in semantic continuity integration, visual content parsing, and hierarchical structure recovery. MPDocBench-Parse provides a unified foundation for advancing document parsing toward more realistic scenarios.

</details>


### [373] [ExComm: Exploration-Stage Communication for Error-Resilient Agentic Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22102)
*Woomin Song,Beomjun Kim,Daewon Choi,Sai Muralidhar Jayanthi,Saket Dingliwal,Jinwoo Shin,Aram Galstyan*

Main category: cs.AI
