This makes it unclear whether optimization is driven by informed actions or trial-and-error. Direct evaluation is needed but costly due to lack of oracle harnesses.

Method: The authors propose "priority ranking," where optimizers rank harness components (e.g., tools) by their potential to improve/hinder agent performance when updated. This quantifies optimizer ability at the step level without expensive rollouts. They also introduce Shor, a collection of 182 human-verified optimization scenarios.

Result: Optimizers' ranking performance correlates with their ability to improve agents in actual multi-step harness optimization, establishing priority ranking as a reliable predictor of optimization ability.

Conclusion: Priority ranking provides a simple, low-cost, and reliable way to directly evaluate harness optimizers, addressing the gap in current evaluation methods.

Abstract: Harness optimization enables automated agent creation by having an optimizer agent iteratively update the harness of target agents. Despite its success, current studies evaluate optimizers solely by observing target agents' performance gains. This indirect end-improvement evaluation neglects optimizers' actions at intermediate steps, which are often erroneous and hinder agent performance. Therefore, it is unclear whether harness optimization is driven by optimizers' informed update actions or simply trial-and-error. This necessitates direct evaluation of harness optimizers. However, evaluating harness optimizers directly is non-trivial and costly due to the lack of oracle harnesses. To address this, we present a simple, low-cost design to directly evaluate them, namely priority ranking. By asking harness optimizers to rank components (e.g., tools) in a given harness by their potential to improve/hinder agent performance when updated, our design quantifies optimizer ability at the step level without expensive rollouts or manual examination. More importantly, optimizers' ranking performance correlates with their ability to improve agents in actual multi-step harness optimization, establishing priority ranking as a reliable predictor of optimization ability. Priority ranking is enabled by Shor, a collection of 182 human-verified optimization scenarios spanning across domains, designs, and time stages. Codes and data can be found at https://github.com/k59118/Harness_Optimizer_Evaluation.

</details>


### [400] [Search-E1: Self-Distillation Drives Self-Evolution in Search-Augmented Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22511)
*Zihan Liang,Yufei Ma,Ben Chen,Zhipeng Qian,Xuxin Zhang,Huangyu Dai,Lingtao Mao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Search-E1 proposes a self-evolution method for search-augmented language agents that uses only vanilla GRPO with offline self-distillation, without extra machinery like external supervision, reward models, or tree search, achieving strong results on QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors question whether the complex augmentations (external supervision, auxiliary modules, tree search, hand-crafted rewards) added on top of standard post-training pipelines for search-augmented reasoning agents are actually necessary, as each addition inflates the training pipeline and creates resource dependencies.

Method: Search-E1 iterates between GRPO rounds and offline self-distillation (OFSD). After each GRPO round, the policy rolls out on training questions. A token-level forward KL objective aligns the policy's inference-time distribution to its own distribution under a privileged context containing a more efficient sibling trajectory, providing dense per-step supervision naturally.

Result: On seven QA benchmarks, Search-E1 achieves 0.440 average EM with Qwen2.5-3B, surpassing all open-source baselines at both 3B and larger scales.

Conclusion: Complex machinery added to search-augmented reasoning agents may not be necessary; simpler self-evolution via GRPO + self-distillation can match or exceed performance, while keeping the pipeline lean and resource-efficient.

Abstract: Post-training has become the dominant recipe for turning a language model into a competent search-augmented reasoning agent. A line of recent work pushes its performance further by adding elaborate machinery on top of this standard pipeline. These augmentations import external supervision from stronger external systems, attach auxiliary modules such as process reward models or retrospective critics, restructure the rollout itself with tree search or multi-stage curricula, or shape the reward with hand-crafted bonuses and penalties. Each addition delivers a measurable gain, but each also inflates the training pipeline and ties the recipe to resources or designs that may not always be available. We take a step back and ask whether any of this machinery is actually necessary, and propose Search-E1, a self-evolution method that lets a search-augmented agent improve through only vanilla GRPO interleaved with offline self-distillation (OFSD). After each GRPO round, the policy rolls out on its own training questions. A token-level forward KL objective then aligns the policy's inference-time distribution to its own distribution under a privileged context that exposes a more efficient sibling trajectory. Despite this simplicity, the procedure naturally provides dense per-step supervision. On seven QA benchmarks, Search-E1 reaches $0.440$ average EM with Qwen2.5-3B, surpassing all open-source baselines at both scales. Code and complete version will be made public soon.

</details>


### [401] [Meta-Learning for Rapid Adaptation in Reference Tracking of Uncertain Nonlinear Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22513)
*Jiaqi Yan,Ankush Chakrabarty,Niklas Schmid,John Lygeros,Alisa Rupenyan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A meta-learning control framework using iMAML that leverages source system data to train controllers for target systems with limited data, demonstrated via neural state-space models and deep Q-networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collecting data from target systems is often challenging; the authors aim to design optimal controllers using limited target system data by leveraging offline data from similar source systems.

Method: Proposes a meta-learning control framework based on iMAML, operating in two phases: (1) offline meta-training learns shared dynamics from source data, (2) online meta-adaptation fine-tunes on target system with few samples. Formulated as bi-level optimization with reduced storage complexity. Two algorithms: neural state-space model (requires system ID) and deep Q-network (model-free).

Result: Numerical simulations and hardware experiments show enhanced control performance and consistent outperformance over baseline approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed meta-learning framework effectively transfers knowledge from source systems to target systems with limited data, and is flexible enough to integrate different learning algorithms.

Abstract: In this paper, we address the problem of reference tracking for uncertain nonlinear systems. Since collecting data from the target system (i.e., the system of interest) is often challenging, our objective is to design optimal controllers using limited target system data. Meta-learning provides a promising paradigm by leveraging offline data from source systems (systems sharing structural similarities with the target system) to accelerate training and enhance control performance. Motivated by this idea, we propose a meta-learning-based control framework that tailors the implicit model-agnostic meta-learning (iMAML) algorithm to the control setting. The framework operates in two phases: an (offline) meta-training phase, where an aggregated representation is learned from source data to capture the shared system dynamics among similar systems, and an (online) meta-adaptation phase, where this representation is fine-tuned on the target system using only a few data samples and limited adaptation steps. We formulate this framework as a bi-level optimization problem and provide an efficient solution with reduced storage complexity and few approximations. The proposed framework is general, allowing various learning algorithms to be integrated. To demonstrate this flexibility, we propose two specific learning algorithms that can be incorporated into our framework based on a neural state-space model and a deep Q-network, respectively. The primary distinction between these approaches is whether explicit system identification is required. Numerical simulations and hardware experiments demonstrate that the proposed methods enhance control performance and consistently outperform baseline approaches.

</details>


### [402] [A Subjective Logic-based method for runtime confidence updates in safety arguments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22530)
*Benjamin Herd,Jessica Kelly,Clarissa Heinemann,João-Vitor Zacchi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A method for dynamic quantitative assurance that enhances static safety cases with runtime confidence updates using Subjective Logic and Safety Performance Indicators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static safety cases lack runtime adaptability; confidence in safety claims should evolve with operational evidence, especially for ML-based components.

Method: Integrates design-time evidence and windowed runtime SPIs within a Subjective Logic-based assurance case, using a rule that increases confidence with no violations and imposes penalties when violations occur.

Result: Demonstrated via a simulation-based construction zone assist function with ML-based cone detection; shows how confidence evolves as SPI evidence is observed.

Conclusion: The method prioritizes safety-relevant responsiveness over exact Bayesian updates, enabling continuous confidence monitoring at runtime.

Abstract: We present a method for dynamic quantitative assurance that enhances static safety cases with continuous, runtime-driven confidence updates. The method quantifies and propagates confidence across the development lifecycle by integrating design-time evidence and windowed runtime Safety Performance Indicators (SPIs) within a single Subjective Logic (SL)-based assurance case. At runtime, SPI evidence is continuously evaluated, and targeted claims are updated using a rule that increases confidence in the absence of violations and imposes prompt penalties when violations occur. This design prioritizes safety-relevant responsiveness over exact classical Bayesian posterior updates. We demonstrate the method using a simulation-based construction zone assist function, focusing on an ML-based construction cone detection component, and show how confidence evolves as SPI evidence is observed in operation.

</details>


### [403] [TerminalWorld: Benchmarking Agents on Real-World Terminal Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22535)
*Zhaoyang Chu,Jiarui Hu,Xingyu Jiang,Pengyu Zou,Han Li,Chao Peng,Peter O'Hearn,Earl T. Barr,Mark Harman,Federica Sarro,He Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TerminalWorld is an automated data engine that creates a benchmark of 1,530 terminal-based evaluation tasks from 80,870 real-world terminal recordings, spanning 18 categories. Testing reveals current AI agents achieve only 62.5% max pass rate, with weak correlation to existing benchmarks (r=0.20).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing terminal benchmarks are expert-curated and may not capture real-world terminal usage diversity and authenticity. There is a need for scalable, authentic evaluation tasks derived from actual developer workflows.

Method: The authors built an automated data engine that processes 80,870 "in-the-wild" terminal recordings to reverse-engineer evaluation tasks, yielding a verified benchmark of 1,530 tasks (200 manually reviewed) across 18 real-world categories.

Result: Eight frontier models and six agents achieved a maximum pass rate of only 62.5% on TerminalWorld-Verified. The benchmark shows weak correlation (Pearson r=0.20) with existing expert-curated benchmarks like Terminal-Bench, indicating it captures distinct capabilities.

Conclusion: Current AI agents still struggle with authentic terminal workflows, and TerminalWorld provides a scalable, authentic benchmark that can evolve with developer practices for more realistic evaluation.

Abstract: We introduce TerminalWorld, a scalable data engine that automatically reverse-engineers high-fidelity evaluation tasks from "in-the-wild" terminal recordings. Processing 80,870 terminal recordings, the engine yields a full benchmark of 1,530 validated tasks, spanning 18 real-world categories, ranging from short everyday operations to workflows exceeding 50 steps, and covering 1,280 unique commands. From these, we curate a Verified subset of 200 representative, manually reviewed tasks. Comprehensive benchmarking on TerminalWorld-Verified across eight frontier models and six agents reveals that current systems still struggle with authentic terminal workflows, achieving a maximum pass rate of only 62.5%. Moreover, TerminalWorld captures real-world terminal capabilities distinct from existing expert-curated benchmarks (e.g., Terminal-Bench), with only a weak correlation to their scores (Pearson r=0.20). The automated engine makes TerminalWorld authentic and scalable by construction, enabling it to evaluate agents in real-world terminal environments as developer practices evolve. Data and code are available at https://github.com/EuniAI/TerminalWorld.

</details>


### [404] [Think Thrice Before You Speak: Dual knowledge-enhanced Theory-of-Mind Reasoning for Persuasive Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22602)
*Minghui Ma,Bin Guo,Runze Yang,Mengqi Chen,Yan Liu,Jingqi Liu,Yahan Pei,Xuehao Ma,Qiuyun Zhang,Zhiwen Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces the ToM-PD task for persuasive dialogue using the BDI framework, constructs the ToM-BPD dataset with fine-grained mental state annotations, and proposes the TTBYS stepwise reasoning framework that enhances LLMs' inference of desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies, achieving superior performance over GPT-5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLMs struggle with Theory of Mind reasoning in persuasive dialogues due to simple prompting strategies and insufficient ToM knowledge, leading to fragmented mental state representations and unstable reasoning.

Method: The authors propose the ToM-PD task grounded in the BDI framework, construct the ToM-BPD dataset, and introduce TTBYS (Think Thrice Before You Speak), a knowledge-enhanced stepwise reasoning framework leveraging explicit and implicit prior experiences.

Result: Qwen3-8B with TTBYS outperforms GPT-5 by 1.20% in desire prediction, 22.80% in belief prediction, and 16.97% in persuasive strategy prediction.

Conclusion: The TTBYS approach enhances interpretability and consistency in ToM reasoning for persuasive dialogues, demonstrating that structured stepwise reasoning with prior knowledge significantly improves LLM performance on mental state inference.

Abstract: Persuasive dialogue requires reasoning about others' latent mental states, a capability known as Theory of Mind (ToM). However, due to reliance on simple prompting strategies and insufficient ToM knowledge, existing LLMs often fail to capture the intrinsic dependencies among mental states, leading to fragmented representations and unstable reasoning. To address these challenges, we introduce the ToM-based Persuasive Dialogue (ToM-PD) task, grounded in the Belief-Desire-Intention (BDI) framework, which explicitly models the sequential dependencies among mental states in multi-turn dialogues. To facilitate research on this task, we construct a large-scale annotated dataset, ToM-based Broad Persuasive Dialogues (ToM-BPD), capturing fine-grained mental states and corresponding persuasive strategies. We further propose Think Thrice Before You Speak (TTBYS), a knowledge-enhanced stepwise reasoning framework that leverages both explicit and implicit prior experiences to improve LLMs' inference of desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies. Experimental results demonstrate that Qwen3-8B equipped with TTBYS outperforms GPT-5 by 1.20%, 22.80%, and 16.97% in predicting desires, beliefs, and persuasive strategies, respectively. Case studies further show that our approach enhances interpretability and consistency in reasoning.

</details>


### [405] [Spreadsheet-RL: Advancing Large Language Model Agents on Realistic Spreadsheet Tasks via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22642)
*Banghao Chi,Yining Xie,Mingyuan Wu,Jingcheng Yang,Jize Jiang,Zhaoheng Li,Shengyi Qian,Minjia Zhang,Klara Nahrstedt,Rui Hou,Xiangjun Fan,Hanchao Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Spreadsheet-RL is a reinforcement learning fine-tuning framework that trains specialized spreadsheet agents in a realistic Excel environment. It uses an automated pipeline to collect spreadsheet data from online forums, introduces the Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark, and provides a Spreadsheet Gym environment for multi-turn RL. The method improves Qwen3-4B-Thinking's Pass@1 from 12.0% to 23.4% on SpreadsheetBench and from 8.4% to 17.2% on domain-specific tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing spreadsheet agents rely on general-purpose LLMs with specialized prompting, which works for simple operations but fails on complex, multi-step real-world workflows. A more specialized, trainable approach is needed.

Method: Spreadsheet-RL includes (1) an automated pipeline for scalable collection of paired start-goal spreadsheets from online forums, (2) the Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark with finance and supply chain tasks, and (3) Spreadsheet Gym — a Python sandbox environment with extensive Excel functionality, comprehensive tool sets, and tool-routing rules for multi-turn RL fine-tuning.

Result: Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507's Pass@1 improved from 12.0% to 23.4% on SpreadsheetBench, and from 8.4% to 17.2% on the Domain-Spreadsheet dataset.

Conclusion: Spreadsheet-RL demonstrates strong potential for generalization and real-world adoption in spreadsheet automation, and shows promise for advancing LLM-based interactions with data interfaces in everyday work.

Abstract: Spreadsheet systems (e.g., Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets) play a central role in modern data-centric workflows. As AI agents grow increasingly capable of automating complex tasks, such as controlling computers and generating presentations, building an AI-driven spreadsheet agent has emerged as a promising research direction. Most existing spreadsheet agents rely on specialized prompting over general-purpose LLMs; while this design has potentials on simple spreadsheet operations, it struggles to manage the complex, multi-step workflows typical of real-world applications.
  We introduce Spreadsheet-RL, a reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning framework designed to train specialized spreadsheet agents within a realistic Microsoft Excel environment. Spreadsheet-RL features an automated pipeline for scalable collection of paired start-goal spreadsheets from online forums, as well as domain-specific evaluation tasks in areas such as finance and supply chain management, which we compile into the new Domain-Spreadsheet benchmark dataset. It also includes a Spreadsheet Gym environment designed for multi-turn RL: Spreadsheet Gym exposes extensive Excel functionality through a Python sandbox, along with a refined harness that incorporates a comprehensive tool set and carefully designed tool-routing rules for spreadsheet tasks. Through comprehensive experiments, we show that Spreadsheet-RL substantially enhances AI agent's performance on both general and domain-specific spreadsheet tasks: it improves Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507's Pass@1 on SpreadsheetBench from 12.0% to 23.4%, and raises Pass@1 from 8.4% to 17.2% on our curated Domain-Spreadsheet dataset. These results highlight Spreadsheet-RL's strong potential for generalization and real-world adoption in spreadsheet automation, and broadly, its promise for advancing LLM-based interactions with data interfaces in everyday work.

</details>


### [406] [AtelierEval: Agentic Evaluation of Humans & LLMs as Text-to-Image Prompters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22645)
*Hanjun Luo,Zhimu Huang,Sylvia Chung,Yiran Wang,Yingbin Jin,Jialin Li,Jiang Li,Xinfeng Li,Hanan Salam*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AtelierEval is the first unified benchmark that quantifies prompting proficiency for both humans and MLLMs across 360 expert-crafted tasks, using a skill-based evaluator (AtelierJudge) that achieves near-human correlation. Experiments reveal that mimicry outperforms planning in prompting, advocating for image-augmented prompting directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing T2I benchmarks only evaluate the downstream T2I models while ignoring the upstream prompters (humans or MLLMs) that translate user intent into prompts. There is no unified way to measure prompting proficiency.

Method: AtelierEval: a benchmark with 360 expert-crafted tasks grounded in cognitive view, spanning three task categories, with a dual interface for humans and MLLMs. AtelierJudge: a skill-based, memory-augmented agentic evaluator producing subjective and objective scores.

Result: AtelierJudge achieves Spearman correlation of 0.79 with human experts. Experiments benchmark 8 MLLMs vs 48 humans across 4 T2I backends, revealing mimicry outperforms planning in prompting tasks.

Conclusion: AtelierEval serves as a robust diagnostic tool for prompting proficiency. The findings advocate for image-augmented direction for future prompters, suggesting planning strategies need improvement over mimicry-based approaches.

Abstract: Text-to-image (T2I) systems increasingly rely on upstream prompters, either humans or multimodal large language models (MLLMs), to translate user intent into detailed prompts. Yet current benchmarks fix the prompt and only evaluate T2I models, leaving the prompting proficiency of this upstream component entirely unmeasured. We introduce AtelierEval, the first unified benchmark that quantifies prompting proficiency across 360 expert-crafted tasks. Grounded in a cognitive view, it spans three task categories and instantiates tasks using a taxonomy of real-world challenges, with a dual interface for both humans and MLLMs. To enable scalable and reliable evaluation, we propose AtelierJudge, a skill-based, memory-augmented agentic evaluator. It produces subjective and objective scores for prompt-image pairs, achieving a Spearman correlation of 0.79 with human experts, approaching human performance. Extensive experiments benchmark 8 MLLMs against 48 human users across 4 T2I backends, validate AtelierEval as a robust diagnostic tool, and reveal the superiority of mimicry over planning, advocating for an image-augmented direction for future prompters. Our work is released to support future research.

</details>


### [407] [Claw AI Lab: An Autonomous Multi-Agent Research Team](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22662)
*Fan Wu,Cheng Chen,Zhenshan Tan,Taiyu Zhang,Xinzhen Xu,Yanyu Qian,Dingcheng Gao,Lanyun Zhu,Qi Zhu,Yi Tan,Deyi Ji,Guosheng Lin,Tianrun Chen,Deheng Ye,Fayao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Claw AI Lab is an interactive autonomous research platform that replaces hidden prompt-to-paper pipelines with a steerable, dashboard-controlled AI laboratory featuring customizable research teams, multi-mode workflows, and a Claw-Code Harness for connecting code/data to experiments, outperforming baselines in novelty, experiment completeness, and paper quality across five AI case studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autonomous research systems operate as hidden prompt-to-paper pipelines centered around a single agent or fixed serial workflow, lacking steerability, inspectability, and practical integration with local codebases, datasets, and execution artifacts.

Method: Claw AI Lab instantiates a full research team from a single prompt with customizable roles and collaborative workflows, provides a unified dashboard for real-time monitoring, artifact inspection, and rollback/resume control, supports distinct research modes (exploration, multi-agent discussion, reproduction), and introduces a Claw-Code Harness that connects local codebases/datasets/checkpoints to runnable experiments while feeding execution artifacts back into the research loop.

Result: In internal evaluation on five AI research case studies using AutoResearchClaw as baseline, Claw AI Lab is consistently preferred by AI expert judges on idea novelty, experiment completeness, and paper presentation quality. The Claw-Code Harness improves execution integration, experimental completion, and result integrity, reducing partial runs and malformed result reporting.

Conclusion: Claw AI Lab represents an early step toward a new paradigm where autonomous research becomes usable, interactive, and reliability-aware scientific infrastructure, moving beyond opaque pipelines toward steerable laboratory-like systems.

Abstract: We present Claw AI Lab, a lab-native autonomous research platform that advances automated research from a hidden prompt-to-paper pipeline into an interactive AI laboratory. Rather than centering the system around a single agent or a fixed serial workflow, we allow users to instantiate a full research team from one prompt, with customizable roles, collaborative workflows, real-time monitoring, artifact inspection, and rollback/resume control through a unified dashboard. The platform also supports distinct research modes for exploration, multi-agent discussion, and reproduction, making autonomous research substantially more steerable and laboratory-like in practice. A key practical contribution of Claw AI Lab lies in its Claw-Code Harness, which connects local codebases, datasets, and checkpoints to runnable experiments and feeds execution artifacts back into the research loop. As a result, the harness improves not only execution integration, but also experimental completion and result integrity: experiments are easier to inspect, iterate on, and faithfully transfer into final papers, reducing common failure modes such as partial runs and malformed result reporting. In our internal evaluation on five AI research case studies, using AutoResearchClaw as the baseline, Claw AI Lab is consistently preferred by AI expert judges on idea novelty, experiment completeness, and paper presentation quality. We view Claw AI Lab as an early step toward a new paradigm: autonomous research as usable, interactive, and reliability-aware scientific infrastructure.

</details>


### [408] [WorkstreamBench: Evaluating LLM Agents on End-to-End Spreadsheet Tasks in Finance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22664)
*Thomson Yen,Julian Poeltl,Harshith Srinivas Gear,Yilin Meng,Joshua Fan,Adam Shen,Yili Liu,Ali Bauyrzhan,Siri Du,Haoyang Liu,Daniel Guetta,Hongseok Namkoong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates LLM agents on end-to-end spreadsheet generation for financial workflows, finding that even the best agents (Claude family) fall short of professional standards, especially as task complexity increases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing spreadsheet benchmarks focus on QA or single-formula edits, failing to measure agents' ability to produce complete, professional-grade artifacts from scratch—a capability increasingly expected in enterprise finance workflows like modeling and scenario analysis.

Method: The authors develop an evaluation taxonomy with three dimensions (Accuracy, Formula, Format) and fine-grained criteria reflecting professional finance standards, then benchmark frontier LLM agents on end-to-end spreadsheet tasks involving financial modeling and scenario analysis.

Result: Claude family leads the benchmark with the most professional-looking outputs, but all agents frequently fail to meet professional finance standards, and performance degrades sharply as task difficulty increases beyond a few chained calculations.

Conclusion: Current LLM agents are not yet capable of reliably producing professional-quality spreadsheets at the complexity level required by real-world financial workflows.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly expected to carry out end-to-end workflows, producing complete artifacts from high-level user instructions. To meet enterprise needs, frontier AI labs have developed agents that can construct entire spreadsheets from scratch. This is especially relevant in finance, where core workflows such as financial modeling, forecasting, and scenario analysis are commonly conducted through spreadsheets. Yet, existing spreadsheet benchmarks do not measure this advanced capability, focusing instead on question-answering or single-formula edits. To address this gap, we provide one of the first evaluations of agents on end-to-end spreadsheet tasks, focusing on economically critical financial workflows such as modeling and scenario analysis. Since deliverables therein are routinely reviewed and revised by multiple stakeholders, judging their quality necessarily involves high-level criteria such as readability or ease of modification. To reflect the multidimensional nature of solution quality, we develop an evaluation taxonomy comprising three dimensions: Accuracy, Formula, and Format, each comprising fine-grained criteria that reflect professional standards. The Claude family leads the benchmark and produces the most professional-looking outputs in our qualitative review, but even the strongest agents frequently fall short of professional finance standards and degrade sharply as the difficulty increases beyond a few chained calculations. This suggests that current agents are not yet able to reliably produce professional-quality spreadsheets at the level of complexity real-world workflows demand.

</details>


### [409] [Is Capability a Liability? More Capable Language Models Make Worse Forecasts When It Matters Most](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22672)
*Nick Merrill,Jaeho Lee,Ezra Karger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit inverse scaling on forecasting problems with superlinear growth and tail risk (e.g., epidemics, finance): more capable models produce worse distributional forecasts, especially at the upper tail. Standard single-threshold metrics mask this failure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To identify and document cases where larger/more capable LLMs perform worse on forecasting tasks, particularly those involving superlinear growth and tail risk (common in finance and epidemiology), and to show that standard evaluation metrics hide this failure.

Method: Created ForecastBench-Sim (FBSim) with synthetic SIR epidemics and linear controls; tested on COVID-19, measles, housing, hyperinflation datasets. Used per-quantile decomposition, within-family study (Llama-3.1), and compared single-threshold vs. tail-inclusive scoring.

Result: Inverse scaling observed: larger models over-predict upper tails due to aggressive growth extrapolation. Post-training also contributes. Single-threshold metrics (common benchmarks) reverse the sign of capability-accuracy relationship; tail-inclusive scoring reveals the true degradation.

Conclusion: LLM forecasting evaluations should adopt continuous, unbounded accuracy measures alongside bounded binary threshold metrics to avoid missing upper-tail cost.

Abstract: We document inverse scaling in LLMs on forecasting problems whose underlying time series exhibit superlinear growth and tail risk of regime change, a structure common in finance and epidemiology. On these tasks, more capable models produce worse distributional forecasts. The pattern appears on ForecastBench-Sim (FBSim), a contamination-free, simulated-world benchmark we release, in forecasting synthetic SIR epidemics with a matched linear control, and replicates in real-world datasets on COVID-19, measles, housing markets, and hyperinflation. A per-quantile decomposition shows the failure concentrates at the upper tail, which more capable models shift upward to track aggressive extrapolations of growth, while the lower tail stays put. A within-family study of Llama-3.1 shows that both model scale and post-training independently contribute to this effect. Domain knowledge does not reliably rescue calibration. This inverse scaling does not appear on single-threshold metrics common in LLM forecasting benchmarks, reversing the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on identical outputs. Single-threshold scoring at conventional cutoffs misses the upper-tail cost; tail-inclusive scoring reverses the sign of the capability--accuracy relationship on the same outputs. We recommend that LLM forecasting evaluations use continuous (and unbounded) measures of accuracy alongside bounded binary threshold metrics.

</details>


### [410] [Forecasting Scientific Progress with Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22681)
*Sean Wu,Pan Lu,Yupeng Chen,Jonathan Bragg,Yutaro Yamada,Peter Clark,David Clifton,Philip Torr,James Zou,Junchi Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces CUSP, a benchmark evaluating whether AI systems can forecast scientific progress. Testing frontier models on 4,760 scientific events across disciplines, they find that current AI systems fail to reliably predict scientific advances, show systematic overconfidence, and benefit more from hindsight information than forward-looking prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether AI systems, despite being increasingly embedded in scientific discovery, can actually anticipate scientific progress—a question that remains unclear and has significant implications for the future of AI-assisted research.

Method: The authors introduce CUSP (Cutoff-conditioned Unseen Scientific Progress), a multi-disciplinary benchmark evaluating AI scientific forecasting through four dimensions: feasibility assessment, mechanistic reasoning, generative solution design, and temporal prediction. They test frontier models on 4,760 scientific events across domains, controlling for knowledge cutoff effects.

Result: Current frontier models show systematic and domain-dependent limitations. They can identify plausible research directions but fail to reliably predict whether scientific advances will be realized and systematically misestimate their timing. Performance varies across domains (AI progress is more predictable than biology/chemistry/physics), is insensitive to training cutoff, and models exhibit overconfidence and response biases.

Conclusion: Current AI systems fall short as predictive tools for scientific progress. Access to prior knowledge does not translate into reliable forecasting, and models benefit more from post-event information than from genuine forward-looking prediction, suggesting fundamental limitations in their ability to anticipate scientific advances.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly embedded in scientific discovery, yet whether it can anticipate scientific progress remains unclear. To study this question, we introduce a temporally grounded evaluation framework for forecasting scientific progress under controlled knowledge constraints. We present CUSP (Cutoff-conditioned Unseen Scientific Progress), a multi-disciplinary and event-level benchmark that evaluates scientific forecasting in AI systems through feasibility assessment, mechanistic reasoning, generative solution design, and temporal prediction. Across 4,760 scientific events, we observe systematic and domain-dependent limitations in current frontier models. While models can identify plausible research directions from competing candidates, they fail to reliably predict whether scientific advances will be realized and systematically misestimate when they will occur. Performance is highly heterogeneous across domains, with the timing of AI progress more predictable than advances in biology, chemistry, and physics. Performance is largely insensitive to whether events occur before or after the training cutoff, suggesting these limitations cannot be explained solely by knowledge exposure in training data. Under controlled information access, additional pre-cutoff knowledge improves performance but does not close the gap to full-information settings, which becomes more pronounced for high-citation advances. Models also exhibit systematic overconfidence and strong response biases, indicating unreliable uncertainty estimation. Taken together, current AI systems fall short as predictive tools for scientific progress. Access to prior knowledge does not translate into reliable forecasting, and performance benefits more from post-event information than from forward-looking prediction.

</details>


### [411] [Beyond the Org Chart: AI and the Transformation of Invisible Work](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22707)
*Stephanie Rosenthal,Shamsi Iqbal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A qualitative study of 24 product professionals at a large tech firm reveals that AI adoption is reshaping both formal job roles and informal cultural practices like mentoring, creating smoother collaboration but risking career growth opportunities like feedback and mentorship.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how AI adoption is changing work processes, role boundaries, and professional interactions in an AI-forward company, particularly the less visible informal cultural practices.

Method: Semi-structured interviews with 24 product-focused individuals at a large technology firm about AI's impact on their work, team dynamics, and professional interactions.

Result: AI changes both formal role responsibilities/collaborations and informal cultural practices like mentoring. Positive outcomes include smoother peer collaboration; risks include reduced feedback from professional networks and diminished leadership/mentorship opportunities.

Conclusion: Companies should make invisible work more visible, while individuals and leaders need to actively support colleagues through AI transformation to preserve healthy cultures that foster diverse thinking, collaboration, and informal interactions.

Abstract: An increasing number of news and research articles report that AI adoption is allowing professionals to blur and extend the boundaries of their corporate roles. With the goal of understanding how work processes might be changing in an AI-forward company, we interviewed 24 product-focused individuals at a large technology firm about how AI has impacted their own work, their work within their product team, and their professional interactions. Our conversations suggest that AI is not only changing formal role responsibilities and collaborations between those roles, but also changing informal cultural practices like professional mentoring that are key to helping professionals settle in their positions, stay engaged with their work, and grow their careers. Some of these changes are positive, such as smoother collaboration between peers, but other changes are more nuanced and put the typical career growth opportunities, like receiving feedback from professional networks and promoting leadership and mentorship, at risk. We propose steps that AI companies can take to make the invisible work more visible. Additionally, we propose efforts that individuals and leaders can take to support their colleagues through AI transformation while preserving healthy company cultures that support diverse thinking, collaboration, and informal interactions.

</details>


### [412] [AMEL: Accumulated Message Effects on LLM Judgments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22714)
*Sid-ali Temkit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prior conversation history biases LLM evaluations—negative history induces 1.62x more bias than positive, and the effect is driven by uncertain items, not context length, with scaling helping but not eliminating it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether the polarity of prior conversation history biases subsequent LLM judgments, especially in automated evaluation pipelines where many items pass through one conversation.

Method: 75,898 API calls to 11 models from 4 providers; identical test items presented in isolation vs. after histories saturated with positive/negative evaluations; analyzed via entropy, context length, position, and token-level probability distributions.

Result: Models shift toward conversation polarity (d=-0.17, p<10^-46); effect concentrated on high-entropy items (d=-0.34); negativity asymmetry (1.62x more bias); bias doesn't grow with context length; scaling reduces but doesn't eliminate bias.

Conclusion: The simplest fix is a fresh context per item; when batching is unavoidable, balancing the history helps.

Abstract: Large language models are routinely used as automated evaluators: to review code, moderate content, or score outputs, often with many items passing through one conversation. We ask whether the polarity of prior conversation history biases subsequent judgments, an effect we call the accumulated message effect on LLM judgments (AMEL). Across 75,898 API calls to 11 models from 4 providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and four open-source models), we present identical test items in isolation or following histories saturated with predominantly positive or negative evaluations.
  Models shift toward the conversation's prevailing polarity (d = -0.17, p < 10^-46). The effect concentrates on items where the model is genuinely uncertain at baseline (d = -0.34 for high-entropy items, vs d = -0.15 when the baseline is deterministic). Bias does not grow with context length: 5 prior turns and 50 produce the same shift (Spearman |r| < 0.01; OLS slope p = 0.80). And there is a negativity asymmetry: paired per item, negative histories induce 1.62x more bias than positive (t = 13.46, p < 10^-39, n = 2,481). Scaling helps but does not solve it (Anthropic: Haiku -0.22 to Opus -0.17; OpenAI: Nano -0.34 to GPT-5.2 -0.17).
  Three follow-ups narrow the mechanism. The token probability distribution shifts continuously, not at a threshold. The negativity asymmetry has both token-level and semantic components, though attributing the balance is exploratory at our sample sizes. Position does not matter: five biased turns anywhere in a 50-turn history produce the same shift. The simplest fix for evaluation pipelines is a fresh context per item; when batching is unavoidable, balancing the history helps.

</details>


### [413] [Parametric Modular Answer Set Programs Made Declarative](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22716)
*Jorge Fandinno,Yuliya Lierler,Torsten Schaub*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces parametric modular logic programs for modular first-order ASP, capturing clingo-programs with collective control and providing theoretical foundations connecting to traditional non-modular ASP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable modularity in first-order answer set programming by introducing subprograms with parameters and intensionality, supporting structured program design and reuse.

Method: Introduces parametric modular logic programs with parameters and intensionality statements; formalizes semantics to capture clingo-programs with collective control.

Result: Demonstrates that the formalism captures clingo-programs with collective control and establishes theoretical connections to traditional non-modular ASP.

Conclusion: Parametric modular logic programs provide a solid theoretical foundation for modular ASP, enabling structured, reusable subprograms while maintaining links to standard ASP semantics.

Abstract: In this paper, we explore the concept of modularity in first-order answer set programming (ASP). We introduce a new formalism called parametric modular logic programs, which allows defining subprograms with parameters and intensionality statements. We demonstrate how this formalism can capture the semantics of clingo-programs with collective control, a feature that enables structuring and instantiating subprograms. We provide theoretical foundations for modular ASP, illustrate its usefulness, and connect to traditional non-modular ASP.

</details>


### [414] [Can AI Make Conflicts Worse? An Alignment Failure in LLM Deployment Across Conflict Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22720)
*Andrii Kryshtal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper tests AI models from four providers across 90 conflict-related scenarios and finds failure rates of 6-47% in recognizing atrocities, genocide denial, and ethnic slurs, with model choice being a critical safety concern.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI models are already deployed in conflict-affected societies with no established practice to check if their outputs worsen armed conflicts.

Method: Tested 9 model configurations from OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, and xAI on 90 multi-turn scenarios designed to surface misaligned behavior in conflict contexts.

Result: Failure rates range from 6% to 47% across models; when users pushed for "balance" in cases with international court rulings, five of nine configurations failed 80-100% of the time.

Conclusion: Model choice is a safety question; the authors release the first evaluation framework for this domain and propose adding it to alignment evaluation portfolios.

Abstract: AI models are already deployed in societies affected by armed conflict, and journalists, humanitarian workers, governments and ordinary citizens rely on them for information or for their work processes. No established practice exists for checking whether their outputs can make those conflicts worse. We tested nine model configurations from four providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, xAI) on 90 multi-turn scenarios designed to surface misaligned behaviour in conflict contexts: false equivalence between documented atrocities, denial of genocide, and failure to recognise ethnic slurs, among others. When such outputs feed into journalism, humanitarian reporting, or public debate, they can deepen divisions in fragile societies. Failure rates span 6\% to 47\% between the best and worst performing models, which makes model choice a safety question in its own right and when users pushed for ``balance'' in cases where international courts have already assigned responsibility, five of nine configurations failed 80 to 100 percent of the time. We release the first evaluation framework for this domain and propose adding it to alignment evaluation portfolios.

</details>


### [415] [Beyond Acoustic Emotion Recognition: Multimodal Pathos Analysis in Political Speech Using LLM-Based and Acoustic Emotion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22732)
*Juergen Dietrich*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper compares acoustic emotion recognition models with LLM-based multimodal analysis for measuring Pathos in political speech, finding that Gemini 2.5 Flash Valence correlates strongly with TRUST-Pathos (rho=+0.664, p<0.001) while acoustic emotion2vec Valence does not (rho=+0.097, p=0.499). The study also reveals limitations of standard SER benchmark corpora (acted speech, cultural bias, category incompatibility).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether acoustic emotion recognition models can effectively serve as proxies for the Pathos dimension in political speech analysis, as operationalised by the TRUST multi-agent LLM pipeline.

Method: Case study using a Bundestag plenary speech by Felix Banaszak (51 segments, 245s). Three modalities compared: (1) emotion2vec_plus_large SER model with Russell Circumplex projection; (2) Gemini 2.5 Flash analyzing full speech audio + transcript; (3) TRUST-Pathos scores from three-advocate LLM supervisor ensemble. Spearman rank correlations and EMO-DB quality evaluation.

Result: Gemini Valence correlates strongly with TRUST-Pathos (rho=+0.664, p<0.001), but emotion2vec Valence does not (rho=+0.097, p=0.499). Standard SER benchmarks suffer from acted speech, cultural bias, and category incompatibility.

Conclusion: LLM-based multimodal analysis captures semantically defined political emotion substantially better than acoustic models alone, though acoustic features remain useful for low-level Arousal estimation. Future work will extend to video-based analysis (facial expression, gaze).

Abstract: We investigate whether acoustic emotion recognition models can serve as proxies for the Pathos dimension in political speech analysis, as operationalised by the TRUST multi-agent large language model (LLM) pipeline. Using a Bundestag plenary speech by Felix Banaszak (51 segments, 245 s) as a case study, we compare three analysis modalities: (1) emotion2vec_plus_large, an acoustic speech emotion recognition (SER) model whose continuous Arousal and Valence values are derived via post-hoc Russell Circumplex projection; (2) Gemini 2.5 Flash, an LLM analysing the full speech audio together with its transcript in an open-ended, context-aware fashion; and (3) TRUST-Pathos scores from a three-advocate LLM supervisor ensemble. Spearman rank correlations reveal that Gemini Valence correlates strongly with TRUST-Pathos (rho = +0.664, p < 0.001), whereas emotion2vec Valence does not (rho = +0.097, p = 0.499). We further demonstrate, via a systematic quality evaluation of the Berlin Database of Emotional Speech (EMO-DB) using Gemini in an open-ended annotation paradigm, that standard SER benchmark corpora suffer from acted speech, cultural bias, and category incompatibility. Our results suggest that LLM-based multimodal analysis captures semantically defined political emotion substantially better than acoustic models alone, while acoustic features remain informative for low-level Arousal estimation. Future work will extend this approach to video-based analysis incorporating facial expression and gaze.

</details>


### [416] [HarnessAPI: A Skill-First Framework for Unified Streaming APIs and MCP Tools](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22733)
*Edwin Jose*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HarnessAPI is a Python framework that eliminates duplication between HTTP endpoints and MCP tool registrations by deriving both from a single typed skill folder, reducing boilerplate by 74%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Python functions deployed as LLM tools require two separate implementations: one HTTP endpoint for humans/CI and one MCP tool for agent runtimes (Claude, Cursor). These representations share business logic but diverge in routing, validation, serialization, streaming, and schema maintenance, causing maintenance drift as code evolves.

Method: Uses a typed skill folder (handler.py + Pydantic schemas) as the single source of truth. Automatically derives a streaming HTTP endpoint with SSE, an interactive OpenAPI/Swagger UI, and a zero-config MCP tool from one process. Employs dual-mode content negotiation and dynamic code-generation to propagate Pydantic type annotations to FastMCP's inspection layer.

Result: Measured across six representative skills using cloc, HarnessAPI reduces framework-facing boilerplate by 74% compared to a manually maintained dual-stack implementation (FastAPI server + FastMCP server).

Conclusion: HarnessAPI subclasses FastAPI, inheriting its full middleware, dependency-injection, and deployment ecosystem. It is available open-source on GitHub and PyPI, offering a practical solution to the dual-implementation problem for LLM tool deployment.

Abstract: Every Python function deployed as an LLM tool must today exist in two forms: an HTTP endpoint for human-facing clients and CI pipelines, and an MCP tool registration for agent runtimes such as Claude and Cursor. These representations share business logic yet diverge in all the surrounding machinery (routing, validation, serialisation, streaming, and schema maintenance), and they drift apart as the underlying code evolves. We present HarnessAPI, a Python framework that eliminates this duplication by treating a typed skill folder as the single source of truth. From one handler.py plus Pydantic schemas, the framework automatically derives a streaming HTTP endpoint with Server-Sent Events, an interactive OpenAPI/Swagger UI, and a zero-configuration MCP tool, all served from a single process. Dual-mode content negotiation lets the same handler serve SSE-streaming and JSON-returning clients with no handler changes. A dynamic code-generation mechanism ensures Pydantic type annotations propagate correctly to FastMCP's inspection layer, resolving a technical limitation that prevents naive closure-based registration. Measured across six representative skills using cloc, HarnessAPI reduces framework-facing boilerplate by 74% compared with a manually maintained dual-stack implementation (FastAPI server + FastMCP server). HarnessAPI subclasses FastAPI, inheriting its full middleware, dependency-injection, and deployment ecosystem. It is available at https://github.com/edwinjosechittilappilly/harnessapi and on PyPI (pip install harnessapi)

</details>


### [417] [Towards a General Intelligence and Interface for Wearable Health Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22759)
*Girish Narayanswamy,Maxwell A. Xu,A. Ali Heydari,Samy Abdel-Ghaffar,Marius Guerard,Kara Vaillancourt,Zhihan Zhang,Jake Garrison,Levi Albuquerque,Dimitris Spathis,Hong Yu,Hamid Palangi,Xuhai "Orson" Xu,David G. T. Barrett,Joseph Breda,Jed McGiffin,Yubin Kim,Yuwei Zhang,Naghmeh Rezaei,Samuel Solomon,Karan Ahuja,Tim Althoff,Jake Sunshine,Ming-Zher Poh,Benjamin Yetton,Ari Winbush,Nicholas B. Allen,James M. Rehg,Isaac Galatzer-Levy,Yun Liu,John Hernandez,Anupam Pathak,Conor Heneghan,Yuzhe Yang,Ahmed A. Metwally,Pushmeet Kohli,Mark Malhotra,Shwetak Patel,Xin Liu,Daniel McDuff*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents a foundation model for wearable health data, pretrained on over a trillion minutes of unlabeled sensor signals from 5 million participants, demonstrating that scaling model capacity and data volume improves performance across 35 health prediction tasks (cardiovascular, metabolic, sleep, mental health). It enables few-shot learning, uses LLM agents to optimize downstream predictors, and integrates into a Personal Health Agent validated by clinician ratings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transforming wearable sensor signals into personalized health insights is challenging due to high phenotypic diversity, individual physiological variation, and scarcity of labeled data (as collecting annotations is laborious and expensive).

Method: (1) Pretrained a foundation model on >1 trillion minutes of unlabeled sensor signals from 5M participants. (2) Evaluated on 35 health prediction tasks. (3) Used LLM agents to autonomously search for optimal downstream predictive heads. (4) Integrated predictors into a Personal Health Agent validated by 1,860 clinician ratings.

Result: Joint scaling of model capacity and pretraining data leads to systematic improvements across 35 diverse health tasks. The representation enables label-efficient few-shot learning and generative daily metric estimation. LLM agents improve downstream predictor performance, and the Personal Health Agent achieves more relevant, contextually aware, and safe responses per clinician ratings.

Conclusion: A large-scale pretrained foundation model on unlabeled wearable sensor data can overcome label scarcity and individual variation, unlocking powerful few-shot and generative capabilities for personalized health monitoring, augmented by LLM-driven optimization and clinician-validated safety.

Abstract: While ubiquitous wearable sensors capture a wealth of behavioral and physiological information, effectively transforming these signals into personalized health insights is challenging. Specifically, converting low-level sensor data into representations capable of characterizing higher-level states is difficult due to high phenotypic diversity and variation in individual baseline health, physiology, and lifestyle factors. Moreover, collecting wearable data paired with health outcome annotations is laborious and expensive, and retrospective annotation remains practically unfeasible, contributing to a scarcity of data with high-quality labels. To overcome these limitations, we propose a foundation model for wearable health that is pretrained on more than one trillion minutes of unlabeled sensor signals drawn from a large cohort of five million participants. We demonstrate that the joint scaling of model capacity and pretraining data volume leads to systematic improvements in performance, as evaluated on a diverse set of 35 health prediction tasks, spanning cardiovascular, metabolic, sleep, and mental health, as well as lifestyle choices and demographic factors. We find that this population scale representation unlocks label-efficient few-shot learning and generative capabilities for robust daily metric estimation. To further leverage this learned representation, we deploy a classroom of LLM agents to autonomously search the space of downstream predictive heads built on the model embeddings, showing broad performance improvements that increase with LLM model capacity. Finally, we show how integrating these downstream predictors into a Personal Health Agent can support model responses that are more relevant, contextually aware, and safe, and we validate this via 1,860 ratings from a cohort of clinicians.

</details>


### [418] [Advancing Mathematics Research with AI-Driven Formal Proof Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22763)
*George Tsoukalas,Anton Kovsharov,Sergey Shirobokov,Anja Surina,Moritz Firsching,Gergely Bérczi,Francisco J. R. Ruiz,Arun Suggala,Adam Zsolt Wagner,Eric Wieser,Lei Yu,Aja Huang,Miklós Z. Horváth,Andrew Ferrauiolo,Henryk Michalewski,Codrut Grosu,Thomas Hubert,Matej Balog,Pushmeet Kohli,Swarat Chaudhuri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An LLM-powered agent using Lean formal verification autonomously solved 9 out of 353 open Erdős problems, proved 44/492 OEIS conjectures, and is being deployed across multiple math research domains at modest cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs show increasing mathematical reasoning ability but remain unreliable for open research problems; formal proof verification in Lean offers a pathway to trustable AI-assisted mathematics.

Method: A framework combining LLM-based proof generation with Lean formal verification; an advanced agent outperforms a basic alternating agent, especially on hard problems, with cost analysis provided.

Result: 9/353 open Erdős problems solved, 44/492 OEIS conjectures proved; advanced agent cost-effective (~$100s/problem); basic agent replicated successes but was costlier on hardest problems.

Conclusion: AI-aided formal proof search is a viable and powerful approach for attacking open mathematical problems, with agent design significantly impacting cost and success rate.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly excel at mathematical reasoning, but their unreliability limits their utility in mathematics research. A mitigation is using LLMs to generate formal proofs in languages like Lean. We perform the first large-scale evaluation of this method's ability to solve open problems. Our most capable agent autonomously resolved 9 of 353 open Erdős problems at the per-problem cost of a few hundred dollars, proved 44/492 OEIS conjectures, and is being deployed in combinatorics, optimization, graph theory, algebraic geometry, and quantum optics research. A basic agent alternating LLM-based generation with Lean-based verification replicated the Erdős successes but proved costlier on the hardest problems. These findings demonstrate the power of AI-aided formal proof search and shed light on the agent designs that enable it.

</details>


### [419] [Deep Reinforcement Learning for Flexible Job Shop Scheduling with Random Job Arrivals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22773)
*Yu Tang,Muhammad Zakwan,Efe Balta,John Lygeros,Alisa Rupenyan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes an event-based Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) approach using Proximal Policy Optimization and Multi-Layer Perceptrons to solve the Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (FJSP) with random job arrivals, outperforming individual dispatching rules and an MILP benchmark on heterogeneous datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: FJSP faces two key challenges: unpredictable arrival of future jobs and combinatorial complexity, making it intractable for conventional MILP solvers.

Method: Uses Proximal Policy Optimization with lightweight Multi-Layer Perceptrons as the DRL agent, with state representations directly from the environment and action selection limited to established dispatching rules.

Result: The DRL approach outperforms individual dispatching rules across datasets with varying heterogeneity and job arrival rates, and achieves good performance against an arrival-triggered MILP solution, especially on heterogeneous datasets.

Conclusion: Event-based DRL is effective for FJSP with random job arrivals, particularly excelling in heterogeneous settings.

Abstract: The Flexible Job Shop Scheduling Problem (FJSP) is the optimal allocation of a set of jobs to machines. Two primary challenges persist in FJSP: the unpredictable arrival of future jobs and the combinatorial complexity of the problem, rendering it intractable for conventional mixed-integer linear programming solvers. This paper proposes an event-based \gls{DRL} approach to solve FJSP with random job arrivals. Specifically, we employ the Proximal Policy Optimization algorithm and use lightweight Multi-Layer Perceptrons to train the \gls{DRL} agent for minimizing the total completion time of all jobs. We design the state representation to be directly accessible from the environment, and limit the learning agent to selecting from among a set of well-established dispatching rules. Simulations show that our \gls{DRL} approach outperforms any of the individual dispatching rules on datasets with varying heterogeneity and job arrival rates. We benchmark our \gls{DRL} against an arrival-triggered mixed-integer linear programming solution and show that our method achieves good performance especially when the datasets are heterogeneous.

</details>


### [420] [LCGuard: Latent Communication Guard for Safe KV Sharing in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22786)
*Sadia Asif,Mohammad Mohammadi Amiri,Momin Abbas,Prasanna Sattigeri,Karthikeyan Natesan Ramamurthy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LCGuard is a framework that protects against sensitive information leakage in KV-cache-based latent communication between LLM agents by learning representation-level transformations that preserve task semantics while reducing reconstructable sensitive information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV caches in multi-agent LLM systems encode contextual inputs, reasoning states, and agent-specific information, creating an opaque channel where sensitive content may propagate across agents without explicit textual disclosure.

Method: Formalizes leakage through reconstruction (unsafe if adversarial decoder recovers sensitive inputs), uses adversarial training where adversary learns to reconstruct sensitive inputs while LCGuard learns transformations that preserve task semantics and reduce reconstructable information.

Result: Consistently reduces reconstruction-based leakage and attack success rates while maintaining competitive task performance across multiple model families and multi-agent benchmarks.

Conclusion: LCGuard effectively safeguards latent communication in multi-agent LLM systems without compromising task performance.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems increasingly rely on intermediate communication to coordinate complex tasks. While most existing systems communicate through natural language, recent work shows that latent communication, particularly through transformer key-value (KV) caches, can improve efficiency and preserve richer task-relevant information. However, KV caches also encode contextual inputs, intermediate reasoning states, and agent-specific information, creating an opaque channel through which sensitive content may propagate across agents without explicit textual disclosure. To address this, we introduce \textbf{LCGuard} (Latent Communication Guard), a framework for safe KV-based latent communication in multi-agent LLM systems. LCGuard treats shared KV caches as latent working memory and learns representation-level transformations before cache artifacts are transmitted across agents. We formalize representation-level sensitive information leakage operationally through reconstruction: a shared cache artifact is unsafe if an adversarial decoder can recover agent-specific sensitive inputs from it. This leads to an adversarial training formulation in which the adversary learns to reconstruct sensitive inputs, while LCGuard learns transformations that preserve task-relevant semantics and reduce reconstructable information. Empirical evaluations across multiple model families and multi-agent benchmarks show that LCGuard consistently reduces reconstruction-based leakage and attack success rates while maintaining competitive task performance compared to standard KV-sharing baselines.

</details>


### [421] [Gated DeltaNet-2: Decoupling Erase and Write in Linear Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22791)
*Ali Hatamizadeh,Yejin Choi,Jan Kautz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Gated DeltaNet-2 is a novel linear attention model that separates erase and write gates (channel-wise) to overcome the scalar tie limitation in prior delta-rule models like Gated DeltaNet and KDA, achieving state-of-the-art results at 1.3B scale on language modeling, reasoning, and especially long-context retrieval tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior delta-rule linear attention models (Gated DeltaNet, KDA) use a single scalar gate to control both erasing old content and writing new content, which is a fundamental limitation. The paper aims to decouple these two operations for more precise memory editing.

Method: Introduces separate channel-wise erase gate (b_t) and channel-wise write gate (w_t), generalizing both Gated DeltaNet and KDA. Derives a fast-weight update, a chunkwise WY algorithm with asymmetric erase factors for channel-wise decay, and a gate-aware backward pass for efficient parallel training.

Result: At 1.3B parameters trained on 100B FineWeb-Edu tokens, Gated DeltaNet-2 outperforms Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet, KDA, and Mamba-3 across language modeling, commonsense reasoning, and retrieval. Gains are most significant on long-context RULER multi-key retrieval benchmarks.

Conclusion: Decoupling erase and write gates with channel-wise granularity is an effective improvement for delta-rule linear attention, providing stronger performance especially on long-context tasks while maintaining efficient training via the proposed chunkwise algorithm and backward pass.

Abstract: Linear attention replaces the unbounded cache of softmax attention with a fixed-size recurrent state, reducing sequence mixing to linear time and decoding to constant memory. The hard part is not just what to forget, but how to edit this compressed memory without scrambling existing associations. Delta-rule models subtract the current read before writing a new value, and Kimi Delta Attention (KDA) sharpens forgetting with channel-wise decay. But the active edit still uses a single scalar gate to control two different things: how much old content to erase on the key side and how much new content to commit on the value side. We introduce Gated DeltaNet-2, which generalizes both Gated DeltaNet and KDA by inheriting adaptive forgetting and channel-wise decay while addressing their shared limitation, the scalar tie between erasing and writing. Gated Delta Rule-2 separates these roles with a channel-wise erase gate b_t and a channel-wise write gate w_t, reducing to KDA when both gates collapse to the same scalar and to Gated DeltaNet when the decay also collapses. We derive a fast-weight update view, a chunkwise WY algorithm with channel-wise decay absorbed into asymmetric erase factors, and a gate-aware backward pass that preserves efficient parallel training. At 1.3B parameters trained on 100B FineWeb-Edu tokens, Gated DeltaNet-2 achieves the strongest overall results among Mamba-2, Gated DeltaNet, KDA, and Mamba-3 variants across language modeling, commonsense reasoning, and retrieval. Its advantage is most pronounced on long-context RULER needle-in-a-haystack benchmarks, where it improves the evaluated multi-key retrieval setting and remains strong in both recurrent and hybrid settings. Code is available at https://github.com/NVlabs/GatedDeltaNet-2.

</details>


### [422] [MOSS: Self-Evolution through Source-Level Rewriting in Autonomous Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22794)
*Qianshu Cai,Yonggang Zhang,Xianzhang Jia,Wei Xue,Jun Song,Xinmei Tian,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOSS is a self-evolving agent system that performs source-level self-rewriting on production agentic substrates, using deterministic pipelines and verified candidate rollouts to fix structural failures that text-only approaches cannot reach, achieving a mean grader score improvement from 0.25 to 0.61 on OpenClaw.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current autonomous agents are static post-deployment and cannot learn from interactions. Self-evolving agents exist but only modify text-mutable artifacts (skills, prompts, memory), leaving structural code (routing, hooks, state invariants) untouched. This limits the scope of evolution and leaves an entire class of failures unreachable.

Method: MOSS performs source-level self-rewriting via a deterministic multi-stage pipeline: (1) automatically curated batch of production-failure evidence, (2) code modification delegated to a pluggable external coding-agent CLI while MOSS controls stage ordering and verdicts, (3) candidate verification by replaying the failure batch in ephemeral trial workers, (4) user-consent-gated in-place container swap with health-probe-gated rollback.

Result: On OpenClaw, MOSS improves the mean grader score from 0.25 to 0.61 across four tasks in a single cycle without human intervention.

Conclusion: Source-level adaptation is a more general medium for agent evolution than text-mutable artifacts — it is Turing-complete, a superset of all text scopes, deterministic, and stable under long-context drift. MOSS demonstrates that structural agentic failures can be autonomously fixed through source self-rewriting with safe deployment rollouts.

Abstract: Autonomous agentic systems are largely static after deployment: they do not learn from user interactions, and recurring failures persist until the next human-driven update ships a fix. Self-evolving agents have emerged in response, but all confine evolution to text-mutable artifacts -- skill files, prompt configurations, memory schemas, workflow graphs -- and leave the agent harness untouched. Since routing, hook ordering, state invariants, and dispatch live in code rather than in any text artifact, an entire class of structural failure is physically unreachable from the text layer. We argue that source-level adaptation is a fundamentally more general medium: it is Turing-complete, a strict superset of every text-mutable scope, takes effect deterministically rather than through base-model compliance, and does not erode under long-context drift. We present MOSS, a system that performs self-rewriting at the source level on production agentic substrates. Each evolution is anchored to an automatically curated batch of production-failure evidence and proceeds through a deterministic multi-stage pipeline; code modification is delegated to a pluggable external coding-agent CLI while MOSS retains stage ordering and verdicts. Candidates are verified by replaying the batch against the candidate image in ephemeral trial workers, then promoted via user-consent-gated, in-place container swap with health-probe-gated rollback. On OpenClaw, MOSS lifts a four-task mean grader score from 0.25 to 0.61 in a single cycle without human intervention.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [423] [Flying Together: Human-Guided Immersive Shared Control for Aerial Robot Teams in Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21680)
*Lou De Bel-Air,Luca Morando,Ruitao Chen,Keru Wang,Benjamin Jarvis,Charbel Toumieh,Yang Zhou,Ken Perlin,Dario Floreano,Giuseppe Loianno*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A VR-based shared control framework for multi-drone teams that combines user-guided motion-primitive planning with admittance control, enabling real-time operator intervention and safe navigation in constrained, unknown environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous multi-robot systems struggle to adapt to unforeseen conditions and capture operator-driven objectives in unstructured environments.

Method: VR-based shared control with a user-guided motion-primitive-based planner for continuous collision-free trajectories, coupled with an admittance controller for flexible operator influence, plus a bilateral VR interface using migration points and visual feedback.

Result: Shared control improved obstacle avoidance, maintained inter-agent spacing, and reduced operator effort compared to fully autonomous approaches.

Conclusion: Immersive human-in-the-loop multi-robot navigation is feasible and advantageous for operating drone teams in constrained, unknown environments.

Abstract: While autonomous multi-robots can achieve safe and coordinated navigation, they often struggle to adapt to unforeseen conditions and to capture operator-driven objectives in unstructured environments. We present a Virtual Reality (VR)-based shared control framework for teams of drones operating in constrained and unknown environments, enabling real-time, user-guided exploration. At the core of our approach is a novel, user-guided motion-primitive-based planner that computes continuous, collision-free trajectories while continuously integrating operator input. This planner is coupled with an admittance controller, allowing the operator to flexibly influence team behavior and guide drones toward regions of interest that autonomous planners may overlook. The system supports mixed-reality operations with both physical and simulated drones, and implements a bilateral VR-based interface, allowing the operator to guide the robot team via migration points while receiving immediate visual feedback of the team state. Experimental results show that shared control improves obstacle avoidance, maintains inter-agent spacing, and reduces operator effort, demonstrating the feasibility and advantages of immersive, human-in-the-loop multi-robot navigation.

</details>


### [424] [Distributed Multi-Coverage for Robot Swarms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21686)
*Mariem Guitouni,Aaron T. Becker*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A distributed multicoverage algorithm for drone swarms that ensures redundant asset coverage using only local sensing and communication, without global coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable reliable multicoverage in practical drone swarm deployments where robots face limited communication, onboard computation constraints, and potential partial failures that prevent centralized solutions.

Method: A distributed multicoverage algorithm designed for robot swarms that operates purely with local sensing, local communication, and no global coordination.

Result: Not explicitly stated in the abstract.

Conclusion: The paper presents a distributed solution to the multicoverage problem that addresses practical constraints of real-world drone swarm deployments.

Abstract: Autonomous drone swarms deployed for surveillance, environmental monitoring, and infrastructure inspection must maintain reliable coverage of critical assets despite robot failures. This requires multicoverage: each asset must be observed by multiple robots for redundancy, with coverage requirements varying by asset importance. While recent work has solved the centralized problem optimally using integer programming, practical deployments face constraints that demand distributed solutions: robots operate with limited communication ranges, onboard computation restricts global planning, and partial system failures must not cause mission abort. We present a distributed multicoverage algorithm for robot swarms operating with local sensing, local communication, and no global coordination.

</details>


### [425] [Closed-Loop Sim-to-Real Reinforcement Learning for Deformable Microfiber Shape Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21688)
*Alessandro Amici,Houari Bettahar,Veeti Jaakkola,Quan Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Closed-loop sim-to-real RL enables microfiber shape control on surfaces using a policy trained in a simplified frictionless simulator and deployed directly to a real dual-gripper system at 40 Hz, achieving sub-millimeter accuracy across diverse fiber sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous contact-based micromanipulation is difficult because microscale surface interactions are hard to model accurately, limiting traditional model-based control and sim-to-real transfer.

Method: Train RL policy in a simplified frictionless simulator for geometric shape regulation, then deploy directly to a real dual-gripper system (40 Hz) using real-time visual feedback to iteratively correct unmodeled surface interactions, without retraining or domain adaptation.

Result: Mean point-wise shape error of 270±80 μm across 24 configurations; sub-millimeter error for 9 specimens varying diameter (50,80,120 μm) and length (10,15,20 mm) without retraining.

Conclusion: A policy learned in a simplified simulator can achieve repeatable real-world microfiber shape regulation under surface contact, as long as sim-to-real mismatches remain observable and correctable within the closed feedback loop.

Abstract: Autonomous contact-based micromanipulation is challenging because surface and interfacial interactions at the microscale are difficult to model accurately, limiting the use of conventional model-based control and sim-to-real learning. We present a closed-loop sim-to-real reinforcement learning (RL) approach for microfiber shape control on a surface. The central idea is to train geometric shape regulation in a simplified frictionless simulator and rely on real-time visual feedback during deployment to iteratively correct the observed effects of unmodeled surface interactions. An RL policy trained entirely in simulation is transferred directly to a physical dual-gripper micromanipulation system operating at 40 Hz, without retraining or domain adaptation. Using silk microfibers as a testbed, the policy achieves a mean point-wise shape error of 270 $\pm$ 80 $μ$m across twenty-four diverse initial configurations. Across nine specimens covering all combinations of three fiber diameters (50, 80, and 120 $μ$m) and three manipulated lengths (10 mm, 15mm, and 20 mm), the same policy achieves sub-millimeter final shape error without any retraining or retuning. These results show that a policy learned in a simplified simulator can achieve repeatable real-world microfiber shape regulation under surface contact, provided that the task-relevant effects of the sim-to-real mismatch remain observable and correctable within the closed feedback loop.

</details>
