### [426] [Motion Design for Grasp-Based Dynamic Locomotion in Microgravity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21704)
*Chaerim Moon,Joohyung Kim,Justin K. Yim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A study on design insights for grasp-based dynamic locomotion with multi-limbed robots in microgravity, using a parameterizable planning framework to evaluate gait patterns, stride length, speed, and posture across two quadruped morphologies. Key findings: enlarging feasible contact wrench space and reducing impulsive dynamics improve locomotion performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Microgravity locomotion relies on sparse, irregular anchors, requiring grasp-based mobility with deliberate whole-body coordination under coupled dynamic and kinematic constraints.

Method: A parameterizable locomotion planning framework supporting variations in gait pattern, stride length, locomotion speed, and nominal posture is proposed. Two quadruped morphologies are evaluated via physics-based simulation.

Result: Enlarging the feasible contact wrench space and attenuating impulsive whole-body dynamics improve locomotion performance in microgravity.

Conclusion: These findings inform strategies for contact configuration selection and whole-body coordination in microgravity locomotion with multi-limbed systems.

Abstract: Locomotion in microgravity often relies on sparsely and irregularly arranged anchors, motivating grasp-based mobility with multiple limbs. In this setting, dynamic locomotion is feasible only through deliberate regulation of both anchored interactions and whole-body coordination under coupled dynamic and kinematic constraints. This paper presents design insights for grasp-based dynamic locomotion with multi-limbed robotic systems in microgravity, targeting scenarios that require 6D limb manipulation to establish contacts with candidate anchors. The investigated design parameters include gait pattern, stride length, locomotion speed, and nominal posture. A parameterizable locomotion planning framework is proposed to support variations of these parameters and to evaluate the resulting locomotion performance in terms of stability and actuation demand. Two representative quadruped morphologies are adopted for evaluation in physics-based simulation. The results demonstrate that enlarging the feasible contact wrench space and attenuating impulsive whole-body dynamics improve locomotion performance. These findings inform strategies for contact configuration selection and whole-body coordination in microgravity locomotion with multi-limbed systems.

</details>


### [427] [PGDG: Physically Grounded Data Generation for Robust Bimanual Policy Learning from a Single Demonstration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21710)
*Cunxi Dai,Haoran Chang,Aditya Nisal,Rahul Kumar,Guofei Chen,Tao Chen,Yuzhe Qin,Guanya Shi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PGDG generates diverse, physically plausible recovery behaviors from a single demonstration for contact-rich bimanual manipulation, improving success rates dramatically (e.g., from 38% to 93% in simulation) without extra human labeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavior cloning for bimanual manipulation is hard due to expensive diverse demonstrations and vulnerability to off-manifold states where no recovery supervision exists.

Method: PGDG iterates between a physics-grounded sampler (generates rollout candidates) and a dataset curator (selects informative, non-redundant, recoverable behaviors) to update sampling distribution, plus short-horizon sampling-based control to relabel risky states.

Result: Outperforms spatial-only augmentation on four bimanual tasks; RotateBox-Pitch success from 38%→93% (sim) and 35%→82% (real); GR00T fine-tuning success from 46%→77%.

Conclusion: Zero-shot data curation from single demonstrations enables effective, diverse recovery behavior learning for bimanual manipulation.

Abstract: Behavior cloning for contact-rich bimanual manipulation remains challenging because diverse demonstrations are expensive to collect, and even small disturbances can push the system into off-manifold states where no recovery supervision is available. We propose PGDG, a data generation framework with zero-shot curation that expands a single demonstration into a compact dataset of physically plausible, successful, and diverse recovery behaviors without additional human labeling. PGDG iterates between a physics-grounded sampler and a dataset curator, where the curator selects informative, non-redundant, and recoverable behaviors to update the sampling distribution toward under-covered recovery modes, and the sampler draws physically plausible rollout candidates from this updated distribution and retains successful trajectories. To further improve data quality, PGDG applies short-horizon sampling-based control to relabel selected risky states with corrective actions. Across four bimanual manipulation tasks, PGDG consistently outperforms spatial-only augmentation in both simulation and zero-shot real-world transfer. On RotateBox-Pitch, success improves from 38% to 93% in simulation and from 35% to 82% in the real world. PGDG also enables effective foundation models fine-tuning such as GR00T, increasing success from 46% to 77%. Additional results are available in our website: https://cunxid.github.io/PGDG/.

</details>


### [428] [Mind the Gaps: Multi-Robot Feedback-Driven Ergodic Coverage in Unknown Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21719)
*Thales Costa Silva,Nora Ayanian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes an adaptive multi-robot coverage strategy that uses real-time environmental feedback and ergodic trajectory optimization to dynamically update target distributions, improving coverage efficiency in unknown or slowly changing environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ergodic search methods for multi-robot coverage assume a known target distribution, which fails to account for unknown prior distributions of the environment.

Method: An adaptive coverage strategy combining real-time environmental feedback with parametric models that are updated online, enhancing traditional ergodic trajectory optimization to construct target spatial information distributions dynamically.

Result: Simulation-based validation demonstrating improved coverage efficiency and resource allocation in unknown environments.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables robots to dynamically prioritize high-interest regions and optimize resource use without requiring prior knowledge of environmental distributions.

Abstract: In this work, we address the problem of multi-robot adaptive coverage, where teams of robots perform dynamic sampling by continuously adjusting their positions to collect data in an environment. This task can be challenging, particularly when robots must be efficiently allocated to new sampling locations over time. Ergodic search methods optimize robot trajectories by ensuring that the robots' time-averaged spatial distribution aligns with the spatial distribution of environmental information. While these methods promote effective exploration provided a target distribution, they often fail to account for unknown prior distributions of the environment. To overcome this limitation, we propose an adaptive coverage strategy that utilizes real-time feedback from an environmental model to adjust robot sampling behavior in response to unknown conditions. Our approach enhances traditional ergodic trajectory optimization by constructing a target spatial information distribution based on parametric models of the environment, which are updated online. This strategy assumes that the environment is either static or changes slowly compared to the robot's motion. Our framework allows robots to dynamically prioritize regions of high interest, improving coverage efficiency, synthesizing effective control policies for individual agents, and optimizing resource use in settings with unknown prior distributions. We validate our approach through simulations, demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing coverage and resource allocation.

</details>


### [429] [Learning Altruistic Collaboration in Heterogeneous Multi-Team Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21723)
*Riwa Karam,Ruoyu Lin,Brooks A. Butler,Magnus Egerstedt*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a graph neural network-based framework for heterogeneous multi-team robot collaboration, using Hamilton's rule from ecology for altruistic decision-making in dynamic robot allocation, validated in firefighting scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of heterogeneous multi-team collaboration where robots are transferable resources, and to leverage ecological altruism principles for efficient resource allocation across teams with different capabilities.

Method: Formulates the allocation problem as NP-hard combinatorial optimization; develops a graph neural network policy with centralized training and decentralized execution that approximates Hamilton's rule-based altruistic allocations over team interaction graphs.

Result: The learned policy achieves near-optimal performance while scaling to larger systems, validated through both simulations and real experiments in a firefighting scenario.

Conclusion: Hamilton's rule provides an effective altruistic decision-making mechanism for robot allocation; GNN-based policies can approximate this rule with good scalability and near-optimality.

Abstract: This paper studies heterogeneous multi-team collaboration through dynamic robot allocation, where robots are treated as transferable resources. Leveraging Hamilton's rule from ecology as an altruistic decision-making mechanism, we propose a multi-team collaborative resource allocation framework with heterogeneous capabilities, transfer costs, and capability-dependent contributions. The resulting allocation problem is combinatorial and is shown to be NP-hard. To address scalability, we develop a graph neural network policy under centralized training and decentralized execution that approximates the altruistic allocations based on Hamilton's rule. The model operates over the team interaction graph and predicts robot-level transfer decisions and next robot-to-team assignments. The proposed approach is validated in a firefighting scenario through simulations and experiments, demonstrating that the learned policy achieves near-optimal performance while scaling to larger systems.

</details>


### [430] [Safe and Steerable Geometric Motion Policies for Robotic Dexterous Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21811)
*Albert Wu,Riccardo Bonalli,Thomas Lew,C. Karen Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SafePBDS is a geometrically consistent framework for robotic dexterous manipulation that computes optimal, certifiably safe accelerations on configuration manifolds from objectives and safety constraints on arbitrary task manifolds. It introduces pullback control barrier functions and a task manifold action interface, achieving high success rates in dexterous grasping (92.5%) and enabling the first fully actuated palm-down in-hand reorientation exceeding 360°.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic dexterous manipulation requires reconciling objectives and constraints across heterogeneous geometric spaces (e.g., ℝ⁷ configuration manifold, SE(3) end effector poses, ℝ obstacle margins), which existing methods struggle to handle in a geometrically consistent and certifiably safe manner.

Method: SafePBDS builds on predefined task manifold dynamical systems. Its two key innovations are: (1) a pullback control barrier function construction that converts task manifold safety conditions into linear constraints on configuration manifold accelerations, and (2) a task manifold action interface that allows high-level policies to inject low-dimensional residual motions while preserving safety and maintaining autonomous behavior under zero input.

Result: On a 23-DOF Franka Panda-Allegro Hand platform: dexterous grasping achieved 92.5% success across 20 objects (120 trials); using the action interface, 94.4% 3-finger grasp success across 3 objects (36 trials); enabled the first model-based, fully actuated palm-down in-hand reorientation exceeding 360° yaw rotation under varying object weight and wrist motion.

Conclusion: SafePBDS provides a geometrically consistent framework with formal safety guarantees for dexterous manipulation, enabling efficient planning and safe exploration across heterogeneous task manifolds, validated through both simulation and real-robot experiments.

Abstract: Robotic dexterous manipulation requires continuously reconciling objectives and constraints defined on heterogeneous geometric spaces: a robot controlled on a $\mathbb{R}^7$ configuration manifold may need to track end effector poses on $\mathrm{SE}(3)$ while satisfying obstacle avoidance margins in $\mathbb{R}$. We present Safe Pullback Bundle Dynamical Systems (SafePBDS), a geometrically consistent framework that computes optimal, certifiably safe configuration manifold accelerations from objectives and safety requirements on arbitrary task manifolds. SafePBDS builds on prior work that combines predefined task manifold dynamical systems to produce autonomous motion. Its first innovation is a pullback control barrier function construction, which converts task manifold safety conditions into linear constraints on configuration manifold accelerations. The second innovation is a task manifold action interface that allows a high-level policy to inject low dimensional residual motions; zero input recovers the autonomous behavior, while safety is preserved under arbitrary inputs. This lets high-level policies efficiently steer exploration while leaving precise motion to the autonomous behavior. We validate SafePBDS in simulation and on a 23-DOF Franka Panda-Allegro Hand platform. On dexterous grasping, SafePBDS achieves a $92.5\%$ success rate across 20 household objects and 120 trials. Using the action interface, the method can exclude any one of the four fingers during grasping via a one-dimensional action, achieving $94.4\%$ 3-finger grasp success across 3 objects and 36 trials. The efficient planning and safety guarantee of SafePBDS also enables the first model-based, fully actuated palm-down in-hand reorientation, exceeding $360^\circ$ of yaw rotation in both directions under varying object weight and wrist motion. Demo video and details: https://tml.stanford.edu/safe-pbds

</details>


### [431] [Analytical and Experimental Force Analysis of a Soft Linear Pneumatic Actuator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21836)
*Mohammed Abboodi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper develops and experimentally validates a quasi-static analytical model for linear soft sleeve actuators (LSSAs), showing that output force (up to ~112 N at 125 kPa) decreases with extension and is influenced by pressure, geometry, displacement, external loading, and axial stiffness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft sleeve actuators (SSAs) offer advantages for wearable robotics by integrating actuation into sleeve-like geometries, but their force-generation behavior—especially regarding extension-dependent force variation, external loading effects, and axial stiffness—remains poorly understood.

Method: A quasi-static analytical model was developed, expressing net axial force as pressure-generated contributions from the cap and folded walls minus axial stiffness force. The model incorporates internal pressure, projected areas, folded wall geometry, axial displacement, and an experimentally fitted stiffness relation. Prescribed-extension and static-load experiments were conducted for validation.

Result: At 125 kPa, force decreased from ~112 N at zero extension to near zero at 40 mm. Static loading delayed measurable force generation and reduced output, especially at low/intermediate pressures. Results confirm coupled effects of pressure, geometry, displacement, loading, and axial stiffness govern LSSA force generation.

Conclusion: The proposed model effectively captures LSSA behavior, highlighting that axial stiffness and external loading are critical factors. This understanding aids future design and application of SSAs in wearable and assistive robotics.

Abstract: Soft sleeve actuators (SSAs) have recently been developed as a pneumatic actuation approach for wearable and assistive robotic systems. By integrating the actuation structure into a sleeve-like geometry, these actuators can reduce reliance on external attachment layers and transmission mechanisms while maintaining compliance with limb-shaped surfaces. However, the force-generation behavior of SSAs remains insufficiently explained, particularly with respect to the variation of output force during extension, the influence of external loading, and the mechanical role of axial stiffness. This paper presents an analytical and experimental force analysis of a linear soft sleeve actuator (LSSA). A quasi-static analytical model was developed by expressing the net axial force as the pressure-generated contribution from the cap and folded walls, reduced by the force associated with axial stiffness. The model incorporates internal pressure, projected pressure areas, folded wall geometry, axial displacement, and an experimentally fitted axial stiffness relation. Prescribed-extension and static-load experiments were conducted to evaluate the actuator response. At 125 kPa, the generated force decreased from approximately 112 N at zero extension to nearly zero at 40 mm. Static loading delayed measurable force generation and reduced force output, particularly at low and intermediate pressures. The results show that LSSA force generation is governed by coupled effects of pressure, geometry, displacement, loading, and axial stiffness.

</details>


### [432] [EvoScene-VLA: Evolving Scene Beliefs Inside the Action Decoder for Chunked Robot Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21862)
*Chushan Zhang,Ruihan Lu,Jinguang Tong,Xuesong Li,Yikai Wang,Hongdong Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EvoScene-VLA introduces a persistent, action-updated scene state across robot control chunks, improving VLA policy performance by maintaining geometry-aware scene priors that combine recent actions with fresh visual observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard chunked VLA policies only condition on current visual observations, ignoring that robot actions change the scene geometry (contact, occlusion, object motion) between visual updates, leading to outdated priors for subsequent decisions.

Method: EvoScene-VLA uses a recurrent scene prefix that carries geometry-aware scene state across chunks. A VLM combines current observation with action-updated prior; the action decoder outputs both actions and a compact scene update. Scene Predictor provides future scene-token targets during training; Geometric Anchor aligns scene slots with frozen depth/3D teachers. Both modules are discarded at deployment.

Result: On 31 RoboTwin tasks, success rate improves from 87.2% to 89.1% (fixed) and 86.1% to 88.5% (randomized). Outperforms all baselines on Galaxea R1-Lite real robot.

Conclusion: Maintaining an action-updated persistent scene prior across control chunks is beneficial for VLA policies, yielding consistent improvements in both simulation and real-world robotic tasks.

Abstract: Chunked vision-language-action (VLA) policies predict multi-step robot controls, conditioning each update on the current visual observation alone. Yet robot actions cause contact, occlusion, and object motion, and the geometry that later decisions depend on can change before the next visual update arrives. Spatial VLAs improve current-frame geometry. Temporal VLAs aggregate past frames. Neither maintains an action-updated scene prior across chunks. We argue for a persistent action-updated scene state across control calls, and introduce EvoScene-VLA. Its recurrent scene prefix carries a geometry-aware scene state across chunks. At each vision-language model (VLM) call, the VLM combines scene information from the current observation with the action-updated prior from the previous chunk; the action decoder outputs both the next action chunk and a compact scene update. This update becomes the next prior, which the VLM corrects against the new observation when the next call arrives. Each control call therefore starts from a scene prior that reflects both recent actions and fresh visual evidence. During training, \textbf{Scene Predictor} supplies future scene-token targets, and Geometric Anchor aligns scene slots with frozen depth and 3D teachers. We discard both modules at deployment. On 31 RoboTwin tasks, EvoScene-VLA raises average success from 87.2% to 89.1% in fixed evaluation and from 86.1% to 88.5% in randomized evaluation. On the Galaxea R1-Lite real robot, EvoScene-VLA outperforms all baselines.

</details>


### [433] [OCELOT: Odometry and Contact Estimation for Legged Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21863)
*Emre Girgin,Cagri Kilic*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a leg odometry pipeline using an Error-State EKF with fused contact detection (GMM+FSM and GLRT) to handle slippage, achieving accurate proprioceptive-only odometry across diverse terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve accurate legged robot odometry using only onboard proprioceptive sensors while robustly handling foot slippage, which is a significant challenge in legged robotics.

Method: An Error-State EKF (ESEKF) pipeline using IMU, joint encoders, and force sensors, with a fused contact detection module combining: 1) a force-based GMM-guided FSM for physical contact confirmation, and 2) a kinematic-based GLRT on foot velocity. Contact quality scores are fused to produce uncertainty signals.

Result: Validated on a 29-sequence, 2.4 km multi-modal dataset across diverse terrains (concrete, grass, pebble, rock). Outperformed both proprioceptive and exteroceptive baselines, especially in slippage-prone environments.

Conclusion: The proposed proprioceptive-only leg odometry with explicit slippage detection and rejection provides robust and accurate odometry estimates. Code and ROS2 package are open-sourced.

Abstract: One of the significant challenges in legged robotics is achieving accurate odometry using only onboard proprioceptive sensors. In this study, we present a complete leg odometry pipeline based on an Error-State EKF (ESEKF) that relies exclusively on proprioceptive data: a body fixed IMU, joint encoders, and force sensors, where filter's state is corrected by feet determined to be in a stationary stance. The core of our contribution is fused contact detection and an uncertainty quantification module designed to explicitly identify and reject slippage. This module runs two detectors in parallel for each foot, 1) a debounced, force-based Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) guided Finite State Machine (FSM) to confirm physical contact, and 2) a kinematic-based Generalized Likelihood Ratio Test (GLRT) on the estimated velocity of the foot. The continuous quality scores from both estimators are fused to detect if the foot is both physically loaded and kinematically stationary and served as an uncertainty signal for each contact. To validate our approach, we collected a multi-modal dataset of 29 sequences spanning diverse indoor and outdoor terrains (e.g., concrete, grass, pebble, and rock) total of 2.4 km long. We benchmarked our approach against both proprioceptive and exteroceptive methods. The results demonstrate our method's efficacy in providing accurate odometry estimates, robustly handling slippage-prone environments. We also share our code and real-time ROS2 package as open-source.

</details>


### [434] [Higher Order Reasoning for Collaborative Communicationless Mobile Robot Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21901)
*Jonathan Reasoner,Nicola Bezzo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a dynamic epistemic planning framework enabling multi-robot systems to implicitly coordinate in communicationless environments via higher-order belief reasoning, Bayesian updates, and MPPI-based trajectory control, outperforming first-order baselines in both simulation and physical experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenge of multi-robot coordination in communicationless environments where constant information exchange is unavailable, requiring implicit coordination strategies.

Method: A dynamic epistemic planning framework with higher-order belief particles, Bayesian belief updates, behavior tree action selection anticipating teammate decisions, and a temporally aware MPPI controller for low-level execution.

Result: Consistently reduces task completion time compared to a first-order baseline in both simulations and physical experiments.

Conclusion: Epistemic logic can serve as a robust foundation for resilient coordination in communication-restricted domains.

Abstract: In communicationless environments, multi-robot systems must operate without the constant information exchange that many coordination strategies typically assume. This paper presents a novel dynamic epistemic planning framework that enables implicit coordination and long horizon planning through higher-order reasoning among robots. With our approach, robots form and propagate higher-order belief particles, update world beliefs using Bayesian inference, and select actions via a behavior tree that anticipates teammates' likely decisions. A temporally aware Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller integrates this reasoning into low-level execution, allowing robots to plan intercepts and adapt trajectories under partial observability. The proposed framework is evaluated in both simulations and physical experiments, where it consistently reduces task completion time compared to a first-order baseline, demonstrating that epistemic logic can serve as a robust foundation for resilient coordination in communication-restricted domains.

</details>


### [435] [Non-Contact Vibration-Based Damage Detection of Civil Structures Using a Cost-Effective Autonomous UAV](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21914)
*Javier Becerril,Maximiliano Vargas,Jennifer Herrera,Joanna Gutierrez,Jorge Rios,Mohsen Amjadian,Constantine Tarawneh,Jinghao Yang,Qi Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a low-cost autonomous UAV system for non-contact vibration-based structural damage detection. Using vision-based motion tracking from various cameras (smartphones, USB camera, custom UAV), the system extracts vibration signals and identifies natural frequency shifts caused by damage. Validated on a lab-scale frame structure, all platforms successfully detect damage, with the UAV showing slightly higher errors (up to 5.7%) but achieving comparable performance to commercial systems at much lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a cost-effective, autonomous, non-contact approach for structural health monitoring using UAVs, particularly for scenarios where contact-based sensing is impractical (e.g., hard-to-reach structures).

Method: Vision-based motion tracking from video recordings (smartphones, USB camera, custom low-cost UAV with onboard camera and autonomous alignment system) to extract vibration displacement signals; frequency domain analysis to identify shifts in natural frequencies; validation against contact accelerometers and finite element model.

Result: All camera platforms successfully captured the fundamental frequency and its damage-induced shift. The custom UAV showed slightly higher errors (up to 5.7%) due to platform disturbances but reliably detected damage. The proposed low-cost system achieved comparable inspection performance to commercial UAV systems.

Conclusion: Low-cost autonomous UAVs provide a practical, flexible, and scalable solution for structural health monitoring, especially where contact sensing is impractical. The findings support potential deployment of multiple cooperative UAVs for enhanced inspection coverage and robustness.

Abstract: This paper presents a non-contact approach for vibration-based structural damage detection using an autonomous and customized cost-effective unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV). Vibration signals are extracted from video recordings through vision-based motion tracking to identify shifts in natural frequencies indicative of structural degradation. A laboratory-scale frame structure is evaluated under healthy and simulated-damage conditions. The proposed system is validated through an experimental study involving two smartphones, a USB camera, and a custom-built low-cost UAV equipped with an onboard camera and an autonomous alignment system for operation in GPS-denied environments. The displacement time is extracted and analyzed in the frequency domain and compared to reference measurements from contact accelerometers and a finite element model. Experimental results show that all platforms successfully capture the fundamental frequency and its shift due to damage. Although the UAV exhibits slightly higher errors (up to 5.7%) due to platform-induced disturbances and sensing limitations, it reliably detects damage-induced frequency changes. Compared to commercial UAV systems, the proposed platform achieves comparable inspection performance at significantly lower cost. These results demonstrate that low-cost autonomous UAVs provide a practical, flexible, and scalable solution for structural health monitoring, particularly in scenarios where contact-based sensing is impractical. The findings also support the potential for the deployment of multiple cooperative UAVs to further enhance inspection coverage and robustness.

</details>


### [436] [Auction-Consensus Algorithm with Learned Bidding Scheme for Multi-Robot Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21932)
*Jose Rodriguez,Constantine Tarawneh,Sven Koenig,Wenjie Dong,Qi Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a learning-enhanced auction-consensus framework that replaces CBBA's hand-crafted greedy scoring with a neural bidding policy trained via reinforcement learning (PPO), using multiple neural architectures (NAM, LSTM, Set Transformer). Results show improved solution quality over classical CBBA while preserving decentralized execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CBBA and similar auction-consensus algorithms rely on hand-crafted greedy scoring functions that often lead to suboptimal task allocations in decentralized multi-robot systems.

Method: The authors replace CBBA's deterministic bidding mechanism with a neural bidding policy trained via PPO under centralized training and decentralized execution. Rewards are shaped by proximity to MILP-optimal solutions. Multiple architectures (NAM, LSTM, Set Transformer) are evaluated.

Result: Learned bidding policies improve solution quality over classical CBBA across varying swarm sizes while preserving decentralized execution.

Conclusion: Integrating reinforcement learning with classical distributed coordination algorithms offers a scalable pathway toward higher-quality decentralized multi-robot task allocation.

Abstract: Multi-Robot Task Allocation (MRTA) is a central challenge in decentralized multi-agent systems, where teams of robots must cooperatively assign and execute tasks under limited communication while optimizing global performance objectives. Auction-consensus algorithms, such as the Consensus-Based Bundle Algorithm (CBBA), provide scalable decentralized coordination with provable convergence, but rely on hand-crafted greedy scoring functions that often lead to suboptimal task allocations. This paper proposes a learning-enhanced auction-consensus framework in which CBBA's deterministic bidding mechanism is replaced by a neural bidding policy trained using reinforcement learning. Under a centralized training and decentralized execution paradigm, agents learn to compute task bids from partial local observations while retaining the standard auction and consensus phases for decentralized coordination. The learned bidding policy is trained using Proximal Policy Optimization with rewards shaped by proximity to globally optimal solutions obtained via mixed-integer linear programming. Multiple neural architectures are evaluated, including a Neural Additive Model, the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model, and the Set Transformer Model. Experimental results across varying swarm sizes demonstrate that learned bidding policies can improve solution quality over classical CBBA while preserving decentralized execution. The proposed approach highlights the effectiveness of integrating reinforcement learning with classical distributed coordination algorithms, offering a scalable pathway toward higher-quality decentralized multi-robot task allocation.

</details>


### [437] [Learning to Evolve: Multi-modal Interactive Fields for Robust Humanoid Navigation in Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21935)
*Peifeng Jiang,Hong Liu,Jin Jin,Wenshuai Wang,Xia Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MIF is a humanoid-oriented multi-modal interactive field system that integrates confidence-aware semantic 3D Gaussian Splatting, discrepancy-triggered spatial memory updates, and task-driven geometric reconstruction to enable safe manipulation-oriented navigation under locomotion-induced perceptual distortion and environmental changes, improving relocation success from 12% to 94% while reducing memory footprint by 91.4%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantic mapping and scene-graph systems assume stable camera trajectories, static environments, or coarse object geometry, making them unsuitable for humanoid robots that experience locomotion-induced perceptual distortion and require manipulation-level geometric safety constraints.

Method: MIF couples three fields: (1) an uncertainty-aware 3DGS Appearance Field that suppresses gait-induced blur, (2) a Spatial Field for topological memory, and (3) a Geometry Field supporting Interaction Pose Safety. A discrepancy detection score separates locomotion-induced false positives from persistent changes, updating only locally inconsistent regions.

Result: On a Unitree-G1 humanoid in a real dynamic office, MIF improves relocation success from 12% to 94% compared to static scene-graph memory, and reduces semantic memory footprint by 91.4% through feature distillation.

Conclusion: MIF provides a robust, closed-loop perception-adaptation pipeline for humanoid manipulation-oriented navigation, achieving high reliability in non-static environments with significantly reduced memory requirements.

Abstract: Safe manipulation-oriented navigation for humanoid robots requires scene memory that remains reliable under locomotion-induced perceptual distortion, environmental changes, and interaction-level geometric safety constraints. Existing semantic mapping and scene-graph systems are difficult to deploy directly in this setting because they often assume stable camera trajectories, static environments, or coarse object geometry. We introduce the Multi-modal Interactive Field (MIF), a humanoid-oriented system that integrates confidence-aware semantic 3D Gaussian Splatting, discrepancy-triggered spatial memory updates, and task-driven geometric reconstruction within a closed-loop perception-adaptation pipeline. MIF couples three fields: an uncertainty-aware 3DGS Appearance Field that suppresses gait-induced blur, a Spatial Field that maintains topological memory, and a Geometry Field that supports Interaction Pose Safety (IPS) before manipulation. A discrepancy detection score is introduced to separate locomotion-induced false-positive changes from persistent changes and updates only locally inconsistent regions. On a Unitree-G1 humanoid in a real dynamic office, MIF improves relocation success in non-static environments from 12% to 94% compared with static scene-graph memory, while reducing semantic memory footprint by 91.4% through feature distillation for practical online operation. Project page and code: https://ziya-jiang.github.io/MIF-homepage/

</details>


### [438] [A Visitation Grid for Complete Coverage Foraging in Robot Swarms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21947)
*Qi Arturo Gonzalez,Yifeng Gao,Li Zhang,Qi Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a grid-based stochastic foraging strategy that reduces redundant visits and accelerates late-stage resource collection in swarm robotics, outperforming CPFA by up to 33% in total collection time and 48% in final-stage efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The final stage of resource collection (collecting remaining sparse resources) consumes a disproportionate amount of mission time in swarm foraging, yet most existing algorithms prioritize early-stage efficiency rather than addressing this end-stage bottleneck.

Method: Partitions unknown area into a grid map maintained by a lightweight central server. Robots report locations, server updates grid-level visitation counts. Each robot selects next search area from a local 3×3 neighborhood probabilistically based on lowest visitation count, biasing exploration toward under-visited regions while maintaining stochasticity.

Result: Extensive simulations show the strategy reduces total collection time by up to 33% and improves final-stage collection efficiency by more than 48% compared to CPFA. The method is robust, flexible, and scalable under limited onboard resources.

Conclusion: The proposed grid-based stochastic strategy effectively addresses the late-stage bottleneck in swarm foraging and can serve as a general enhancement for stochastic swarm foraging methods with limited computational resources.

Abstract: The complete collection of sparse resources in large, unknown environments remains a challenging problem for autonomous robot swarms. Previous studies have shown that a substantial portion of total mission time is consumed during the final stage of collection, where only a small fraction of randomly scattered resources remain. Consequently, many existing swarm foraging algorithms (search and collection) focus on collecting most resources within a limited time window, rather than improving end-stage efficiency for collecting all resources. We propose a grid-based stochastic foraging strategy that explicitly reduces redundant visits and accelerates late-stage collection. The unknown search area is partitioned into a grid map, which is maintained by a lightweight central server. To maintain scalability, both robots and the server operate within limited memory and computational constraints. The server updates the grid-level visitation counts based on robot-reported locations, producing a global estimate of the exploration density. For each new foraging trip, a robot selects its next search area from a local 3 X 3 neighborhood of grids probabilistically with the lowest visitation count, thus biasing exploration toward under-visited regions while maintaining stochasticity. Extensive simulation experiments demonstrate that the proposed strategy consistently outperforms the canonical centrally placed baseline foraging algorithm (CPFA). Compared to CPFA, the proposed method reduces the total collection time by up to 33% and improves collection efficiency by more than 48% during the final stage of the mission. These results indicate that the proposed strategy is robust, flexible, and scalable for near-complete and complete resource collection in robot swarms and can serve as a general enhancement for stochastic swarm foraging methods under limited onboard resources.

</details>


### [439] [TacO: Benchmarking Tactile Sensors for Object Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21976)
*Anya Zorin,Zilin Si,Myungsun Park,Junsung Park,Alexiy Buynitsky,Sachin Bhadang,Taejun Park,Sohee John Yoon,Yong-Lae Park,Oliver Kroemer,Zeynep Temel,Michael T. Tolley,Sha Yi,Xiaolong Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper systematically evaluates four tactile sensor modalities (visual, acoustic, magnetic, resistive) across three manipulation tasks (pick-and-place, reorientation, plug insertion), showing that tactile sensor utility depends heavily on task, sensor modality, and material properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While tactile sensing is widely believed to improve contact-rich manipulation, there is no empirical guidance on which tactile sensors work best for which manipulation tasks.

Method: The authors train separate manipulation policies for four tactile sensor modalities (visual, acoustic, magnetic, resistive) across three tasks (pick-and-place with unknown mass, object reorientation, plug insertion), and analyze how sensor properties (spatial resolution, shear sensing, tactile representation, material friction) affect task performance.

Result: Tactile sensing is not universally beneficial; its usefulness strongly depends on sensor modality, material properties, and the specific manipulation task.

Conclusion: The paper provides a framework for selecting and evaluating tactile sensors based on manipulation policy performance, and will release all sensors, code, data, and hardware setup publicly.

Abstract: Vision-based learning from demonstrations has achieved remarkable success in enabling robots to perform manipulation tasks and high-level semantic reasoning, yet it remains insufficient for complex, contact-rich manipulation. While there is broad agreement that tactile sensing improves manipulation, there is no empirical guidance on which tactile sensors are best suited for which manipulation tasks. In this paper, we provide a systematic, task-driven evaluation of tactile sensors for robot manipulation and propose a framework for selecting and evaluating sensors based on manipulation policy performance. Separate manipulation policies are trained for tactile sensors of four distinct modalities: visual, acoustic, magnetic, and resistive, across three tasks: pick-and-place with unknown mass, object reorientation, and plug insertion. For each task, an analysis of how sensor properties such as spatial resolution, shear sensing, and tactile representation, and the inherent material friction affect task performances is done. Rather than tactile sensing being universally beneficial in the same way, our results show that the usefulness of tactile information depends strongly on sensor modality, material properties, and the specific manipulation tasks. All of the tactile sensors, code, data, and hardware setup will be publicly available on the project website.

</details>


### [440] [Industrial Dual-Arm Box Handling via Online Inertial Estimation and Convex Wrench Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22021)
*Kenzhi Iskandar Wong,Lin Yang,Qian Ying Lee,Domenico Campolo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A friction-aware dual-arm box-handling framework that estimates unknown object mass and center of mass online, computes friction-feasible contact forces via SOCP under ellipsoidal friction-limit constraints, and achieves stable lifting without separately tuning slip avoidance and squeezing objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial robotic handling of boxes/packages with unknown mass and center of mass creates uncertainty in force-moment balance, leading to risks of slip, dropping, orientation deviation, or excessive squeezing.

Method: Online estimation of object mass and center of mass from measured contact wrenches, friction-feasible contact force/torque computation via second-order cone program (SOCP) under ellipsoidal friction-limit-surface constraints, and offline trajectory refinement to reduce undesired object-environment contact.

Result: Experiments on a real dual-arm robotic system with different center-of-mass configurations demonstrate stable lifting of objects with unknown inertial properties while maintaining stable frictional contact.

Conclusion: Enforcing friction feasibility as a hard constraint and minimizing contact effort within the feasible region enables stable lifting without treating slip avoidance and excessive squeezing as separately tuned objectives.

Abstract: Industrial robotic object handling often involves boxes and packages whose mass and center of mass are not known in advance. These uncertainties affect the force--moment balance required for stable lifting, and improper regulation of contact wrenches can lead to slip, object drop, orientation deviation, or excessive squeezing. This paper presents a friction-aware dual-arm box-handling framework for objects with unknown inertial properties. The proposed approach estimates the object mass and center of mass online from measured contact wrenches, and computes friction-feasible contact forces and torsional moments through a second-order cone program (SOCP) under ellipsoidal friction-limit-surface constraints. An offline trajectory refinement stage is also included to reduce undesired object--environment contact when geometric constraints are present. By enforcing friction feasibility as a hard constraint and minimizing contact effort within the feasible region, the framework achieves stable lifting without treating slip avoidance and excessive squeezing as separately tuned objectives. Experiments on a real dual-arm robotic system under different center-of-mass configurations demonstrate that the method lifts objects with unknown inertial properties while maintaining stable frictional contact.

</details>


### [441] [CoRMA: Contrastive RMA for Contact-Rich Meta-Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22082)
*Wentian Wang,Chutong Wen,Hongxu Ma,Wuhao Wang,Zhexiong Xue,Abdul Haseeb Nizamani,Dandi Zhou,Xinhai Sun,Jianqiao Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CoRMA is a contrastive meta-adaptation framework that uses semantic contact context (inferred online by a causal Transformer) to replace raw simulator-parameter adaptation for force-dominant robotic assembly tasks, achieving better real-world success than FORGE baselines without demonstrations or privileged inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing domain adaptation methods (like RMA) rely on raw simulator parameters and degrade substantially when transferred to real hardware for force-dominant assembly tasks. The authors aim to create a reusable adaptation interface using semantic contact inference.

Method: CoRMA replaces raw simulator-parameter adaptation with a compact 6D semantic contact context (contact onset, lateral engagement, guided transition, contact direction, jamming). A causal Transformer adapter infers this context online from force, proprioceptive, and action histories using semantic regression and a force-regime contrastive objective. At deployment, oracle context is replaced by inferred context, enabling within-episode adaptation without demonstrations, privileged inputs, or gradient updates.

Result: Evaluated on PegInsert, GearMesh, and NutThread in simulation and on a real Marvin arm. CoRMA retains higher verified real-world success under controlled target-pose noise compared to FORGE baselines, which achieve high simulation success but degrade substantially on hardware.

Conclusion: Semantic contact inference serves as a reusable adaptation interface within a related assembly task family. Broader unseen-task generalization and Real2Sim calibration remain future work.

Abstract: We present CoRMA(Contrastive Robotic Motor Adaptation), a context-based meta-adaptation framework that modifies RMA for force-dominant assembly. CoRMA replaces raw simulator-parameter adaptation with a compact 6D simulator-only semantic contact context describing contact onset, lateral engagement, guided transition, contact direction, and jamming. A deployable causal Transformer adapter infers this context online from force, proprioceptive, and action histories using semantic regression and a force-regime contrastive objective. At deployment, oracle context is removed and replaced by the inferred context, enabling within-episode adaptation without demonstrations, privileged inputs, or gradient updates. We evaluate CoRMA on PegInsert, GearMesh, and NutThread in Isaac Lab / Isaac Sim~5.0 and on a real Marvin arm. Compared with FORGE baselines that achieve high simulation success but degrade substantially on hardware, CoRMA retains higher verified real success under controlled target-pose noise. These results support semantic contact inference as a reusable adaptation interface within a related assembly task family, while broader unseen-task generalization and Real2Sim calibration remain future work.

</details>


### [442] [Beyond Pixels: Learning Invariant Rewards for Real-World Robotics From a Few Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22123)
*Tengye Xu,Yangting Sun,Ziju Shen,Guanqi Chen,Zhen Fu,Chen yizhou,Hua Chen,Jia Pan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a framework for learning invariant symbolic reward functions from as few as five demonstrations, enabling zero-shot generalization across position, viewpoint, and object variations in robotic manipulation tasks by discovering behavioral invariants rather than fitting visual features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing reward functions that generalize beyond controlled lab settings is a fundamental challenge in RL for robotics. Existing vision-based reward models memorize specific pixel distributions and fail to generalize across different object instances, positions, and camera viewpoints in open-world manipulation problems.

Method: A framework with two components: (1) a structural reward formulation encoding task-level strategies and physical constraints while preserving optimal policy invariance, and (2) a hybrid symbolic-numerical procedure that distills behavioral invariants from as few as five demonstrations without online interaction.

Result: On 8 Meta-World tasks and 3 Franka manipulation tasks, the method achieves stronger process alignment and policy rollout ranking abilities compared to baselines, accelerating downstream policy learning. Three real-world OOD experiments show zero-shot generalization to position, viewpoint, and object variations.

Conclusion: The proposed approach shifts from visual feature-fitting to discovering behavioral invariants, enabling a single reward representation to be reused across diverse task variants in practice, demonstrating strong generalization without requiring online interaction.

Abstract: Designing reward functions that generalize beyond controlled laboratory settings remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning for robotics. In open-world manipulation problems, a single task can appear in numerous variants through different object instances, positions, and camera viewpoints. Recent vision-based reward models tend to memorize specific pixel distributions and fail to generalize beyond their training conditions. To address this, we propose a framework that learns invariant symbolic reward functions from as few as five demonstrations. The insight is to shift from visual feature-fitting to the discovery of behavioral invariants: task-level properties that remain constant across diverse visual instantiations. The framework has two coupled components: a structural reward formulation that encodes task-level strategies and physical constraints while preserving optimal policy invariance, and a hybrid symbolic-numerical procedure that distills these invariants from demonstrations without online interaction. Experiments on eight Meta-World tasks and three Franka manipulation tasks demonstrate that our method achieves stronger process alignment and policy rollout ranking abilities compared to baselines, accelerating downstream policy learning. Three real-world out-of-distribution experiments further show that the same learned reward generalizes zero-shot to position, viewpoint, and object variations, enabling a single reward representation to be reused across diverse task variants in practice.

</details>


### [443] [Action with Visual Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22183)
*Weilong Guo,Yuchen Wang,Renping Zhou,Yunfeng Zhang,Rui Fang,Yue Meng,Wenda Xu,Yuan He,Gao Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AVP introduces a visual-primitive-centric interface that decouples VLM reasoning from action generation via flow-matching, achieving 27.61% improvement over π₀.5 on pick-and-place tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models entangle instruction comprehension, scene understanding, and motor control into a single objective, forcing the action expert to implicitly relearn perceptual capabilities already present in the pretrained VLM, limiting efficiency and generalization.

Method: AVP proposes an end-to-end architecture where the VLM infers next-stage targets and emits visual-primitive tokens that condition a flow-matching action expert, with supervision from end-effector kinematics.

Result: AVP improves success rate by 27.61% over π₀.5 on real-robot pick-and-place tasks, with consistent gains in data efficiency, spatial-compositional generalization, and object-level transfer.

Conclusion: Decoupling visual reasoning from action generation via visual-primitive tokens is an effective design principle for generalist robotic manipulation, significantly outperforming entangled VLA architectures.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist robotic manipulation. A common design in current architectures maps language instructions and visual observations to actions in a single forward pass. While conceptually simple, this formulation entangles instruction comprehension, spatial scene understanding, and motor control within a single learning objective. As a result, the action expert must implicitly relearn cognitive and perceptual capabilities already present in the pretrained VLM, which can limit both learning efficiency and generalization. We introduce AVP (Action with Visual Primitives), an end-to-end architecture that implements this visual-primitive-centric interface: the VLM infers the next-stage target and emits visual-primitive tokens that condition a flow-matching action expert, with supervision derived from end-effector kinematics. Real-robot experiments on general pick-and-place tasks show that AVP improves the success rate by 27.61% over pi_0.5 and outperforms other recent methods, with consistent gains in data efficiency, spatial-compositional generalization, and object-level transfer.

</details>


### [444] [Learning A Unified Risk Map for Autonomous Driving in Partially Observable Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22189)
*Jie Jia,Yaofeng Su,Zeyu Bao,Yun Hong,Bingzhao Gao,Zhongxue Gan,Wenchao Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A unified risk map framework for occlusion-aware prediction in autonomous driving, integrating traffic flow and collision risks with diffusion-based scenario generation, achieving significant improvements on Waymo dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing occlusion-aware methods either overestimate risk based on reachable states or fail to predict accurate trajectories under high occlusion uncertainty. A more balanced and fine-grained risk assessment approach is needed.

Method: (1) Unified risk map integrating traffic flow risk and collision risk via spatiotemporal modeling. (2) Diffusion-based scenario generation for realistic adversarial occluded interactions. (3) Risk-aware planning under partial observability within a unified framework.

Result: Outperforms SOTA occlusion-aware baseline on Waymo Open Motion Dataset: minimum time-to-collision improved by 0.78x, average time-to-collision improved by 1.67x.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers a comprehensive and practical solution for risk-aware planning in partially observable environments, addressing occlusion uncertainty more effectively than prior methods.

Abstract: Occlusion-aware prediction remains a critical challenge in autonomous driving due to the inherent uncertainty of unobserved regions. Existing approaches either overestimate risk based on reachable states or struggle to predict accurate trajectories under high occlusion uncertainty. To address these limitations, we propose a unified risk map modeling and learning framework for partially observable environments. Our method integrates traffic flow risk and collision risk through spatiotemporal modeling, enabling fine-grained assessment of occlusion-induced hazards. To address the scarcity of scenarios involving occluded interactions, we introduce a diffusion-based scenario generation framework that produces realistic yet adversarial scenarios. We integrate the modeling and learning of a unified risk map into a framework that supports risk-aware planning under partial observability. Experiments on the Waymo Open Motion Dataset show that our method significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art occlusion-aware baseline, improving minimum time-to-collision by 0.78 times and average time-to-collision by 1.67 times. The proposed framework offers a comprehensive and practical solution for risk-aware planning in partially observable environments.

</details>


### [445] [Imagine2Real: Towards Zero-shot Humanoid-Object Interaction via Video Generative Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22272)
*Jiahe Chen,ZiRui Wang,Feiyu Jia,Xiao Chen,Xiaojie Niu,Weishuai Zeng,Tianfan Xue,Xiaowei Zhou,Jiangmiao Pang,Jingbo Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Imagine2Real is a zero-shot framework for whole-body humanoid-object interaction that bypasses geometric priors and retargeting complexity by representing motions as unified 4D point trajectories and tracking only sparse keypoints via a Behavior Foundation Model's latent space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The scarcity of high-fidelity 3D data for whole-body humanoid-object interaction is a major bottleneck, and existing methods using video generative priors suffer from representation misalignment (reliance on geometric priors like CAD models) and retargeting complexity (intensive morphing and morphological mismatch).

Method: (1) Formulates robot and object motions as unified 4D point trajectories to resolve representation misalignment. (2) Uses a Keypoints Tracker that tracks only sparse critical points (base, hands, object) to bypass retargeting. (3) Employs the latent space of a Behavior Foundation Model (BFM) as the tracker's search domain for natural gaits. (4) Progressive training strategy with simple tracking rewards enables zero-shot physical deployment.

Result: Achieves zero-shot physical deployment within a motion capture (mocap) system, learning robust behaviors with only simple tracking rewards.

Conclusion: Imagine2Real provides a flexible, geometry-free alternative for whole-body humanoid-object interaction that overcomes both representation misalignment and retargeting complexity challenges.

Abstract: Whole-body Humanoid-Object Interaction (HOI) is bottlenecked by the scarcity of high-fidelity 3D data. While video generative priors offer a promising alternative, existing methods suffer from \textit{Representation Misalignment} due to their reliance on geometric priors (e.g., explicit CAD models), and \textit{Retargeting Complexity} arising from intensive morphing and morphological mismatch. We propose Imagine2Real, a zero-shot HOI framework for flexible, geometry-free interaction. To resolve misalignment, we formulate robot and object motions as unified 4D point trajectories. To overcome retargeting complexity, our Keypoints Tracker tracks only sparse critical points (base, hands, and object), entirely bypassing the error-amplifying retargeting process. To maintain natural gaits despite these sparse signals, we utilize the latent space of a Behavior Foundation Model (BFM) as the tracker's search domain. Using a progressive training strategy, Imagine2Real learns robust behaviors with simple tracking rewards, enabling zero-shot physical deployment within a motion capture(mocap) system.

</details>


### [446] [Spatial Memory for Out-of-Vision Manipulation in Vision-Language-Action](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22283)
*Pengteng Li,Weiyu Guo,He Zhang,Tiefu Cai,Xiao He,Yandong Guo,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SOMA is a Spatial Memory framework for VLA models that enables reasoning about objects outside the camera's view by constructing, refining, and retrieving spatial-semantic memory from multi-view head camera observations, improving task success and enabling near one-shot grasping in out-of-vision manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models assume task-relevant objects are always visible, leading to brittle and reactive behaviors when targets fall outside the camera's field of view.

Method: SOMA has three components: (1) Spatial Memory Construction — aggregates multi-view angular observations into a unified spatial-semantic representation via scanning; (2) Dynamic Memory Refinement — maintains global consistency over time; (3) Contextual Memory Retrieval — activates instruction-relevant spatial cues during manipulation.

Result: SOMA improves task success rates on five real-world out-of-vision manipulation tasks (including multi-step and dual-arm scenarios), with faster target localization, reduced viewpoint search, and near one-shot grasping under partial observability.

Conclusion: SOMA effectively equips VLAs with persistent spatial memory for out-of-vision reasoning, and its benefits also hold in conventional fully observable settings as validated on RoboCasa GR1 and SimplerEnv.

Abstract: We introduce SOMA, the Spatial Memory framework for Out-of-Vision Manipulation in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models. Most existing VLAs implicitly assume that task-relevant objects are always visible, leading to brittle and reactive behaviors when targets fall outside the camera's field of view. SOMA addresses this limitation by equipping VLAs with a persistent spatial memory constructed from multi-view observations acquired via a movable head camera, enabling reasoning beyond the current visual frustum. The framework consists of three components: Spatial Memory Construction, which aggregates angular-wise observations into a unified spatial-semantic representation through scanning; Dynamic Memory Refinement, which maintains global consistency over time; and Contextual Memory Retrieval, which activates instruction-relevant spatial cues during manipulation. We evaluate SOMA on five challenging real-world out-of-vision manipulation tasks, including multi-step and dual-arm scenarios where target objects are initially invisible. Experimental results show that SOMA not only improves task success rates, but also induces qualitatively different manipulation behaviors, with faster target localization, reduced viewpoint search, and near one-shot grasping under partial observability. Additional experiments on RoboCasa GR1 and SimplerEnv further validate the effectiveness of SOMA's memory design under conventional fully observable settings. Code will be released soon.

</details>


### [447] [How can reasoning capability empower the AI copilot robot in endoscopic surgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22322)
*Guankun Wang,Long Bai,Hongliang Ren*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper explores integrating reasoning capabilities into AI copilot robots for endoscopic surgery using Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, aiming to transform them from reactive executors into cognitive collaborators that enhance precision and safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The potential of reasoning-driven AI copilot robots in endoscopic surgery remains unexplored, despite advances in general domains. Current systems lack the ability to integrate multimodal cues, interpret surgical intent, and infer hidden tissue dynamics, leaving surgeons with unresolved intraoperative uncertainty and cognitive burden.

Method: Proposes implementing reasoning capabilities within Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models for AI copilot robots in endoscopic surgery, enabling integration of multimodal cues, surgical intent interpretation, and inference of hidden tissue dynamics.

Result: The paper presents a conceptual framework suggesting that reasoning-driven autonomy can transform AI copilot robots from reactive executors into cognitive collaborators.

Conclusion: Properly implemented reasoning can enhance precision, safety, and sustainability in clinical endoscopic surgery practice through AI copilot robots.

Abstract: Reasoning capability has significantly advanced complex logical inference and robotic decision-making in general domains. However, its potential in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) copilot robot-particularly implemented based on the Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model-remains unexplored in endoscopic surgery. Effective reasoning should enable AI copilot robots to integrate multimodal cues, interpret surgical intent, and infer hidden tissue dynamics, thereby alleviating intraoperative uncertainty and cognitive burden on surgeons. Properly implemented, reasoning-driven autonomy can transform AI copilot robots from reactive executors into cognitive collaborators, enhancing precision, safety, and sustainability in clinical practice.

</details>


### [448] [Real-Time Auto-Optimization in Unknown Environments via Structure-Exploiting Dual Control for Exploration and Exploitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22431)
*Shiying Dong,Haoyang Yang,Qiwei Liu,Wen-Hua Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a fast numerical dual control for exploration and exploitation (DCEE) method that exploits a convex-over-nonlinear structure to achieve approximately one order of magnitude speedup (83 μs max on vehicle CPU) for auto-optimization problems in unknown environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DCEE methods suffer from high computational burden, limiting real-time applications. The numerical structure of DCEE has not been fully exploited, leading to reliance on standard optimization packages or gradient-type update laws that are computationally expensive.

Method: The paper identifies that the DCEE reward function has an inherent convex-over-nonlinear structure (exploitation + exploration = unified nonlinear residual map with convex outer loss). A structure-exploiting numerical method linearizes only the nonlinear residual map while preserving the convex outer loss, transforming each subproblem into a structured convex form solved via generalized Gauss-Newton with positive semidefinite Hessian approximation using only first-order derivatives.

Result: Evaluated on a vehicle cruising auto-optimization problem via simulation and hardware-in-the-loop experiments. The method improves control performance and achieves approximately one order of magnitude speedup with microsecond-level max computation time of 83 μs on a typical vehicle embedded CPU.

Conclusion: The proposed structure-exploiting numerical DCEE method significantly reduces computational burden while maintaining principled exploration-exploitation trade-offs, making it feasible for real-time embedded control applications.

Abstract: This paper develops a fast numerical dual control for exploration and exploitation (DCEE) method to address auto-optimization problems in unknown environments. In auto-optimization problems, the optimal operating condition is unknown a priori and may vary with the environment. As in classical dual control techniques, computational burden remains a major concern in DCEE for active learning. Existing DCEE methods provide a principled exploration-exploitation objective, but mainly realized through standard optimization packages or explicit gradient-type update laws, where the numerical structure of the DCEE has not been fully exploited. This paper shows that the reward function in DCEE has an inherent convex-over-nonlinear structure, where the exploitation and exploration terms form a unified nonlinear residual map equipped with a convex outer loss. Benefiting from this structure, a structure-exploiting numerical method is developed by linearizing only the nonlinear residual map while preserving the convex outer loss. Thus, each subproblem is transformed into a structured convex form that can be solved reliably. The resulting generalized Gauss-Newton Hessian approximation is positive semidefinite and depends only on first-order derivatives, thereby supporting fast online computation. The proposed method is evaluated on a vehicle cruising auto-optimization problem and compared with existing methods. Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop experimental results show that the proposed method improves control performance and achieves a speedup of approximately one order of magnitude, with a microsecond-level maximum computation time of only 83 μs on a typical vehicle embedded CPU.

</details>


### [449] [Terminal Constraint Model Predictive Control for Image-Based Visual Servoing of UAVs with Kalman Filter-Based Moment Loss Compensation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22443)
*X. Wang,Y. Cao,W. L. W. Leong,Y. R. Tan,S. Huang,S. H. R. Teo,C. Xiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A TC-MPC framework with Kalman filter prediction is proposed to solve stability and visual feature loss issues in IBVS for UAVs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional IBVS controllers suffer from closed-loop instability near the target due to constraints, and control failure from intermittent loss of visual features during aggressive maneuvers.

Method: Proposes TC-MPC with terminal constraints/cost for recursive feasibility and stability, plus a Kalman filter to predict image moments during visual degradation.

Result: Validated through real-time UAV visual servoing experiments.

Conclusion: The TC-MPC + KF framework effectively addresses IBVS stability and visual feature loss problems.

Abstract: Image-Based Visual Servoing (IBVS) provides an efficient vision-guided control paradigm for unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) by directly regulating image-space errors. However, conventional IBVS controllers are vulnerable to two critical issues: loss of closed-loop stability near the target due to input and state constraints, and control failure caused by intermittent loss of moment-based visual features under aggressive motion. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a terminal-constraint model predictive control (TC-MPC) framework for IBVS, integrated with a Kalman filter (KF)-based state-prediction mechanism. The TC-MPC explicitly incorporates terminal-state constraints and a terminal cost into the IBVS error dynamics, ensuring recursive feasibility, improved convergence behavior, and closed-loop stability under control and state constraints. In parallel, the Kalman filter predicts the temporal evolution of image moments during short-term visual degradation, enabling the controller to preserve control continuity when moment measurements are partially unavailable. The proposed approach is validated through real-time UAV visual servoing experiments.

</details>


### [450] [Steins;Gate Drive: Semantic Safety Arbitration over Structured Futures for Latency-Decoupled LLM Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22456)
*Anjie Qiu,Hans D. Schotten*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SteinsGateDrive is a latency-decoupled planner-runtime architecture for cloud-hosted LLM driver agents that uses the worldline metaphor to select counterfactual driving futures before the final control instant, reducing effective lag from +3.07s to -0.01s while preserving safety boundaries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cloud-hosted LLM driver agents provide useful semantic judgments but suffer from inference latency that exceeds stepwise vehicle-control windows. Learned world models predict futures but typically keep future generation and action selection inside large coupled loops, creating latency bottlenecks for real-time autonomous driving.

Method: The paper proposes a planner-runtime architecture where an LLM selects counterfactual driving futures (worldlines) before the final control instant, and a runtime reuses the selected forecast while safety contracts remain valid. Three worldline roles are built: alpha (nominal ego-conditioned), beta (interaction counterfactuals), and gamma (hazard-stress futures like braking/cut-ins). The selected branch becomes a typed StrategicForecast with horizon, validity/abort conditions, fallback, and authority.

Result: On a within-subject, matched-seed normal-highway protocol with 10 seeds and 20 steps using GPT-5.4 mini, the architecture reduces effective lag from +3.07s at 1-second horizon to -0.01s at 4-second horizon while preserving the measured no-collision safety boundary.

Conclusion: The architecture's safety contribution comes from the atom-predicate runtime check, not from the drift score, which functions as a refresh-frequency knob. This demonstrates that latency decoupling via worldline selection is an effective approach for integrating cloud LLMs into real-time vehicle control.

Abstract: Cloud-hosted LLM driver agents provide useful semantic judgments, but their inference latency exceeds stepwise vehicle-control windows. Learned world models predict futures, but they usually keep future generation and action selection inside large coupled loops. We present SteinsGateDrive, a latency-decoupled planner-runtime architecture in which the worldline metaphor from the eponymous story names one plausible consequence of an intervention: the LLM selects counterfactual driving futures before the final control instant, and a runtime reuses the selected forecast only while safety contracts remain valid. The generator builds three world-line roles: alpha nominal ego-conditioned futures, beta interaction counterfactuals around nearby vehicles, and gamma hazard-stress futures such as braking, cut-ins, or blocked corridors. The selected branch becomes a typed StrategicForecast with horizon, validity/abort conditions, fallback, and authority. On a within-subject, matched-seed normal-highway protocol with 10 seeds and 20 steps, GPT-5.4 mini reduces effective lag from +3.07 s at 1-second horizon to -0.01 s at 4-second horizon while preserving the measured no-collision safety boundary. The architecture's safety contribution comes from the atom-predicate runtime check, not from the drift score, which functions as a refresh-frequency knob.

</details>


### [451] [Quantifying Full-Body Immersion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22521)
*Alihan Bakir,Ekrem Yüksel,Fabio Zuliani,Neil Chennoufi,Francesco Bruno,Jamie Paik*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A scalable modular robotic platform enables whole-body kinetic interactions for three levels of immersion (audio-visual, physical, and full-body immersion), bridging real and virtual worlds through adaptive surface units that dynamically render force, shape, and motion feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To dissolve the boundaries between real and virtual worlds and create unprecedented immersive experiences through whole-body kinetic interaction, moving beyond traditional limited sensory feedback.

Method: A scalable, distributable platform using modular robotic surface units inspired by nature's adaptive designs, which render immersive environments at any scale by distributing force, shape, and motion feedback across entire spaces in real-time.

Result: Achieves three levels of immersion: audio-visual (sensory realism), physical (haptic feedback), and full-body immersion (FBI) where whole-body dynamic interaction seamlessly integrates with virtual environments.

Conclusion: This framework bridges real and virtual worlds, enabling symbiotic whole-body interactions with virtual spaces and laying the foundation for a new era of human-environment synthesis.

Abstract: Humanity is at the forefront of yet another digital revolution, where the lines between real and virtual worlds are dissolving, reshaping how we perceive and interact with our surroundings. In this context, we introduce a transformative paradigm for immersive virtual experiences centered around whole-body kinetic interactions. Our approach redefines immersion through three distinct levels: audio-visual immersion, capturing sensory realism; physical immersion, delivering haptic feedback; and full-body immersion (FBI), where dynamic bodily interaction integrates seamlessly with virtual environments. At the core of this innovation lies a scalable, distributable platform based on modular robotic surface units inspired by the adaptive designs of nature. These units enable the rendering of immersive environments at any scale, from intimate personal experiences to expansive multi-user settings, dynamically adapting to interactions in real-time. The modular system distributes force, shape, and motion feedback throughout entire spaces, replicating the physical characteristics of the environment and enabling new depth of engagement through FBI. By combining scalability, adaptability, and dynamic physical engagement, this framework bridges the gap between real and virtual worlds. It offers an unprecedented level of immersion where users can engage their entire bodies in symbiotic interactions with the virtual space. This work not only advances immersive technology but also redefines how humans and virtual environments coexist, setting a foundation for a new era of human-environment synthesis.

</details>


### [452] [Branch-Stochastic Model Predictive Control for Motion Planning under Multi-Modal Uncertainty with Scenario Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22600)
*Zekun Xing,Ramkrishna Chaudhari,Marion Leibold,Dirk Wollherr,Martin Buss*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a method combining Stochastic Model Predictive Control with branching structures for autonomous driving motion planning, which handles multi-modal uncertainty in vehicle intentions and trajectories, reduces conservatism, and achieves real-time performance through scenario clustering and adaptive branching-time computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motion planning must handle multi-modal uncertainty in both intentions and trajectories of surrounding vehicles. Existing methods either suffer from excessive conservatism (worst-case) or remain conservative under intention uncertainty (SMPC with chance constraints).

Method: Combines SMPC with branching structure to generate distinct trajectories for different intentions while maintaining safety. Introduces scenario clustering to merge predictions based on high-level decision similarity, and adaptive branching-time computation to postpone commitment to separate plans until uncertainty is reduced.

Result: Simulations in challenging highway scenarios show the method improves safety, reduces conservatism, and achieves real-time computational performance.

Conclusion: The proposed branching SMPC approach effectively addresses intention uncertainty with less conservatism than prior methods while maintaining real-time feasibility.

Abstract: Motion planning for autonomous driving must account for multi-modal uncertainty in both the intentions and trajectories of surrounding vehicles. Handling uncertainty in a worst-case manner guarantees robustness but often leads to excessive conservatism.
